cases.title: Fussy Toddler Pulling at His Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever and ear-tugging for one day after a week of cold symptoms.
cases.history: An 18-month-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother for fever and irritability. He had a runny nose, nasal congestion, and mild cough for about a week that seemed to be improving, but yesterday he developed a fever to 38.9 °C at home, became fussy, slept poorly, and has been tugging at his right ear. He is feeding somewhat less but still taking fluids and making wet diapers. There has been no ear discharge, no neck stiffness, and no rash. He has had no vomiting or diarrhea. Past medical history is unremarkable; he was born full-term with an uncomplicated course. Immunizations are up to date, including PCV and annual influenza. He takes no daily medications and has no known drug allergies. He attends daycare three days a week and lives with both parents and an older sibling; there are no smokers in the home and he was breastfed until 12 months. Family history is notable for recurrent ear infections in the older sibling. Mother gave one dose of acetaminophen this morning with mild improvement in fussiness.
cases.exam: Alert but fussy toddler, consolable in mother's arms, well-hydrated with moist mucous membranes and brisk capillary refill. The right tympanic membrane is erythematous, opacified, and bulging with loss of the normal bony landmarks and light reflex; mobility is markedly reduced on pneumatic otoscopy. The left tympanic membrane is pearly gray and mobile. There is no mastoid tenderness, swelling, or posterior auricular erythema, and no postauricular protrusion of the pinna. Oropharynx is mildly erythematous without exudate. Anterior nares have clear rhinorrhea. Neck is supple with small mobile cervical nodes. Lungs are clear and heart is regular without murmur.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which examination finding is most specific for acute otitis media rather than otitis media with effusion or simple viral URI?
options: Erythema of the tympanic membrane alone
Clear rhinorrhea and nasal congestion
Small mobile anterior cervical lymph nodes
A bulging tympanic membrane with impaired mobility
stages.explanation: A bulging tympanic membrane is the single most specific sign of acute otitis media (AOM), reflecting middle-ear pus under pressure, and impaired mobility on pneumatic otoscopy confirms a middle-ear effusion. Diagnosis requires a middle-ear effusion PLUS signs of acute inflammation. Erythema alone (option A) is nonspecific—it occurs with crying, fever, or viral inflammation—and cannot establish AOM by itself. Clear rhinorrhea and congestion (option C) reflect the antecedent viral URI but do not localize disease to the middle ear. Mobile cervical nodes (option D) are a normal/reactive finding in any childhood infection. The combination of a bulging, immobile, opacified membrane is what distinguishes true AOM from a mere effusion or upper respiratory inflammation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this 18-month-old with unilateral AOM, fever of 38.7 °C, and only mild-to-moderate symptoms, which management strategy is most appropriate?
options: Immediate high-dose amoxicillin as the first step today
Immediate amoxicillin-clavulanate as the first step today
Tympanocentesis for culture before any therapy as the first step today
Observation with a 48–72 hour follow-up plan is reasonable, with analgesia
stages.explanation: For a child 6–23 months with UNILATERAL AOM and non-severe symptoms (temperature <39 °C, mild otalgia <48 h), AAP guidelines permit either immediate antibiotics OR observation with a reliable follow-up/safety-net plan, alongside adequate analgesia. This child fits the non-severe, unilateral criteria, so watchful waiting with analgesia is an evidence-based option that avoids unnecessary antibiotics. Immediate amoxicillin (A) is also acceptable but is not the only correct choice and is not 'most appropriate' when observation is offered. Amoxicillin-clavulanate (C) is reserved for amoxicillin in the prior 30 days, concurrent purulent conjunctivitis, or treatment failure—not first-line here. Tympanocentesis (D) is reserved for treatment failures, toxic/immunocompromised children, or research, not routine outpatient AOM.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The parents are anxious and prefer to start antibiotics now rather than wait. Which regimen is the appropriate first-line choice?
options: High-dose amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided twice daily
Azithromycin for 5 days as initial therapy
Cefdinir once daily as initial therapy
Amoxicillin-clavulanate at high dose as initial therapy
stages.explanation: High-dose amoxicillin, 80–90 mg/kg/day divided BID, is the first-line antibiotic for AOM in a child who has not received amoxicillin recently, has no penicillin allergy, and has no purulent conjunctivitis. The high dose overcomes intermediate penicillin resistance in Streptococcus pneumoniae. Azithromycin (A) has high pneumococcal resistance and poor middle-ear penetration; it is not preferred. Amoxicillin-clavulanate (B) is reserved for recent amoxicillin use, treatment failure, or concurrent conjunctivitis (which suggests Haemophilus influenzae)—none present here. Cefdinir (D) is a second-line agent used mainly for non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy. With no allergy and no recent antibiotics, plain high-dose amoxicillin is correct.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
Day 3 telephone follow-up:
Fever resolved within 36 hours of starting amoxicillin
Ear-tugging and fussiness markedly improved
Eating and sleeping back to baseline
No ear discharge, no new rash
Making normal number of wet diapers

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The child has improved on amoxicillin. What is the most appropriate guidance regarding follow-up and the residual middle-ear effusion?
options: Schedule urgent ENT referral for tympanostomy tubes now with appropriate follow-up
Switch to amoxicillin-clavulanate to clear the effusion faster with appropriate follow-up
Complete the antibiotic course
Repeat a hearing test today and re-treat with antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After clinical improvement, the family should finish the prescribed course. A middle-ear effusion commonly persists for weeks after AOM resolves and does NOT require additional antibiotics; it is followed expectantly. Referral and consideration of tympanostomy tubes are appropriate only for bilateral effusion persisting ~3 months with hearing impairment, or recurrent AOM (e.g., ≥3 episodes in 6 months or ≥4 in a year), so urgent ENT referral now (A) is premature. Re-treating or repeating audiometry today (B) is unnecessary in an improving child. Switching to amoxicillin-clavulanate (D) is for treatment FAILURE, not for clearing a residual effusion, which antibiotics do not accelerate. Reassurance and routine follow-up are correct.

cases.diagnosis: Acute otitis media (right), following a viral upper respiratory infection
pearls: AOM diagnosis requires a middle-ear effusion PLUS acute inflammation; a bulging tympanic membrane is the most specific single sign.
Watchful waiting with a 48–72 h safety-net is appropriate for children 6–23 months with unilateral, non-severe AOM, or ≥2 years with non-severe disease.
High-dose amoxicillin (80–90 mg/kg/day) is first-line; use amoxicillin-clavulanate for recent amoxicillin use, treatment failure, or concurrent purulent conjunctivitis.
A residual middle-ear effusion can persist for weeks and needs only follow-up—not more antibiotics—unless it lasts ~3 months with hearing loss.

cases.title: Wheezy, Struggling Infant in Winter
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of worsening cough, congestion, and fast, labored breathing in mid-winter.
cases.history: A previously healthy 4-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department in January for three days of rhinorrhea and cough that has progressed to noisy, rapid, labored breathing and decreased feeding. Mother reports the baby is taking only about half her usual bottle volume and had two episodes at home where she seemed to 'stop breathing' for a few seconds and turn slightly dusky before resuming on her own. She has had a low-grade fever to 38.2 °C. She has made fewer wet diapers than usual today. She was born at 38 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery and has had no prior hospitalizations or wheezing. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She attends home care with a 3-year-old sibling who currently has a cold. There are no smokers in the household, and immunizations are up to date for age. Family history is notable for asthma in the mother and eczema in the father. There has been no choking episode, no travel, and no sick contacts outside the home.
cases.exam: Small infant in moderate respiratory distress, alert and tracking but tachypneic. There is nasal flaring, subcostal and intercostal retractions, and visible tracheal tug. Auscultation reveals diffuse bilateral expiratory wheezes and fine inspiratory crackles with a prolonged expiratory phase. Nares are full of clear-to-white secretions. Mucous membranes are slightly tacky; anterior fontanelle is flat. Capillary refill is about 2–3 seconds. There is no stridor at rest, no grunting, and no cyanosis while crying. Heart sounds are regular without murmur. Abdomen is soft and non-distended with the liver edge palpable due to hyperinflation. No rash.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the age, season, and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bacterial pneumonia in this clinical context
Congestive heart failure from a congenital lesion
Viral bronchiolitis
Foreign-body aspiration
stages.explanation: A previously healthy infant under 2 years with a winter URI prodrome progressing to wheeze, crackles, retractions, and hypoxia has classic viral bronchiolitis—most often caused by respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). Apnea is a well-recognized feature in young infants. Bacterial pneumonia (A) typically produces focal findings, higher fever, and a toxic appearance rather than diffuse wheeze with a clear URI prodrome. Foreign-body aspiration (B) usually presents with a sudden choking event and focal/unilateral findings, absent here. Congestive heart failure (D) would more often show a murmur, hepatomegaly out of proportion, poor feeding from birth, and failure to thrive; the hyperinflation-related liver edge here is a red herring. The seasonal, diffuse, post-URI picture fits bronchiolitis best.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial intervention is most appropriate for this hypoxic, tachypneic infant?
options: Intravenous ceftriaxone after blood cultures
Supplemental oxygen, nasal suctioning,
A chest CT to evaluate the lung parenchyma
Nebulized albuterol every 20 minutes in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Bronchiolitis management is supportive: maintain oxygenation (supplemental O2 for sustained SpO2 below ~90%), clear the nares with suctioning to relieve obstruction, and monitor hydration and work of breathing. This child is hypoxic at 90% and feeding poorly, so oxygen, suction, and close observation are first-line. Routine bronchodilators (A) are not recommended—trials show no consistent benefit and AAP advises against routine albuterol. Empiric antibiotics (C) are unwarranted without evidence of a bacterial process; bronchiolitis is viral. Chest CT (D) exposes the infant to radiation without changing management; even routine chest X-ray is discouraged in typical bronchiolitis. Supportive care targeting oxygenation and secretions is correct.

stages.label: BEDSIDE TESTING
stages.content:
Nasopharyngeal respiratory panel: positive for RSV
Capillary blood gas: pH 7.34, pCO2 46 mmHg
Point-of-care glucose: 78 mg/dL
Chest X-ray (obtained for apnea): hyperinflation with peribronchial cuffing, no focal consolidation
After nasal suctioning and 0.5 L/min nasal cannula O2: SpO2 95%

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Considering her age, apnea episodes, hypoxia requiring oxygen, and reduced feeding, what is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Discharge after a single dose of oral dexamethasone
Admit for inpatient supportive care and monitoring
Discharge with a ribavirin prescription
Discharge home with albuterol and oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: Admission is warranted because this infant has multiple high-risk features: young age, witnessed apnea, hypoxia requiring supplemental oxygen, and inadequate oral intake with early dehydration. Inpatient supportive care—oxygen, suctioning, hydration, and apnea/respiratory monitoring—is appropriate. Discharge with albuterol and antibiotics (A) is wrong: neither is indicated in bronchiolitis and her instability precludes discharge. Corticosteroids (B) do not benefit bronchiolitis and should not be given. Ribavirin (C) is reserved for select severely immunocompromised patients and is not used routinely. Safe disposition here is admission for monitoring and supportive care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On the ward, the infant has recurrent apnea and rising work of breathing despite low-flow oxygen. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Begin empiric IV antibiotics for presumed sepsis as the leading concern
Escalate respiratory support with close monitoring
Start systemic corticosteroids as the leading concern
Administer a trial of nebulized epinephrine and discharge
stages.explanation: Worsening work of breathing and recurrent apnea call for escalation of respiratory support—high-flow nasal cannula or CPAP—which reduces work of breathing, supports oxygenation, and can decrease intubation rates while the viral illness runs its course. Empiric antibiotics (A) are not indicated unless there is specific evidence of bacterial coinfection; viral bronchiolitis alone does not warrant them. Nebulized epinephrine (C) may give transient relief in some settings but is not a durable therapy, and discharging a deteriorating infant is unsafe. Corticosteroids (D) have no proven benefit in bronchiolitis. Stepwise escalation of supportive respiratory care with continued monitoring is the correct response to clinical worsening.

cases.diagnosis: Viral bronchiolitis due to respiratory syncytial virus (RSV)
pearls: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in infants under 2 with a viral URI prodrome, wheeze/crackles, and retractions; routine X-ray and viral testing are usually unnecessary.
Management is supportive—oxygen, nasal suctioning, and hydration; bronchodilators, steroids, and antibiotics are NOT routinely indicated.
Apnea is a feared complication in young infants (especially <2 months or ex-premature) and is an indication for admission and monitoring.
Admit for hypoxia, apnea, poor feeding/dehydration, or significant respiratory distress; escalate to HFNC or CPAP if work of breathing worsens.

cases.title: Barky Nighttime Cough and Noisy Breathing
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden harsh, barking cough and noisy breathing that woke the child overnight.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department around midnight after waking abruptly with a harsh, barking, 'seal-like' cough and noisy breathing. For two days he has had a runny nose and low-grade fever, but tonight the cough became dramatic and his parents heard a high-pitched sound when he breathed in, especially when he became upset. He has been drinking fluids and has had no drooling, no difficulty swallowing, and no refusal to lie flat. There was no choking episode or witnessed aspiration of a foreign object. He was born full-term, has no prior intubations or airway surgery, and has never had a similar episode. Immunizations are up to date, including Hib and diphtheria-containing vaccines. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He attends daycare where several children have colds. He lives with both parents; no one smokes indoors. Family history is unremarkable. On the car ride over in the cool night air, the parents noted his breathing seemed to improve somewhat before worsening again in the waiting room when he cried.
cases.exam: Alert, interactive toddler sitting comfortably on the parent's lap, not toxic-appearing and not drooling. There is intermittent inspiratory stridor that is audible at rest and clearly worsens with crying and agitation; the cough is harsh and barky. There are mild suprasternal and intercostal retractions when he becomes upset but minimal retraction at rest. Voice/cry is slightly hoarse. The oropharynx is mildly injected without exudate, asymmetry, or trismus; he has no difficulty handling secretions. Lungs have good bilateral air entry without focal wheeze or crackles once he is calm. Heart is regular without murmur. There is no cyanosis and capillary refill is brisk. No tripod posturing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains this toddler's barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarse voice after a brief URI prodrome?
options: Retropharyngeal abscess
Bacterial tracheitis
Epiglottitis in this clinical context
Croup
stages.explanation: The classic triad of a barking ('seal-like') cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarseness following a mild viral prodrome in a toddler is croup, usually caused by parainfluenza virus. The non-toxic appearance, comfortable posture, and absence of drooling support it. Epiglottitis (A) presents with rapid onset of high fever, a toxic child, drooling, muffled 'hot-potato' voice, and tripod positioning—now rare due to Hib vaccination, and not seen here. Bacterial tracheitis (C) features high fever, a toxic appearance, and worsening stridor with copious purulent secretions, typically after a viral illness; this child is well-appearing. Retropharyngeal abscess (D) causes neck stiffness, dysphagia, drooling, and trismus, none present. The barky cough with stridor points to croup.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The child has stridor at rest and mild retractions. What is the single most appropriate initial pharmacologic intervention?
options: A single dose of oral or IM dexamethasone
Lateral neck radiograph before any treatment
Nebulized albuterol as the first step today
Empiric IV ceftriaxone and vancomycin
stages.explanation: A single dose of dexamethasone (commonly 0.6 mg/kg, oral or IM) is the cornerstone of croup management and is recommended for essentially all severities—mild, moderate, and severe—because it reduces symptom duration, return visits, and need for additional care. With stridor at rest, this child clearly warrants steroids. Empiric broad antibiotics (B) treat bacterial airway infections like tracheitis or epiglottitis, which are not suggested in this well-appearing, vaccinated child. Albuterol (C) treats bronchospasm/lower-airway disease, not the subglottic edema of croup, and is ineffective. A lateral neck film (D) is not required to diagnose typical croup and should never delay treatment in a child with airway symptoms; imaging is reserved for atypical or uncertain cases.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Thirty minutes later he has worsening stridor at rest with increased retractions and appears more agitated. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Administer a second dose of dexamethasone immediately
Give nebulized racemic epinephrine
Discharge home with humidified air instructions as monotherapy
Proceed directly to endotracheal intubation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For moderate-to-severe croup with stridor at rest and increased work of breathing, nebulized racemic epinephrine provides rapid reduction of subglottic edema. Because its effect can wane in ~2 hours, the child must be observed for at least 3–4 hours afterward to ensure no 'rebound' stridor before discharge. A second steroid dose now (A) is unnecessary—one dose is sufficient and onset takes a few hours. Immediate intubation (C) is reserved for impending respiratory failure or failure to respond to epinephrine and steroids; this child has not reached that point. Discharge with humidified air (D) is unsafe given worsening rest stridor, and humidity has no proven efficacy. Racemic epinephrine with observation is the correct escalation.

stages.label: OBSERVATION PERIOD
stages.content:
After nebulized racemic epinephrine:
Stridor at rest resolved within 15 minutes
Retractions resolved; comfortable on parent's lap
3-hour observation: no recurrence of rest stridor or rebound symptoms
SpO2 98% on room air, tolerating oral fluids
Remains afebrile after antipyretics, alert and playful

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After 3 hours of observation he has no rest stridor and looks well. What is the most appropriate disposition and counseling?
options: Discharge with a 5-day course of oral prednisolone with appropriate follow-up
Discharge with a prescription for oral antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
Discharge home with return precautions
Admit for IV antibiotics and overnight monitoring with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: A child with croup who has received dexamethasone, responded to epinephrine, and shows no rest stridor after an adequate observation period (no rebound) can be safely discharged with clear return precautions. Parents should know croup is viral and self-limited—cough and stridor typically peak on days 2–3 and resolve within roughly a week—and should return for stridor at rest, retractions, or difficulty breathing. Admission for IV antibiotics (A) is unnecessary in uncomplicated viral croup. Oral antibiotics (C) do not treat a viral illness. A multi-day prednisolone course (D) is not needed; a single dose of dexamethasone is the standard and sufficient steroid treatment. Discharge with anticipatory guidance is correct.

pearls: Croup = barking cough + inspiratory stridor + hoarseness after a viral prodrome; a toxic, drooling, tripod child suggests epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis instead.
Give dexamethasone (~0.6 mg/kg, single dose) for ALL severities of croup, including mild disease.
Add nebulized racemic epinephrine for stridor at rest; observe 3–4 hours afterward for rebound before discharge.
Imaging (the 'steeple sign') is not needed for typical croup and should never delay treatment of airway symptoms.

cases.title: Sore Throat and Fever Without a Cough
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of painful sore throat, fever, and trouble swallowing without any cough.
cases.history: A 7-year-old girl presents to clinic with two days of progressively painful sore throat, fever to 38.8 °C at home, and difficulty swallowing. She points to the back of her throat as the source of pain and complains that her neck is tender. Notably, she has had NO cough, no runny nose, and no hoarseness. She has had a mild headache and a stomachache with one episode of non-bloody emesis this morning. She has no rash and no joint pain. She is otherwise healthy, born full-term, fully immunized, takes no daily medications, and has no drug allergies. She is in second grade, where a classmate was recently sent home with 'strep throat.' She lives with her parents and a younger brother. There is no family history of rheumatic fever, kidney disease, or immune disorders. She has been drinking less because swallowing hurts but is still making urine. Acetaminophen has provided partial relief of her pain and fever.
cases.exam: Well-appearing but uncomfortable child, not toxic and not drooling, speaking in a normal voice. The oropharynx shows symmetric tonsillar enlargement with white-yellow exudate over both tonsils and palatal petechiae; there is no asymmetry, uvular deviation, or trismus. There are tender, enlarged anterior cervical lymph nodes bilaterally. The skin is without rash; specifically no sandpaper-like erythematous rash and no strawberry tongue noted today. Conjunctivae are clear without injection. There is no cough during the visit. Lungs are clear and the abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness but no organomegaly. No nuchal rigidity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Using the Centor/McIsaac criteria, which combination of this child's features most increases the likelihood of group A streptococcal pharyngitis?
options: Presence of cough and rhinorrhea as the first step today
Conjunctival injection and hoarseness as the first step today
Diffuse myalgias and clear nasal discharge as the first step today
Tonsillar exudate, tender anterior cervical nodes, fever, absence of cough,
stages.explanation: The Centor criteria (with McIsaac age modification) score the likelihood of group A strep: tonsillar exudate, tender/swollen anterior cervical nodes, fever >38 °C, and ABSENCE of cough each add a point, and age 3–14 adds a point. This child has all of these, yielding a high score and a high pretest probability of strep. Cough and rhinorrhea (A) point toward a VIRAL cause and would LOWER the score. Conjunctival injection and hoarseness (C) are viral features. Myalgias and clear rhinorrhea (D) also suggest a virus. The exudate-plus-tender-nodes-plus-fever-plus-no-cough pattern in a school-aged child is the classic strep picture and drives the decision to test.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given her high Centor/McIsaac score, what is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Treat empirically with antibiotics without any testing as the primary diagnosis
Obtain a throat culture only and withhold all treatment until it results in 48 hours
Order a monospot (heterophile antibody) test as the primary diagnosis
Perform a rapid antigen detection test, with throat culture backup if negative in a child
stages.explanation: Even with a high clinical score, guidelines recommend confirming group A strep with a rapid antigen detection test (RADT) before treating, because clinical features overlap with viral causes. In CHILDREN, a negative RADT should be backed up by throat culture due to lower test sensitivity and the higher consequence of missed strep (rheumatic fever). Empiric treatment without testing (A) leads to antibiotic overuse for what is often viral pharyngitis. A monospot (C) screens for infectious mononucleosis, not strep, and is not the confirmatory test here. Culture-only with delayed treatment (D) is unnecessarily slow when a positive RADT allows immediate treatment; culture is the backup for negatives, not the primary test in this setting.

stages.label: POINT-OF-CARE TEST
stages.content:
Rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for group A Streptococcus: POSITIVE
Reflex throat culture: not required given positive RADT
No signs of peritonsillar abscess on exam
Patient tolerating sips of fluid after analgesia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her rapid strep test is positive and she has no drug allergies. What is the first-line antibiotic?
options: Azithromycin for 5 days as initial therapy
Ciprofloxacin for 7 days as initial therapy
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 10 days
Oral penicillin V or amoxicillin for 10 days
stages.explanation: Penicillin V or amoxicillin for a full 10-day course is first-line for group A streptococcal pharyngitis in a non-allergic patient. Group A strep remains universally susceptible to penicillin, and the 10-day course is required to prevent acute rheumatic fever. Azithromycin (A) is reserved for penicillin allergy and faces rising macrolide resistance, so it is not first-line. Ciprofloxacin (C) is a fluoroquinolone with poor activity against group A strep and is inappropriate (and generally avoided in children). Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (D) does not reliably eradicate group A strep and does not prevent rheumatic fever, so it must not be used for strep pharyngitis. Penicillin/amoxicillin is the correct, narrow-spectrum, rheumatic-fever-preventing choice.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point about return to school and complications is most accurate?
options: She should avoid antibiotics now because treatment increases the risk of rheumatic fever
She must finish all 10 days of antibiotics before returning to school based on current risk
She may return to school once she has been on antibiotics for at least 24 hours
Antibiotics are mainly given to prevent post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
stages.explanation: A child with strep pharyngitis can return to school after at least 24 hours of antibiotics AND clinical improvement (afebrile, feeling better), because she is no longer considered contagious. She does not need to complete all 10 days first (B)—the full course is to prevent rheumatic fever, not a requirement for return. Antibiotics primarily prevent acute rheumatic FEVER and suppurative complications (peritonsillar abscess) and shorten symptoms modestly. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (C) is NOT reliably prevented by antibiotics, so that is not the main rationale. Treatment lowers, not raises, the risk of rheumatic fever (D is the opposite of the truth). The 24-hour rule with clinical improvement is the correct return-to-school guidance.

pearls: Centor/McIsaac criteria: fever, tonsillar exudate, tender anterior cervical nodes, NO cough, and age 3–14 raise the probability of group A strep.
Confirm with a rapid antigen test; in children, back up a negative RADT with throat culture.
Penicillin or amoxicillin for 10 days is first-line and prevents acute rheumatic fever; macrolides only for true penicillin allergy.
Children may return to school after ~24 hours of antibiotics and clinical improvement; antibiotics prevent rheumatic fever but not glomerulonephritis.

cases.title: Mouth Sores and a Spotty Rash on Hands and Feet
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful mouth sores, fever, and a new spotty rash on the hands and feet.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic for two days of fever to 38.5 °C, fussiness, and reduced eating because of painful mouth sores, followed today by a rash on his hands and feet that his parents noticed at bath time. He has been drooling more than usual and refuses solid foods, though he will take cold liquids and popsicles. He has had no vomiting or diarrhea, no difficulty breathing, and no neck stiffness. He attends a daycare where several children have recently had similar mouth sores and fevers; it is late summer. He was born full-term, is fully immunized, takes no daily medications, and has no known allergies. He lives with his parents and an infant sibling. There is no history of immunodeficiency or recurrent infections. Family history is unremarkable. His parents are worried because he is eating so little, though he continues to make wet diapers and produces tears when he cries. No one in the home has cold sores or a history of recurrent oral herpes.
cases.exam: Fussy but consolable, well-hydrated toddler with moist eyes and a moist (if slightly tacky) oral mucosa. The oropharynx and anterior mouth show several shallow yellow-gray ulcers with erythematous halos scattered on the tongue, buccal mucosa, and soft palate; the gingivae are NOT swollen, friable, or bleeding. On the palms, soles, and a few spots on the buttocks there are small oval gray-white vesicles and erythematous macules, some with a thin surrounding rim of erythema; the lesions are mildly tender but not pruritic. There is no periorificial honey-crusting and no grouped vesicles in a single dermatome. Cervical nodes are small and mobile. Lungs are clear and the abdomen is soft.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis given oral ulcers plus vesicles on the palms, soles, and buttocks in a daycare-attending toddler in summer?
options: Aphthous stomatitis in this clinical context
Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis
Hand-foot-and-mouth disease
Varicella (chickenpox)
stages.explanation: The combination of painful oral ulcers with vesicles distributed on the palms, soles, and buttocks in a daycare-attending toddler during late summer/early fall is classic hand-foot-and-mouth disease, usually caused by coxsackievirus A16 (or enterovirus 71). Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis (A) causes prominent, swollen, friable, bleeding gingivae and perioral vesicles but does NOT produce the acral palm/sole distribution. Aphthous stomatitis (B) causes isolated oral ulcers without fever or a hand/foot rash. Varicella (C) produces a generalized, intensely pruritic rash with lesions in different stages ('dewdrops on a rose petal') over the trunk and face rather than a palm/sole/buttock-predominant pattern, and this child is vaccinated. The acral distribution with oral ulcers points to HFMD.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature on history/exam is most reassuring that this child does not currently need intravenous fluids?
options: He has vesicles on the palms and soles in this clinical context
He attends daycare with other sick children
He has a temperature of 38.4 °C as the first step today
He is making wet diapers, producing tears,
stages.explanation: The main risk in HFMD is dehydration from painful oral ulcers limiting intake. Reassuring hydration signs—ongoing wet diapers (urine output), tears with crying, and moist/only mildly tacky mucous membranes—indicate he is maintaining adequate hydration and can be managed with oral fluids and analgesia, not IV fluids. Fever (A) reflects the viral illness but does not by itself indicate dehydration. Palm/sole vesicles (C) confirm the diagnosis but say nothing about hydration status. Daycare exposure (D) is epidemiologic context, not a hydration marker. Tracking urine output, tears, and mucous membranes is how you decide whether oral rehydration suffices, and here it clearly does.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan for this child?
options: Oral acyclovir to shorten the course as initial therapy
Topical and oral antibiotics for the skin lesions as initial therapy
Supportive care: analgesia/antipyretics, cold soft foods,
Systemic corticosteroids to reduce mucosal inflammation as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease is a self-limited viral illness; management is purely supportive. Adequate analgesia (acetaminophen or ibuprofen), offering cool, soft, non-acidic foods and fluids (popsicles, cold milk/water), and watching hydration are the core measures, with symptoms resolving in about 7–10 days. Acyclovir (A) treats herpes infections, not coxsackievirus, and has no role in HFMD. Antibiotics (B) are unnecessary because the lesions are viral, not bacterial, with no signs of secondary infection. Systemic corticosteroids (D) are not indicated and could be harmful in a viral infection. The correct approach is symptomatic relief and ensuring the child keeps drinking.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
1-week telephone follow-up:
Fever resolved by day 3
Oral ulcers healed; eating normally again
Palm and sole lesions fading without scarring
No dehydration; stayed on oral fluids throughout
Weeks later: mother reports peeling of skin around the fingernails and a transient nail line

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The parents ask about contagiousness and the late nail finding. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Nail shedding indicates a bacterial superinfection needing antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
He is no longer contagious the moment the fever breaks and sheds no virus afterward with appropriate follow-up
The virus spreads via fecal-oral
He must be excluded from daycare for a full month with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Coxsackievirus is shed in respiratory secretions for a week or two and in stool for several weeks, so transmission can continue after symptoms resolve—good hand hygiene is key, and exclusion is generally tied to fever resolution and ability to participate, not a fixed long quarantine. Onychomadesis (nail-plate separation/shedding) and Beau's lines are well-recognized, benign, self-limited findings weeks after HFMD. A month-long daycare exclusion (A) is unnecessary. Nail shedding is not a bacterial superinfection (C) and needs no antibiotics. The virus is NOT cleared the instant the fever breaks (D)—prolonged fecal shedding is characteristic. The accurate counseling covers prolonged shedding and reassurance about the late nail changes.

pearls: HFMD = painful oral ulcers plus vesicles on the palms, soles, and often buttocks in a young child, peaking in summer/fall.
Herpetic gingivostomatitis is distinguished by swollen, bleeding gingivae and perioral vesicles without an acral rash.
Treatment is supportive; the main risk is dehydration from painful oral intake—watch urine output, tears, and mucous membranes.
Coxsackievirus is shed in stool for weeks; onychomadesis (nail shedding) is a benign late finding.

cases.title: Itchy Red Patches in the Skin Folds
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent itchy, red, scaly patches on the cheeks and behind the knees that disrupt sleep.
cases.history: A 9-month-old girl is brought to clinic for a chronic, intensely itchy rash that flares and remits. Her mother reports red, dry, scaly patches that began on the cheeks and scalp around 3 months of age and have since spread to the antecubital and popliteal folds. The infant scratches constantly, sleeps poorly, and is fussy during flares. The rash worsens in winter with dry indoor heat and after baths with scented soap. There is no fever, no oozing honey-colored crust, and no spreading red streaks. She was born full-term and is otherwise growing and developing normally along her curves. She takes vitamin D drops and no other medications; she has no known drug allergies. She lives with both parents and a cat; no smokers in the home. Family history is strongly atopic: the mother has asthma and the father has seasonal allergic rhinitis, and an older sibling had eczema as a baby. The parents have been using only fragranced lotion intermittently and bathing her daily with bubble bath.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful, well-nourished infant at her growth percentiles. The skin shows ill-defined erythematous, dry, scaly patches with excoriations on the cheeks, the extensor surfaces, and notably the antecubital and popliteal flexures; the diaper area is spared. The skin overall is dry (xerotic). There is no honey-colored crusting, no pustules, no fluctuant areas, and no surrounding cellulitic erythema or warmth. There is mild post-inflammatory hypopigmentation in healed areas. No lymphangitic streaking and no regional lymphadenopathy. Mucous membranes are normal and there is no conjunctival involvement. The remainder of the exam, including the lungs and abdomen, is normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this chronic, relapsing, intensely pruritic rash in the flexures of an atopic-family infant?
options: Seborrheic dermatitis
Scabies as the primary diagnosis
Allergic contact dermatitis
Atopic dermatitis (eczema)
stages.explanation: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing, intensely pruritic, xerotic dermatitis with age-typical distribution—face and extensors in infancy, flexures (antecubital/popliteal) as the child grows—occurring in a child with a strong atopic family history (asthma, allergic rhinitis). All of these are present. Seborrheic dermatitis (A) favors the scalp ('cradle cap'), eyebrows, and diaper area with greasy yellow scale and is typically NON-pruritic, and the diaper area here is spared. Allergic contact dermatitis (C) is localized to areas of allergen contact in a specific pattern, not this diffuse flexural distribution. Scabies (D) causes burrows in web spaces, wrists, and axillae with nocturnal itch and often family-wide itching; the flexural, atopic pattern here is classic eczema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of maintenance therapy that should be the foundation of this infant's daily regimen?
options: Daily oral antibiotics to prevent infection
Oral antihistamines around the clock as the first step today
Liberal emollients/moisturizers plus gentle skin care
A long course of oral corticosteroids as the first step today
stages.explanation: The foundation of atopic dermatitis care is restoring the skin barrier: liberal, frequent emollient (moisturizer) use—especially right after short, lukewarm baths—along with gentle, fragrance-free cleansers and avoiding triggers like harsh soaps, bubble bath, and overheating. This reduces flares and the need for medications. Around-the-clock antihistamines (A) do not control the underlying inflammation; sedating antihistamines may help sleep during severe itch but are adjunctive, not foundational. Systemic corticosteroids (C) are avoided in routine pediatric eczema because of rebound and systemic side effects. Daily prophylactic antibiotics (D) are not used; antibiotics are reserved for clear secondary infection. Emollients and skin-care basics are the maintenance cornerstone.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Despite diligent moisturizing, she has an active inflamed, itchy flare on the cheeks and flexures. What is the most appropriate next step for the flare?
options: Start systemic immunosuppression as initial therapy
Apply a low-potency topical corticosteroid to affected areas for the flare
Apply a high-potency topical steroid (e.g., clobetasol) to the face
Stop all moisturizers until the flare resolves as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Active flares are treated with topical anti-inflammatory therapy layered on top of emollients. A LOW-potency topical corticosteroid such as hydrocortisone is appropriate for an infant and for delicate areas like the face and flexures, used for short courses during flares. High-potency steroids like clobetasol (B) should NOT be used on an infant's face or flexures because of skin atrophy, striae, and systemic absorption risk. Systemic immunosuppression (C) is reserved for severe, refractory disease managed by specialists—far beyond first-line for this child. Stopping moisturizers (D) is exactly wrong; emollients should continue during flares as the barrier-repair base. Low-potency topical steroid plus continued emollients is the correct flare management.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
4-week follow-up after starting daily emollients and short-course low-potency steroid for flares:
Markedly reduced erythema and excoriations; sleeping through the night
Parents switched to fragrance-free cleanser, shorter lukewarm baths, immediate post-bath moisturizing
No signs of secondary infection
Growth and development continue normally
Mother asks what other allergic conditions to anticipate as her daughter grows

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The mother asks about the 'atopic march.' Which sequence best describes the typical progression she should be counseled about?
options: Atopic dermatitis and food allergy in infancy, often followed by asthma and allergic rhinitis in later childhood
Allergic rhinitis first, then eczema, then food allergy as the immediate next step
Asthma in infancy progressing to eczema in adolescence as the immediate next step
A single fixed disease that never changes over time as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The 'atopic march' describes the typical temporal progression of atopic disease: atopic dermatitis and food allergies tend to appear earliest in infancy, frequently followed by the later development of asthma and allergic rhinitis during childhood. Counseling parents about this helps set expectations and prompts attention to respiratory symptoms later. Option A reverses the order—allergic rhinitis usually appears later, not first. Option C is incorrect because asthma typically follows, rather than precedes, infantile eczema. Option D is wrong because atopic disease characteristically evolves over time, with eczema often improving while respiratory allergy emerges. The infancy-eczema/food-allergy-to-later-asthma/rhinitis sequence is the correct description of the atopic march.

pearls: Atopic dermatitis: chronic, relapsing, intensely pruritic, xerotic rash—face/extensors in infancy, flexures later—on a backdrop of family atopy.
Emollients plus gentle, fragrance-free skin care and trigger avoidance are the maintenance cornerstone; moisturize immediately after short, lukewarm baths.
Treat flares with topical corticosteroids matched to site and age—low potency on the face/flexures and in infants; avoid high-potency steroids on delicate skin.
The 'atopic march': eczema and food allergy in infancy often precede asthma and allergic rhinitis later in childhood.

cases.title: Toddler With Vomiting, Diarrhea, and Fewer Wet Diapers
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of vomiting and watery diarrhea with decreased urination and reduced activity.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department for two days of frequent watery, non-bloody diarrhea and vomiting. He started with vomiting that has since slowed, but he continues to have 6–8 watery stools per day. He has had a low-grade fever and reduced appetite. His mother reports he has had only one wet diaper in the past 8 hours and seems more tired and less playful than usual, though he is still arousable and cries with tears (somewhat fewer than normal). There has been no blood or mucus in the stool, no bilious vomiting, and no severe localized abdominal pain. He attends daycare where a 'stomach bug' has been circulating. He was born full-term, is fully immunized including rotavirus vaccine, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He lives with his parents and a school-aged sibling who had a similar brief illness last week. He has had no recent travel, no new foods, and no sick pets. Family history is unremarkable. Attempts at giving plain water and juice at home have been only partly successful because of the vomiting.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing but arousable and consolable toddler who makes a small amount of tears when crying. Mucous membranes are dry, eyes appear mildly sunken, and the anterior fontanelle is closed (age-appropriate). Capillary refill is about 2–3 seconds and skin turgor shows mild delay in recoil. He is tachycardic but with palpable, normal-volume distal pulses and warm extremities. The abdomen is soft, mildly diffusely tender, with hyperactive bowel sounds, no guarding, no rebound, and no palpable mass. There is no rash or petechiae. He is not lethargic and has no respiratory distress; lungs are clear and heart is regular without murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on his exam (tachycardia, mildly dry mucous membranes, slightly reduced tears, ~2–3 second cap refill, mild turgor delay, still alert), how is his dehydration best categorized?
options: Severe dehydration with shock
Fluid overload
Some/mild-to-moderate dehydration
No/minimal dehydration
stages.explanation: His findings—tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, mildly sunken eyes, decreased (not absent) tears, capillary refill of 2–3 seconds, and mild skin-turgor delay while remaining alert with warm extremities and normal pulses—are consistent with SOME (mild-to-moderate) dehydration, roughly 3–9% volume loss. No/minimal dehydration (A) would show normal mucous membranes, normal tears, brisk cap refill, and normal urine output. Severe dehydration with shock (B) would feature lethargy/obtundation, very dry mucosa, absent tears, sunken eyes, cap refill >3 seconds, cool mottled extremities, weak pulses, and possibly hypotension—he is not at that level. Fluid overload (D) is the opposite picture. The mild-to-moderate category drives the choice of oral rehydration first.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial rehydration strategy for this alert toddler with mild-to-moderate dehydration who has only been vomiting intermittently?
options: Give an antidiarrheal agent (loperamide) and discharge as initial therapy
Make him NPO and admit for total parenteral nutrition as initial therapy
Oral rehydration therapy with an oral rehydration solution given in small, frequent amounts
Immediate large-bolus IV normal saline as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For mild-to-moderate dehydration in an alert child who can take fluids, ORAL rehydration therapy with a balanced oral rehydration solution (proper glucose-to-sodium ratio) in small, frequent aliquots is first-line and is as effective as IV fluids with fewer complications. An ondansetron dose can reduce vomiting and improve ORT success. Immediate large IV boluses (A) are reserved for severe dehydration/shock or failed oral rehydration. Making him NPO with TPN (C) is inappropriate—early enteral fluids and resuming feeding are encouraged. Antidiarrheals like loperamide (D) are not recommended in young children with acute gastroenteritis due to risk of ileus and adverse effects, and discharging an under-resuscitated child is unsafe. Oral rehydration is the correct first step.

stages.label: ED COURSE
stages.content:
Given ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg orally for vomiting
Oral rehydration solution offered 5 mL every 1–2 minutes by syringe
After 1 hour: tolerating fluids, no further vomiting
HR down to 120 bpm, mucous membranes now moist
Point-of-care glucose 84 mg/dL; child more interactive and asking to play
Mother confident she can continue oral fluids at home

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He is tolerating oral fluids, his heart rate has normalized, and he looks improved. What is the most appropriate disposition and dietary advice?
options: Discharge home with continued oral rehydration, early resumption of an age-appropriate regular diet,
Discharge with a prescription for oral antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
Admit for IV fluids despite his improvement with appropriate follow-up
Discharge but keep him on a strict clear-liquids-only (BRAT) diet for a week based on current risk
stages.explanation: A child who responds to oral rehydration—tolerating fluids, normalizing heart rate, improving clinically—can be discharged with instructions to continue oral rehydration solution to replace ongoing losses, RESUME an age-appropriate regular diet early (prolonged restriction is harmful), and return for warning signs (no urine, lethargy, bloody stools, persistent vomiting, signs of worsening dehydration). Admission for IV fluids (A) is unnecessary once he is rehydrating orally and improving. A prolonged restrictive BRAT/clear-liquid diet (C) is outdated and can impair nutrition and recovery; early refeeding is recommended. Antibiotics (D) are not indicated for typical viral gastroenteritis without blood/mucus or systemic bacterial features. Discharge with oral rehydration, early refeeding, and clear return precautions is correct.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which clinical change should prompt the family to return immediately and would shift management toward IV fluids?
options: Lethargy, inability to keep down oral fluids, no urine output for many hours, or sunken eyes with absent tears
Mild abdominal cramping that resolves after a bowel movement as the leading concern
One more loose stool than yesterday but otherwise playful and drinking as the leading concern
A transient low-grade fever that responds to antipyretics as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Red-flag features signaling progression to severe dehydration or failure of oral rehydration—lethargy or altered mental status, persistent vomiting with inability to keep fluids down, prolonged absence of urine output, and sunken eyes with absent tears—warrant immediate return and a shift to IV rehydration. An extra loose stool in a child who remains playful and drinking (A) is expected and not alarming. Self-limited cramping relieved by defecation (B) is part of gastroenteritis. A transient fever that responds to antipyretics (C) is common and not by itself dangerous. Teaching families these specific warning signs ensures timely escalation while avoiding unnecessary returns for the expected course of viral gastroenteritis.

cases.diagnosis: Acute viral gastroenteritis with mild-to-moderate dehydration
pearls: Estimate dehydration clinically (mucous membranes, tears, eyes, cap refill, turgor, mental status, urine output) rather than by a single number.
Oral rehydration solution in small, frequent amounts is first-line for mild-to-moderate dehydration—as effective as IV with fewer complications.
A single dose of ondansetron can reduce vomiting and improve oral rehydration success; avoid loperamide in young children.
Reserve IV fluids for severe dehydration/shock, altered mental status, or failed oral rehydration; resume a regular diet early rather than prolonged restriction.

cases.title: Brief Shaking Spell During a Fever
cases.chiefComplaint: A brief whole-body shaking episode at home as her fever rose rapidly.
cases.history: A 16-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department by frightened parents after a 'whole-body shaking' episode at home. She had been mildly fussy with a runny nose earlier in the day, and as her temperature climbed she suddenly stiffened, her eyes rolled back, and all four limbs shook rhythmically for about 90 seconds before stopping on their own. She was briefly sleepy afterward but returned to her usual self within 15–20 minutes. There was no preceding head trauma, no toxic ingestion, and only this single episode. She has had no prior seizures. She was born full-term with normal development and is meeting milestones (walking, several words). She is fully immunized, takes no medications, and has no allergies. She lives with her parents and a toddler cousin who has had a recent cold. Family history is notable: her father had 'fever fits' as a young child that he outgrew. On arrival she is back to baseline, alert, smiling, and interactive, with a febrile illness apparent. There has been no neck stiffness noted and no rash.
cases.exam: Now at her baseline: alert, interactive, smiling, and consolable toddler who tracks and plays. She is febrile and flushed but not toxic-appearing. The neck is supple with full, painless range of motion and a negative Brudzinski sign; the anterior fontanelle is closed and appropriate for age. There is no rash or petechiae. The tympanic membranes are mildly erythematous bilaterally and the oropharynx is injected, consistent with a viral upper respiratory infection. Neurologic exam is non-focal: normal tone, symmetric movement of all four limbs, normal reflexes, and no focal deficit. Pupils are equal and reactive. Lungs are clear; heart is regular without murmur; abdomen is soft and non-tender.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which features make this most consistent with a SIMPLE (rather than complex) febrile seizure?
options: Generalized, lasting under 15 minutes, single episode in 24 hours, in a child 6 months–5 years with fever
A seizure lasting more than 15 minutes as the primary diagnosis
Focal onset with prolonged post-ictal weakness as the primary diagnosis
Two seizures within the same 24-hour period as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A simple febrile seizure is GENERALIZED, BRIEF (lasts under 15 minutes), occurs only ONCE in a 24-hour period, and happens in a developmentally normal child aged 6 months to 5 years during a febrile illness without CNS infection. This child's ~90-second generalized convulsion, single episode, normal development, and age fit perfectly. Focal onset with prolonged post-ictal (Todd's) weakness (B) defines a COMPLEX febrile seizure. Recurrence within 24 hours (C) and duration over 15 minutes (D) are also complex features. Distinguishing simple from complex matters because simple febrile seizures are benign and require no workup, whereas complex features prompt further evaluation. All of this child's features are simple.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She is now back to baseline with a clearly identifiable viral source and a normal, non-focal neuro exam. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture on every child after a first febrile seizure
Obtain emergency head CT and EEG as the first step today
No neuroimaging or EEG
Start a daily antiepileptic drug to prevent recurrence as the first step today
stages.explanation: After a simple febrile seizure in a well-appearing, back-to-baseline child with a clear fever source and a normal neurologic exam, no routine neuroimaging, EEG, or seizure labs are indicated; the focus is identifying and managing the febrile illness and reassuring the family. Emergency CT and EEG (A) are reserved for complex features, focal deficits, or persistent altered mental status. Universal lumbar puncture (C) is NOT recommended for simple febrile seizures; LP is considered when meningitis is suspected (e.g., meningeal signs, toxic appearance, or in incompletely immunized/very young infants)—this child has a supple neck and is at baseline. Daily antiepileptics (D) are not used for simple febrile seizures because risks outweigh benefits. Reassurance and source evaluation are correct.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Neuro exam remains non-focal; child playful and back to baseline
No meningeal signs; well-appearing and fully immunized
Clinical source identified: bilateral otitis media and viral URI
No labs or imaging obtained given classic simple febrile seizure
Fever controlled with weight-based antipyretic; tolerating fluids

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The parents are terrified she will die or develop epilepsy. Which counseling point is most accurate?
options: Simple febrile seizures commonly cause permanent brain damage with appropriate follow-up
Simple febrile seizures are benign with an excellent prognosis
Aggressive scheduled antipyretics will reliably prevent all future febrile seizures with appropriate follow-up
She should start a daily antiepileptic to prevent epilepsy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The key message is reassurance: simple febrile seizures are benign and do NOT cause brain damage, developmental delay, or death. About a third of children will have a recurrence (higher with younger onset, lower fever threshold, and family history), but the long-term risk of developing epilepsy is only slightly higher than the baseline population risk. Permanent brain damage (A) is false. Scheduled antipyretics (C) improve comfort but have NOT been shown to reliably prevent febrile seizures, so parents should not feel guilty if a seizure recurs. Daily antiepileptic therapy (D) is not indicated for simple febrile seizures—the risks outweigh benefits. Accurate, reassuring counseling about recurrence and the very low epilepsy risk is correct.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most useful home-management instruction to give the parents for any future febrile-seizure event?
options: Give a double dose of antipyretic plus a sedative at the first sign of fever as the immediate next step
Restrain the child and place an object in her mouth to protect the tongue as the immediate next step
Immerse her in an ice bath to break the fever quickly as the immediate next step
Keep her safe on her side, do not put anything in her mouth, time the seizure,
stages.explanation: Correct first-aid for a seizure is to protect the child—lay her on her side (recovery position) to keep the airway clear, clear the area of hazards, do NOT put anything in the mouth, time the event, and seek emergency care (or use rescue medication if prescribed) if it lasts beyond ~5 minutes or if breathing is compromised. Restraining and placing objects in the mouth (A) risks injury and aspiration and is contraindicated. Ice-bath immersion (C) is dangerous and ineffective. Doubling antipyretics or adding sedatives prophylactically (D) is unsafe and does not prevent seizures. Teaching safe positioning, the 'nothing in the mouth' rule, timing, and the ~5-minute threshold is the appropriate guidance.

pearls: Simple febrile seizure: generalized, <15 minutes, once in 24 hours, in a neurologically normal child aged 6 months–5 years with fever and no CNS infection.
A well-appearing, back-to-baseline child with a clear fever source needs no routine LP, neuroimaging, EEG, or seizure labs—focus on the febrile illness.
Prognosis is excellent: recurrence occurs in ~1/3, but epilepsy risk is only marginally above baseline; antipyretics do not prevent recurrences.
First aid: side-lying position, nothing in the mouth, time the event, and seek emergency care if it lasts beyond ~5 minutes.

cases.title: A Week of Runny Nose, Cough, and Low-Grade Fever
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of runny nose, cough, sneezing, and mild fever, with a parent requesting antibiotics.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his father, who is requesting antibiotics for a 'sinus infection.' For about 4–5 days the boy has had nasal congestion, clear-then-thicker rhinorrhea, sneezing, a mild sore throat, and an intermittent cough that is worse at night. He had a low-grade fever to 38.0 °C the first two days that has since resolved. He is eating a bit less but drinking well, sleeping reasonably, and remains playful between coughing spells. There has been no difficulty breathing, no wheezing, no ear pain, and no facial pain or swelling. Several classmates in his kindergarten have colds. He was born full-term, is fully immunized, takes no daily medications, and has no allergies. He lives with both parents; no smokers in the home. He has had no prior pneumonia or recurrent infections. The father is worried because the nasal discharge turned yellow-green for a day and assumes that means a bacterial infection requiring antibiotics. There has been no recent travel and no known COVID exposure.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful child in no distress, well-hydrated with moist mucous membranes. The nasal mucosa is erythematous with clear-to-mucoid discharge; there is no sinus tenderness to palpation and no periorbital swelling. The oropharynx is mildly injected without exudate or tonsillar asymmetry. Tympanic membranes are normal bilaterally with good mobility and no effusion. The neck is supple with a few small, non-tender anterior cervical nodes. Lungs are clear to auscultation with good air entry and no wheeze, crackles, or retractions. The cough is occasional and non-paroxysmal. Heart is regular without murmur, and the abdomen is soft and non-tender. There is no rash.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis for this well-appearing child with several days of congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, and a resolving low-grade fever?
options: Acute bacterial sinusitis
Viral upper respiratory infection
Bacterial pneumonia in this clinical context
Streptococcal pharyngitis
stages.explanation: A few days of congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, mild sore throat, and a brief low-grade fever in a playful, non-toxic child with a normal lung and ear exam is a viral upper respiratory infection (common cold). Acute bacterial sinusitis (A) is suggested by symptoms persisting ≥10 days without improvement, 'double-worsening' after initial improvement, or severe onset with high fever and purulent discharge for ≥3 days—none of which fit this improving 4–5 day course. Bacterial pneumonia (B) would cause focal lung findings, tachypnea, hypoxia, or a toxic appearance, all absent with clear lungs. Streptococcal pharyngitis (D) typically lacks cough and rhinorrhea and features exudate, tender nodes, and fever; this cough/coryza picture argues against it. The self-limited viral cold is most likely.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The father insists that the yellow-green nasal discharge proves a bacterial infection. What is the most appropriate response and plan?
options: Order sinus radiographs to confirm sinusitis as initial therapy
Prescribe a course of azithromycin to be safe as initial therapy
Prescribe amoxicillin because the discharge is discolored as initial therapy
Explain that discolored nasal discharge is common in viral colds
stages.explanation: Thick, discolored (yellow or green) nasal discharge is a normal part of the natural course of a viral cold and does NOT by itself indicate a bacterial infection or warrant antibiotics. The appropriate response is to educate the family and recommend supportive care, with a return plan if the illness worsens or persists beyond ~10 days without improvement. Prescribing amoxicillin (A) or azithromycin (C) for a viral URI drives antibiotic resistance and side effects without benefit—antibiotic stewardship is key. Sinus radiographs (D) are not recommended to diagnose uncomplicated sinusitis and cannot reliably distinguish viral from bacterial disease in children. Education plus supportive care, reserving antibiotics for true bacterial sinusitis criteria, is correct.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which symptomatic measure is appropriate to recommend for this 5-year-old?
options: Codeine-containing cough syrup at bedtime as the immediate next step
A prescription antitussive with a sedating antihistamine as the immediate next step
Over-the-counter cough-and-cold (decongestant/antihistamine) combination products in this setting
Supportive care: fluids, saline nasal drops/saline irrigation, humidified air,
stages.explanation: Supportive care is the mainstay for a viral cold: adequate fluids, saline nasal drops or irrigation and gentle suction/blowing, humidified air, and weight-appropriate acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever or discomfort; honey can help cough in children over 1 year. Over-the-counter cough-and-cold combination products (A) are NOT recommended in young children—they lack proven efficacy and carry risks; many labels advise against use under age 6. Codeine-containing syrups (B) are contraindicated in children due to respiratory depression risk and variable metabolism. Sedating prescription antitussive/antihistamine combinations (C) are likewise not recommended for routine pediatric colds. Simple, safe supportive measures are the correct recommendation.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
Phone check-in at day 9 of illness:
Nasal symptoms improving; discharge thinned and decreasing
Cough less frequent, now mostly at night and improving
Afebrile for several days; eating and playing normally
No ear pain, no facial pain/swelling, no difficulty breathing
Father reassured; no antibiotics were given

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given his improvement by day 9, what counseling about expected course and return precautions is most appropriate?
options: He needs a chest X-ray now because the cough has not fully resolved with appropriate follow-up
Any residual cough at this point means he has developed asthma with appropriate follow-up
Cold symptoms can linger 1–2 weeks while improving
He should now start antibiotics since symptoms have lasted over a week with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Families should be counseled that viral cold symptoms—particularly cough—commonly linger for 1–2 weeks even as the child steadily improves, and that this slow tail is normal. Clear return precautions matter: worsening symptoms, a new high or persistent fever, ear or facial pain, difficulty breathing, or congestion that PERSISTS or WORSENS beyond ~10 days without improvement (suggesting secondary bacterial sinusitis) should prompt re-evaluation. Starting antibiotics simply because symptoms exceeded a week (A) is wrong when the child is clearly improving. A residual cough does not equal asthma (C) in an otherwise well child. A chest X-ray (D) is unnecessary without focal findings, tachypnea, hypoxia, or a toxic appearance. Anticipatory guidance with specific return precautions is correct.

pearls: Discolored (yellow-green) nasal discharge is part of the normal viral cold course and does NOT by itself justify antibiotics.
Suspect acute bacterial sinusitis only with persistent symptoms ≥10 days without improvement, 'double-worsening,' or severe onset (high fever + purulent discharge ≥3 days).
Manage colds with supportive care; avoid OTC cough-and-cold products and codeine in young children.
Counsel that cough can linger 1–2 weeks; give clear return precautions for worsening, high fever, or symptoms persisting beyond ~10 days.

cases.title: Sneezing, Itchy Eyes, and a Constant Nose Rub
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent springtime sneezing, nasal congestion, and itchy, watery eyes.
cases.history: An 8-year-old girl presents to clinic with several weeks of bouts of sneezing, clear watery nasal discharge, nasal congestion, and itchy, watery eyes. Her mother reports this happens every spring and worsens outdoors and on high-pollen days, improving when she stays inside with the windows closed. The girl frequently rubs her nose upward with her palm and complains her eyes and the roof of her mouth itch. There is no fever, no facial pain, and no purulent discharge. She has had a chronic 'stuffy nose' and mouth-breathing at night with snoring during these spells. She is otherwise healthy, born full-term, fully immunized, takes no daily medications, and has no drug allergies, though she had eczema as a toddler. She lives with her parents and a dog; no smokers in the home. Family history is strongly atopic: her mother has seasonal allergies and her father has asthma. Her symptoms interfere with school concentration and sleep during peak pollen season but resolve in winter.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile child in no distress, breathing through her mouth. There is darkening and mild puffiness of the lower eyelids ('allergic shiners') and a horizontal crease across the lower nose (the 'nasal salute'/transverse crease). The nasal mucosa is pale, boggy, and bluish with clear watery secretions; the turbinates are swollen and there is no purulent discharge or unilateral mass. The conjunctivae are mildly injected and watery with a cobblestone appearance of the palpebral conjunctiva, without purulent exudate. The oropharynx shows a 'cobblestone' posterior pharyngeal wall from lymphoid hyperplasia. Tympanic membranes are normal. There is no sinus tenderness. Lungs are clear with no wheeze, and the heart is regular. No rash currently.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains this child's seasonal sneezing, itchy watery eyes, pale boggy nasal mucosa, allergic shiners, and nasal salute?
options: Viral upper respiratory infection
Allergic rhinitis (seasonal)
Acute bacterial sinusitis
Nonallergic vasomotor rhinitis
stages.explanation: Seasonal, recurrent sneezing with itchy, watery eyes and palate, pale/boggy/bluish nasal mucosa, allergic shiners, a transverse nasal crease from the 'allergic salute,' and a strong atopic family history are classic for seasonal allergic rhinitis driven by pollen. The pattern (worse outdoors/high pollen, better indoors, resolves in winter) confirms an allergic, seasonal trigger. A viral URI (B) is acute and self-limited over ~1–2 weeks with fever and sore throat, not recurrent every spring with prominent itch. Acute bacterial sinusitis (C) features purulent discharge, facial pain/pressure, and is not itch-predominant or seasonal. Nonallergic vasomotor rhinitis (D) lacks the itch and the strong seasonal/atopic pattern and is triggered by irritants/temperature changes rather than pollen. Allergic rhinitis fits best.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Is any testing needed to begin treatment, and what is the most appropriate initial step?
options: The diagnosis is clinical
Order a CT of the sinuses before treating as the first step today
Obtain a nasal swab culture to identify the pathogen as the first step today
Prescribe a course of oral antibiotics empirically as the first step today
stages.explanation: Allergic rhinitis is a CLINICAL diagnosis based on the characteristic history and exam; treatment can begin without testing. The appropriate first step is environmental allergen avoidance (e.g., limiting outdoor exposure on high-pollen days, keeping windows closed, showering after outdoor play) combined with pharmacotherapy. Specific allergy testing (skin or serum IgE) is reserved for unclear diagnoses, identifying triggers, or planning allergen immunotherapy—not required to start treatment. Sinus CT (A) is not indicated for uncomplicated allergic rhinitis and adds radiation/cost. A nasal culture (B) is unhelpful because this is allergic, not infectious. Empiric antibiotics (D) are inappropriate for a non-infectious, allergic process. Clinical diagnosis with avoidance plus pharmacotherapy is correct.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her symptoms are persistent and bothersome enough to disrupt sleep and school. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?
options: A short course of oral corticosteroids as first-line therapy as initial therapy
An oral decongestant used daily for several months as initial therapy
An intranasal corticosteroid spray, with a non-sedating oral antihistamine
A first-generation sedating oral antihistamine (e.g., diphenhydramine) around the clock
stages.explanation: For persistent or moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis, an INTRANASAL CORTICOSTEROID is the most effective first-line therapy, addressing congestion, sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itch; a second-generation (non-sedating) oral antihistamine can be added, especially for itch/sneezing and ocular symptoms. First-generation antihistamines like diphenhydramine (A) cause sedation that impairs school performance and sleep quality and are not preferred for maintenance. Daily oral decongestants for months (C) risk side effects (irritability, sleep disturbance, rebound with topical forms) and are not appropriate long-term. Systemic corticosteroids (D) are reserved for severe, refractory cases and are not first-line because of systemic side effects. An intranasal steroid plus a non-sedating antihistamine is the correct first-line regimen.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
4-week follow-up after starting an intranasal corticosteroid daily plus a non-sedating antihistamine as needed:
Marked reduction in sneezing, congestion, and nasal itch
Eye itch improved; added a topical antihistamine eye drop on high-pollen days
Sleeping better with less mouth-breathing; concentration improved at school
No medication side effects; using correct spray technique aiming away from the septum
Mother asks whether anything can address the underlying allergy long-term

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The mother asks about a potentially disease-modifying option for her daughter's pollen allergy. Which is the most appropriate counseling?
options: Lifelong daily oral corticosteroids are the only long-term solution as the immediate next step
Annual antibiotic courses will prevent future seasonal symptoms as the immediate next step
Allergen immunotherapy can modify the underlying allergic response
There is nothing further that can be done beyond as-needed antihistamines as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Allergen immunotherapy—delivered subcutaneously (allergy shots) or sublingually—is the disease-modifying option for allergic rhinitis: it retrains the immune response to specific allergens and can provide durable symptom reduction and decreased medication need, with benefit that may persist after completing a multi-year course. It is considered for patients with inadequate control on medications/avoidance, significant quality-of-life impact, or a desire to reduce long-term medication use, and is typically guided by allergy testing. Lifelong oral steroids (A) are inappropriate due to systemic toxicity. Saying nothing more can be done (C) is incorrect given immunotherapy. Antibiotics (D) have no role in an allergic, non-infectious condition. Allergen immunotherapy is the correct disease-modifying counseling.

pearls: Allergic rhinitis is a clinical diagnosis: seasonal sneezing, clear rhinorrhea, nasal/ocular itch, pale boggy mucosa, allergic shiners, and the nasal salute.
Allergen avoidance plus pharmacotherapy is first-line; reserve allergy testing for unclear cases or to guide immunotherapy.
Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective first-line drug; add a non-sedating oral antihistamine and avoid sedating first-generation agents for maintenance.
Allergen immunotherapy (subcutaneous or sublingual) is the disease-modifying option for inadequately controlled or medication-burdened patients.

cases.title: A Heart Sound Heard at a Well-Child Visit
cases.chiefComplaint: A heart murmur was noted during a routine pre-kindergarten physical.
cases.history: A previously healthy 5-year-old girl is brought in for a pre-kindergarten well-child visit. Her mother reports no concerns. The child is active, runs and plays with peers without tiring, and has never had chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or cyanosis. There is no history of poor feeding in infancy, sweating with feeds, recurrent respiratory infections, or failure to thrive. She has met all developmental milestones and follows the 50th percentile for height and weight. She had a mild viral upper respiratory illness two weeks ago that has fully resolved; she is currently afebrile and well. Past medical history is unremarkable, with no hospitalizations or surgeries. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. Social history: she lives with both parents and a younger sibling, attends preschool, and has no tobacco-smoke exposure. Family history is negative for congenital heart disease, sudden cardiac death, arrhythmias, connective tissue disorders, or unexplained death before age 50. There is no family history of rheumatic fever.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful child in no distress. Pink, well-perfused, with brisk capillary refill under 2 seconds. No dysmorphic features. Cardiac exam reveals a grade 2/6 soft, low-pitched, musical, vibratory systolic ejection murmur best heard at the lower left sternal border. The murmur is loudest when she is supine and diminishes markedly when she sits up and stands. S1 and S2 are normal, with physiologic splitting of S2. No diastolic murmur, no click, no thrill, and no gallop. Femoral pulses are strong and symmetric with no radio-femoral delay. Lungs are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft without hepatomegaly. No peripheral edema, clubbing, or cyanosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this murmur most strongly suggests it is benign rather than pathologic?
options: It is a systolic murmur as the first step today
It is grade 2 in intensity as the first step today
It is best heard at the lower left sternal border
It is soft, musical/vibratory,
stages.explanation: A soft, musical/vibratory quality with intensity that decreases on sitting/standing is the hallmark of a Still's murmur, the classic innocent murmur of childhood; reduced venous return when upright lessens flow and softens an innocent murmur. Systolic timing alone does not distinguish benign from pathologic, as many pathologic murmurs (VSD, AS) are also systolic. Location at the lower left sternal border is typical but nonspecific. Grade 2 intensity is reassuring only in combination with other innocent features; pathologic murmurs can also be soft, so intensity alone is insufficient. The constellation of quality plus dynamic change with position is what confirms a functional murmur.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which additional finding, if present, would be a red flag prompting referral and further workup?
options: Normal and symmetric femoral pulses
A murmur that softens with standing
Physiologic splitting of S2 as the primary diagnosis
A harsh holosystolic murmur with an associated thrill
