stages.explanation: A harsh, holosystolic murmur with a palpable thrill (grade 4 or higher) indicates turbulent flow across an abnormal communication or stenotic valve, such as a VSD, and warrants echocardiography and cardiology referral. A murmur that softens with standing is characteristic of an innocent murmur and is reassuring. Normal, symmetric femoral pulses argue against coarctation and are reassuring. Physiologic (normal) splitting of S2 is a normal finding; it is fixed splitting that suggests an ASD. Diastolic murmurs, continuous murmurs, abnormal S2, clicks, and any symptoms also constitute red flags requiring evaluation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the clinical picture, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer urgently to pediatric cardiology as initial therapy
Start amoxicillin prophylaxis before dental procedures
Order an echocardiogram and chest radiograph
Provide reassurance and continue routine well-child care
stages.explanation: An asymptomatic child with a classic Still's murmur, normal vitals, normal growth, normal S2, normal pulses, and a reassuring family history needs no testing; the appropriate management is reassurance and routine follow-up. Echocardiography and chest radiography are not indicated when the murmur has clear innocent features and the exam is otherwise normal; ordering them adds cost and anxiety without benefit. Urgent cardiology referral is reserved for red-flag features. Endocarditis prophylaxis is unnecessary because there is no structural heart disease; antibiotic prophylaxis is reserved for specific high-risk cardiac conditions.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should the family be counseled about the murmur's natural history?
options: It is benign and typically disappears by adolescence
It indicates a hole in the heart that needs surgery
It will require lifelong activity restriction based on current risk
It signals a high risk of future heart failure based on current risk
stages.explanation: Innocent murmurs, including Still's murmur, reflect normal turbulent flow in a structurally normal heart and commonly become inaudible by adolescence; children need no activity restriction and have an excellent prognosis. Lifelong activity restriction is inappropriate and could harm the child's development and self-image. The murmur does not represent a structural defect such as a VSD, so surgery is not indicated. There is no increased risk of heart failure because the heart is structurally normal. Clear, confident reassurance helps reduce parental anxiety and avoids unnecessary cardiac testing or cardiac nondisease labeling.

pearls: Still's murmur is soft, musical/vibratory, systolic, loudest at the lower left sternal border, and softens when the child sits or stands (reduced venous return).
Innocent murmurs are grade 1-2, never diastolic, never holosystolic, and lack thrills, clicks, or abnormal S2.

Red flags requiring echo/cardiology: diastolic or continuous murmurs, holosystolic murmurs, thrills, abnormal S2, clicks, cyanosis, abnormal pulses, or symptoms (syncope, chest pain, failure to thrive).
An asymptomatic child with a classic innocent murmur and normal exam needs reassurance, not testing or activity restriction.

cases.title: A Swollen, Red Eye After a Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening eyelid swelling and redness around the right eye with pain on looking around.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the ED with 2 days of progressively worsening swelling and redness of the right upper and lower eyelids. He had a runny nose and nasal congestion for about a week before the swelling began. Over the past 12 hours his mother noticed that his eye appears to bulge, he complains of pain when he moves his eye, and he has been intermittently complaining of double vision. He has had fevers up to 39.2 °C at home. He is eating less but taking fluids. There has been no trauma, insect bite, or recent dental work. Past medical history is notable for recurrent sinus infections; no prior hospitalizations or surgeries. He takes no daily medications. He has no known drug allergies. Immunizations, including Hib and pneumococcal conjugate vaccine, are up to date. Social history: lives with parents and attends first grade; no sick contacts with similar symptoms. Family history is noncontributory, with no immunodeficiency or bleeding disorders.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, febrile boy. Right eye: marked erythema and edema of the upper and lower eyelids with proptosis. There is pain with attempted extraocular movements and limited lateral and upward gaze (ophthalmoplegia). Mild conjunctival injection and chemosis are present. Visual acuity is mildly decreased on the right; pupil is round and reactive without a relative afferent pupillary defect at present. There is tenderness over the right maxillary and ethmoid sinuses. The left eye is entirely normal with full painless movements. No periorbital crepitus. Neck is supple. No meningismus. Remainder of the cranial nerve exam is intact. Lungs clear; heart tachycardic but regular.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings best distinguishes orbital (postseptal) cellulitis from preseptal cellulitis?
options: Fever and elevated white count
Eyelid erythema and edema in this clinical context
Proptosis, pain with eye movement,
A recent upper respiratory infection
stages.explanation: Proptosis, pain with eye movement, and restricted/painful extraocular movements (ophthalmoplegia) indicate that infection has spread posterior to the orbital septum into the orbit itself, defining orbital cellulitis. Vision changes and chemosis support this. Eyelid erythema and edema occur in both preseptal and orbital cellulitis and do not localize the infection. Fever and leukocytosis can accompany either entity and are nonspecific. A preceding URI/sinusitis is a common precursor to both but most often drives ethmoid sinus spread in orbital cellulitis; it does not by itself differentiate the two. The orbital signs are the key clinical discriminators that mandate urgent imaging and admission.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to confirm the diagnosis and evaluate for complications?
options: Plain orbital radiographs in this setting
Ocular ultrasound at the bedside in this setting
MRI of the brain without contrast as the first-line test
Contrast-enhanced CT of the orbits
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT of the orbits and paranasal sinuses is the standard initial study; it confirms postseptal involvement, identifies the source (usually ethmoid sinusitis), and detects complications such as subperiosteal or orbital abscess and early cavernous sinus or intracranial extension. Plain radiographs lack the resolution to assess the orbit or detect abscess and are obsolete here. Bedside ocular ultrasound cannot reliably evaluate the posterior orbit, sinuses, or intracranial structures. MRI is more sensitive for intracranial and cavernous sinus complications and may follow, but CT is faster, more available, and the appropriate first-line test in the acute setting; a noncontrast study would also miss abscess enhancement.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.4 ×10³/µL (82% neutrophils)
CRP 96 mg/L
Blood culture: pending
Contrast CT orbits/sinuses: opacification of right ethmoid air cells with a small medial subperiosteal abscess; proptosis of the right globe; stranding of orbital fat; no cavernous sinus thrombosis or intracranial extension

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Discharge on oral amoxicillin-clavulanate with next-day recheck as initial therapy
Give a single dose of IV antibiotics and discharge home as initial therapy
Admit and start empiric IV antibiotics with ophthalmology consult
Observe without antibiotics and repeat CT in 48 hours as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Orbital cellulitis with a subperiosteal abscess requires hospital admission, prompt empiric IV antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA), streptococci, and sinus anaerobes—typically vancomycin plus ceftriaxone or ampicillin-sulbactam—and urgent ophthalmology and ENT involvement. Oral antibiotics with outpatient recheck are inadequate for a vision- and life-threatening orbital infection. Observation without antibiotics is dangerous and risks rapid progression to vision loss or intracranial spread. A single IV dose followed by discharge does not provide the sustained therapy and serial monitoring of vision and proptosis that this condition demands.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding during monitoring would most strongly indicate the need for urgent surgical drainage?
options: Persistent low-grade fever after 12 hours of IV antibiotics
Transient diplopia on extreme lateral gaze that is improving
Mild residual eyelid edema as the leading concern
A new relative afferent pupillary defect with worsening proptosis
stages.explanation: A new relative afferent pupillary defect with worsening proptosis and declining visual acuity signals optic nerve compromise from rising orbital/abscess pressure and is a surgical emergency requiring drainage to preserve vision. Persistent low-grade fever in the first 12-24 hours can occur as antibiotics take effect and is not by itself an indication for surgery. Mild residual eyelid edema is expected during recovery. Improving, transient diplopia on extreme gaze suggests the infection is responding to therapy. Indications for surgery include a large or enlarging abscess, failure to improve on IV antibiotics, or any evidence of visual deterioration or optic nerve threat.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which intracranial complication should be suspected if he develops bilateral eye findings, cranial nerve VI palsy, and rapidly worsening headache?
options: Migraine with aura
Cavernous sinus thrombosis
Bell palsy as the leading concern
Subdural hygroma
stages.explanation: Spread of orbital infection posteriorly can seed the cavernous sinus, producing cavernous sinus thrombosis, classically heralded by bilateral eye involvement (as the sinuses communicate), multiple cranial neuropathies including abducens (CN VI) palsy, severe headache, and toxicity; this warrants MRI/MR venography and aggressive therapy. A subdural hygroma is a CSF collection unrelated to this infectious cascade and does not cause these cranial nerve findings. Bell palsy is an isolated peripheral facial (CN VII) palsy without proptosis or systemic toxicity. Migraine with aura does not produce fever, proptosis, or ophthalmoplegia. Recognizing bilateral findings and multiple cranial neuropathies as a red flag for cavernous sinus thrombosis is critical because it is life-threatening.

cases.diagnosis: Orbital (postseptal) cellulitis with subperiosteal abscess complicating ethmoid sinusitis
pearls: Proptosis, pain with eye movement, ophthalmoplegia, and vision change distinguish orbital (postseptal) from preseptal cellulitis.
Ethmoid sinusitis is the most common source; contrast CT of orbits and sinuses confirms postseptal spread and detects subperiosteal/orbital abscess.
Treat with admission and empiric IV antibiotics covering S. aureus (incl. MRSA), strep, and anaerobes, plus ophthalmology and ENT consultation.
Surgical drainage is indicated for large/enlarging abscess, failure to improve, or any sign of optic nerve compromise (afferent pupillary defect, falling acuity).

cases.title: A Toddler Who Will Not Move His Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever, neck stiffness, drooling, and refusal to move the neck.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the ED with 3 days of fever and progressively worsening sore throat, now with neck stiffness and refusal to turn his head. For the past day he has been drooling, refusing solid food, and taking only small sips of liquid. His mother reports muffled crying and that he holds his neck rigidly extended. He had a sore throat about a week ago that seemed to improve before worsening. There is no history of trauma, foreign body ingestion that was witnessed, or choking episode. Past medical history is unremarkable; born at term, no prior hospitalizations. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Immunizations, including Hib, are up to date. Social history: attends daycare; lives with parents and an infant sibling. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival he is anxious, sitting upright, and resists attempts to flex or rotate his neck. He has had no rash, no joint swelling, and no recent dental procedures.
cases.exam: Toxic-appearing, anxious toddler sitting upright and drooling, holding the neck stiffly and resisting passive movement, especially extension and rotation. Voice is muffled. The oropharynx is difficult to fully visualize due to limited cooperation; there is no obvious unilateral tonsillar bulge and the uvula appears midline. Tender, enlarged cervical lymph nodes are palpable. There is no stridor at rest, but he has mild tachypnea. Lungs are clear to auscultation. There is no trismus appreciated. No meningismus on careful exam (no Kernig or Brudzinski sign), and the neck stiffness appears to be pain-limited rather than true nuchal rigidity. Skin is without rash. Cardiac exam shows sinus tachycardia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important immediate priority in this child?
options: Lay the child flat to obtain better IV access as the first step today
Forcibly examine the posterior pharynx with a tongue depressor
Perform immediate lumbar puncture for suspected meningitis
Assess and protect the airway while keeping the child calm and upright
stages.explanation: A toxic, drooling toddler with neck stiffness and a muffled voice may have a deep neck space infection threatening the airway; the priority is airway assessment and protection while keeping the child calm and in a position of comfort (usually upright). Forceful pharyngeal examination can precipitate gagging, bleeding, or abscess rupture and acute airway obstruction, and is contraindicated. Lumbar puncture is not indicated when the neck stiffness is pain-limited and there is no meningismus; it does not address the airway risk. Laying the child flat can worsen airway compromise and increase distress and should be avoided.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which initial imaging study best evaluates the suspected diagnosis at the bedside?
options: Lateral neck radiograph in extension
Bedside abdominal ultrasound
Panorex dental film in this setting
Anteroposterior chest radiograph
stages.explanation: A lateral soft-tissue neck radiograph taken in inspiration with the neck in extension can demonstrate widening of the prevertebral (retropharyngeal) soft tissue space, suggesting a retropharyngeal abscess, and is a quick first step in a stable patient. A chest radiograph evaluates the lungs and mediastinum, not the retropharyngeal space, though it may detect mediastinal extension later. A Panorex assesses the teeth and mandible and is irrelevant here. Abdominal ultrasound has no role in evaluating the neck. Note that proper technique matters: a film taken in flexion or expiration can falsely suggest prevertebral widening, so positioning is important. Contrast CT of the neck is the confirmatory study.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 19.8 ×10³/µL (85% neutrophils)
CRP 142 mg/L
Lateral neck radiograph (extension/inspiration): widened prevertebral soft tissue space anterior to C2-C4
Contrast CT neck: rim-enhancing fluid collection in the retropharyngeal space with surrounding inflammatory stranding; airway patent but mildly narrowed

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: On a properly performed lateral neck film, what measurement supports a retropharyngeal process in a young child?
options: Loss of the normal cervical lordosis alone as the primary diagnosis
Prevertebral soft tissue narrower than half the adjacent vertebral body width
An enlarged cardiac silhouette as the primary diagnosis
Prevertebral soft tissue at C2 wider than the width of the adjacent vertebral body
stages.explanation: In children, prevertebral (retropharyngeal) soft tissue at the level of C2 that exceeds the width of the adjacent vertebral body (a common rule of thumb is that the retropharyngeal space should be less than about half the C2 body width and the retrotracheal space less than the C6 body width) suggests retropharyngeal swelling or abscess. Soft tissue narrower than half the vertebral body width is normal and reassuring. Loss of cervical lordosis is nonspecific and can result from muscle spasm or positioning. An enlarged cardiac silhouette is a chest/cardiac finding unrelated to the retropharyngeal space. CT with contrast confirms an abscess and defines its extent.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Treat with antipyretics and observation only as initial therapy
Admit for IV antibiotics, ENT consultation,
Discharge on oral antibiotics with primary care follow-up as initial therapy
Start oral steroids alone and reassess in the morning as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A retropharyngeal abscess requires admission, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics covering streptococci, S. aureus (including MRSA), and oral anaerobes (e.g., ampicillin-sulbactam, or clindamycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin), with ENT consultation; surgical incision and drainage is performed for large abscesses or those failing medical therapy, with continuous airway monitoring. Oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up are inadequate for a deep neck space infection that can threaten the airway and spread to the mediastinum. Antipyretics and observation alone leave the infection untreated. Steroids alone do not treat the bacterial abscess and could mask progression.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feared complication results from inferior extension of a retropharyngeal abscess along fascial planes into the chest?
options: Pericardial calcification
Descending necrotizing mediastinitis
Pyloric stenosis
Acute appendicitis
stages.explanation: The retropharyngeal space communicates inferiorly with the posterior mediastinum via the 'danger space,' so an untreated abscess can track downward and cause descending necrotizing mediastinitis, a highly lethal complication that may present with chest pain, widened mediastinum on imaging, and rapid clinical deterioration. Acute appendicitis is an unrelated intra-abdominal process. Pyloric stenosis is a congenital gastric outlet obstruction of infancy with no connection to deep neck space infection. Pericardial calcification is a chronic finding (e.g., from prior tuberculous pericarditis), not an acute extension of a neck abscess. The threat of mediastinal spread is a major reason these infections demand prompt drainage and close monitoring.

pearls: Suspect retropharyngeal abscess in a febrile toddler with neck stiffness, drooling, muffled voice, and refusal to extend or rotate the neck after a recent pharyngitis.
Avoid forceful oropharyngeal exam; prioritize airway, keep the child calm and upright, and image in a stable patient.
A properly positioned lateral neck film (extension, inspiration) shows widened prevertebral soft tissue; contrast CT confirms a rim-enhancing collection.
Treat with admission, IV antibiotics covering strep/staph (incl. MRSA)/anaerobes, ENT consult, and drainage for large or refractory abscesses; watch for mediastinal extension.

cases.title: A Teenager With a Severe Sore Throat and a Strange Voice
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of worsening sore throat, now with difficulty opening the mouth and a muffled voice.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy presents to the ED with 4 days of progressively worsening throat pain, now predominantly on the right side. Over the last day he has developed difficulty fully opening his mouth, a muffled 'hot potato' voice, and pain radiating to the right ear. He has been drooling slightly and avoiding swallowing because it hurts. He was seen 3 days ago and started on amoxicillin for presumed strep pharyngitis but has not improved. He reports subjective fevers and chills. He denies difficulty breathing or stridor. Past medical history includes recurrent tonsillitis (three episodes in the past year). He takes no other medications and reports no drug allergies. Social history: high school sophomore, denies tobacco, alcohol, or vaping; up to date on immunizations. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no neck trauma or dental procedures. He is anxious and uncomfortable, holding his head tilted slightly toward the affected side, and is spitting saliva into a tissue rather than swallowing.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable adolescent with a muffled voice and trismus limiting mouth opening to about two fingerbreadths. Oropharyngeal exam, as best tolerated, shows a markedly swollen, erythematous right tonsil and peritonsillar fullness pushing the uvula across the midline toward the left. The soft palate on the right is bulging. There is tender right anterior cervical and jugulodigastric lymphadenopathy. No bilateral tonsillar exudates suggesting symmetric pharyngitis, no respiratory distress, and no stridor at rest. The neck is supple without midline swelling. Lungs are clear. There is no rash. Cardiac exam reveals mild sinus tachycardia. Hydration appears mildly reduced with slightly dry mucous membranes.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings is most characteristic of this diagnosis?
options: Posterior pharyngeal bulge with neck extension refusal
Stridor at rest with a barking cough as the primary diagnosis
Hot potato voice, trismus,
Bilateral tonsillar exudates with symmetric swelling
stages.explanation: A muffled 'hot potato' voice, trismus from pterygoid muscle irritation, and contralateral uvular deviation caused by unilateral peritonsillar/soft palate bulging are the classic triad of a peritonsillar abscess. Bilateral symmetric tonsillar exudates suggest uncomplicated pharyngitis or mononucleosis, not a localized abscess. Stridor with a barking cough describes croup, a subglottic viral process. A posterior pharyngeal bulge with refusal to extend the neck points to a retropharyngeal abscess, which is more common in younger children. The unilateral findings with uvular shift in an adolescent who failed oral antibiotics strongly favor a peritonsillar abscess (quinsy).

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: In a cooperative, stable patient with a clear unilateral peritonsillar bulge, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Proceed with clinical diagnosis
Discharge on a different oral antibiotic without drainage in this setting
Obtain a CT neck before any intervention in all cases in this setting
Order a monospot test before any treatment as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When the clinical picture is classic and the bulge is well defined in a cooperative, stable patient, peritonsillar abscess is largely a clinical diagnosis, and drainage by needle aspiration or incision and drainage is both diagnostic and therapeutic. CT with contrast is reserved for cases where the diagnosis is uncertain, the exam is limited by trismus, complications are suspected, or drainage is unsuccessful—it is not mandatory in every case. Switching oral antibiotics without drainage leaves a walled-off purulent collection inadequately treated. A monospot may be relevant if mononucleosis is suspected but does not address the acute abscess.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After drainage, which antibiotic regimen provides the most appropriate empiric coverage?
options: Ampicillin-sulbactam , covering streptococci, S. aureus,
Continue amoxicillin alone as initial therapy
Oral azithromycin monotherapy as initial therapy
Topical antibiotic gargle only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Peritonsillar abscesses are typically polymicrobial, with group A streptococcus, S. aureus, and oral anaerobes; ampicillin-sulbactam (or clindamycin in penicillin-allergic patients) provides appropriate empiric coverage after drainage. Continuing amoxicillin alone is inadequate—he already failed it and it lacks reliable anaerobic and staphylococcal coverage. Azithromycin monotherapy has rising streptococcal resistance and insufficient coverage for this polymicrobial infection. A topical gargle cannot treat a deep peritonsillar collection. Adequate analgesia, hydration, and consideration of a single dose of corticosteroid to reduce pain and swelling round out management; ENT follow-up is arranged, and recurrent disease may prompt tonsillectomy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which feature would most strongly prompt escalation to CT imaging and urgent ENT involvement rather than bedside drainage alone?
options: A single low-grade fever with appropriate follow-up
Mild dehydration responsive to oral fluids with appropriate follow-up
Pain radiating to the ipsilateral ear with appropriate follow-up
Trismus so severe that the oropharynx cannot be adequately examined or drained, with concern for spread to deep neck spaces
stages.explanation: Severe trismus that prevents adequate examination or bedside drainage, especially with signs suggesting extension into the parapharyngeal or deep neck spaces, warrants contrast CT to define the anatomy and urgent ENT involvement for drainage in a controlled setting. Mild dehydration responsive to oral fluids is managed supportively and does not by itself require CT. A single low-grade fever is expected with the infection and is nonspecific. Referred otalgia (ear pain) is a common and expected feature of peritonsillar abscess due to shared glossopharyngeal innervation and does not change management. The concern is anatomic uncertainty and possible deep neck space spread.

pearls: Classic peritonsillar abscess triad: muffled 'hot potato' voice, trismus, and uvular deviation away from a unilateral peritonsillar bulge.
In a stable, cooperative patient with a clear bulge, diagnosis is clinical; needle aspiration or incision and drainage is both diagnostic and therapeutic.
Reserve contrast CT for uncertain diagnosis, severe trismus limiting exam/drainage, or suspected deep neck space extension.
Treat with drainage plus antibiotics covering strep, S. aureus, and anaerobes (ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin); recurrent disease may warrant tonsillectomy.

cases.title: A Tired Teenager With a Rash After Antibiotics
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of profound fatigue and sore throat, now with a new diffuse rash.
cases.history: A 16-year-old girl presents to clinic with about 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, low-grade fevers, and a sore throat. She has been sleeping more than usual and missing school because she is too tired to get through the day. One week ago she was seen at an urgent care, told she had a throat infection, and prescribed amoxicillin. A few days into the antibiotic she developed a diffuse, blotchy, maculopapular rash over her trunk and extremities. She also reports mild left-sided abdominal fullness and decreased appetite. She denies dysuria, headache, photophobia, or joint swelling. Past medical history is unremarkable. Her only medication is the recently started amoxicillin; she takes no others. She has no previously known drug allergies. Social history: high school junior, runs cross-country, denies alcohol or drug use, and has a steady boyfriend; she reports kissing but no other sexual activity. Immunizations are up to date. Family history is noncontributory. She is concerned the rash means she is allergic to penicillin.
cases.exam: Fatigued-appearing but nontoxic adolescent. There is symmetric, tender posterior cervical lymphadenopathy as well as anterior cervical and mild axillary nodes. The pharynx is erythematous with enlarged tonsils and gray-white exudates; the palate shows scattered petechiae. A faint, diffuse maculopapular rash is present over the trunk and proximal extremities, without target lesions, mucosal sloughing, or facial swelling. Abdominal exam reveals a palpable spleen tip about 2 cm below the left costal margin, mildly tender; no hepatomegaly. There is no jaundice. Lungs are clear and the cardiac exam is normal. There is no joint swelling, no meningismus, and no periorbital or lip edema to suggest a true anaphylactic drug reaction.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most specifically points to the likely diagnosis?
options: Anterior cervical lymphadenopathy as the primary diagnosis
Low-grade fever as the primary diagnosis
Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy with splenomegaly
Tonsillar exudates alone as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Prominent posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and a morbilliform rash precipitated by amoxicillin are highly characteristic of infectious mononucleosis from Epstein-Barr virus; the amoxicillin-induced rash is a classic, nonallergic phenomenon in EBV infection. Anterior cervical adenopathy is common but nonspecific and seen in ordinary streptococcal pharyngitis. Tonsillar exudates occur in both strep throat and mononucleosis and do not distinguish them. Low-grade fever is nonspecific. The combination of posterior chain nodes, an enlarged spleen, the prolonged fatigue, and the post-amoxicillin rash in an adolescent is the constellation that strongly favors EBV mononucleosis over uncomplicated bacterial pharyngitis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which test best confirms the diagnosis, and what limitation should you keep in mind?
options: Heterophile antibody test, which can be falsely negative early in illness
Anti-nuclear antibody, which is diagnostic of this condition in this setting
Throat culture, which is the gold standard for this diagnosis in this setting
Chest radiograph, which typically confirms the diagnosis in this setting
stages.explanation: The heterophile antibody (monospot) test supports a diagnosis of EBV mononucleosis but can be falsely negative early (especially within the first week) and in younger children; EBV-specific serologies (VCA IgM/IgG, EBNA) can be used when timing or suspicion warrants. A throat culture detects group A strep, not EBV, and does not confirm mononucleosis. ANA evaluates for autoimmune disease such as lupus and is not diagnostic of EBV. A chest radiograph plays no role in confirming mononucleosis. A CBC often shows lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes, and mild transaminase elevation is common, supporting the clinical picture.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.6 ×10³/µL with 62% lymphocytes and atypical lymphocytes on smear
Heterophile (monospot): positive
AST 78 U/L
ALT 84 U/L
Throat rapid strep antigen: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management and counseling for this patient?
options: Start acyclovir to shorten the illness as initial therapy
Supportive care, label the amoxicillin reaction appropriately,
Restart amoxicillin since the rash was not a true allergy as initial therapy
Prescribe a course of corticosteroids for all patients with this condition as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Mononucleosis is managed supportively with rest, fluids, and analgesics; patients with splenomegaly should avoid contact and collision sports for at least 3-4 weeks (and until splenomegaly resolves) to prevent splenic rupture. The amoxicillin-associated morbilliform rash in EBV is not a true penicillin allergy and should be documented as such rather than labeled a lifelong allergy. Routine corticosteroids are not recommended and are reserved for specific complications such as impending airway obstruction from tonsillar hypertrophy. Antivirals like acyclovir do not meaningfully alter the clinical course. There is no indication to restart amoxicillin since strep testing is negative.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the most important to counsel this patient about given her exam findings?
options: Pulmonary fibrosis
Permanent hearing loss
Splenic rupture
Chronic kidney disease
stages.explanation: Splenomegaly in mononucleosis predisposes to splenic rupture, which can occur spontaneously or with minor trauma and is a potentially life-threatening complication; this is why contact sports are restricted until the spleen returns to normal size. Chronic kidney disease is not a recognized complication of EBV mononucleosis. Permanent hearing loss is unrelated to this illness. Pulmonary fibrosis is not associated with mononucleosis. Other complications to keep in mind include airway obstruction from marked tonsillar enlargement, hepatitis (reflected by her mildly elevated transaminases), and rarely hematologic effects, but the enlarged, palpable spleen makes rupture the key counseling point.

pearls: EBV mononucleosis: prolonged fatigue, exudative pharyngitis, posterior cervical adenopathy, splenomegaly, and a morbilliform rash after amoxicillin/ampicillin (not a true penicillin allergy).
Monospot can be falsely negative in the first week; EBV-specific serologies (VCA IgM/IgG, EBNA) help when timing is early; expect atypical lymphocytosis and mild transaminitis.
Manage supportively; avoid contact sports for at least 3-4 weeks and until splenomegaly resolves to prevent splenic rupture.
Reserve corticosteroids for complications such as impending airway obstruction; antivirals do not change the course.

cases.title: A Toddler With a Swollen Knee and Morning Stiffness
cases.chiefComplaint: Limping and a swollen knee that is worse in the mornings.
cases.history: A 3-year-old girl is brought to clinic because her parents have noticed about 8 weeks of intermittent limping, most pronounced when she first wakes up. In the mornings she seems stiff and reluctant to walk, but after she has been moving for 30-60 minutes she loosens up and plays normally. Her right knee has appeared swollen on and off. There has been no clear trauma. She has had no fevers, no rash, no weight loss, and no night sweats. Her appetite and energy are normal. She has not complained much of pain, but she favors the leg. Past medical history is unremarkable; she is fully immunized and developmentally on track. She takes no medications and has no drug allergies. Social history: lives with both parents; attends daycare. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with psoriasis and a grandparent with rheumatoid arthritis. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no eye redness or pain that the parents have noticed, though they admit she is too young to reliably report eye symptoms.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile, playful toddler. The right knee is visibly swollen with a warm effusion and mildly reduced range of motion at the extreme of flexion; there is no overlying erythema and only mild tenderness. She bears weight but with a subtle antalgic gait. The left knee and other joints are normal, with no swelling of the wrists, ankles, hips, or small joints. There is no rash, no nail pitting, and no dactylitis. Eyes appear white and quiet on gross inspection, with no obvious redness, but a careful uveitis screen has not yet been performed. There is no lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly, and no heart murmur. Growth parameters are tracking normally along prior percentiles.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly suggests an inflammatory arthritis rather than mechanical or transient joint pain?
options: A single episode of pain after a fall as the first step today
Pain only at night that wakes her, with normal daytime function
Morning stiffness that improves with movement over several weeks
Pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest
stages.explanation: Morning stiffness that eases as the child moves, persisting for weeks, is the classic pattern of inflammatory arthritis such as juvenile idiopathic arthritis; inflammation accumulates with rest and loosens with activity. Pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest is more typical of mechanical or overuse problems. A single episode after a fall suggests trauma rather than chronic inflammation. Isolated night pain with entirely normal daytime function raises concern for so-called growing pains or, if severe and progressive, malignancy, but it is not the inflammatory pattern. The chronicity (over 6 weeks) plus morning stiffness in a single warm, swollen joint points toward oligoarticular JIA.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: JIA is largely a clinical diagnosis of exclusion. Which initial workup is most appropriate?
options: Immediate joint replacement evaluation as the immediate next step
Empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed septic arthritis
No testing; reassure that it is growing pains as the immediate next step
Inflammatory markers, CBC, ANA,
stages.explanation: JIA is a clinical diagnosis requiring arthritis for at least 6 weeks before age 16 with exclusion of other causes; appropriate initial evaluation includes inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), CBC with differential (to help exclude malignancy and infection), ANA (which informs uveitis risk), and referral to pediatric rheumatology. Joint replacement is not relevant in a toddler. Empiric IV antibiotics are reserved for suspected septic arthritis, which typically presents acutely with a hot, exquisitely painful, immobile joint, high fever, and toxicity—not this subacute, low-pain, afebrile picture. Dismissing it as growing pains ignores the objective effusion and chronic morning stiffness.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 8.1 ×10³/µL (normal differential)
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Platelets 340 ×10³/µL
ESR 32 mm/hr
CRP 14 mg/L
ANA: positive (titer 1:320)
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Right knee radiograph: soft tissue swelling/effusion, no fracture or lytic lesion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a positive ANA in oligoarticular JIA, which silent but vision-threatening complication must be actively screened for?
options: Retinal detachment from trauma
Chronic anterior uveitis
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Bacterial conjunctivitis
stages.explanation: ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA, especially in young girls, carries the highest risk of chronic anterior uveitis (iridocyclitis), which is typically asymptomatic ('white' eye) yet can cause cataracts, glaucoma, and permanent vision loss if undetected; this mandates regular slit-lamp screening by ophthalmology. Retinal detachment from trauma is unrelated to JIA. Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with a painful red eye and is not the characteristic JIA-associated eye disease. Bacterial conjunctivitis causes discharge and redness and is not a chronic JIA complication. Because the uveitis is silent, screening is based on subtype, ANA status, age, and disease duration rather than symptoms.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial treatment approach for oligoarticular JIA limited to one knee?
options: Immediate biologic therapy for all patients before any other treatment as initial therapy
Systemic high-dose corticosteroids indefinitely as initial therapy
Opioid analgesics as the mainstay as initial therapy
NSAIDs and consideration of intra-articular corticosteroid injection, with scheduled ophthalmologic surveillance
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for oligoarticular JIA includes NSAIDs and, for a persistently active single joint, intra-articular corticosteroid injection, combined with mandatory scheduled slit-lamp eye exams to detect silent uveitis; methotrexate or biologics are escalated if disease is refractory or uveitis develops. Indefinite systemic high-dose corticosteroids carry unacceptable toxicity (growth suppression, osteoporosis) and are not first-line for limited oligoarticular disease. Opioids are inappropriate for a chronic inflammatory arthritis and do not treat the underlying inflammation. Starting a biologic in every patient up front is not standard for limited oligoarticular disease, which often responds to local therapy and NSAIDs.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If this child instead had daily spiking fevers, an evanescent salmon-colored rash, and hepatosplenomegaly, which JIA subtype would you suspect, and what life-threatening complication accompanies it?
options: Enthesitis-related JIA; risk of nephrotic syndrome
Oligoarticular JIA; risk of permanent blindness
Psoriatic JIA; risk of aortic dissection based on current risk
Systemic JIA ; risk of macrophage activation syndrome
stages.explanation: Daily (quotidian) spiking fevers, an evanescent salmon-pink rash, arthritis, and hepatosplenomegaly characterize systemic JIA (Still disease), which can be complicated by macrophage activation syndrome (MAS)—a form of secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis marked by sustained fever, falling cell counts, very high ferritin, hypofibrinogenemia, and a paradoxically falling ESR, which can be rapidly fatal. Oligoarticular JIA is the subtype in this case and is linked to silent uveitis, not the systemic-fever syndrome. Psoriatic JIA features psoriasis, nail pitting, and dactylitis, not the salmon rash, and is not associated with aortic dissection. Enthesitis-related JIA involves enthesitis and sacroiliitis, not nephrotic syndrome. Recognizing MAS in systemic JIA is high-yield because it is an emergency.

pearls: JIA requires arthritis for ≥6 weeks before age 16 with exclusion of infection, malignancy, and other causes; morning stiffness improving with activity is the inflammatory hallmark.
Oligoarticular JIA (≤4 joints, often the knee) in young ANA-positive girls carries the highest risk of chronic, asymptomatic anterior uveitis—mandate regular slit-lamp screening.
Initial therapy: NSAIDs and intra-articular steroid injection for a persistent single joint; escalate to methotrexate/biologics if refractory or if uveitis develops.
A subacute, low-pain, afebrile swollen joint argues against septic arthritis, which is acute, hot, exquisitely painful, and toxic.

cases.title: A Boy With Sickle Cell Disease Who Is Suddenly Pale and Exhausted
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of fatigue and pallor with a recent fever and facial rash.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy with known hemoglobin SS sickle cell disease is admitted for evaluation of profound fatigue, pallor, and decreased activity over the past 4 days. About a week ago he had a low-grade fever, headache, and a bright red 'slapped cheek' facial rash followed by a lacy rash on his arms; several classmates had a similar rash. Now the rash has faded, but he has become increasingly tired, short of breath with exertion, and his mother says he looks 'washed out.' He has not had a vaso-occlusive pain crisis. His baseline hemoglobin runs around 8 g/dL. Past medical history: HbSS diagnosed in infancy, on hydroxyurea and penicillin prophylaxis; up to date on pneumococcal vaccination. No known drug allergies. He takes folic acid daily. Social history: lives with his mother and attends fourth grade. Family history: a sibling also has sickle cell disease. On admission he is tired but conversant. He has not had bloody stools, dark urine, or recent transfusion. There is no chest pain or cough at this time.
cases.exam: Pale, fatigued boy who is alert and oriented. Conjunctivae and palms are notably pale. There is mild scleral icterus at his baseline. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia with a soft 2/6 systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border, consistent with a high-output anemic state; pulses are strong. Lungs are clear with no focal findings or crackles. The faint reticular rash on the extensor arms is fading. The abdomen is soft; the spleen is not palpable (autosplenectomy expected at this age), and the liver edge is at baseline. There is no focal bone tenderness, no swelling of the hands or feet, and no neurologic deficit. Capillary refill is brisk despite the pallor.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the preceding 'slapped cheek' illness and worsening anemia, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hemolytic crisis from a new medication
Acute splenic sequestration crisis
Acute chest syndrome as the primary diagnosis
Transient aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19
stages.explanation: Erythema infectiosum (slapped-cheek rash) is caused by parvovirus B19, which infects erythroid precursors and transiently halts red cell production; in a patient with chronic hemolysis like sickle cell disease, this causes a transient aplastic crisis with abrupt worsening anemia and an inappropriately low reticulocyte count. Acute splenic sequestration causes anemia with a rapidly enlarging, tender spleen and a high reticulocyte count, unlikely here given expected autosplenectomy and an impalpable spleen. Acute chest syndrome features fever, hypoxia, chest pain, and a new pulmonary infiltrate, none of which dominate here. A medication-induced hemolytic crisis would show a high reticulocyte response, not the suppressed marrow expected with parvovirus.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which single laboratory finding best distinguishes aplastic crisis from an acute hemolytic or sequestration event?
options: An elevated reticulocyte count in this setting
An elevated total bilirubin in this setting
A markedly low reticulocyte count
A high lactate dehydrogenase in this setting
stages.explanation: In a transient aplastic crisis the bone marrow temporarily stops producing red cells, so despite severe anemia the reticulocyte count is inappropriately low—this is the key distinguishing feature. In hemolytic or sequestration crises the marrow responds appropriately, producing a high reticulocyte count. Elevated total bilirubin reflects ongoing hemolysis and is often present at baseline in sickle cell disease; during aplastic crisis hemolysis markers may actually fall as red cell turnover decreases, so bilirubin is not the discriminator. LDH likewise reflects hemolysis and cell turnover and does not specifically identify marrow suppression. The low reticulocyte count in the face of worsening anemia is the hallmark.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 4.2 g/dL (baseline ~8 g/dL)
Reticulocyte count 0.3% (markedly low)
WBC 7.0 ×10³/µL
Platelets 210 ×10³/µL
Total bilirubin 1.4 mg/dL
Parvovirus B19 IgM: positive
Reticulocyte index: profoundly depressed

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With a hemoglobin of 4.2 g/dL, tachycardia, and exertional dyspnea, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Emergent splenectomy as initial therapy
Discharge with outpatient iron supplementation as initial therapy
Start corticosteroids to suppress hemolysis as initial therapy
Simple red blood cell transfusion with supportive care
stages.explanation: A transient aplastic crisis with symptomatic, severe anemia (Hb 4.2 g/dL with tachycardia and dyspnea) is treated with simple red cell transfusion to support oxygen delivery while the marrow recovers over 1-2 weeks; supportive care and monitoring of the reticulocyte count to confirm recovery are essential. Outpatient iron is wrong—this is not iron deficiency, and the problem is transient marrow suppression. Splenectomy is irrelevant; the issue is erythroid arrest, not sequestration, and autosplenectomy is expected at this age. Corticosteroids do not treat parvovirus-induced red cell aplasia. Droplet isolation is also important because the patient is contagious and parvovirus poses risks to pregnant staff.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which infection-control measure is most important during this admission?
options: No precautions are necessary once the rash has faded based on current risk
Strict contact isolation for resistant bacteria with appropriate follow-up
Droplet precautions, with caution to keep pregnant
Airborne (negative-pressure) isolation as for tuberculosis
stages.explanation: Parvovirus B19 spreads by respiratory droplets, and patients in aplastic crisis are still viremic and contagious (unlike immunocompetent children with classic erythema infectiosum, who are no longer infectious once the rash appears). Droplet precautions are warranted, and pregnant or immunocompromised contacts should avoid exposure because of risks of fetal hydrops and severe aplasia. Contact isolation for resistant bacteria does not match this viral, droplet-spread infection. Airborne negative-pressure isolation is reserved for pathogens like tuberculosis, measles, or varicella, not parvovirus. Assuming no precautions are needed once the rash fades is correct for the typical immunocompetent child but dangerously wrong in an aplastic-crisis patient who remains viremic.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: On hospital day 4 after transfusion, which laboratory trend best confirms that the marrow is recovering?
options: A rising reticulocyte count toward
A widening anion gap in this setting
A rising total bilirubin in this setting
A falling platelet count in this setting
stages.explanation: Recovery from a transient aplastic crisis is signaled by the return of erythropoiesis, so a rising reticulocyte count—often climbing toward and even above baseline as the marrow rebounds—confirms resolution; the hemoglobin then stabilizes and improves. A falling platelet count would suggest worsening marrow function or another process, not recovery. A rising total bilirubin reflects increasing hemolysis or red cell turnover and does not specifically indicate marrow recovery from aplasia. A widening anion gap points to a metabolic acidosis unrelated to erythroid recovery. Tracking the reticulocyte response is the standard way to confirm that the parvovirus-induced erythroid arrest has resolved and transfusion support can be weaned.

cases.diagnosis: Parvovirus B19-induced transient aplastic crisis in sickle cell disease
pearls: Parvovirus B19 (erythema infectiosum/'slapped cheek') arrests erythroid precursors; in chronic hemolysis (sickle cell), this causes a transient aplastic crisis.
The hallmark is severe anemia with an inappropriately LOW reticulocyte count, distinguishing it from hemolytic or sequestration crises (high reticulocytes).
Treat symptomatic severe anemia with simple transfusion and supportive care; marrow recovery occurs in 1-2 weeks.
Aplastic-crisis patients remain viremic and contagious—use droplet precautions and keep pregnant/immunocompromised contacts away (fetal hydrops risk).

cases.title: A Toddler With Repeated Fevers and Urinary Infections
cases.chiefComplaint: A third febrile urinary tract infection in the past several months.
cases.history: A 2-year-old girl is referred to clinic after her third culture-confirmed febrile urinary tract infection in 6 months. Each episode presented with high fever and irritability without an obvious source, and urine cultures grew Escherichia coli; she responded to oral antibiotics each time. Between episodes she is well, growing normally, and developmentally appropriate. She was toilet-training but has regressed slightly. There is no history of constipation severe enough to be obstructive, though her parents report somewhat infrequent stools. She was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy; a prenatal ultrasound was reportedly normal. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. She takes no chronic medications and has no drug allergies. Social history: lives with both parents; up to date on immunizations. Family history is notable for a mother who had recurrent childhood UTIs and a cousin with a known kidney reflux problem. On exam between infections she is afebrile and well-appearing, with no flank tenderness and a normal abdominal and genitourinary exam.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile toddler in no distress, interactive and playful. Growth parameters are tracking along prior percentiles. The abdomen is soft and nontender, with no palpable masses or flank fullness and no costovertebral angle tenderness. There is no suprapubic tenderness. External genitourinary exam is normal with no labial adhesions, no signs of trauma, and no abnormal discharge. The lower back shows no sacral dimple, hair tuft, or other midline cutaneous marker of occult spinal dysraphism. Neurologic exam, including lower-extremity tone and reflexes, is normal. There is no peripheral edema. Blood pressure is normal for age. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After recurrent febrile UTIs in a young child, which imaging is the appropriate first step to evaluate the urinary tract anatomy?
options: MRI of the lumbar spine as the first step today
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast
Renal and bladder ultrasound
Immediate voiding cystourethrogram without any prior imaging
stages.explanation: Renal and bladder ultrasound is the appropriate initial, noninvasive study after recurrent or atypical febrile UTIs; it assesses kidney size, hydronephrosis, scarring, duplication, and bladder abnormalities without radiation. CT delivers significant radiation and is not first-line for routine evaluation of pediatric UTI. A voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) is the test that actually diagnoses and grades vesicoureteral reflux, but it is invasive and involves bladder catheterization and radiation, so it is generally obtained after ultrasound, particularly with recurrent febrile UTIs or abnormal ultrasound findings. Lumbar spine MRI would be considered only if there were signs of neurogenic bladder or spinal dysraphism, which are absent here.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which study specifically diagnoses and grades vesicoureteral reflux, the suspected underlying problem?
options: Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG)
Plain abdominal radiograph (KUB)
Renal ultrasound alone in this setting
Dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) scan
stages.explanation: A voiding cystourethrogram diagnoses vesicoureteral reflux and assigns the grade (I-V) based on how far urine refluxes and the degree of ureteral/pelvicalyceal dilation; it is the definitive test for VUR. Renal ultrasound can suggest hydronephrosis or scarring but cannot reliably diagnose or grade reflux, since reflux is dynamic and may not be seen on a static study. A DMSA scan evaluates for renal cortical scarring and pyelonephritis (split function and parenchymal defects) but does not demonstrate or grade reflux itself. A plain KUB radiograph provides no information about reflux. Grading on VCUG directly informs prognosis and management decisions.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Renal/bladder ultrasound: mild right-sided hydronephrosis; left kidney normal; bladder wall normal
VCUG: grade III vesicoureteral reflux on the right (reflux into a mildly dilated ureter and renal pelvis with mild calyceal blunting); no reflux on the left
Urinalysis (current, asymptomatic): negative leukocyte esterase and nitrites

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 2-year-old with grade III reflux and recurrent febrile UTIs, which management approach is most appropriate?
options: Continuous low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis with monitoring, plus treatment of bowel/bladder dysfunction
No intervention and no follow-up as initial therapy
Immediate open ureteral reimplantation surgery as initial therapy
Long-term indwelling urinary catheter as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For lower-to-moderate grade reflux (such as grade III) with recurrent febrile UTIs, continuous antibiotic prophylaxis (commonly trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or nitrofurantoin) is a reasonable approach to reduce recurrent infections and renal scarring while many cases resolve spontaneously with growth; addressing bowel and bladder dysfunction (e.g., the regression and infrequent stools) is an essential adjunct. Immediate open surgery is reserved for high-grade reflux, breakthrough febrile UTIs on prophylaxis, or progressive scarring, not as the initial step here. Doing nothing without follow-up risks recurrent pyelonephritis and renal scarring. A chronic indwelling catheter would increase infection risk and is not a treatment for reflux.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which long-term complication is the primary reason to monitor and treat this child's reflux?
options: Bladder cancer with appropriate follow-up
Adrenal insufficiency with appropriate follow-up
Kidney stones with appropriate follow-up
Renal scarring leading to hypertension
stages.explanation: The chief long-term concern in vesicoureteral reflux with recurrent febrile UTIs is reflux nephropathy—renal cortical scarring from repeated pyelonephritis—which can lead to hypertension, proteinuria, and chronic kidney disease over time; this drives the rationale for preventing infections and monitoring renal growth and function. Bladder cancer is not a recognized complication of pediatric VUR. Kidney stones are not the principal risk associated with reflux. Adrenal insufficiency is unrelated to the urinary reflux pathway. Periodic blood pressure checks, growth monitoring, and follow-up imaging help detect scarring and guide whether reflux is resolving or whether escalation to surgical correction is warranted.

cases.diagnosis: Vesicoureteral reflux (grade III) with recurrent febrile UTIs
pearls: After recurrent/atypical febrile UTIs in young children, start with renal-bladder ultrasound; VCUG is the test that diagnoses and grades vesicoureteral reflux (I-V).
Lower-to-moderate grade VUR often resolves spontaneously; continuous antibiotic prophylaxis reduces recurrent febrile UTIs and scarring while awaiting resolution.
Always evaluate and treat bowel/bladder dysfunction (constipation, dysfunctional voiding)—it both causes and worsens UTIs and reflux.
The key long-term risk is reflux nephropathy: scarring from recurrent pyelonephritis causing hypertension and chronic kidney disease; surgery is reserved for high grade or breakthrough infections.

cases.title: An Infant With an Empty Scrotal Sac
cases.chiefComplaint: Routine visit at which one testis cannot be felt in the scrotum.
cases.history: A 9-month-old boy is brought in for a well-child visit. His parents have no specific concerns, but the pediatrician notes at this and the prior visit that the right side of the scrotum feels empty. The parents recall being told at birth that one testicle 'had not come down yet' and that it might descend on its own. He was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy with an appropriate birth weight. He is feeding well, growing along the 50th percentile, and meeting developmental milestones. There is no history of pain, swelling, vomiting, or irritability to suggest an acute problem. He has had no prior surgeries and takes no medications; he has no known drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. Social history: lives with both parents; no concerning exposures. Family history is notable for a paternal uncle who required an operation in childhood for an undescended testicle. There is no family history of disorders of sexual development, infertility, or testicular cancer that the parents are aware of.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable infant. The left hemiscrotum contains a normally positioned, normal-sized testis. The right hemiscrotum is empty and slightly underdeveloped (hypoplastic). With warm hands and the child relaxed, the right testis can be palpated in the inguinal canal but cannot be brought down into the scrotum and does not remain there—consistent with a true undescended (not retractile) testis. There is no inguinal bulge to suggest a hernia, no scrotal swelling, no tenderness, and no overlying skin changes. The penis is normally formed with the urethral meatus at the tip (no hypospadias). The abdominal exam is benign with no masses. There are no dysmorphic features. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How is a true undescended testis best distinguished from a retractile testis on exam?
options: They cannot be distinguished without surgery as the first step today
A retractile testis can be manipulated into the scrotum
An undescended testis is always painful as the first step today
A retractile testis can never be brought into the scrotum as the first step today
stages.explanation: A retractile testis is pulled up by an active cremasteric reflex but can be gently coaxed into the scrotum and will stay there at least briefly when the reflex is overcome; it is a normal variant requiring observation. A true undescended (cryptorchid) testis either cannot be brought into the scrotum or springs back immediately, as in this infant. Saying a retractile testis can never reach the scrotum is incorrect—that describes cryptorchidism. An undescended testis is typically painless unless complicated by torsion; pain is not the distinguishing feature. The two can usually be differentiated clinically by an experienced examiner with warm hands and a relaxed child, without surgery.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: At 9 months, his right testis has not descended. What does current guidance say about spontaneous descent?
options: Spontaneous descent rarely occurs after about 6 months of age, so persistence warrants referral
It will descend at puberty without intervention as the primary diagnosis
Descent timing cannot be predicted at all as the primary diagnosis
Most undescended testes descend on their own after the first year, so continued waiting is appropriate
stages.explanation: Most spontaneous descent occurs in the first few months of life, and a testis that remains undescended beyond about 6 months (corrected for gestational age) is unlikely to descend on its own; this is why persistent cryptorchidism at this age warrants surgical referral rather than further watchful waiting. Expecting most testes to descend after the first year is incorrect and leads to harmful delay. Relying on descent at puberty is a myth and risks impaired fertility and delayed cancer surveillance. While exact timing for any individual is variable, the general principle that the window for spontaneous descent has effectively closed by around 6 months is well established.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Order an abdominal ultrasound to locate the palpable testis before any referral
Refer to pediatric urology/surgery for orchiopexy
Start hormonal therapy with hCG as first-line treatment
Reassure and recheck in 2 years as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A palpable undescended testis persisting beyond 6 months should be referred to pediatric urology or surgery for orchiopexy, ideally performed between roughly 6 and 18 months of age to optimize fertility potential and facilitate future testicular examination. Imaging such as ultrasound is generally not helpful or necessary for a palpable testis—it does not change management, and for nonpalpable testes the standard is diagnostic laparoscopy rather than ultrasound. Hormonal therapy is not recommended as standard first-line treatment in current guidelines. Reassurance and waiting another 2 years inappropriately delays correction past the optimal surgical window and increases risks.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Why is timely orchiopexy important even though the condition is currently asymptomatic?
options: It prevents future hernias only with appropriate follow-up
It guarantees normal fertility and eliminates all cancer risk with appropriate follow-up
It improves fertility potential
It is purely cosmetic with no medical benefit with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Orchiopexy improves fertility outcomes by relocating the testis to the cooler scrotal environment and makes the testis palpable for self- and clinician examination, which matters because cryptorchidism is associated with a modestly increased lifelong risk of testicular cancer that persists even after correction. It does not guarantee normal fertility or eliminate cancer risk—it mitigates and enables surveillance, which is why the option claiming a guarantee is wrong. The procedure is not merely cosmetic; it has clear medical benefits. While an associated hernia may be repaired at the same time, preventing hernias is not the primary rationale for orchiopexy.

pearls: Distinguish a true undescended testis (cannot stay in the scrotum) from a retractile testis (can be coaxed in and stays briefly—a normal variant).
Spontaneous descent is unlikely after about 6 months of age; persistent cryptorchidism warrants surgical referral.
Orchiopexy is ideally performed between ~6 and 18 months; imaging is unhelpful for a palpable testis, and nonpalpable testes are evaluated by laparoscopy, not ultrasound.
Correction improves fertility and enables exam, but a modestly increased lifelong testicular cancer risk persists—counsel on self-examination.

cases.title: A Teen With Sudden, Severe One-Sided Scrotal Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt onset of severe left scrotal pain with nausea.
cases.history: A 14-year-old boy presents to the ED with sudden, severe pain in the left side of the scrotum that began about 3 hours ago while he was playing video games. The pain was abrupt and intense from the start, associated with nausea and one episode of vomiting. He denies dysuria, urethral discharge, or fever. He has had no recent trauma, though he recalls a brief similar episode of milder pain weeks ago that resolved spontaneously. He is not sexually active. Past medical history is unremarkable; no prior surgeries. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. Social history: ninth grader, denies tobacco, alcohol, or drug use. Family history is noncontributory. He is in obvious discomfort, reluctant to move, and reports the pain has not improved since onset. There has been no penile swelling, no abdominal surgery, and no inguinal bulge that he has noticed. He localizes the pain to the testicle itself rather than the lower abdomen.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable adolescent in acute distress. The left testis is exquisitely tender, swollen, and lies higher in the scrotum than the right with a horizontal (transverse) orientation. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the left but present on the right. There is no relief of pain with elevation of the testis (negative Prehn sign). There is no overlying erythema early on, no penile discharge, and no inguinal hernia. The right testis is normal in position, size, and sensitivity, with an intact cremasteric reflex. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no guarding. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Skin is warm and well perfused. No 'blue dot' sign is seen at the upper pole to suggest torsion of the appendix testis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings most strongly supports testicular torsion over epididymitis?
options: A small tender nodule at the upper pole with a blue discoloration
Abrupt severe pain, a high-riding horizontal testis,
Pain relieved by lying still and worsened by walking only as the first step today
Gradual pain, dysuria, fever, and pain relief with testicular elevation
stages.explanation: Sudden, severe pain with a high-riding, transversely oriented testis and an absent cremasteric reflex is the classic picture of testicular torsion, which results from twisting of the spermatic cord and acute ischemia. Gradual pain with dysuria, fever, and relief on elevation (positive Prehn sign) points toward epididymitis rather than torsion. Pain modulated only by lying still versus walking is nonspecific and does not localize the cause. A small tender upper-pole nodule with a blue-dot sign suggests torsion of the appendix testis, a self-limited condition distinct from torsion of the testis itself. The abrupt onset plus the absent cremasteric reflex makes spermatic cord torsion the leading concern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the high clinical suspicion and the time course, what is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Obtain urgent urology/surgery consultation for emergent scrotal exploration without delaying for imaging
Start oral antibiotics for presumed epididymitis and arrange outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Administer analgesia and discharge with next-day urology clinic referral as initial therapy
Wait for a Doppler ultrasound before involving surgery, regardless of delay as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When clinical suspicion for testicular torsion is high, the priority is urgent surgical consultation and emergent scrotal exploration, because testicular salvage is time-dependent and imaging should not delay definitive care. Waiting for ultrasound regardless of delay can cost the testis; while Doppler ultrasound (showing decreased/absent blood flow) can support the diagnosis, it must not postpone surgery when suspicion is high. Treating empirically as epididymitis would dangerously miss torsion. Discharging with next-day follow-up risks irreversible ischemia. Manual detorsion can be attempted as a temporizing maneuver, but it does not replace exploration with detorsion and bilateral orchiopexy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: no pyuria, no nitrites, no bacteriuria
Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound: decreased to absent arterial blood flow to the left testis with a 'whirlpool' appearance of the spermatic cord; normal flow to the right testis
Time from symptom onset: now approximately 4 hours

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Why does the roughly 4-6 hour window from symptom onset matter, and what does it imply here?
options: The window only matters for the contralateral testis with appropriate follow-up
Testicular salvage rates are highest when detorsion occurs within about 6 hours, so emergent surgery should proceed now
After 4-6 hours antibiotics become effective, so medical therapy is preferred with appropriate follow-up
After 6 hours the testis always recovers spontaneously with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Testicular viability depends on restoring blood flow promptly: salvage rates are highest (often well above 90%) when detorsion occurs within about 6 hours of onset and decline substantially thereafter, so at roughly 4 hours this patient should go emergently to the operating room. Antibiotics do not treat ischemic torsion, and the time window has nothing to do with antibiotic efficacy. The testis does not recover spontaneously after 6 hours—the opposite is true, with rising risk of infarction. The window is critical for the affected testis itself, not merely the contralateral side, though bilateral orchiopexy is performed because the contralateral testis is also at risk for future torsion.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: At surgery the left testis is detorsed and remains viable. What additional step should be performed?
options: Orchiopexy of the contralateral testis to prevent future torsion
Removal of the contralateral normal testis in this presentation
Routine biopsy of both testes as the leading concern
No further intervention beyond detorsion as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Because the underlying anatomic predisposition (often a bell-clapper deformity with inadequate fixation) is typically bilateral, the contralateral testis is also at risk for torsion; therefore, after detorsing and fixing the affected side, the surgeon performs orchiopexy on the contralateral testis as well. Removing the normal contralateral testis is never appropriate. Doing nothing beyond detorsion leaves both testes inadequately fixed and at risk for recurrence. Routine bilateral biopsy is not indicated and adds unnecessary risk. If the affected testis is found nonviable at exploration, it is removed (orchiectomy), and the healthy side is still fixed prophylactically.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If definitive surgery will be delayed and manual detorsion is attempted at the bedside, which technique and endpoint are correct?
options: Rotate the testis medially ('closing a book') and stop as soon as it moves as the immediate next step
Rotate the testis laterally , guided by relief of pain
Pack the scrotum in ice for one hour before any manipulation as the immediate next step
Apply firm continuous downward traction until the pain stops as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Most torsions twist medially, so manual detorsion is usually performed by rotating the testis laterally—'opening a book'—with pain relief and restoration of a normal vertical lie indicating success; it is only a temporizing bridge to definitive surgical exploration and orchiopexy, which must still occur. Rotating medially would worsen the typical torsion, and stopping as soon as it 'moves' ignores the symptom-guided endpoint. Firm continuous traction is not the technique and risks injury. Packing the scrotum in ice does not relieve cord torsion or restore perfusion and only delays definitive care. Manual detorsion never substitutes for surgery because the cord may be incompletely untwisted and fixation is still required.

pearls: Testicular torsion: abrupt severe pain, nausea/vomiting, a high-riding transverse testis, and an absent cremasteric reflex; a prior self-resolving episode suggests intermittent torsion.
It is a clinical diagnosis and surgical emergency—do not let Doppler ultrasound delay exploration when suspicion is high.
Salvage is time-critical: detorsion within ~6 hours yields the best outcomes; manual detorsion can temporize but does not replace surgery.
At surgery, detorse and fix the affected testis (or remove if nonviable) and perform contralateral orchiopexy, since the predisposition is usually bilateral.

cases.title: A 4-Year-Old Boy Soiling His Underwear
cases.chiefComplaint: Hard, infrequent stools and recurrent soiling of the underwear for several months.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for stooling problems over the past 4 months. He passes a large, hard, painful stool roughly every 4-5 days, often clogging the toilet, and cries and crosses his legs or stands on tiptoe when he feels the urge. Over the past 6 weeks she has found loose, foul-smelling stool streaks in his underwear nearly daily, which she initially thought was diarrhea. He was toilet-trained at 3 years but recently refuses to sit on the toilet. Appetite is reduced and he complains of intermittent crampy abdominal pain. There is no vomiting, blood in stool, or weight loss. He passed meconium on day 1 of life. Diet is high in cow's milk and low in fruits, vegetables, and water. PMH is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. Growth has tracked along the 50th percentile. He attends preschool. Family history is notable for a mother with irritable bowel syndrome. Development is age-appropriate.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful, interactive boy in no distress. Abdomen is soft, nondistended, with a palpable firm, nontender mass in the left lower quadrant and suprapubic region consistent with stool; bowel sounds are normal. No hepatosplenomegaly. Perianal inspection shows a small streak of liquid stool and a tiny anterior anal fissure; anus is normally positioned with normal tone and an intact anal wink. No sacral dimple, hair tuft, or gluteal cleft deviation. Lower-extremity tone, strength, and reflexes are normal. No abdominal distension suggesting obstruction.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this boy's daily underwear soiling?
options: Infectious gastroenteritis causing diarrhea
Inflammatory bowel disease with fecal urgency
Hirschsprung disease presenting with enterocolitis
Overflow incontinence from retained stool (encopresis)
stages.explanation: The combination of infrequent painful hard stools, withholding posturing, a palpable fecal mass, and continuous leakage of liquid stool is classic for overflow (paradoxical) incontinence from functional fecal retention — encopresis. Liquid stool seeps around an impacted rectal mass and stains the underwear, mimicking diarrhea. Infectious gastroenteritis is acute and self-limited, not chronic with withholding. Hirschsprung disease typically presents in the neonatal period with delayed meconium passage (he passed meconium on day 1) and an empty rectal vault rather than overflow soiling. IBD would be expected to produce blood, weight loss, or systemic signs, all absent here.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate next step in evaluating this child?
options: Obtain a thyroid panel and serum calcium as the first step today
Order an abdominal CT scan to quantify stool burden in this clinical context
Refer immediately to pediatric surgery for rectal biopsy
Take a thorough history
stages.explanation: Functional constipation is a clinical diagnosis made by history and physical exam per the Rome IV criteria; routine labs and imaging are not needed when the history and exam are reassuring and red flags are absent. A focused exam to exclude red flags (delayed meconium, failure to thrive, abnormal anal placement, sacral anomalies, neurologic deficits) is the essential next step. Abdominal CT carries unneeded radiation and is not indicated. Thyroid and calcium testing is reserved for atypical presentations or growth failure. Rectal biopsy for Hirschsprung disease is unwarranted given a normal exam and meconium passage on day 1.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the palpable fecal mass and overflow soiling, what is the first step in treatment?
options: Initiate scheduled toilet-sitting without any medication
Disimpaction with high-dose oral polyethylene glycol
Begin a high-fiber diet and increased water intake alone
Start a low-dose daily maintenance laxative only as monotherapy
stages.explanation: When a child is impacted, disimpaction must come first — starting maintenance dosing alone will worsen overflow soiling because liquid stool continues to leak around the impaction. High-dose oral PEG 3350 (e.g., 1-1.5 g/kg/day for 3-6 days) is first-line and effective; enemas are an alternative. Once disimpaction is achieved, a lower maintenance PEG dose is started. Dietary changes and behavioral toilet-sitting are essential adjuncts for long-term success but are inadequate as initial therapy for an impacted child and must follow clean-out, not replace it.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful disimpaction, what is the most important component of the long-term maintenance plan?
options: Switching entirely to a stimulant laxative such as senna as monotherapy indefinitely
Eliminating all dairy and gluten from the diet permanently with appropriate follow-up
A 7-day course of laxatives, then stop abruptly once stools normalize
Sustained daily PEG maintenance for months with scheduled toileting
stages.explanation: Functional constipation requires prolonged maintenance — typically months — to keep stools soft, allow the stretched rectum to regain tone, and break the cycle of painful withholding. Daily PEG 3350 is titrated to one soft stool per day, paired with scheduled post-meal toilet-sitting (using the gastrocolic reflex), a reward system, and adequate fiber and fluids. Tapering should be gradual once the child is symptom-free for an extended period, not abrupt — premature discontinuation leads to relapse. Senna may supplement PEG but is not preferred monotherapy long-term. Routine elimination of dairy and gluten is unwarranted without evidence of allergy or celiac disease.

cases.diagnosis: Functional constipation with overflow fecal incontinence (encopresis)
pearls: Continuous liquid stool staining in a constipated child is overflow incontinence around an impaction, not true diarrhea.
