Functional constipation is a clinical (Rome IV) diagnosis; labs/imaging are unnecessary absent red flags.
Always disimpact before starting maintenance laxatives — maintenance alone worsens soiling.
Maintenance PEG 3350 plus scheduled toileting and behavioral support must continue for months to prevent relapse.

cases.title: A 6-Year-Old Boy With Crusted Sores Near the Nose
cases.chiefComplaint: Itchy crusted sores around the nose and mouth that have spread over several days.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy presents with skin lesions that began 5 days ago as a few small red bumps below the right nostril after a recent upper respiratory infection with rhinorrhea. The bumps quickly turned into blisters that ruptured and formed thick, golden-yellow crusts. The lesions itch, and he has been picking at them and rubbing his nose; new spots have appeared on his upper lip and right cheek. There is no fever, malaise, joint pain, or dark/cola-colored urine. He attends first grade, where a classmate reportedly has 'a skin infection.' PMH includes mild atopic dermatitis controlled with moisturizer. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. He lives with his parents and a younger sibling; no recent travel. Family history is unremarkable. He is otherwise active, eating and drinking well, with no recent weight change.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile, playful child. On the right perinasal skin, upper lip, and right cheek there are several discrete erythematous erosions covered by thick, adherent honey-colored (golden) crusts, some with surrounding erythema; a few intact thin-walled vesicles are present at the periphery. No deep ulceration, induration, or fluctuance. No facial swelling, warmth, or streaking to suggest cellulitis. Mucous membranes are clear; oropharynx normal. No cervical or submandibular lymphadenopathy that is tender or enlarged beyond shotty nodes. Remainder of the skin exam shows only chronic dry atopic patches on the antecubital fossae.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports the diagnosis of nonbullous impetigo?
options: A targetoid rash with central clearing on the trunk in this clinical context
Grouped vesicles confined to a single dermatome as the primary diagnosis
Honey-colored crusts overlying superficial erosions, spreading by contact
Deep, painful ulcers with violaceous borders as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Honey-colored (golden) crusts over superficial erosions, often perinasal/perioral, that spread by autoinoculation and contact define nonbullous impetigo — most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes. Deep painful ulcers with violaceous borders suggest ecthyma or pyoderma gangrenosum, not superficial impetigo. A targetoid rash with central clearing suggests erythema multiforme or erythema migrans. Grouped vesicles in a single dermatome describe herpes zoster. The classic crust, superficial location, recent URI, and contagious classmate all fit impetigo.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Impetigo is usually diagnosed clinically. When is a bacterial culture most useful in this setting?
options: When lesions fail to respond to therapy or there is concern for MRSA or an outbreak
Only if a viral cause is suspected as the first step today
Never, because cultures are always falsely negative in impetigo
In every case before starting any therapy as the first step today
stages.explanation: Impetigo is a clinical diagnosis, so routine culture is not required to start treatment. Culture (swab of the crust base or exudate) becomes useful when lesions do not respond to first-line therapy, when methicillin-resistant S. aureus is a concern given local resistance patterns or risk factors, or to guide management during outbreaks. Culturing every case before therapy delays care unnecessarily. Cultures target bacteria, not viruses. The claim that cultures are always falsely negative is incorrect — they reliably grow the causative organism from active lesions.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His impetigo is limited to a few lesions on the face. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical mupirocin ointment to affected areas three times daily
Intravenous vancomycin as initial therapy
Topical hydrocortisone cream as initial therapy
Oral clindamycin for 10 days as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For limited, localized nonbullous impetigo, topical antibiotic therapy is first-line — mupirocin (or retapamulin) applied to lesions, typically for 5 days, plus gentle removal of crusts with soap and water. It is as effective as oral antibiotics for limited disease with fewer systemic side effects. Oral antibiotics (e.g., cephalexin, or clindamycin if MRSA is suspected) are reserved for numerous lesions, widespread/bullous disease, or treatment failure. IV vancomycin is for severe systemic infection, not localized impetigo. Topical hydrocortisone is a steroid that does not treat bacterial infection and could worsen it.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The mother asks what to watch for after treatment. Which complication of impetigo is NOT prevented by antibiotic therapy?
options: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Transmission to household contacts
Spread of lesions to other body sites
Local cellulitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) — presenting with edema, hypertension, and cola-colored urine 1-2 weeks after streptococcal skin infection — is an immune-mediated sequela that antibiotic treatment of impetigo does NOT reliably prevent (unlike rheumatic fever, which follows pharyngitis and is preventable). Antibiotics and good hygiene do reduce lesion spread, local cellulitis, and transmission to contacts by clearing the organism. Families should be counseled on hand hygiene, avoiding shared towels, covering lesions, and keeping the child home until lesions are treated, and to report dark urine or facial swelling that would prompt evaluation for PSGN.

pearls: Honey-colored crusts over superficial erosions, often perinasal, are the hallmark of nonbullous impetigo (S. aureus/S. pyogenes).
Limited impetigo is treated with topical mupirocin; oral antibiotics are for extensive, bullous, or refractory disease.
Impetigo is highly contagious — emphasize hand hygiene, no shared towels, and covering lesions.
Antibiotics do not reliably prevent post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis; counsel families about dark urine and edema.

cases.title: A 7-Year-Old Boy With Sudden Difficulty Breathing
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening cough, wheeze, and trouble breathing for the past day.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the ED with 1 day of progressive cough, wheezing, and difficulty breathing that began after a cold and worsened tonight. He has known asthma diagnosed at age 4, with two prior ED visits and one admission, but no intubations. His controller is low-dose inhaled fluticasone twice daily, and he uses albuterol as needed; tonight his mother gave four albuterol puffs at home with only brief relief, prompting the visit. He has had increased work of breathing and is unable to speak in full sentences. There is no fever measured at home, no choking episode, no chest trauma, and no known new exposures other than a household member with a cold. He has eczema and allergic rhinitis; allergies include cats and dust. No drug allergies. Immunizations are current. He lives with his mother who smokes outside the home. Family history is notable for asthma in the mother and atopy in a sibling. He attends second grade and has missed several days this year for wheezing.
cases.exam: Anxious, sitting upright (tripoding), speaking in short 3-4 word phrases. Moderate-to-severe respiratory distress with suprasternal and subcostal retractions and nasal flaring. Chest auscultation reveals diffuse expiratory and inspiratory wheezing with a prolonged expiratory phase and markedly decreased air entry at both bases (poor air movement). No focal crackles, stridor, or asymmetry suggesting foreign body. No cyanosis at rest but oxygen saturation is low. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia, regular rhythm, no murmur. No urticaria, facial/lip swelling, or drooling. Mental status is alert but agitated.

stages.label: SEVERITY ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Based on his presentation, how would you classify the severity of this asthma exacerbation?
options: Severe — speaking in short phrases, marked retractions, SpO2 90%, poor air movement
Impending respiratory failure with silent chest and lethargy in this setting
Moderate — some retractions, saturation 92-95% as the immediate next step
Mild — minimal distress, normal saturation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: He has a severe exacerbation: inability to speak full sentences (short 3-4 word phrases), marked accessory muscle use and retractions, tachypnea, agitation, SpO2 of 90% on room air, and significantly decreased air entry (poor air movement) — the latter a worrisome sign that obstruction is limiting airflow. Mild and moderate categories do not include hypoxemia with poor air movement and inability to speak in sentences. Impending respiratory failure (the next step if untreated) is marked by a silent chest, lethargy/confusion, bradycardia, or rising CO2 — he is agitated and still moving some air, so he is severe but not yet in failure. Recognizing severity drives aggressive, prompt therapy.

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment for this severe exacerbation?
options: Nebulized racemic epinephrine and observation as the immediate next step
Supplemental oxygen, frequent/continuous inhaled albuterol with ipratropium,
Intravenous magnesium alone, deferring bronchodilators as the immediate next step
Oral amoxicillin and a chest radiograph before any therapy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Severe asthma exacerbation management is oxygen to keep SpO2 greater than 92%, inhaled short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol) given frequently or continuously, the addition of inhaled ipratropium (anticholinergic) for severe cases, and early systemic corticosteroids (oral or IV) to reduce inflammation and prevent relapse. Antibiotics and routine chest x-ray are not indicated absent signs of bacterial pneumonia and should not delay therapy. Racemic epinephrine treats croup/upper-airway edema, not lower-airway bronchospasm. IV magnesium is a useful adjunct for severe/refractory cases but should be added to — not substituted for — bronchodilators and steroids.

stages.label: REASSESSMENT AFTER FIRST-LINE THERAPY
stages.content:
After 1 hour: oxygen, three back-to-back albuterol/ipratropium nebs, and oral dexamethasone given
SpO2 92% on 2 L/min nasal cannula
RR 34 /min, HR 142 bpm
Exam: continued diffuse wheeze, persistent moderate-to-severe retractions, still speaking in short phrases
Peak flow effort poor; venous blood gas pH 7.36, pCO2 41 mmHg
Chest radiograph (obtained for persistent distress): hyperinflation, no focal infiltrate or pneumothorax

stages.label: ESCALATION
stages.question: He remains in severe distress after maximal inhaled therapy and steroids. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge home with an albuterol prescription and follow-up
Begin a beta-blocker to control the tachycardia in this setting
Stop bronchodilators and start a cough suppressant in this setting
Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate
stages.explanation: For a severe exacerbation not responding to oxygen, continuous beta-agonist, ipratropium, and systemic steroids, intravenous magnesium sulfate is the recommended next adjunct — it relaxes bronchial smooth muscle and can avert the need for ICU-level escalation. Continuous albuterol should continue. Stopping bronchodilators or starting a cough suppressant would be dangerous in active bronchospasm. He is far too unstable for discharge — note the rising/normalizing pCO2 of 41 mmHg in a tachypneic child is concerning for fatigue. Beta-blockers are contraindicated in acute asthma because they can precipitate bronchospasm; the tachycardia is expected from albuterol and the disease itself.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After IV magnesium he improves, with SpO2 96% on room air and easier breathing. What is the most appropriate discharge plan?
options: Discharge on a short course of oral corticosteroids, an albuterol action plan, review of inhaler technique,
Admit to the ICU regardless of improvement with appropriate follow-up
Discharge with oral antibiotics for 10 days with appropriate follow-up
Discharge with no medication changes and routine yearly follow-up with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: A child discharged after an asthma exacerbation should complete a short systemic corticosteroid course (e.g., 3-5 days oral, or the dexamethasone regimen already begun), receive a written asthma action plan with as-needed albuterol, have inhaler/spacer technique reviewed, and have early primary care or asthma follow-up — plus counseling on trigger avoidance (here, secondhand smoke and cat/dust exposure) and controller adherence. No medication change with only yearly follow-up misses the chance to improve control after a severe event. Antibiotics are not indicated without bacterial infection. ICU admission is unnecessary once he has clearly stabilized to room-air saturations with reassuring work of breathing.

pearls: Poor air movement (decreased breath sounds) and inability to speak in sentences signal a severe exacerbation — a quiet chest is ominous.
Cornerstones of acute therapy: oxygen, frequent/continuous albuterol, ipratropium for severe cases, and early systemic steroids.
A 'normalizing' pCO2 in a tachypneic, distressed asthmatic suggests fatigue and impending failure, not improvement.
Add IV magnesium for severe exacerbations refractory to first-line therapy; avoid beta-blockers and sedatives.

cases.title: A 15-Month-Old Boy Who Is Pale and Tired
cases.chiefComplaint: Increasing pallor and fatigue noted by parents over the past few weeks.
cases.history: A 15-month-old boy is brought in for a well-child visit; his parents have noticed he looks pale and tires easily, sometimes napping more than usual. He was a full-term infant with no neonatal problems. He transitioned to whole cow's milk at 12 months and now drinks roughly 36-40 ounces (about 1 liter) per day from a bottle he carries throughout the day, with little interest in solid foods. His mother reports he sometimes chews on the carpet and tries to eat dirt from potted plants. He has no overt blood in the stool, no melena, and no jaundice. He has had no fevers, recurrent infections, or bruising. He was breastfed until 11 months without iron supplementation. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; no medications, no allergies. Immunizations are current. He lives with both parents in a home built in the 1990s. Family history is negative for thalassemia or known hemoglobinopathies. Development is appropriate; he is walking and says a few words.
cases.exam: Alert but pale toddler, with conjunctival and palmar pallor. No scleral icterus. Cardiac exam reveals a soft I-II/VI systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border without other abnormal sounds. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, nontender, no hepatosplenomegaly. No lymphadenopathy. No petechiae, purpura, or bruising. No koilonychia. Mucous membranes moist. Neurologic exam is nonfocal and developmentally appropriate. Skin without rash. Weight and length track along the 40th-50th percentiles.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial laboratory test to evaluate his pallor and fatigue?
options: Bone marrow biopsy as the first step today
Serum lead level only as the first step today
Complete blood count with red cell indices
Hemoglobin electrophoresis as the first test
stages.explanation: A CBC with red cell indices (MCV, RDW) and a peripheral smear is the appropriate first step in evaluating suspected anemia — it characterizes the anemia (micro-, normo-, or macrocytic) and guides further testing efficiently and noninvasively. The history (excessive cow's milk, low iron intake, pica) strongly suggests iron deficiency. Bone marrow biopsy is invasive and reserved for unexplained cytopenias or marrow failure concerns. Hemoglobin electrophoresis is appropriate later if thalassemia/hemoglobinopathy is suspected after the CBC, not as the initial test. A lead level is reasonable given pica and an older home but does not replace a CBC as the first study.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL (low)
MCV 64 fL (low; microcytic)
RDW 18.5% (elevated)
Reticulocyte count 0.8% (low for degree of anemia)
Peripheral smear: microcytic, hypochromic red cells with anisocytosis; occasional target cells
Serum ferritin 6 ng/mL (low)
Serum iron low, TIBC elevated, transferrin saturation 8% (low)
Lead level 4 µg/dL (not elevated)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory pattern best confirms iron deficiency anemia rather than thalassemia trait?
options: High MCV with elevated ferritin as the primary diagnosis
Normal MCV with elevated reticulocyte count and high ferritin
Low MCV with a normal or low RDW and normal ferritin in this clinical context
Low MCV with elevated RDW, low ferritin, low transferrin saturation,
stages.explanation: Iron deficiency produces a microcytic (low MCV), hypochromic anemia with an elevated RDW (heterogeneous cell size) and depleted iron stores: low ferritin, low serum iron, low transferrin saturation, and elevated TIBC. This matches the labs and the dietary/pica history. Thalassemia trait also lowers MCV but classically shows a normal/low RDW, normal-to-high ferritin, and a disproportionately high red cell count for the degree of microcytosis, with normal iron studies. High MCV indicates macrocytic (e.g., B12/folate) anemia. A normal MCV with high reticulocytes suggests hemolysis or acute blood loss, not this picture. The low ferritin (6 ng/mL) is the most specific finding for iron deficiency.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of this child's iron deficiency anemia?
options: Immediate packed red blood cell transfusion as initial therapy
Observation with repeat CBC in 6 months and no intervention as initial therapy
Intramuscular iron dextran as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Oral ferrous sulfate plus limiting cow's milk to less than 24 oz/day
stages.explanation: The treatment is oral iron supplementation (e.g., ferrous sulfate ~3-6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron) combined with dietary modification: limiting cow's milk to under 24 oz/day (excess milk displaces iron-rich foods, irritates the gut, and reduces iron absorption) and adding iron-rich foods; vitamin C aids absorption. He is hemodynamically stable, so transfusion is not warranted — transfusion is reserved for severe symptomatic anemia or hemodynamic compromise. Parenteral iron is reserved for malabsorption or intolerance of oral iron, not first-line. Observation alone is inappropriate for symptomatic, confirmed iron deficiency, which can impair neurodevelopment.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After starting oral iron, what is the earliest expected laboratory sign of a response, and when should it be checked?
options: A reticulocyte rise within about 1 week, confirming marrow response
Normalization of MCV within 48 hours as the immediate next step
Hemoglobin fully normalizes within 5 days before discharge planning
Ferritin returns to normal within 3 days as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The earliest measurable response to iron therapy is a reticulocytosis, typically peaking around 5-7 days after starting effective treatment, signaling that the marrow is producing red cells. Hemoglobin then rises over weeks (often ~1 g/dL every 2-4 weeks), and therapy is continued for 2-3 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replenish stores (reflected by ferritin recovery, which lags). MCV does not normalize in 48 hours; it improves over weeks. Ferritin does not normalize in 3 days — stores replete slowly. Hemoglobin does not fully normalize in 5 days. A failure to respond should prompt reassessment of adherence, dosing, ongoing blood loss, or an alternate diagnosis.

cases.diagnosis: Iron deficiency anemia from excessive cow's milk intake
pearls: Excessive cow's milk (over ~24 oz/day) in toddlers displaces iron-rich foods and is a classic cause of iron deficiency anemia.
Iron deficiency: low MCV, high RDW, low ferritin, low transferrin saturation, high TIBC — ferritin is the most specific marker.
Pica (e.g., eating dirt/carpet) is a clue to iron deficiency and warrants a lead level given overlapping risk.
Reticulocytosis within ~1 week confirms response to oral iron; continue iron 2-3 months after Hgb normalizes to refill stores.

cases.title: A 10-Month-Old Girl With Fever and No Obvious Source
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of fever up to 39.4 °C without localizing symptoms.
cases.history: A 10-month-old girl is brought to the ED for 2 days of fever, measured at home up to 39.4 °C, with fussiness and decreased appetite. Parents report no cough, congestion, rash, vomiting, or diarrhea, and no ear pulling. She has had fewer wet diapers and a stronger-smelling urine over the past day. She was born full-term and is fully immunized for age. She has had no prior febrile illnesses or urinary infections. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She lives with both parents; no sick contacts and no recent travel or antibiotic use. Family history is unremarkable. On review, she is taking some fluids but less than usual. There has been no known trauma. Parents deny seizures, lethargy, or difficulty breathing. She has been afebrile when dosed with acetaminophen but spikes again when it wears off.
cases.exam: Fussy but consolable infant who makes eye contact and tracks. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. No nuchal rigidity. Tympanic membranes normal bilaterally. Oropharynx clear, moist mucous membranes. Lungs clear, no retractions. Heart tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen soft, nondistended, mildly suprapubic tenderness without guarding; no masses or hepatosplenomegaly. No rash or petechiae. Capillary refill 2 seconds. External genitalia normal without discharge. Neurologic exam nonfocal; she moves all extremities and has normal tone.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this 10-month-old with fever without an obvious source, what is the most appropriate way to obtain urine for testing?
options: Catheterized urine specimen for urinalysis
Clean-catch midstream sample as the first step today
Adhesive perineal bag urine specimen as the first step today
Wait and rely on a follow-up examination without urine testing
stages.explanation: In a nontoilet-trained febrile infant who cannot provide a reliable clean catch, urine for both urinalysis and culture should be obtained by urethral catheterization (or suprapubic aspiration) to avoid contamination. Bag specimens have unacceptably high false-positive rates from skin/perineal flora and are acceptable only to screen; a positive bag sample must be confirmed by a catheter/suprapubic specimen before treating. A reliable clean-catch midstream sample is not feasible at this age. Deferring urine testing is inappropriate in a febrile infant without a source, where UTI is the most common serious bacterial infection.

stages.label: URINALYSIS & LABS
stages.content:
Catheterized urinalysis:
  Leukocyte esterase: positive (large)
  Nitrites: positive
  WBC: 45 /hpf
  Bacteria: many
  Blood: trace
Urine Gram stain: gram-negative rods
Urine culture: pending
CBC: WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL with neutrophil predominance
Blood culture: pending
Basic metabolic panel within normal limits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The catheterized urinalysis suggests a urinary tract infection. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start empiric antibiotics after the culture is sent
Withhold all antibiotics until the urine culture results in 48 hours
Treat with a single dose of oral nitrofurantoin as initial therapy
Begin acyclovir for presumed viral cystitis as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A febrile infant with a positive urinalysis (positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites, pyuria, bacteriuria) has a presumed febrile UTI/pyelonephritis and should receive empiric antibiotics promptly after a catheterized culture is obtained — commonly a third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., cefdinir or ceftriaxone/cefotaxime if parenteral is needed), later tailored to culture sensitivities. Delaying antibiotics 48 hours risks renal scarring and bacteremia. Nitrofurantoin does not achieve adequate renal tissue/serum levels and is inappropriate for febrile UTI/pyelonephritis. Acyclovir treats herpes, not a gram-negative bacterial UTI; there is no indication for antiviral therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She is well-hydrated, tolerating oral fluids, and nontoxic. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Discharge with no antibiotics and instructions to return only if seizures occur
Admit and start vancomycin for MRSA coverage with appropriate follow-up
Mandatory ICU admission for all infant UTIs with appropriate follow-up
Outpatient management with oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: An infant older than ~2-3 months who is nontoxic, hydrated, and tolerating oral intake with reliable follow-up can be managed as an outpatient on oral antibiotics; admission and parenteral therapy are reserved for ill-appearing/toxic infants, those unable to tolerate orals, very young infants, or failure of outpatient therapy. ICU admission is not warranted for an uncomplicated, stable febrile UTI. Vancomycin targets gram-positive/MRSA infection, but the Gram stain shows gram-negative rods (typically E. coli). Discharging without antibiotics is unsafe given a confirmed presumptive febrile UTI.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP IMAGING
stages.question: Per guidelines, what imaging is recommended after a first febrile UTI in a young child like this?
options: Renal and bladder ultrasound, with VCUG reserved for abnormal ultrasound or recurrent febrile UTIs
CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast as the immediate next step
Immediate voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) for every first febrile UTI as the immediate next step
No imaging is ever indicated after a febrile UTI as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Current AAP guidance recommends a renal and bladder ultrasound after a first febrile UTI in young children to screen for anatomic abnormalities or hydronephrosis. A voiding cystourethrogram (to evaluate for vesicoureteral reflux) is NOT routine after a first uncomplicated febrile UTI; it is reserved for an abnormal ultrasound, atypical/complex course, or recurrent febrile UTIs. Routine VCUG for every first UTI causes unnecessary catheterization and radiation. CT is not the screening modality of choice and adds radiation. Doing no imaging at all misses correctable anatomic problems, so ultrasound is the appropriate first study.

cases.diagnosis: Febrile urinary tract infection (pyelonephritis) in an infant
pearls: In febrile nontoilet-trained infants, obtain urine by catheter or suprapubic aspiration — bag specimens have high false-positive rates.
UTI is the most common serious bacterial infection in young infants with fever without a source; E. coli is the usual pathogen.
Start empiric antibiotics promptly after culture; nitrofurantoin is inadequate for febrile UTI/pyelonephritis.
After a first febrile UTI, obtain a renal/bladder ultrasound; reserve VCUG for abnormal ultrasound or recurrent febrile UTIs.

cases.title: An 11-Year-Old Boy With Worsening Belly Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: About 18 hours of abdominal pain that started near the navel and moved to the right lower side.
cases.history: An 11-year-old boy presents to the ED with abdominal pain that began roughly 18 hours ago as a dull, vague ache around the umbilicus. Over the past several hours the pain shifted to the right lower quadrant and became sharper and constant. He has had nausea, two episodes of nonbilious vomiting, and anorexia — he refused his favorite dinner. He had a low-grade fever at home. He reports the car ride and walking made the pain worse. He has had no diarrhea, dysuria, hematuria, or testicular pain, and no recent trauma. There is no prior abdominal surgery. He last ate a few hours before pain onset. PMH is unremarkable; no medications, no known allergies. Immunizations are current. He lives with his parents and attends fifth grade. No recent travel, no sick contacts with similar symptoms. Family history is noncontributory. He has not had a similar episode previously.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable boy lying still, reluctant to move on the bed. Abdomen is flat; maximal tenderness localizes to the right lower quadrant at McBurney's point with voluntary guarding. There is rebound tenderness and a positive Rovsing sign (RLQ pain on left-sided palpation). Psoas and obturator signs are positive. Bowel sounds are diminished. No CVA tenderness. Testes are descended bilaterally, nontender, without swelling, with a normal cremasteric reflex. No inguinal hernia. No skin rash. He winces with coughing, which reproduces RLQ pain.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings best supports a clinical diagnosis of acute appendicitis?
options: Periumbilical pain migrating to the RLQ with anorexia, low-grade fever,
Colicky flank pain radiating to the groin with hematuria as the primary diagnosis
Diffuse crampy pain with watery diarrhea and normal abdominal exam as the primary diagnosis
Epigastric burning pain relieved by eating as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The classic appendicitis sequence is vague periumbilical (visceral) pain that migrates to the right lower quadrant (somatic) as the inflamed appendix irritates the parietal peritoneum, accompanied by anorexia, nausea/vomiting, and low-grade fever. Focal McBurney's point tenderness with peritoneal signs (rebound, Rovsing, psoas, obturator, pain with cough/movement) strongly supports it. Diffuse crampy pain with watery diarrhea suggests gastroenteritis. Colicky flank-to-groin pain with hematuria suggests nephrolithiasis. Epigastric burning relieved by eating suggests peptic ulcer disease. The migration pattern plus localized peritoneal findings here is highly characteristic of appendicitis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging modality to confirm appendicitis in this child?
options: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast as the first-line study in all children
MRI of the brain as the immediate next step
Graded-compression abdominal ultrasound of the right lower quadrant
Plain abdominal radiographs as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In children, graded-compression ultrasound is the preferred first-line imaging for suspected appendicitis because it avoids ionizing radiation and can be highly specific; findings include a noncompressible, dilated appendix greater than 6 mm, wall thickening, periappendiceal fluid, or an appendicolith. If ultrasound is nondiagnostic and suspicion remains, MRI or CT can follow. CT is excellent but is reserved when ultrasound is inconclusive, to limit radiation in children. Plain films are insensitive and rarely helpful. Brain MRI is irrelevant to abdominal pain. Laboratory studies (CBC, CRP, urinalysis, pregnancy test in adolescent girls) complement imaging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 15.6 ×10³/µL with left shift (neutrophils 82%)
CRP 6.2 mg/dL (elevated)
Urinalysis: no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase, few WBCs
Electrolytes within normal limits
RLQ ultrasound: noncompressible, dilated blind-ending tubular structure measuring 9 mm in diameter with wall hyperemia and a small amount of periappendiceal fluid; no appendicolith; no abscess

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms uncomplicated acute appendicitis. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Begin a bowel prep and schedule an elective colonoscopy as initial therapy
Discharge home with oral analgesics and outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Oral antibiotics alone at home without surgical evaluation as initial therapy
Surgical consultation for appendectomy with IV fluids, analgesia, NPO status,
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated acute appendicitis is managed with prompt surgical consultation for appendectomy (typically laparoscopic), with supportive care while awaiting the OR: NPO status, IV isotonic fluids, analgesia, and preoperative IV antibiotics. Discharging a child with confirmed appendicitis risks perforation and peritonitis. Nonoperative antibiotic-only management is investigational/selective and is not the standard outpatient approach for a child with confirmed appendicitis; it would never be done at home without surgical evaluation. Colonoscopy has no role in acute appendicitis and bowel prep would dangerously delay definitive care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If diagnosis and treatment had been delayed by another day, which complication would be of greatest concern?
options: Acute glomerulonephritis as the leading concern
Appendiceal perforation with peritonitis or abscess formation
Gallstone pancreatitis as the leading concern
Spontaneous resolution with no risk as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Untreated appendicitis progresses to perforation, typically within 48-72 hours of symptom onset, releasing intestinal contents and causing generalized peritonitis or a walled-off periappendiceal abscess. Perforation increases morbidity, length of stay, and the need for percutaneous drainage or interval appendectomy. Signs include diffuse abdominal rigidity, high fever, and worsening systemic illness. Gallstone pancreatitis and acute glomerulonephritis are unrelated to appendicitis. Appendicitis does not reliably resolve spontaneously, and assuming no risk would be dangerous — hence the emphasis on timely surgical management before perforation occurs.

pearls: Classic appendicitis: periumbilical pain migrating to the RLQ with anorexia, low-grade fever, and McBurney's point peritoneal signs.
Ultrasound is first-line imaging in children to avoid radiation; an appendix greater than 6 mm and noncompressible is suggestive.
Management is surgical: appendectomy with IV fluids, analgesia, NPO, and preoperative antibiotics.
Untreated appendicitis perforates within ~48-72 hours, causing peritonitis or abscess — timely diagnosis prevents this.

cases.title: A 9-Year-Old Girl With Excessive Thirst and Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of excessive thirst and urination, now with vomiting and lethargy.
cases.history: A 9-year-old girl is brought to the ED for several weeks of increasing thirst and frequent urination, including new bedwetting after being dry for years. Her parents note she has lost about 4 kg despite eating well. Over the past day she developed nausea, vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain, and progressive fatigue, and today she has been breathing fast and is hard to keep awake. There is no diarrhea, dysuria, cough, or known ingestion. She had a viral illness a few weeks ago. PMH is unremarkable; she takes no medications and has no allergies. Immunizations are current. She lives with both parents and a sibling. Family history is notable for an aunt with type 1 diabetes and a grandmother with hypothyroidism. No recent travel. She has had no prior hospitalizations. Parents report her breath smells unusual, like fruit or nail-polish remover.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, drowsy but arousable girl. Deep, rapid (Kussmaul) respirations with a fruity (acetone) odor on the breath. Mucous membranes dry; eyes mildly sunken; capillary refill 3 seconds; skin tenting present — signs of moderate dehydration. Abdomen diffusely tender without guarding or rebound; no organomegaly. Heart tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs clear with increased respiratory effort. Neurologic exam: sleepy but follows commands, no focal deficit, no neck stiffness. No rash. She is oriented to name and place but slow to respond.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, Kussmaul breathing, and a fruity odor, what bedside/initial tests should you obtain first?
options: Point-of-care glucose, blood gas, serum electrolytes,
Thyroid-stimulating hormone and morning cortisol only
Head CT and lumbar puncture before anything else in this clinical context
Abdominal ultrasound for the abdominal pain as the first step today
stages.explanation: This presentation is classic for new-onset type 1 diabetes with diabetic ketoacidosis. The fastest, highest-yield initial tests are a point-of-care glucose, a blood gas (for pH and bicarbonate), serum electrolytes (including the anion gap, sodium, and crucially potassium), and ketone testing (urine or serum beta-hydroxybutyrate). These confirm hyperglycemia, anion-gap metabolic acidosis, and ketosis — the diagnostic triad of DKA. Head CT/LP are not first-line without focal neurologic signs or meningismus. TSH/cortisol do not explain this acute picture. Abdominal ultrasound would miss the metabolic emergency; abdominal pain in DKA is from the acidosis itself.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Point-of-care glucose: 512 mg/dL
Venous blood gas: pH 7.18, pCO2 24 mmHg, HCO3 9 mEq/L
Serum bicarbonate 9 mEq/L
Anion gap 24 mEq/L (elevated)
Beta-hydroxybutyrate: markedly elevated; urine ketones large
Sodium 132 mEq/L (corrected ~136)
Potassium 5.1 mEq/L (serum)
BUN 24 mg/dL, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
HbA1c 11.6%
Urinalysis: glucose 4+, ketones large, no nitrites/leukocyte esterase

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How do you classify this child's metabolic state?
options: Lactic acidosis from sepsis as the primary diagnosis
Simple dehydration with a normal acid-base status
Diabetic ketoacidosis
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state without acidosis
stages.explanation: The labs define diabetic ketoacidosis: hyperglycemia (glucose 512 mg/dL), metabolic acidosis (pH 7.18, bicarbonate 9 mEq/L) with an elevated anion gap (24), and ketosis (large ketones, high beta-hydroxybutyrate). Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state features very high glucose and osmolality but minimal ketosis and little or no acidosis — not the case here given marked ketosis and acidosis. Lactic acidosis from sepsis would not explain the hyperglycemia, ketonuria, and elevated HbA1c (11.6%, indicating chronic hyperglycemia). This is not simple dehydration — there is a clear high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. The HbA1c confirms new-onset diabetes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the correct order of initial DKA management in this child?
options: Give an IV insulin bolus immediately, then fluids as initial therapy
Start isotonic IV fluid resuscitation first, ensure potassium is adequate, then begin a continuous insulin infusion
Begin oral hydration and subcutaneous sliding-scale insulin at home as initial therapy
Administer IV sodium bicarbonate to correct the pH rapidly as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Pediatric DKA management begins with isotonic fluid resuscitation (e.g., normal saline) to restore intravascular volume, followed by a continuous low-dose insulin infusion (typically 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr) started after fluids and once potassium is known to be adequate — insulin drives potassium intracellularly, so giving insulin before correcting/anticipating hypokalemia can precipitate dangerous hypokalemia. Insulin boluses are avoided in children (increase cerebral edema risk and cause rapid shifts). Routine bicarbonate is not recommended — it can worsen outcomes and is reserved for extreme acidosis with hemodynamic compromise. Oral/home therapy is unsafe in active DKA, which requires close inpatient monitoring.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During treatment, the child develops a worsening headache, becomes confused, and her heart rate falls with rising blood pressure. What complication must you suspect?
options: Pulmonary embolism
Cerebral edema
Hypoglycemia in this presentation
Acute appendicitis
stages.explanation: Cerebral edema is the most feared complication of pediatric DKA and a leading cause of death; it typically appears within the first several hours of treatment. Warning signs include worsening headache, altered mental status/confusion, and the Cushing response — bradycardia with hypertension — plus other neurologic changes. Treatment is urgent: hypertonic saline or mannitol, head-of-bed elevation, and reduction of fluid rate. Hypoglycemia causes tremor, sweating, and altered mentation but not the Cushing response, and her glucose was high. Pulmonary embolism is rare in this context. Appendicitis would not produce these neurologic and vital-sign changes; the abdominal pain of DKA resolves as acidosis corrects.

cases.diagnosis: New-onset type 1 diabetes mellitus presenting as diabetic ketoacidosis
pearls: DKA triad: hyperglycemia, anion-gap metabolic acidosis, and ketosis — suspect it with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and Kussmaul breathing.
Treat DKA with fluids first, then a continuous insulin infusion; avoid insulin boluses in children.
Insulin shifts potassium intracellularly — monitor and replace potassium even when serum levels look normal or high.
Cerebral edema is the dreaded complication: watch for headache, altered mental status, and bradycardia with hypertension (Cushing response).

cases.title: A 6-Year-Old Boy With a Rash on His Legs and Joint Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: A spreading rash on the buttocks and legs with painful, swollen ankles for several days.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy presents with a rash that started 4 days ago on his buttocks and the backs of both legs and has not faded with pressure. Over the same period he has had pain and swelling in both ankles and his knees, making him reluctant to walk, and intermittent crampy abdominal pain with one episode of nonbloody emesis. He had an upper respiratory infection about 2 weeks ago. His urine has looked normal to his parents, and there has been no gross blood; he has had no swelling of the face or eyes. He is afebrile at home. PMH is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and has no allergies. Immunizations are current. He lives with his parents and attends kindergarten. No recent travel. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no prior similar rashes or bleeding problems, and there is no history of easy bruising.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile boy who walks with a stiff, guarded gait. Skin shows symmetric palpable purpura and a few petechiae concentrated on the buttocks and extensor surfaces of both lower extremities; lesions do not blanch and are raised. Both ankles are mildly swollen, warm, and tender with limited range of motion; knees mildly tender. Abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness, no rebound or guarding, normal bowel sounds, and no hepatosplenomegaly; stool is brown and Hemoccult negative on exam. No facial or periorbital edema. Scrotum nontender without swelling. No mucosal bleeding. Neurologic exam normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical tetrad most strongly suggests IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura)?
options: Fever, conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and cervical lymphadenopathy
Palpable purpura , arthritis/arthralgia, abdominal pain,
Target lesions, mucosal erosions, and recent drug exposure as the primary diagnosis
Malar rash, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, and arthritis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis), the most common childhood small-vessel vasculitis, classically presents with the tetrad of palpable purpura with normal platelets and coagulation (predominantly on the buttocks and lower extremities), arthritis/arthralgia (often ankles and knees), colicky abdominal pain, and renal involvement (hematuria/proteinuria). It frequently follows an upper respiratory infection. Fever, conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and cervical adenopathy describe Kawasaki disease. Malar rash, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, and arthritis suggest systemic lupus erythematosus. Target lesions with mucosal erosions and drug exposure describe erythema multiforme/Stevens-Johnson syndrome. The symmetric, dependent palpable purpura plus arthritis and abdominal pain here fit HSP.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which initial evaluation is most important to assess for the major organ complication of HSP?
options: Echocardiogram to evaluate the coronary arteries in this setting
Urinalysis and blood pressure measurement to screen for nephritis
Brain MRI to evaluate for vasculitic stroke in this setting
Skin biopsy in every patient to confirm the diagnosis
stages.explanation: Renal involvement (IgA nephritis) is the most important determinant of long-term prognosis in HSP, so a urinalysis (looking for hematuria and proteinuria) plus blood pressure measurement are essential at presentation and during follow-up to detect and monitor nephritis. HSP is typically a clinical diagnosis; skin biopsy (showing leukocytoclastic vasculitis with IgA deposition) is reserved for atypical cases, not required in every patient. Coronary echocardiography is for Kawasaki disease, not HSP. Brain MRI is unnecessary without neurologic findings. CBC and coagulation studies are often checked to confirm normal platelets/clotting and exclude other causes of purpura.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Platelet count 320 ×10³/µL (normal)
PT/PTT within normal limits
Urinalysis: 2+ blood, RBCs 20-30 /hpf, trace protein, no casts
BUN 12 mg/dL, creatinine 0.4 mg/dL (normal for age)
Serum IgA mildly elevated
C3/C4 normal
Stool guaiac negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His diagnosis is HSP with microscopic hematuria, joint pain, and abdominal discomfort but normal renal function. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Platelet transfusion to stop the purpura as initial therapy
High-dose IV cyclophosphamide immediately as initial therapy
Supportive care with close monitoring of urinalysis
Empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Most HSP is self-limited and managed supportively: adequate hydration, rest, and analgesia (acetaminophen or NSAIDs, used cautiously if there is significant renal involvement or GI bleeding) for joint and abdominal pain, with serial monitoring of urinalysis and blood pressure to detect evolving nephritis over the following months. Corticosteroids are reserved for severe abdominal pain, significant GI involvement, or severe nephritis. Cyclophosphamide/immunosuppression is for severe, biopsy-proven progressive nephritis — not this mild presentation. Antibiotics are not indicated; HSP is not a bacterial infection. Platelet transfusion is pointless and wrong — the platelet count is normal (320), and purpura here is vasculitic, not thrombocytopenic.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which acute abdominal complication is HSP classically associated with and must be considered if abdominal pain acutely worsens?
options: Acute cholecystitis
Hirschsprung enterocolitis
Ileoileal intussusception
Sigmoid volvulus
stages.explanation: HSP can cause bowel-wall edema and hemorrhage that act as a lead point for intussusception — characteristically ileoileal (small bowel-to-small bowel), in contrast to the more common idiopathic ileocolic intussusception of infancy. Worsening or colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, or bloody stools should prompt evaluation (ultrasound). Because HSP intussusception is often ileoileal, it may be missed by air-contrast enema and may require ultrasound or surgical reduction. Acute cholecystitis, sigmoid volvulus, and Hirschsprung enterocolitis are not typical HSP complications. Other GI complications of HSP include GI bleeding and, rarely, bowel ischemia or perforation.

pearls: HSP tetrad: palpable purpura on buttocks/lower extremities, arthritis/arthralgia, colicky abdominal pain, and renal involvement — often after a URI.
Platelets and coagulation are normal in HSP; purpura is vasculitic, distinguishing it from thrombocytopenic causes.
Renal involvement drives prognosis — monitor urinalysis and blood pressure at presentation and during follow-up.
Suspect ileoileal intussusception if abdominal pain acutely worsens; most HSP otherwise resolves with supportive care.

cases.title: A 3-Year-Old Boy With Five Days of High Fever and Red Eyes
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent high fever for five days with red eyes, cracked lips, and a rash.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy admitted to the pediatric ward has had 5 days of high fevers up to 40 °C, poorly responsive to antipyretics, plus marked irritability. His parents noticed red eyes without discharge, very red and cracked lips, and a rash over his trunk and groin. His hands and feet have appeared swollen and red. He has had decreased oral intake. There is no cough, runny nose, or significant diarrhea, and no known sick contacts with similar illness. He was seen 2 days ago and given amoxicillin for presumed pharyngitis without improvement; a rapid strep test was negative. PMH is unremarkable; immunizations are current. He takes no chronic medications and has no known allergies. He lives with his parents and an older sibling. No recent travel. Family history is noncontributory. He has been increasingly fussy and difficult to console, which is unusual for him.
cases.exam: Irritable, ill-appearing but nontoxic boy. Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection without exudate, with perilimbal sparing. Lips are erythematous, dry, and cracked; tongue is erythematous with prominent papillae (strawberry tongue); oropharynx diffusely red without exudate or ulcers. There is a single enlarged (greater than 1.5 cm) tender right anterior cervical lymph node. A polymorphous erythematous (morbilliform) rash is present on the trunk and is accentuated in the groin. Hands and feet show firm, indurated edema with palmar/plantar erythema. No vesicles. Heart tachycardic without murmur; lungs clear; abdomen soft. No nuchal rigidity.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of findings meets the clinical criteria for Kawasaki disease in this child?
options: Fever for at least 5 days plus at least 4 of: bilateral nonexudative conjunctivitis, oral mucosal changes, polymorphous rash, extremity changes,
Fever plus vesicular rash and a single dermatomal distribution as the primary diagnosis
Fever 5 days plus 2 of: rash, conjunctivitis, oral changes, extremity changes, cervical node as the primary diagnosis
Fever plus a positive monospot test as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Classic (complete) Kawasaki disease requires fever lasting at least 5 days plus at least 4 of the 5 principal clinical criteria: bilateral nonexudative (often limbic-sparing) conjunctival injection; oral mucosal changes (cracked red lips, strawberry tongue, pharyngeal erythema); polymorphous rash (often accentuated in the groin); extremity changes (erythema/edema of hands and feet, later periungual desquamation); and cervical lymphadenopathy (typically unilateral, greater than 1.5 cm). This boy meets all five. Two of five with fever would be incomplete/atypical KD, requiring supportive labs and echocardiography. A positive monospot suggests EBV mononucleosis. A vesicular dermatomal rash suggests herpes zoster, not KD.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which study is essential at diagnosis to evaluate for the principal complication of Kawasaki disease?
options: Lumbar puncture to evaluate for meningitis
Echocardiography to assess the coronary arteries
Electroencephalogram for irritability
Abdominal CT to evaluate the lymph nodes
stages.explanation: The major morbidity of Kawasaki disease is coronary artery aneurysm/ectasia, so a baseline echocardiogram is essential at diagnosis to assess coronary artery dimensions and ventricular function; it is repeated during follow-up (e.g., at ~1-2 weeks and 4-6 weeks). Untreated, up to ~25% of children develop coronary aneurysms. Lumbar puncture is not routine — KD can cause aseptic meningitis/irritability, but LP is reserved for specific concerns. Abdominal CT for lymph nodes is unnecessary. EEG is not indicated for the irritability of KD. Supportive labs (elevated CRP/ESR, thrombocytosis after the first week, sterile pyuria, transaminitis, anemia) support but do not replace echocardiography.

stages.label: LABS & ECHO
stages.content:
WBC 16.5 ×10³/µL with neutrophilia
Platelets 480 ×10³/µL (rising)
ESR 68 mm/hr; CRP 9.4 mg/dL (both elevated)
ALT 78 U/L (mildly elevated)
Albumin 3.0 g/dL (low)
Urinalysis: sterile pyuria (WBCs present, no bacteria)
Throat culture: negative; blood culture: no growth at 48 h
Echocardiogram: normal coronary arteries, no aneurysm; normal ventricular function

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He meets criteria for Kawasaki disease on day 5 of fever. What is the most appropriate treatment to prevent coronary complications?
options: A 10-day course of oral amoxicillin as initial therapy
Corticosteroids alone as monotherapy for all cases
Supportive care with antipyretics only and observation as initial therapy
Intravenous immunoglobulin plus aspirin, given promptly within 10 days of fever onset
stages.explanation: Treatment of Kawasaki disease is high-dose IVIG (2 g/kg) plus aspirin, ideally started within the first 10 days of fever onset, which dramatically reduces the risk of coronary artery aneurysms (from ~25% to roughly 3-5%). Aspirin (anti-inflammatory dosing, then low-dose antiplatelet once afebrile) is part of standard therapy. Antibiotics like amoxicillin do not treat KD — this is not a bacterial infection (cultures negative, no strep). Corticosteroids are added for IVIG-refractory cases or high-risk patients but are not standard monotherapy. Supportive care alone leaves the child at high risk for coronary aneurysms; timely IVIG is the priority — hence treating now on day 5 rather than waiting.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After IVIG, the child defervesces. What is an important consideration for his routine immunizations?
options: He can never receive any vaccines again as the immediate next step
Live-virus vaccines should be deferred for about 11 months after high-dose IVIG
All vaccines must be given immediately while admitted as the immediate next step
Only inactivated vaccines are contraindicated after IVIG before discharge planning
stages.explanation: High-dose IVIG contains passive antibodies that can blunt the immune response to live-virus vaccines, so MMR and varicella should generally be deferred for approximately 11 months after IVIG administration. Inactivated vaccines are not affected and may be given on schedule. Giving live vaccines immediately would risk an inadequate response. He can absolutely be vaccinated in the future — the deferral is temporary, not permanent. The statement that only inactivated vaccines are contraindicated is backwards; it is the live vaccines that need deferral. Children on long-term aspirin should also receive annual influenza vaccination given the (theoretical) Reye syndrome concern.

pearls: Complete KD: fever at least 5 days plus 4 of 5 — conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, extremity changes, cervical adenopathy.
Obtain a baseline echocardiogram; coronary artery aneurysm is the principal complication.
Treat with IVIG plus aspirin within 10 days of fever onset to cut aneurysm risk from ~25% to ~3-5%.
Defer live-virus vaccines (MMR, varicella) for about 11 months after high-dose IVIG.

cases.title: A 5-Week-Old Boy With Forceful Vomiting After Feeds
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening forceful vomiting after every feed for about a week.
cases.history: A 5-week-old boy is brought to the ED for progressively forceful vomiting after feeds over the past week. The emesis is described as 'shooting across the room,' is nonbilious (milky, sometimes curdled), and occurs shortly after feeding. He acts hungry immediately afterward and eagerly refeeds. His parents report fewer wet diapers and that he seems less active. Stools are smaller and less frequent. He was a full-term, first-born boy with an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery, formula-fed. There has been no fever, diarrhea, or bilious (green) vomiting, and no blood in the emesis. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; no medications other than vitamin D drops; no allergies. He lives with both parents. Family history is notable for the father having had 'stomach surgery as a baby.' He had been gaining weight well until the past week, when his weight has plateaued and slightly declined per home scale.
cases.exam: Fussy, hungry-appearing infant with signs of dehydration: dry mucous membranes, a mildly sunken anterior fontanelle, decreased skin turgor, and delayed capillary refill (~3 seconds). Eyes slightly sunken. Abdomen is soft and nondistended; after a test feed, visible peristaltic waves are seen moving across the upper abdomen left-to-right, and a firm, mobile, olive-shaped mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant/epigastrium. No tenderness, guarding, or hepatosplenomegaly. No inguinal hernia; testes descended. No jaundice. Heart tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs clear. Neurologic exam: alert, fussy, moves all extremities.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings is most characteristic of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?
options: Bilious vomiting with a distended abdomen and absent bowel sounds as the primary diagnosis
Nonbilious projectile vomiting in a hungry infant, visible gastric peristalsis,
Vomiting with currant-jelly stools and intermittent inconsolable crying in this clinical context
Effortless regurgitation that improves with upright positioning and thickened feeds
stages.explanation: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis classically presents in a 3- to 6-week-old (more often a first-born boy) with progressive nonbilious projectile vomiting, a persistently hungry infant who refeeds eagerly, visible left-to-right gastric peristaltic waves, and a palpable firm 'olive' mass in the epigastrium/right upper quadrant. Bilious vomiting with distension and absent bowel sounds points to a distal/obstructive process like malrotation with volvulus — bilious emesis is a red flag against simple pyloric stenosis. Currant-jelly stools with paroxysmal crying suggest intussusception. Effortless regurgitation improving with positioning/thickened feeds describes gastroesophageal reflux, which is not projectile and does not cause dehydration or an olive.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Sodium 136 mEq/L
Potassium 3.0 mEq/L (low)
Chloride 92 mEq/L (low)
Bicarbonate (HCO3) 34 mEq/L (elevated)
Venous blood gas: pH 7.50 (alkalemic)
BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 0.4 mg/dL
Glucose 78 mg/dL
Urine: paradoxically acidic

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How would you characterize this infant's acid-base and electrolyte abnormality?
options: Hypernatremic dehydration with normal pH
Respiratory acidosis with compensation
Anion-gap metabolic acidosis
Hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis
stages.explanation: Recurrent vomiting of gastric (HCl-rich) contents produces the classic hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis of pyloric stenosis: loss of chloride and hydrogen ions raises bicarbonate (alkalosis), while volume depletion and renal compensation drive potassium loss. The labs fit — low chloride (92), low potassium (3.0), high bicarbonate (34), and an alkalemic pH (7.50). The paradoxically aciduria occurs because the volume-depleted kidney preferentially reabsorbs sodium (and excretes H+) to preserve volume despite alkalosis. This is not an anion-gap acidosis, not a respiratory disorder, and not hypernatremic dehydration with a normal pH. Recognizing this guides resuscitation before surgery.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: If the diagnosis remains uncertain after exam, what is the confirmatory imaging study of choice?
options: Upper GI contrast series as the first-line test in all infants
Abdominal ultrasound measuring pyloric muscle thickness
Non-contrast CT of the abdomen as the immediate next step
Plain abdominal radiograph alone as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the confirmatory study of choice for pyloric stenosis — it is noninvasive, avoids radiation, and directly measures the hypertrophied pylorus; criteria include pyloric muscle wall thickness greater than ~3-4 mm and channel length greater than ~14-16 mm, with failure of the channel to open. An upper GI series can show a 'string sign' or 'shoulder sign' and is useful when ultrasound is equivocal or to evaluate for malrotation, but it is not the first-line test here and uses radiation/contrast. CT adds unnecessary radiation. A plain radiograph alone is nonspecific (may show a distended stomach) and cannot confirm the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Ultrasound confirms pyloric stenosis. What must be done before the infant goes to the operating room?
options: Administer IV antibiotics for 48 hours prior to surgery as initial therapy
Correct dehydration and electrolyte/acid-base abnormalities with IV fluids before surgery
Begin oral feeds to maintain nutrition before the operation as initial therapy
Proceed to emergent surgery immediately, as pyloric stenosis is a surgical emergency
stages.explanation: Pyloric stenosis is a medical urgency but a surgical semi-elective procedure — it is NOT an emergency. The infant must first be rehydrated and have the hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis corrected with IV isotonic fluids (with potassium added once urine output is established) before anesthesia and pyloromyotomy (the definitive treatment). Operating on an alkalotic, hypokalemic, dehydrated infant increases anesthetic risk, including postoperative apnea. Rushing to surgery without correction is dangerous. Oral feeds would worsen vomiting and aspiration risk and are withheld preoperatively. Antibiotics are not indicated — pyloric stenosis is not an infection. The definitive cure is a Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after metabolic correction.

pearls: Pyloric stenosis: nonbilious projectile vomiting in a hungry 3-6 week old, visible peristalsis, and a palpable 'olive.'
Vomiting gastric acid produces a hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria.
Ultrasound confirms the diagnosis (muscle thickness greater than ~3-4 mm, channel length greater than ~14-16 mm).
Correct dehydration and alkalosis with IV fluids BEFORE pyloromyotomy — it is not a surgical emergency.

cases.title: Episodes of Drawing Up the Legs in an Infant
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden inconsolable crying with the infant drawing his knees to his chest.
cases.history: A previously healthy 9-month-old boy is brought to the ED for episodes of sudden, severe crying that began about 8 hours ago. During each episode he pulls his knees toward his abdomen, appears pale, and is inconsolable for several minutes, then becomes limp and quiet between attacks. Episodes now recur roughly every 20-30 minutes. He vomited twice (non-bilious initially) and refused his last two feeds. The parents report one stool earlier that looked like 'mucus mixed with blood.' He had a mild upper respiratory illness with low-grade fevers the prior week. Birth history was uncomplicated; he is up to date on vaccines including rotavirus. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. He lives with both parents and an older sibling; no recent travel or sick contacts beyond the cold. Family history is unremarkable. Between paroxysms today he has been increasingly drowsy, which is worrying the parents, prompting them to come in.
cases.exam: The infant is lethargic between episodes but cries vigorously during a paroxysm, drawing up his legs. Mucous membranes are slightly tacky; capillary refill is 2 seconds. The abdomen is soft with a vaguely sausage-shaped fullness palpable in the right upper quadrant; the right lower quadrant feels relatively empty. There is no rigidity, guarding, or distension at rest. A digital rectal exam reveals a small amount of dark, mucoid, blood-tinged stool. Bowel sounds are present. There are no hernias, no testicular swelling, and no skin lesions or palpable purpura. The remainder of the exam, including lungs and heart, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly points toward intussusception over other causes of infant abdominal pain?
options: A single episode of blood-tinged mucoid stool as the first step today
Paroxysmal colicky pain with lethargy between episodes
Low-grade fever following a recent viral illness as the first step today
Non-bilious vomiting after refusing feeds as the first step today
stages.explanation: The classic triad of intussusception is intermittent (paroxysmal) colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, and bloody/mucoid stool, but the full triad appears in under half of cases. The most specific combination here is paroxysmal pain with striking lethargy or altered consciousness between attacks plus a palpable sausage-shaped mass in the RUQ with an empty RLQ (Dance sign). Low-grade fever after a viral illness is nonspecific and common in many infants. Non-bilious vomiting and refusing feeds occur in countless infant illnesses and do not localize the diagnosis. A single bloody-mucoid stool raises suspicion but, alone, overlaps with gastroenteritis or a Meckel diverticulum; it is the constellation with paroxysmal pain and the mass that is most discriminating for intussusception.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic imaging study in a hemodynamically stable child with suspected ileocolic intussusception?
options: Non-contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis
Plain supine and upright abdominal radiographs only
Abdominal ultrasound
Upper GI contrast series in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the imaging modality of choice for suspected intussusception: it is highly sensitive and specific, involves no radiation, and shows the classic 'target' or 'doughnut' sign on transverse view and a 'pseudokidney' sign longitudinally. CT exposes the child to radiation and is not first-line for this indication. An upper GI series evaluates the proximal tract and is not used to diagnose ileocolic intussusception. Plain films may show a paucity of gas in the RLQ or a soft-tissue mass and are useful to screen for free air before reduction, but they are insensitive and cannot confirm or exclude the diagnosis—ultrasound is the definitive initial test.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 12.8 ×10³/µL
Hgb 11.4 g/dL
Platelets 322 ×10³/µL
Na 138 mEq/L
K 4.1 mEq/L
HCO3 20 mEq/L
Venous lactate 1.8 mmol/L
Abdominal ultrasound: 3.2 cm target sign (concentric rings) in the right upper quadrant with a 'pseudokidney' appearance; no free intraperitoneal fluid; no lead point mass identified
Abdominal radiograph: soft-tissue density in the RUQ, paucity of gas in the right lower quadrant, no free air or pneumatosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The child remains stable with a soft, non-peritonitic abdomen and no free air. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Immediate exploratory laparotomy with manual reduction as initial therapy
Broad-spectrum antibiotics and discharge with outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Trial of nasogastric decompression and observation for 24 hours as initial therapy
Air or contrast enema for nonoperative reduction after surgical consultation
stages.explanation: In a stable child without peritonitis, perforation (free air), or shock, the treatment of choice is nonoperative reduction by air (pneumatic) or hydrostatic contrast enema, which is both diagnostic and therapeutic with success rates of 80-90%. IV access and fluids should be in place and pediatric surgery notified in case reduction fails or perforation occurs. Laparotomy is reserved for failed enema reduction, perforation, peritonitis, or a pathologic lead point. NG decompression alone does not treat the obstruction and risks bowel compromise. Antibiotics and discharge are inappropriate—untreated intussusception progresses to ischemia, necrosis, and perforation. Time-sensitive reduction is the priority.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Air enema successfully reduces the intussusception. What is the most appropriate disposition and counseling point for the family?
options: Admit for observation, monitor for recurrence,
Schedule an elective bowel resection within one week to prevent recurrence with appropriate follow-up
Discharge home immediately once the child tolerates clear liquids in the radiology suite
Begin a prolonged course of oral antibiotics to prevent recurrence with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After successful pneumatic reduction, children are typically admitted for a period of observation because recurrence happens in about 10% of cases, most often within the first 24-72 hours. Monitoring allows early detection of recurrence (return of paroxysmal pain) or a missed perforation. Immediate discharge from radiology is unsafe given the recurrence risk and need to confirm tolerance of feeds and stable vitals. Elective resection is not indicated after a successful reduction unless a pathologic lead point is identified. Antibiotics do not prevent recurrence and are not routinely indicated after uncomplicated reduction. Parents should be told to return for repeat episodes of crying, vomiting, or bloody stools.

pearls: The classic triad—colicky pain, vomiting, and currant-jelly (bloody-mucoid) stool—is present in fewer than half of cases; lethargy between paroxysms is an important and easily missed clue.
Ultrasound is first-line: look for the 'target/doughnut' sign on transverse imaging and the 'pseudokidney' sign longitudinally.
Air or contrast enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic in stable children without peritonitis or perforation, with 80-90% success.
Recurrence after nonoperative reduction occurs in ~10% of cases, usually within 24-72 hours, so observation is warranted.

cases.title: Fever and a Lingering Cough in a Preschooler
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of fever and worsening cough with fast breathing noted by the mother.
cases.history: A 4-year-old girl presents to clinic with 3 days of fever to 39.4 °C, a productive-sounding cough, and decreased oral intake. Her mother noticed she has been breathing faster and tugging at her right side when she coughs. Symptoms began fairly abruptly after a few days of mild rhinorrhea. There has been no wheezing, stridor, or choking episode. She attends daycare, where several children have had colds. She is fully immunized, including PCV (pneumococcal conjugate) and Hib vaccines, and had no prior hospitalizations. She takes no daily medications; her mother gave acetaminophen with transient improvement in fever. She has no drug allergies. There is no history of recurrent pneumonias, foreign-body aspiration, or underlying lung disease. She lives with both parents; no smokers in the home. Family history is notable only for seasonal allergies. Review of systems is negative for ear pain, vomiting, rash, and urinary symptoms. Today she is alert but tired-appearing and drinking less than usual but still making wet diapers and tears.
cases.exam: She is alert but flushed and breathing fast with mild subcostal retractions and occasional nasal flaring. On auscultation there are focal coarse crackles and bronchial breath sounds at the right lung base with decreased air entry there; the left lung is clear. There is dullness to percussion over the right base. There is no stridor, no wheeze, and no audible grunting. Oropharynx is mildly erythematous without exudate; tympanic membranes are normal. Capillary refill is brisk and she is well perfused. There is no rash, no neck stiffness, and no hepatosplenomegaly. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical finding most strongly supports a diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia in this child?
options: Decreased oral intake with preserved tears and wet diapers in this clinical context
A mildly erythematous oropharynx as the first step today
Focal crackles with bronchial breath sounds
Rhinorrhea preceding the illness as the first step today
stages.explanation: The combination of fever, tachypnea, and focal auscultatory findings—coarse crackles, bronchial breath sounds, and dullness to percussion localized to one lobe—is the most specific clinical evidence of lobar pneumonia. Tachypnea is the single most sensitive sign of pneumonia in children. Rhinorrhea is a nonspecific viral prodrome present in many upper respiratory infections. A mildly erythematous oropharynx is a nonspecific finding. Decreased oral intake with preserved hydration markers reflects illness severity and hydration status but does not localize disease to the lungs. Focal, lateralizing exam findings are what distinguish a lobar pneumonia from bronchiolitis or a simple viral URI.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: In an otherwise well-appearing child being managed as an outpatient with classic lobar findings, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?
options: Routine chest CT to characterize the infiltrate as the primary diagnosis
Immediate blood cultures and CBC for all outpatients before starting therapy as the primary diagnosis
Sputum Gram stain and culture obtained from the child as the primary diagnosis
A clinical diagnosis of CAP without mandatory routine chest radiograph or blood tests in the outpatient setting
stages.explanation: Pediatric CAP in an otherwise well, immunized outpatient is largely a clinical diagnosis; per IDSA/PIDS guidelines, routine chest radiographs and blood tests are not required for mild outpatient CAP and do not change management. CT is not indicated for uncomplicated pneumonia and adds radiation without benefit. Routine blood cultures in nontoxic outpatients have low yield and are reserved for those requiring hospitalization or who are severely ill. Reliable sputum samples are difficult to obtain in young children and rarely guide outpatient therapy. Imaging and labs are appropriately reserved for severe disease, hospitalization, diagnostic uncertainty, suspected complications, or failure to respond to therapy.

stages.label: CLINICAL COURSE & DATA
stages.content:
Point-of-care testing: respiratory viral panel negative
Repeat SpO2 after rest: 94% on room air
Chest radiograph (obtained because of focal findings and hypoxemia): right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms; no pleural effusion
No signs of respiratory distress severe enough to require admission; child tolerating small sips in clinic

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the lobar consolidation and her vaccination status, what is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic for outpatient bacterial CAP in this preschooler?
options: Oral azithromycin as monotherapy
Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
High-dose oral amoxicillin
Oral ciprofloxacin as monotherapy
stages.explanation: For previously healthy, fully immunized children with suspected typical bacterial CAP, high-dose oral amoxicillin (about 90 mg/kg/day divided) is first-line because Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the leading bacterial cause and amoxicillin provides reliable coverage with an excellent safety profile. Azithromycin is first-line when atypical pathogens (Mycoplasma) are most likely—typically school-age children with gradual onset, diffuse findings, and 'walking pneumonia'—but is less reliable against pneumococcus due to resistance. Ciprofloxacin lacks optimal pneumococcal coverage and is avoided in children when alternatives exist. TMP-SMX is not a reliable agent for pneumococcal pneumonia. The acute onset and focal lobar findings here favor a typical bacterial process best treated with amoxicillin.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: You discharge her on amoxicillin. Which counseling point and follow-up plan is most appropriate?
options: Switch to an inhaled corticosteroid if cough persists beyond a few days with appropriate follow-up
Obtain a follow-up chest radiograph in all children at 2 weeks to confirm resolution with appropriate follow-up
Expect complete resolution within 24 hours; return only if no better by then with appropriate follow-up
Anticipate clinical improvement within 48-72 hours
stages.explanation: Families should be counseled that bacterial CAP typically improves within 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics, with reduced fever and improved activity and intake. Worsening work of breathing, persistent high fever beyond 48-72 hours, hypoxia, or inability to maintain hydration are red flags prompting reassessment for complications such as effusion/empyema or treatment failure. Expecting full resolution in 24 hours is unrealistic and could cause premature concern or inappropriate antibiotic escalation. Routine follow-up radiographs are not recommended for uncomplicated CAP that responds clinically; they are reserved for recurrent, round, or non-resolving pneumonia. Inhaled corticosteroids treat asthma, not bacterial pneumonia, and are not indicated here.

pearls: Tachypnea is the most sensitive sign of pneumonia in children; focal crackles, bronchial breath sounds, and dullness suggest lobar consolidation.
Outpatient CAP in a well-appearing, immunized child is a clinical diagnosis—routine radiographs and labs are not required.
High-dose amoxicillin is first-line for typical bacterial CAP; reserve azithromycin for suspected atypical (Mycoplasma) 'walking pneumonia' in school-age children.
Expect improvement in 48-72 hours; failure to improve should prompt evaluation for effusion/empyema or an alternative diagnosis.

cases.title: Easy Bruising and Leg Pain in a Pale Child
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of worsening fatigue, bruising, and complaints of leg pain with refusal to walk.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ED with about 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, pallor, and increasing irritability. His parents note numerous bruises on his shins and trunk that seem out of proportion to his activity, and he has been complaining of leg pain, at times refusing to walk or asking to be carried. He has had intermittent low-grade fevers and decreased appetite. There were two recent episodes of prolonged gum bleeding after brushing. He has had no major bleeding history previously and no recent trauma. He is up to date on immunizations and takes no medications. He has no known drug allergies. He attends preschool; there are no unusual exposures or travel. Family history is negative for bleeding disorders or childhood cancers. On review of systems, parents endorse night sweats and that he 'looks washed out.' There has been no weight loss documented but clothes seem looser. He has had a few nosebleeds this month that stopped with pressure.
cases.exam: He is pale and tired-appearing but alert. There are scattered ecchymoses over the shins and trunk and several scattered petechiae on the lower legs. Conjunctivae are pale. There is non-tender cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy with several 1-2 cm mobile nodes. The abdomen is soft with a spleen tip palpable 3 cm below the costal margin and a liver edge 2 cm below the costal margin. There is point tenderness over the proximal tibiae bilaterally without swelling or erythema. There is no joint effusion. Heart exam reveals a soft flow murmur; lungs are clear.
