There is no focal neurologic deficit and no meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial laboratory study is most likely to rapidly point toward the underlying diagnosis?
options: Serum uric acid alone as the first step today
Complete blood count with differential
Coagulation studies (PT/PTT) alone
Plain radiographs of both legs as the first step today
stages.explanation: A CBC with differential plus peripheral smear is the highest-yield first step: the combination of fatigue/pallor (anemia), bruising/petechiae and bleeding (thrombocytopenia), fever and lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and bone pain strongly suggests a marrow-infiltrative process such as acute leukemia. The smear may reveal blasts, and the CBC typically shows cytopenias. PT/PTT evaluate the coagulation cascade but would not explain pancytopenia, lymphadenopathy, and organomegaly; bleeding here is from thrombocytopenia. Uric acid is useful for tumor lysis risk but is not diagnostic of the underlying condition. Leg radiographs might show leukemic lines or lucencies but are nonspecific and would not establish the diagnosis—blood counts and smear come first.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
WBC 22.5 ×10³/µL (with circulating blasts)
Hgb 6.8 g/dL
Platelets 28 ×10³/µL
ANC 0.4 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: numerous lymphoblasts; marked decrease in mature neutrophils and platelets
LDH 820 U/L
Uric acid 7.9 mg/dL
K 4.6 mEq/L  |  Phosphorus 5.4 mg/dL  |  Ca 9.1 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.4 mg/dL
PT/PTT within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The smear shows lymphoblasts with pancytopenia. Which test is required to definitively confirm the diagnosis and classify the leukemia?
options: Whole-body PET-CT as the primary diagnosis
Repeat peripheral smear in one week as the primary diagnosis
Lymph node excisional biopsy as the first confirmatory step
Bone marrow aspiration
stages.explanation: Definitive diagnosis and classification of acute leukemia require bone marrow aspiration and biopsy, with morphology, flow cytometry/immunophenotyping (to distinguish ALL from AML and assign lineage), cytogenetics, and molecular studies that guide risk stratification and therapy. A marrow with ≥20-25% blasts confirms acute leukemia. Repeating the smear delays diagnosis in a child who already has circulating blasts and cytopenias. Lymph node biopsy is not the standard first confirmatory step for suspected acute leukemia—marrow evaluation is definitive and less invasive for this purpose. PET-CT does not establish a tissue diagnosis of leukemia and is not part of the standard initial diagnostic workup. Marrow examination is the gold standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before starting chemotherapy, which complication should be anticipated and prevented given his high blast count, elevated LDH, and uric acid?
options: Adrenal insufficiency requiring stress-dose steroids as the leading concern
Disseminated intravascular coagulation requiring fresh frozen plasma as the leading concern
Tumor lysis syndrome
Hemolytic uremic syndrome requiring plasma exchange as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A high blast burden with elevated LDH and uric acid signals high risk for tumor lysis syndrome (TLS), in which rapid cell turnover releases potassium, phosphate, and uric acid, causing hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, and acute kidney injury. Prevention centers on aggressive IV hydration and a hypouricemic agent—allopurinol for lower risk, rasburicase for high risk or established hyperuricemia—plus close electrolyte monitoring. DIC is classically associated with acute promyelocytic leukemia (a subtype of AML), and his coagulation studies are normal. HUS is not a feature of leukemia onset. Adrenal insufficiency is not anticipated here; while steroids are part of ALL therapy, that is treatment, not a complication to prevent. TLS prophylaxis is the priority before cytoreduction.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is profoundly anemic (Hgb 6.8) and severely neutropenic with fever. Which immediate supportive management step is most appropriate?
options: Administer aspirin for the leg pain as initial therapy
Discharge with outpatient hematology follow-up in one week as initial therapy
Begin oral iron supplementation for the anemia as initial therapy
Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for febrile neutropenia after obtaining blood cultures
stages.explanation: Fever with an ANC of 0.4 ×10³/µL is febrile neutropenia, a medical emergency: after promptly obtaining blood cultures, empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., an antipseudomonal beta-lactam such as cefepime) must be started without delay because neutropenic patients can deteriorate rapidly from occult bacteremia. Oral iron does not address marrow-replacement anemia and is irrelevant to leukemia. Aspirin is contraindicated with severe thrombocytopenia (bleeding risk) and in children (Reye risk); pain should be managed with safer analgesics. Discharge is unsafe—this child needs admission, urgent hematology/oncology involvement, transfusion support as indicated, and TLS prophylaxis. Empiric antibiotics for febrile neutropenia take precedence among the immediate supportive measures.

pearls: The combination of pallor/fatigue, bruising/petechiae, fever, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and bone pain should prompt a CBC with smear to look for blasts.
Bone marrow aspiration/biopsy with flow cytometry, cytogenetics, and molecular studies is the definitive confirmatory and classifying test.
High blast burden with elevated LDH/uric acid means tumor lysis risk—hydrate aggressively and give allopurinol or rasburicase before cytoreduction.
Fever with neutropenia is an emergency: cultures then immediate empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics; childhood B-ALL is highly curable (~85-90%).

cases.title: Sudden Severe Limb Pain in a Boy with a Known Blood Disorder
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute, severe pain in both legs and the lower back, worse than his usual episodes.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents to the ED with 1 day of severe, deep, aching pain in both thighs, lower legs, and his lower back. The pain began after a soccer game on a cold, windy day during which he drank little water. He rates it 9/10 and says it feels like his typical 'pain crises' but more intense. He has had several prior vaso-occlusive episodes, two requiring admission. He takes hydroxyurea and folic acid daily and is up to date on vaccinations including pneumococcal and meningococcal series; he takes penicillin prophylaxis. He has no drug allergies. There has been no fever, cough, chest pain, or shortness of breath at home, and no recent travel. His last transfusion was over a year ago. Family history is significant for sickle cell disease in a maternal cousin. On review of systems he denies dysuria, headache, and abdominal pain. His mother gave ibuprofen at home with minimal relief before arrival.
cases.exam: He is in obvious distress, guarding both lower extremities and reluctant to move. There is diffuse tenderness over the thighs and calves without swelling, warmth, erythema, or joint effusion; distal pulses are intact and capillary refill is brisk. Lungs are clear bilaterally with good air entry and no crackles or focal findings; there is no increased work of breathing. The abdomen is soft and non-tender with no hepatomegaly; the spleen is not palpable. There is mild scleral icterus. Neurologic exam is non-focal. There is no rash, no localized bone point tenderness suggestive of osteomyelitis, and no signs of dactylitis at this age.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management priority for this child's acute vaso-occlusive crisis?
options: Withhold analgesia until imaging excludes osteomyelitis as the first step today
Immediate exchange transfusion for all pain crises as the first step today
High-flow oxygen regardless of oxygen saturation as the primary therapy
Prompt analgesia and IV/oral hydration with frequent reassessment
stages.explanation: The cornerstones of uncomplicated vaso-occlusive crisis are prompt, adequate analgesia and hydration. Severe pain warrants opioids (with NSAIDs as adjuncts) given quickly—guidelines recommend analgesia within 30-60 minutes of arrival—plus IV or oral fluids at maintenance, avoiding overhydration. Pain should not be withheld pending imaging; delaying analgesia causes needless suffering and is not standard care. Exchange transfusion is reserved for specific severe complications (e.g., acute chest syndrome with hypoxia, stroke, multiorgan failure), not routine pain crises. Supplemental oxygen is given for hypoxia; his SpO2 is 97%, and routine high-flow oxygen does not treat vaso-occlusion and can theoretically suppress erythropoiesis. Rapid analgesia and hydration are the immediate priorities.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He has a low-grade temperature of 37.4 °C and a history of penicillin prophylaxis. What is the most appropriate approach to infection risk in a febrile child with sickle cell disease?
options: Maintain a low threshold to evaluate for serious bacterial infection
Stop penicillin prophylaxis to avoid masking infection as the immediate next step
Reassure the family that functional asplenia poses no added infection risk as the immediate next step
Defer any infection workup unless the child becomes hypotensive as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Children with sickle cell disease are functionally asplenic and at high risk for overwhelming infection with encapsulated organisms (e.g., Streptococcus pneumoniae). The standard of care is a low threshold for sepsis evaluation: a febrile SCD child (typically ≥38.5 °C) needs prompt blood cultures and empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (commonly ceftriaxone) without waiting. Reassuring the family that asplenia is harmless is dangerously wrong. Stopping prophylactic penicillin during acute illness is incorrect; prophylaxis reduces pneumococcal sepsis and is continued. Waiting for hypotension misses the window to prevent fulminant sepsis. Although his current temperature is just below the usual fever threshold, vigilance and reassessment are essential because fever can develop and infection can precipitate or accompany crises.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL
Hgb 7.6 g/dL (baseline ~8.0)
Reticulocytes 9% (elevated)
Platelets 410 ×10³/µL
Total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL (indirect predominant)
LDH 540 U/L
Chest radiograph: clear lung fields, no new infiltrate
Blood culture: pending
Pulse oximetry on room air: 97%

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On hospital day 2, he develops a new fever to 38.8 °C, chest pain, increasing oxygen requirement (SpO2 91% on room air), and a new infiltrate appears. What complication should be suspected and what is the management priority?
options: Pulmonary embolism; start full anticoagulation without further evaluation as the leading concern
Acute chest syndrome
Anxiety-related hyperventilation; provide reassurance only as the leading concern
Simple pneumonia; continue current care with oral antibiotics only as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A new infiltrate with fever, chest pain, and hypoxia in a child with SCD is acute chest syndrome (ACS), a leading cause of mortality. Management includes supplemental oxygen to maintain saturation, empiric antibiotics covering typical and atypical pathogens (a cephalosporin plus a macrolide), adequate analgesia (while avoiding oversedation and splinting), incentive spirometry, and transfusion—simple transfusion for moderate cases or exchange transfusion for severe/hypoxic disease—coordinated with hematology. Treating it as simple outpatient pneumonia underestimates a life-threatening process. Empiric anticoagulation for presumed PE is not the first move without evidence and addresses the wrong mechanism. Attributing hypoxia and a new infiltrate to anxiety is unsafe. Early recognition and aggressive treatment of ACS are critical.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After recovery, which long-term measure is most important for reducing the frequency of vaso-occlusive crises and acute chest episodes in this child?
options: Continued adherence to hydroxyurea, hydration, immunizations,
Permanent activity restriction from all sports and exercise with appropriate follow-up
Long-term daily opioids at home to suppress pain with appropriate follow-up
Routine prophylactic exchange transfusions every month for all patients based on current risk
stages.explanation: Hydroxyurea is disease-modifying: it raises fetal hemoglobin and reduces the frequency of pain crises, acute chest syndrome, transfusions, and hospitalizations, so adherence is paramount along with staying hydrated, keeping immunizations current, continuing penicillin prophylaxis as indicated, and avoiding known triggers like cold exposure, dehydration, and overexertion. Routine monthly exchange transfusions are reserved for specific indications (e.g., stroke prevention) and are not standard for all patients. Chronic home opioids are not a preventive strategy and carry dependence and safety risks. Blanket prohibition of all physical activity is neither necessary nor healthy; children are encouraged to stay active while hydrating well and pacing exertion. Optimizing hydroxyurea and lifestyle measures best lowers future crisis risk.

cases.diagnosis: Sickle cell disease with acute vaso-occlusive crisis (complicated by acute chest syndrome)
pearls: Treat vaso-occlusive crisis with prompt analgesia (opioids for severe pain within 30-60 min) and judicious hydration; do not delay pain control for imaging.
SCD patients are functionally asplenic—any significant fever demands cultures and prompt empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone).
Acute chest syndrome (new infiltrate + fever/chest pain/hypoxia) is a leading cause of death—treat with oxygen, antibiotics covering atypicals, analgesia, and transfusion.
Hydroxyurea reduces crisis frequency, ACS, and transfusion needs; trigger avoidance and up-to-date immunizations are key to prevention.

cases.title: Puffy Eyes and Foamy Urine in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: A week of progressive puffiness around the eyes and swelling that the parents first thought was an allergy.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic with about a week of swelling that began around the eyes—worst in the mornings—and has progressed to puffy feet, a swollen scrotum, and a distended abdomen. The parents initially suspected an allergic reaction. They note his urine appears foamy and that he is making somewhat less urine. He had a mild cold roughly two weeks ago that resolved. He has had no gross hematuria, no joint pain, and no rash. He is otherwise developing normally, is fully immunized, and takes no medications. He has no drug allergies. There is no family history of kidney disease or autoimmune disorders. He attends daycare. On review of systems, appetite is mildly decreased and he is more tired than usual, but there is no fever, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, or recent sore throat or skin infection. His parents deny any recent insect bites, new foods, or medication exposures that would suggest an allergic etiology, which is why they sought further evaluation.
cases.exam: He is well-appearing but has marked periorbital edema and puffy eyelids. There is pitting edema of the lower extremities to the mid-shin, scrotal edema, and mild abdominal distension with a fluid wave suggesting ascites. He is not in respiratory distress; lungs are clear. Heart sounds are normal with no murmur or gallop. The abdomen is soft and non-tender without organomegaly. There is no rash, no purpura, and no joint swelling or tenderness. Blood pressure is normal for age. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness, and he is well perfused with brisk capillary refill. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside/initial test will most quickly support the suspected diagnosis of nephrotic syndrome?
options: Abdominal ultrasound to evaluate the ascites
Serum creatinine alone as the first step today
Urinalysis showing heavy proteinuria with little or no blood
Throat culture as the first step today
stages.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome is defined by heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, and edema (with hyperlipidemia), so a urinalysis (or urine dipstick) demonstrating 3+ to 4+ protein—typically without significant hematuria—is the fastest confirmatory bedside step and points to a glomerular protein-losing process. Serum creatinine assesses renal function but is often normal in minimal change disease and does not establish proteinuria. A throat culture would be relevant if post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (a nephritic picture with hematuria/hypertension) were suspected, but this child has bland puffy edema and foamy urine, not cola-colored urine or hypertension. Abdominal ultrasound can document ascites but does not explain its cause. Demonstrating heavy proteinuria is the key initial diagnostic move.

stages.label: LABS & URINE
stages.content:
Urinalysis: protein 4+, no blood, no leukocyte esterase/nitrites; hyaline casts; no RBC casts
Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 8.5 mg/mg (markedly elevated)
Serum albumin: 1.9 g/dL
Total cholesterol: 320 mg/dL
Serum creatinine: 0.4 mg/dL
BUN: 14 mg/dL
C3 and C4: normal
Electrolytes: Na 136, K 4.2, HCO3 22 mEq/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, normal complement, and a bland sediment in a 3-year-old, what is the most likely underlying pathology?
options: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Minimal change disease
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis
Membranous nephropathy
stages.explanation: Minimal change disease (MCD) is the most common cause of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in young children (peak ~2-6 years), characterized by selective heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, edema, normal complement, and a bland urinary sediment without significant hematuria—exactly this picture. FSGS also causes nephrotic syndrome but more often presents with hematuria, hypertension, reduced renal function, or steroid resistance, and is more common in older children/adolescents and certain populations. Membranous nephropathy is rare in young children and is a more common cause in adults. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is a nephritic illness (hematuria, hypertension, low C3) rather than nephrotic, and the normal C3 and absence of hematuria argue against it. MCD is the leading diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In this 3-year-old with a classic presentation and normal complement, what is the most appropriate initial treatment approach?
options: Renal biopsy first in all children before any therapy as monotherapy
Empiric high-dose oral corticosteroids without an initial renal biopsy
Long-term diuretics alone without immunosuppression as initial therapy
Cyclophosphamide as first-line monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In a young child (roughly 1-10 years) with a classic presentation of nephrotic syndrome—normal complement, no significant hematuria, normal renal function—minimal change disease is presumed, and empiric corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone/prednisolone) are first-line without an initial biopsy; the majority are steroid-responsive. Renal biopsy is reserved for atypical features (age <1 or >12 years, gross hematuria, hypertension, low complement, renal insufficiency) or steroid resistance, not for every child. Cyclophosphamide and other steroid-sparing agents are reserved for frequent relapsers, steroid dependence, or steroid resistance, not first-line. Diuretics may cautiously help symptomatic edema but do not treat the underlying glomerular disease and are adjunctive at most. Empiric steroids are the standard initial therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While nephrotic and hypoalbuminemic, which serious complication is this child at increased risk for, warranting vigilance?
options: Hyperkalemic cardiac arrest from potassium retention as the leading concern
Increased risk of infection
Acute hemolytic anemia as the leading concern
Diabetes insipidus as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome causes urinary loss of immunoglobulins and complement factors and an overall immunocompromised state, increasing risk of serious infections—especially spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (classically pneumococcal) given the ascites, as well as cellulitis and sepsis. Patients are also hypercoagulable from urinary loss of antithrombin and other regulatory proteins plus hemoconcentration, raising thromboembolism risk. Hyperkalemic arrest is not a typical feature; potassium is usually normal and renal function preserved in MCD. Acute hemolytic anemia is not characteristic of nephrotic syndrome. Diabetes insipidus is unrelated. Recognizing infection and thrombosis risk guides prompt evaluation of fever or abdominal pain and informs preventive measures such as pneumococcal vaccination and careful fluid management.

cases.diagnosis: Nephrotic syndrome due to minimal change disease
pearls: Nephrotic syndrome = heavy proteinuria + hypoalbuminemia + edema + hyperlipidemia; periorbital edema worst in the morning is a classic early sign.

In children 1-10 years with normal complement, no hematuria, and normal renal function, minimal change disease is presumed and treated with empiric steroids—biopsy is reserved for atypical or steroid-resistant cases.
Watch for infection (notably pneumococcal SBP) and thromboembolism due to urinary loss of immunoglobulins and antithrombin.
Most children are steroid-responsive with a good renal prognosis, but relapses are common—teach home urine dipstick monitoring.

cases.title: Dark Urine and Puffy Face After a Skin Infection
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of cola-colored urine and facial puffiness noticed by his mother.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy presents to clinic with 2 days of dark, cola-colored urine and puffiness around the eyes, especially in the morning. His mother reports he has been making less urine and complaining of a headache. About three weeks ago he had impetigo on his legs—crusted, honey-colored sores—that were treated incompletely with a topical preparation at home. He has had no dysuria, frequency, or flank pain, and no recent sore throat. He is otherwise healthy, fully immunized, and on no medications, with no drug allergies. There is no family history of kidney disease. He attends second grade and has had no travel. On review of systems, appetite is mildly down and he is more tired; there is no rash currently, no joint pain, no abdominal pain, and no fever. His mother notes mild ankle swelling. She is alarmed by the urine color and the new headache, which prompted the visit; he has not had seizures or visual changes.
cases.exam: He is alert and in no acute distress but has mild periorbital edema and trace pitting edema of the ankles. Blood pressure is elevated for age and height. There is no rash currently; healing post-impetigo hyperpigmented areas are noted on the lower legs. The cardiovascular exam is normal without murmur or gallop, and lungs are clear with no signs of pulmonary edema. The abdomen is soft and non-tender with no organomegaly or costovertebral angle tenderness. There is no joint swelling or tenderness. Neurologic exam is non-focal with no papilledema appreciated on the limited fundoscopic exam. He is well perfused.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation best characterizes this child's presentation?
options: Prerenal azotemia from dehydration as the first step today
Nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, bland sediment)
Acute nephritic syndrome
Lower urinary tract infection as the first step today
stages.explanation: The picture of gross (cola-colored) hematuria, hypertension, edema, and decreased urine output defines an acute nephritic syndrome—glomerular inflammation causing red-cell leakage, salt and water retention, and hypertension. Nephrotic syndrome is dominated by heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, and prominent edema with a bland sediment and typically normal blood pressure, which does not match the hematuria and hypertension here. A lower UTI would cause dysuria, frequency, and pyuria rather than cola-colored urine with hypertension and periorbital edema. Prerenal azotemia from dehydration would not produce gross hematuria, edema, and hypertension; this child is well perfused. Recognizing the nephritic pattern, particularly after a recent skin infection, points toward post-infectious glomerulonephritis.

stages.label: LABS & URINE
stages.content:
Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 1+, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts present
Serum creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (mildly elevated for age)
BUN: 28 mg/dL
C3: low (decreased)
C4: normal
Anti-DNase B: elevated
ASO titer: elevated
Electrolytes: Na 137, K 4.6, HCO3 21 mEq/L
Urine protein/creatinine: 0.9 mg/mg (sub-nephrotic)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The labs show RBC casts, low C3 with normal C4, and elevated anti-DNase B. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hemolytic uremic syndrome
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis
IgA nephropathy in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) fits best: a nephritic syndrome arising 1-2 weeks after streptococcal pharyngitis or 3-6 weeks after streptococcal skin infection (impetigo), with RBC casts and dysmorphic RBCs, transiently low C3 with normal C4 (alternative complement pathway activation), and serologic evidence of recent strep infection—anti-DNase B is the most reliable marker after skin infection (ASO is less sensitive for cutaneous strep). IgA nephropathy classically causes synpharyngitic hematuria (during, not weeks after, infection) and has normal complement. Membranoproliferative GN can lower C3 but tends to be persistent and is less tied to a preceding strep skin infection. HUS features microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and AKI, usually after bloody diarrhea, none of which are present here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this child's hypertension and fluid overload?
options: High-dose corticosteroids to suppress the immune response as initial therapy
Immediate plasma exchange as initial therapy
Supportive care with sodium
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics plus dialysis for all patients as initial therapy
stages.explanation: PSGN is treated supportively because it is typically self-limited. Management of volume overload and hypertension centers on sodium and fluid restriction and a loop diuretic (e.g., furosemide) to remove excess salt and water; additional antihypertensives (such as calcium channel blockers) are added if needed. Any ongoing streptococcal infection should be treated with antibiotics, though antibiotics do not reverse established glomerulonephritis. Corticosteroids are not standard for typical PSGN, which resolves without immunosuppression. Plasma exchange is not indicated for PSGN. Dialysis is reserved for the minority with severe acute kidney injury, refractory hyperkalemia, or fluid overload unresponsive to medical therapy—not routine. Salt/fluid restriction plus diuretics targets the pathophysiology of fluid retention driving the hypertension.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most accurate counseling regarding prognosis for this child?
options: Prognosis is excellent in children
He must remain on lifelong immunosuppression to prevent recurrence with appropriate follow-up
Recurrence is extremely common, so prophylactic antibiotics for years are required with appropriate follow-up
He will almost certainly progress to chronic kidney disease and require transplant with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Childhood PSGN carries an excellent prognosis: the great majority recover renal function completely. Gross hematuria and hypertension typically resolve over days to a few weeks, while microscopic hematuria can linger for months and proteinuria for somewhat longer; importantly, the low C3 should return to normal within about 6-8 weeks—failure to normalize suggests an alternative diagnosis like MPGN or lupus nephritis and warrants further workup. Progression to chronic kidney disease is uncommon in children. Lifelong immunosuppression is not needed for a self-limited, immune-mediated post-infectious process. Recurrence of PSGN is rare, so years of prophylactic antibiotics are unnecessary. Reassurance with planned follow-up to confirm resolving hematuria and normalizing complement is the appropriate counseling.

pearls: Nephritic syndrome (gross hematuria, hypertension, edema, oliguria) after a streptococcal skin infection (3-6 weeks) or pharyngitis (1-2 weeks) suggests PSGN.
Look for RBC casts/dysmorphic RBCs, low C3 with normal C4, and serologic evidence of strep—anti-DNase B is most reliable after skin infection.
Treatment is supportive: sodium/fluid restriction and loop diuretics for hypertension and volume overload; treat active strep infection.
Prognosis in children is excellent; C3 should normalize within ~6-8 weeks—persistently low C3 suggests MPGN or lupus and needs further evaluation.

cases.title: A Drooling Child Who Will Not Lie Down
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapid onset of high fever, sore throat, drooling, and difficulty breathing over several hours.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the ED for several hours of high fever, severe sore throat, and a muffled voice, now with drooling and difficulty breathing. His parents say he was well this morning, then declined rapidly; he refuses to swallow and is sitting forward, leaning on his hands. He has not eaten or drunk for hours and looks frightened and ill. His parents declined routine childhood vaccinations, so he has not received Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) immunization. He has no chronic illnesses and takes no medications, with no known allergies. There has been no foreign-body ingestion or choking witnessed, and no trauma. There are no sick contacts identified. On review of systems, there is no cough, no rash, and no recent insect sting; the illness has been dominated by throat pain and now respiratory difficulty. The parents are very anxious about his breathing and the alarming amount of drooling, which prompted the urgent visit. He has had no prior episodes like this.
cases.exam: He appears toxic and anxious, sitting upright in a tripod position with his neck extended and chin forward, drooling. His voice is muffled ('hot potato') and there is soft inspiratory stridor. He has mild suprasternal and intercostal retractions. He resists lying down. There is no audible barky cough. The skin is warm without rash or petechiae. No attempt is made to examine the oropharynx with a tongue depressor given the airway concern. Lungs have transmitted upper-airway sounds but no focal crackles. Heart is tachycardic with normal rhythm. He is alert but distressed; there is no meningismus and the neurologic exam is otherwise non-focal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate action in this toxic child with drooling, tripod positioning, and stridor?
options: Obtain IV access and draw blood cultures before anything else as the first step today
Lay the child flat for a thorough cardiopulmonary exam as the first step today
Keep the child calm in a position of comfort
Immediately examine the throat with a tongue depressor to visualize the epiglottis as the first step today
stages.explanation: Suspected epiglottitis is an airway emergency. The priority is to avoid anything that could precipitate complete airway obstruction: keep the child calm, allow a position of comfort (often sitting on a caregiver's lap), provide blow-by oxygen if tolerated, and urgently mobilize the team best able to secure the airway in a controlled setting—anesthesia and ENT, ideally in the OR. Examining the throat with a tongue depressor can trigger laryngospasm and complete obstruction and is contraindicated until the airway is controlled. Establishing IV access or performing invasive procedures may agitate the child and precipitate obstruction; these are deferred until the airway is secured. Laying the child flat can worsen obstruction. Calm, controlled airway management comes first—diagnostics follow once the airway is safe.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Once the airway team is mobilized, which finding would best confirm the suspected diagnosis if imaging is obtained in a stable, monitored setting?
options: A 'thumbprint sign' on lateral neck radiograph
A retropharyngeal air-fluid level on lateral neck film
A 'steeple sign' (subglottic narrowing) on AP neck radiograph
Bilateral peribronchial cuffing on chest radiograph
stages.explanation: Epiglottitis classically shows a 'thumbprint sign'—an enlarged, swollen epiglottis—on a lateral neck radiograph; direct visualization at laryngoscopy reveals a cherry-red, swollen epiglottis and is definitive. The 'steeple sign' of subglottic narrowing on an AP film is characteristic of croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), which presents with a barky cough and lower fever, not toxic appearance and drooling. A retropharyngeal air-fluid level or widened prevertebral space suggests a retropharyngeal abscess, a different deep-neck infection. Peribronchial cuffing reflects lower-airway/viral processes, not epiglottitis. Importantly, imaging should only be obtained if the child is stable and continuously monitored with airway support available; never send an unstable child to radiology unaccompanied.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate plan for securing this child's airway?
options: Insert a nasopharyngeal airway and observe on the ward as the immediate next step
Attempt rapid-sequence intubation immediately in the ED triage area as the immediate next step
Avoid intubation entirely and rely on high-flow nasal oxygen alone as the immediate next step
Perform controlled endotracheal intubation in the operating room with the most experienced personnel
stages.explanation: The safest approach to a threatened airway in epiglottitis is controlled, planned intubation in the operating room by the most experienced available clinicians (anesthesia and ENT), with equipment and personnel ready to perform an emergency surgical airway (tracheostomy/cricothyrotomy) if intubation fails. The swollen epiglottis can make intubation difficult and attempts can precipitate complete obstruction, so this should not be a rushed ED triage procedure unless the child arrests. A nasopharyngeal airway will not bypass supraglottic obstruction and ward observation is unsafe. High-flow oxygen alone does not protect against impending complete obstruction in a deteriorating child. A controlled OR intubation with surgical backup minimizes the risk of catastrophic airway loss.

stages.label: AIRWAY & DIAGNOSTIC DATA
stages.content:
Controlled intubation performed in the OR by anesthesia/ENT; direct laryngoscopy shows a swollen, cherry-red epiglottis
Lateral neck radiograph (taken with airway team present): thumbprint sign, enlarged epiglottis
Blood culture: drawn after airway secured; pending
CBC: WBC 19.5 ×10³/µL with left shift
Unimmunized for Hib

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After the airway is secured, what is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic therapy?
options: IV third-generation cephalosporin , with consideration of anti-staphylococcal coverage
Oral azithromycin as initial therapy
No antibiotics; this is a viral process requiring only supportive care as monotherapy
Oral amoxicillin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Bacterial epiglottitis—classically Haemophilus influenzae type b in unimmunized children, with Streptococcus pneumoniae, group A streptococcus, and Staphylococcus aureus also implicated—requires prompt IV antibiotics after the airway is secured. A third-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone or cefotaxime provides appropriate empiric coverage; adding anti-staphylococcal coverage (e.g., for MRSA in the right setting) may be considered. Oral amoxicillin is inadequate for a critically ill child needing IV therapy and reliable Gram-negative coverage. Oral azithromycin does not reliably cover the typical pathogens and is not appropriate empiric monotherapy. This is a bacterial airway emergency, not a viral illness; withholding antibiotics would be dangerous. IV ceftriaxone is the standard empiric choice once the airway is protected.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional public-health and prophylaxis consideration is most appropriate in this Hib-related case?
options: Confirm the organism
Recommend the family avoid all future vaccines because of this illness with appropriate follow-up
Place all household members on lifelong antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
No follow-up is needed once the child recovers with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: If cultures confirm Haemophilus influenzae type b, public-health measures apply: rifampin chemoprophylaxis is recommended for certain close/household contacts (especially households with under-immunized young children or immunocompromised individuals) to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage, per public-health guidance. The recovering child should receive catch-up immunization, including Hib, and the family should be counseled on the protective value of vaccination—this case is a direct consequence of being unimmunized. Doing nothing ignores transmissible risk to vulnerable contacts. Lifelong antibiotics for household members is not indicated; prophylaxis is a short, targeted course. Advising the family to avoid all future vaccines is medically harmful and contradicts the lesson of this illness. Confirming the pathogen and providing targeted prophylaxis plus catch-up vaccination is the correct, evidence-based step.

cases.diagnosis: Acute epiglottitis (Haemophilus influenzae type b in an unimmunized child)
pearls: Epiglottitis presents with the 4 D's—drooling, dysphagia, dysphonia (muffled voice), and distress—plus toxic appearance and tripod posture; it is an airway emergency.
Do NOT examine the throat with a tongue depressor or agitate the child—keep calm and mobilize anesthesia/ENT for controlled airway management first.
Lateral neck radiograph shows the 'thumbprint sign'; laryngoscopy reveals a cherry-red swollen epiglottis—but only image a stable, monitored child.
After securing the airway, give IV ceftriaxone/cefotaxime; confirm Hib and consider rifampin prophylaxis for close contacts plus catch-up immunization.

cases.title: A Febrile Toddler with a Spreading Rash and Cold Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: High fever and a rapidly spreading rash with the child becoming increasingly lethargic.
cases.history: A 2-year-old girl is brought to the ED with about 12 hours of high fever, irritability, and poor feeding, and in the last 2 hours a rash that her parents say is 'spreading fast' and 'not going away when pressed.' She became progressively more lethargic and her hands and feet feel cold. She had mild cold symptoms over the prior day. She attends daycare; her parents are unsure whether she completed her meningococcal-containing vaccinations. She has no chronic medical problems, takes no medications, and has no known drug allergies. There has been no recent travel. On review of systems she has had no clear neck stiffness reported at home, but has been very fussy and difficult to console, with decreased wet diapers. Family history is unremarkable. The parents are frightened by how quickly she has deteriorated and by the new purple spots, which prompted immediate presentation. She has had a few episodes of vomiting but no diarrhea, and no known sick contacts with similar rashes.
cases.exam: She is lethargic and ill-appearing with cool, mottled extremities and delayed capillary refill of about 4 seconds. There are scattered non-blanching petechiae and several larger purpuric lesions over the trunk and lower extremities that appear to be increasing. She is tachycardic with weak peripheral pulses and a narrow pulse pressure; blood pressure is low for age. Mucous membranes are dry. The anterior fontanelle is closed (age-appropriate). There is no obvious focal neurologic deficit, but she is difficult to arouse and irritable when stimulated. Lungs are clear. There is no localized source of infection identified on skin exam beyond the purpura, and no joint swelling.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this lethargic, hypotensive toddler with rapidly spreading petechiae/purpura, what is the most critical immediate intervention?
options: Obtain a CT head before any treatment as the first step today
Administer oral antipyretics and observe for improvement as the first step today
Recognize septic shock
Perform a lumbar puncture first, then start antibiotics based on results as the first step today
stages.explanation: Rapidly spreading petechiae/purpura with shock in a febrile child is meningococcemia until proven otherwise—a fulminant emergency. The priority is immediate empiric IV (or intraosseous if access is difficult) antibiotics—a third-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone or cefotaxime—together with aggressive fluid resuscitation for septic shock; antibiotics must not be delayed for diagnostic procedures. Blood cultures can be drawn as antibiotics are given if it causes no delay. CT before treatment wastes precious time and is not required to start lifesaving therapy. A lumbar puncture is contraindicated or deferred in hemodynamic instability/shock and coagulopathy, and antibiotics should never wait for it. Antipyretics and observation are grossly inadequate for impending septic shock. Antibiotics plus resuscitation now is the key action.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hypotensive with cold extremities and 4-second capillary refill. What is the most appropriate fluid resuscitation strategy for pediatric septic shock?
options: Give a slow maintenance infusion of dextrose-containing fluid only as initial therapy
Rapid isotonic crystalloid boluses with frequent reassessment, repeating as needed
Administer a single small bolus and reassess only after 6 hours as initial therapy
Withhold fluids to avoid pulmonary edema and start vasopressors first as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Pediatric septic shock is treated with rapid isotonic crystalloid boluses (commonly 10-20 mL/kg) given quickly and titrated to clinical response—improving perfusion, capillary refill, mental status, urine output, and blood pressure—with frequent reassessment for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, rales). If shock persists despite adequate fluids (fluid-refractory shock), vasoactive agents (e.g., epinephrine or norepinephrine) are started. Withholding fluids entirely is wrong; fluid resuscitation is foundational, though one monitors for overload. Maintenance-rate dextrose fluids are far too slow to reverse shock and do not restore intravascular volume. A single small bolus with reassessment delayed for hours abandons the child during a rapidly evolving emergency. Aggressive but monitored fluid resuscitation, escalating to vasoactives as needed, is the standard.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which empiric antibiotic is most appropriate to administer immediately for suspected meningococcal disease in this toddler?
options: Oral amoxicillin as the immediate next step
A third-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone or cefotaxime
Gentamicin monotherapy as the immediate next step
Metronidazole as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Empiric coverage for suspected meningococcal disease (and the overlapping possibility of bacterial meningitis) is a third-generation cephalosporin—ceftriaxone or cefotaxime—given immediately by IV or IO route; these agents reliably cover Neisseria meningitidis and achieve good CNS penetration, and ceftriaxone also helps eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage. Oral amoxicillin is inappropriate for a critically ill, shocky child and cannot be relied upon for absorption or rapidity. Gentamicin monotherapy does not adequately cover meningococcus or penetrate the CNS and is not appropriate as sole therapy. Metronidazole targets anaerobes and has no role against Neisseria. In a young child where pneumococcal meningitis is also possible, vancomycin may be added, but the single most appropriate immediate agent here is a third-generation cephalosporin given without delay.

stages.label: LABS & DATA
stages.content:
WBC 2.8 ×10³/µL (leukopenia)
Platelets 60 ×10³/µL
INR 1.9, fibrinogen low, D-dimer elevated (consistent with DIC)
Lactate 5.2 mmol/L
Glucose 64 mg/dL
Blood culture (drawn with first antibiotics): Gram-negative diplococci reported on preliminary Gram stain
CRP markedly elevated

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The labs show low platelets, prolonged INR, low fibrinogen, and elevated D-dimer with spreading purpura. Which complication is occurring, and what is its significance?
options: Disseminated intravascular coagulation, reflecting severe sepsis
Hemophilia A unmasked by infection as the leading concern
A simple viral exanthem requiring no further action as the leading concern
Immune thrombocytopenic purpura, a benign isolated low platelet count as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Thrombocytopenia, prolonged INR, hypofibrinogenemia, and elevated D-dimer in the setting of spreading purpura indicate disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), a hallmark of severe meningococcal sepsis (purpura fulminans). DIC reflects the severity of the systemic process and is associated with higher mortality and risk of tissue ischemia/limb loss. Management is supportive—continue antibiotics and resuscitation, support coagulation as clinically indicated (e.g., platelets, fresh frozen plasma, cryoprecipitate for active bleeding/very low fibrinogen), and provide intensive care—while treating the underlying infection. ITP is an isolated thrombocytopenia without consumptive coagulopathy and does not explain the prolonged INR and low fibrinogen. Hemophilia A causes prolonged PTT with normal platelets and would not present this way acutely. A benign viral exanthem does not cause shock and DIC. Recognizing DIC underscores the gravity of the illness.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding infection control and public health for confirmed meningococcal disease, which step is most appropriate?
options: No isolation or contact measures are necessary with appropriate follow-up
Place the child on droplet precautions
Only the treating physician needs antibiotic prophylaxis with appropriate follow-up
Administer prophylaxis to the entire daycare regardless of contact level without consulting public health
stages.explanation: Neisseria meningitidis is transmitted by respiratory droplets, so the patient should be on droplet precautions until at least 24 hours of effective therapy. Close contacts—household members, those with prolonged close contact, and anyone exposed to oral secretions—should receive chemoprophylaxis (rifampin, a single dose of ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone) to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage and prevent secondary cases; healthcare workers need prophylaxis only after intimate exposure such as unprotected mouth-to-mouth or intubation. Saying no measures are needed ignores serious secondary transmission risk. Limiting prophylaxis to the physician alone misses high-risk household/close contacts. Blanket prophylaxis of an entire daycare without risk stratification or public-health coordination is not appropriate; exposure-based decisions should be made in conjunction with public-health authorities. Droplet precautions plus targeted prophylaxis is the correct approach.

cases.diagnosis: Meningococcemia (Neisseria meningitidis) with septic shock and DIC
pearls: Fever with rapidly spreading non-blanching petechiae/purpura and shock is meningococcemia until proven otherwise—give empiric IV/IO ceftriaxone immediately, do not wait for an LP.
Defer lumbar puncture in hemodynamic instability or coagulopathy; antibiotics and fluid resuscitation come first.
Treat pediatric septic shock with rapid isotonic crystalloid boluses (10-20 mL/kg) titrated to perfusion, escalating to vasoactive agents for fluid-refractory shock.
DIC/purpura fulminans signals severe disease and high mortality; place on droplet precautions and give chemoprophylaxis to close contacts.

cases.title: An Irritable, Feverish Infant Who Will Not Settle
cases.chiefComplaint: A day of high fever, inconsolable irritability, poor feeding, and lethargy.
cases.history: A 6-month-old boy is brought to the ED with about 18 hours of fever to 39.5 °C, increasing irritability, poor feeding, and now intermittent lethargy. His parents report he is inconsolable when picked up and arches his back, and he has vomited twice. He had mild congestion a few days ago. He was born full-term without complications and is up to date on vaccines for age, including the primary PCV and Hib series. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. There are no ill contacts with known serious infections. On review of systems, the parents deny diarrhea, rash, or known seizure activity, though he had one brief episode of staring this morning that they were unsure about. He lives with both parents; no recent travel. Family history is unremarkable. The parents are alarmed because he is normally an easy baby and today seems 'not himself,' difficult to wake fully between bouts of crying, which prompted them to come in promptly.
cases.exam: He is irritable and difficult to console, with a high-pitched cry, and is intermittently lethargic. The anterior fontanelle is full and bulging. There is paradoxical irritability—he cries more when held and rocked. He is mildly tachycardic with warm extremities and capillary refill of about 2-3 seconds. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. Classic neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity) is unreliable at this age and is not clearly present, and Kernig/Brudzinski signs are not reliably elicited in infants. There is no obvious focal neurologic deficit and no rash or petechiae. Lungs are clear, and the abdomen is soft. The remainder of the exam is otherwise unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature in this 6-month-old most strongly raises concern for bacterial meningitis?
options: Slightly dry mucous membranes as the first step today
Two episodes of vomiting as the first step today
Mild congestion a few days ago as the first step today
A bulging/full anterior fontanelle with paradoxical irritability
stages.explanation: In infants, classic meningeal signs (nuchal rigidity, Kernig/Brudzinski) are unreliable, so clinicians must rely on subtler clues. A bulging or full anterior fontanelle (reflecting raised intracranial pressure), paradoxical irritability (crying that worsens with handling/consoling), lethargy, high-pitched cry, and poor feeding in a febrile young infant strongly suggest meningitis. Mild antecedent congestion is nonspecific and common. Vomiting can occur with many illnesses and, while it may accompany raised ICP, is not specific by itself. Mildly dry mucous membranes indicate some dehydration but are nonspecific. The combination of a bulging fontanelle, paradoxical irritability, and fluctuating consciousness is the most meningitis-specific constellation in this age group and should prompt urgent evaluation and treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is irritable but hemodynamically stable without focal deficits, signs of herniation, or coagulopathy. What is the most appropriate sequence of management?
options: Perform CT head on every infant before LP, then defer antibiotics until imaging and LP are both complete as initial therapy
Start oral antibiotics and arrange outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Delay antibiotics until after lumbar puncture regardless of how long it takes to arrange as initial therapy
Obtain blood cultures
stages.explanation: When bacterial meningitis is suspected, the overriding principle is that antibiotics must not be delayed. Obtain blood cultures and start empiric IV antibiotics promptly; ideally an LP is performed first to obtain CSF before antibiotics, but if the LP cannot be done quickly, antibiotics are given without delay (CSF chemistry/Gram stain/culture may still help, and blood cultures plus PCR aid diagnosis). Delaying antibiotics for a hard-to-arrange LP risks death and disability. Oral antibiotics and outpatient management are unsafe for suspected bacterial meningitis. Routine CT before LP is not required in a child without focal deficits, signs of raised ICP/herniation, or other contraindications; mandating CT for every infant needlessly delays both LP and antibiotics. Prompt cultures and empiric IV therapy, with timely LP, is correct.

stages.label: CSF & LABS
stages.content:
CSF: WBC 1,800 /µL (90% neutrophils)
CSF glucose: 25 mg/dL (serum glucose 90 mg/dL)
CSF protein: 180 mg/dL
CSF Gram stain: Gram-positive diplococci
Blood culture: pending
Serum WBC: 21 ×10³/µL with left shift
CRP: elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein, and Gram-positive diplococci. Which empiric regimen best covers the likely pathogens for bacterial meningitis at this age?
options: Acyclovir monotherapy as the primary diagnosis
Ampicillin plus gentamicin as the sole regimen for this age
Oral amoxicillin alone as the primary diagnosis
Vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin
stages.explanation: For a 6-month-old beyond the neonatal period, empiric therapy for bacterial meningitis is vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime). This covers Streptococcus pneumoniae (the Gram-positive diplococci here, including possible cephalosporin-resistant strains, hence vancomycin) and Neisseria meningitidis, the leading pathogens in this age group. Oral amoxicillin alone is inadequate for life-threatening CNS infection and provides neither reliable CNS penetration nor coverage of resistant pneumococcus. Acyclovir treats HSV encephalitis and is added when HSV is suspected, but it does not cover bacterial pathogens and is not appropriate as monotherapy for clear bacterial meningitis. Ampicillin plus gentamicin targets neonatal pathogens (group B Streptococcus, Listeria, E. coli) and is the regimen for neonates, not a 6-month-old. Vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin is correct.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: On the second hospital day his serum sodium drops to 126 mEq/L with concentrated urine and clinical euvolemia. What is the most likely explanation and appropriate response?
options: Aggressive saline and salt tablets for primary sodium loss as the immediate next step
Diabetes insipidus; give free water and desmopressin as the immediate next step
Laboratory error; ignore and recheck in 48 hours as the immediate next step
Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone
stages.explanation: Hyponatremia with concentrated (inappropriately high osmolality) urine in a euvolemic child with meningitis is most consistent with SIADH, a common complication of CNS infection driven by nonosmotic ADH release. Management is judicious fluid restriction with close monitoring of sodium and neurologic status, avoiding overly rapid correction. Diabetes insipidus produces the opposite picture—dilute urine with rising sodium and large volume losses—so free water and desmopressin would worsen hyponatremia. Aggressive saline for presumed cerebral salt wasting is reserved for true salt wasting with hypovolemia and ongoing renal sodium loss; this child is euvolemic, making SIADH more likely, and aggressive saline could cause overcorrection. Dismissing it as lab error is unsafe given the clinical context. Recognizing and appropriately managing SIADH prevents avoidable neurologic harm.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: For confirmed pneumococcal meningitis, which adjunctive therapy and follow-up consideration is most appropriate?
options: Routine prophylactic anticoagulation for all patients as the leading concern
Long-term antiepileptic therapy for every child regardless of seizures as the leading concern
Immediate surgical drainage of the subarachnoid space as the leading concern
Adjunctive dexamethasone consideration
stages.explanation: Adjunctive dexamethasone is considered in bacterial meningitis to reduce inflammatory complications—most notably hearing loss—and is most effective when given with or just before the first antibiotic dose; its benefit is best established for Haemophilus influenzae type b and considered for pneumococcal disease per local guidance. Because sensorineural hearing loss is the most common long-term sequela of bacterial meningitis, audiologic (hearing) evaluation before or shortly after discharge is essential. Routine anticoagulation is not indicated and could be harmful. Lifelong antiepileptics for every child are not warranted; seizures are treated as they arise and managed based on individual course. Surgical drainage of the subarachnoid space is not a treatment for bacterial meningitis. Timely steroid consideration plus hearing assessment reflects standard, guideline-based aftercare.

pearls: In infants, meningeal signs are unreliable—rely on a bulging fontanelle, paradoxical irritability, high-pitched cry, lethargy, and poor feeding.
Never delay antibiotics for a hard-to-arrange LP; obtain blood cultures, start empiric IV therapy promptly, and do the LP as soon as feasible.
Empiric therapy beyond the neonatal period is vancomycin + a third-generation cephalosporin; neonates get ampicillin + cefotaxime/gentamicin to cover GBS and Listeria.
Consider adjunctive dexamethasone with/before the first antibiotic dose and arrange audiologic follow-up—hearing loss is the most common sequela.

cases.title: A Premature Infant with a Distended Belly and Bloody Stools
cases.chiefComplaint: Feeding intolerance with a distended abdomen and bloody stools in a premature NICU infant.
cases.history: A 3-week-old boy, born at 28 weeks gestation and now in the NICU, develops feeding intolerance over the last 12 hours: increasing gastric residuals, bilious-tinged aspirates, abdominal distension, and two episodes of grossly bloody stool. He had been advancing on fortified enteral feeds. Today he has become more lethargic with temperature instability and several apneic and bradycardic episodes. His birth course included respiratory distress syndrome treated with surfactant; he has a history of a patent ductus arteriosus. He has been on no recent antibiotics. He has no known drug allergies. His mother had preterm labor; pregnancy was otherwise unremarkable, and family history is noncontributory. On review of the chart, feeds had recently been advanced, and there were no prior episodes of bloody stool. The bedside nurse notes the abdomen looks shinier and more distended than earlier in the shift and that he is less active and more mottled-appearing, prompting urgent evaluation by the team.
cases.exam: He is lethargic and mottled with cool extremities and sluggish capillary refill. The abdomen is distended, shiny, and firm with visible loops; it is tender to palpation with decreased bowel sounds. There is no obvious abdominal wall erythema or discoloration appreciated yet, but the distension has worsened over hours. He has had grossly bloody stool documented. He is tachycardic with weak pulses and borderline-low blood pressure for a preterm infant, and he has had apneic/bradycardic spells. Respirations are labored with mild retractions. There is no palpable inguinal hernia and no obvious external source of bleeding. He appears systemically ill with signs of early shock.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this preterm infant with feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, and bloody stools, what is the most important immediate management step?
options: Give a single oral dose of probiotics and observe as the first step today
Continue and advance enteral feeds to maintain nutrition as the first step today
Discharge planning once the bloody stool stops on its own as the first step today
Make the infant NPO, place an orogastric tube for gastric decompression, obtain abdominal radiographs, send cultures/labs,
stages.explanation: This is necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) until proven otherwise. The immediate bundle is to stop enteral feeds (make NPO) to rest the gut, place an orogastric tube for bowel decompression, obtain abdominal radiographs (looking for pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, or free air), send a sepsis workup (blood culture, CBC, coagulation studies, electrolytes, blood gas), and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics. Continuing or advancing feeds would worsen bowel injury and is dangerous. A single probiotic dose does not treat established NEC and is not a substitute for resuscitation and bowel rest. Considering discharge in a deteriorating, shocky infant is grossly inappropriate. The NPO/decompression/imaging/cultures/antibiotics bundle is the cornerstone of initial NEC management while the infant is stabilized.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Abdominal radiograph: pneumatosis intestinalis (intramural gas) involving the right lower quadrant; dilated bowel loops; portal venous gas noted; no free air on this view
WBC 3.0 ×10³/µL (low)
Platelets 80 ×10³/µL (falling)
ABG: pH 7.21, metabolic acidosis
Lactate elevated
CRP elevated
Blood culture: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which radiographic finding is considered pathognomonic for necrotizing enterocolitis?
options: A single dilated loop of bowel
Gasless abdomen alone as the primary diagnosis
Air-fluid levels in the stomach
Pneumatosis intestinalis
stages.explanation: Pneumatosis intestinalis—gas within the bowel wall, seen as curvilinear or bubbly lucencies along the intestinal contour—is the pathognomonic radiographic sign of NEC and reflects gas produced by bacteria within the ischemic, injured wall. Portal venous gas is another characteristic and ominous finding, and free intraperitoneal air (e.g., football sign, Rigler sign) indicates perforation. A single dilated loop is nonspecific and can be seen in many conditions, including early/evolving NEC, but is not diagnostic. A gasless abdomen alone is nonspecific. Air-fluid levels in the stomach reflect gastric contents and do not establish NEC. Identifying pneumatosis confirms the diagnosis and, along with portal venous gas and any free air, helps stage severity (Bell staging) and guides escalation of care.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With pneumatosis and portal venous gas but no free air, and the infant in early shock, what is the appropriate management course?
options: Resume feeds immediately to stimulate gut recovery as initial therapy
Immediate laparotomy for all infants with pneumatosis regardless of clinical status as initial therapy
Aggressive medical management
Antibiotics for 24 hours then discharge if stable as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Medical NEC (pneumatosis and/or portal venous gas without perforation) is managed nonoperatively but intensively: continue bowel rest (NPO, typically for a defined course such as ~7-14 days depending on severity), orogastric decompression, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, and supportive care including IV fluids/parenteral nutrition and hemodynamic support (pressors as needed), with serial abdominal exams and serial radiographs (often every 6-8 hours) to detect progression. Pediatric surgery should be consulted, with operation reserved for absolute indications—pneumoperitoneum (perforation) or clinical deterioration despite maximal medical therapy. Mandating laparotomy for all pneumatosis overtreats infants who can be managed medically. Resuming feeds would worsen bowel injury. Twenty-four hours of antibiotics then discharge is dangerously inadequate for a serious, evolving illness. Intensive medical management with surgical backup is the correct course.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Several hours later the infant deteriorates with worsening acidosis, and a repeat film shows free intraperitoneal air. What does this indicate and what is the next step?
options: Improvement; continue medical management unchanged as the leading concern
A normal finding in preterm infants requiring no action as the leading concern
Intestinal perforation
Resolved NEC; restart feeds as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Free intraperitoneal air (pneumoperitoneum) signifies intestinal perforation, which is an absolute indication for surgical intervention in NEC. Depending on the infant's size and stability, options include exploratory laparotomy with resection of necrotic bowel or placement of a primary peritoneal drain, decided with the surgical team. This is a deterioration, not improvement, so continuing unchanged medical management is unsafe. Perforation does not mean NEC has resolved, and restarting feeds in a perforated, acidotic infant would be catastrophic. Free air is never a normal finding and always demands urgent evaluation. Recognizing pneumoperitoneum as the trigger for operative management—alongside ongoing resuscitation and antibiotics—is the key teaching point for advanced/surgical NEC (Bell stage III).

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding prevention and long-term considerations for NEC in preterm infants, which statement is most accurate?
options: Human milk feeding and standardized, cautious feeding protocols reduce NEC risk
NEC has no long-term gastrointestinal consequences with appropriate follow-up
Rapid advancement of formula feeds reduces NEC risk with appropriate follow-up
Prophylactic surgery prevents NEC in all preterm infants with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Human (breast) milk is protective against NEC compared with formula, and standardized, cautious feeding-advancement protocols are associated with lower NEC rates; these are cornerstones of prevention in preterm infants. Survivors require monitoring for long-term complications, including intestinal strictures (which can cause later obstruction) and, after significant resection, short bowel syndrome with malabsorption and dependence on parenteral nutrition. Rapid advancement of formula feeds increases, not decreases, NEC risk. NEC frequently has significant long-term GI consequences, so saying it has none is incorrect. There is no prophylactic surgery that prevents NEC; prevention is feeding-based and supportive. Emphasizing human milk, careful feeding protocols, and vigilant follow-up for strictures and short bowel syndrome reflects best practice.

cases.diagnosis: Necrotizing enterocolitis (progressing to perforation, Bell stage III)
pearls: Suspect NEC in a preterm infant with feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic signs (apnea, temperature instability, lethargy).
Initial bundle: NPO, orogastric decompression, abdominal films, sepsis workup, and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics with resuscitation.
Pneumatosis intestinalis is pathognomonic; portal venous gas is ominous; free air signals perforation—an absolute indication for surgery.
Human milk and standardized cautious feeding protocols reduce NEC risk; survivors need monitoring for strictures and short bowel syndrome.

cases.title: A newborn struggling to breathe with a sunken belly
cases.chiefComplaint: The delivery team called us because this baby turned blue and could not breathe right after birth.
cases.history: A term boy is born at 39 weeks to a 27-year-old G2P1 mother by spontaneous vaginal delivery. Within minutes of birth he develops severe respiratory distress with grunting, retractions, and central cyanosis that worsens with bag-mask ventilation. Apgar scores were 4 at 1 minute and 5 at 5 minutes. Pregnancy was complicated by polyhydramnios noted on the third-trimester ultrasound, but the mother received no formal fetal echocardiography and limited prenatal care. There was no maternal fever, prolonged rupture of membranes, or meconium-stained fluid. The mother took prenatal vitamins only; she denies tobacco, alcohol, or drug use. There are no known allergies. Family history is unremarkable for congenital anomalies or genetic syndromes. The infant has not voided or stooled. He was placed on continuous positive airway pressure briefly, but oxygen saturations remained low and his abdomen appeared unusually flat. He is now intubated and transferred to the NICU for further stabilization and workup of refractory hypoxemia and respiratory failure.
cases.exam: Term male in severe respiratory distress with grunting, nasal flaring, and intercostal retractions. The chest is barrel-shaped and the abdomen is markedly scaphoid (sunken). Breath sounds are diminished on the left, and bowel sounds are audible over the left hemithorax. Heart sounds are best heard shifted toward the right side of the chest. There is central cyanosis improving slightly with intubation. No cardiac murmur appreciated. Anterior fontanelle is soft and flat. Abdomen is soft and non-distended with no palpable masses. Extremities are well-perfused after resuscitation with 2+ pulses. No dysmorphic features.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After the infant is found to have refractory hypoxemia and a scaphoid abdomen, what is the most appropriate immediate airway management step?
options: Endotracheal intubation
Apply high-flow nasal cannula and observe as the first step today
Administer surfactant and extubate to CPAP as the first step today
Perform immediate needle thoracostomy of the left chest
Continue aggressive bag-mask ventilation with high tidal volumes
stages.explanation: In suspected congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH), prolonged bag-mask ventilation forces air into the herniated bowel, distending it and worsening lung compression and hypoxemia; the infant should be promptly intubated and an orogastric (or nasogastric) tube placed to low suction to decompress the gut. Continued aggressive bag-mask ventilation is harmful for exactly that reason. High-flow nasal cannula is inadequate for respiratory failure and also insufflates the GI tract. Needle thoracostomy treats a pneumothorax, not bowel-filled chest, and risks perforating herniated viscera. Surfactant with extubation is wrong because these infants have pulmonary hypoplasia and pulmonary hypertension, not primary surfactant deficiency, and need controlled mechanical ventilation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which single study will most rapidly confirm the suspected diagnosis at the bedside?
options: Echocardiogram
Arterial blood gas
Karyotype and microarray
Upper GI contrast series
Chest and abdominal radiograph
stages.explanation: A plain chest/abdomen radiograph is the fastest confirmatory test for CDH: it shows bowel gas (loops) in the hemithorax, mediastinal shift away from the lesion, a paucity of gas in the abdomen, and the tip of the orogastric tube coiling into the chest. An arterial blood gas quantifies gas exchange but does not establish the anatomic diagnosis. Echocardiography is essential to assess associated pulmonary hypertension and rule out cardiac disease, but it is confirmatory of physiology, not the hernia itself. An upper GI series is unnecessary, slow, and risky in an unstable intubated neonate. Karyotype/microarray screens for associated anomalies later but is not a bedside diagnostic for the structural defect.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Chest/abdomen radiograph: multiple loops of bowel gas in the left hemithorax
Mediastinum and cardiac silhouette shifted to the right
Paucity of gas in the abdomen; orogastric tube tip coiled in the left chest
Arterial blood gas (on conventional ventilation): pH 7.18, PaCO2 62 mmHg, PaO2 44 mmHg
Preductal SpO2 82%, postductal SpO2 70%
Echocardiogram: structurally normal heart, suprasystemic right ventricular pressures with right-to-left shunting across the patent ductus arteriosus

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The echocardiogram shows suprasystemic pulmonary pressures with right-to-left ductal shunting and a preductal–postductal saturation gradient. What is the most appropriate next management priority?
options: Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and high-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
Pursue gentle ventilation
Initiate prostaglandin E1 to widen the ductus arteriosus as initial therapy
Begin aggressive hyperventilation to drive PaCO2 below 25 mmHg as initial therapy
Take the infant emergently to the operating room for hernia repair within the first hour of life
stages.explanation: Modern CDH care delays surgical repair until the infant is physiologically stabilized; the acute killer is pulmonary hypoplasia with persistent pulmonary hypertension, so the priority is gentle, permissive-hypercapnia ventilation (avoiding volutrauma), and treatment of pulmonary hypertension with measures such as inhaled nitric oxide, with ECMO available for refractory cases. Emergent repair within an hour does not improve survival and may worsen pulmonary hypertension. Antibiotics may be started empirically but are not the primary priority, and steroids have no established role. Aggressive hyperventilation to very low PaCO2 causes barotrauma and worsens outcomes. Prostaglandin keeps a duct open for ductal-dependent heart lesions; here the heart is structurally normal and the duct is already patent.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite optimal ventilation, the infant's preductal saturations remain in the 70s with suprasystemic pulmonary pressures and rising lactate. Which therapy is the appropriate next escalation?
options: Emergent hernia repair at the bedside
Intravenous indomethacin to close the ductus
High-dose intravenous furosemide
Discontinuation of mechanical ventilation in favor of CPAP
Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO)
stages.explanation: When a CDH infant has refractory hypoxemia and circulatory compromise from severe pulmonary hypertension despite optimal gentle ventilation and pulmonary vasodilators, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) provides cardiopulmonary support, allowing the pulmonary vasculature time to recover and serving as a bridge to repair. Emergent bedside repair does not address the lethal pulmonary hypertension and worsens instability. Furosemide does not treat the underlying pulmonary vascular crisis. Switching to CPAP abandons needed controlled ventilation and would be catastrophic. Indomethacin closes the ductus, but the right-to-left ductal shunt is a 'pop-off' that protects the failing right ventricle, so closing it acutely can precipitate right heart failure; it is not the rescue therapy. ECMO is the recognized escalation for refractory cases.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: The parents ask what most strongly determines whether their son will survive. Which factor is the most important determinant of mortality in this condition?
options: The presence of a patent ductus arteriosus in this setting
Birth weight under 3 kilograms as the immediate next step
The degree of pulmonary hypoplasia
Whether delivery was vaginal or by cesarean section
The specific side (left versus right) of the defect
stages.explanation: Survival in CDH is determined chiefly by the degree of pulmonary hypoplasia and the severity of associated pulmonary hypertension, which reflect how much lung had room to develop in utero. Left-sided defects are more common but side alone is a weaker predictor than lung development and associated anomalies. Birth weight matters far less than lung physiology in term infants. A patent ductus is expected in neonates and is not itself the survival determinant. Mode of delivery does not meaningfully affect CDH mortality. Counseling emphasizes that lung volume and pulmonary vascular resistance, often estimated antenatally by lung-to-head ratio and liver position, drive prognosis.

cases.diagnosis: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (left-sided, Bochdalek) with pulmonary hypoplasia and persistent pulmonary hypertension
pearls: Scaphoid abdomen plus respiratory distress and bowel sounds in the chest in a neonate is congenital diaphragmatic hernia until proven otherwise.
Intubate and place an orogastric tube immediately; prolonged bag-mask ventilation distends herniated bowel and worsens lung compression.
The lethal physiology is pulmonary hypoplasia and persistent pulmonary hypertension, not the hernia itself, so stabilize before operating.
Most defects are left-sided (Bochdalek, posterolateral); polyhydramnios is a common antenatal clue.

cases.title: A baby boy who turns blue and squats when he cries
cases.chiefComplaint: He suddenly turned deep blue while crying this morning and seemed to struggle to breathe.
cases.history: A 6-month-old boy is brought in after an episode this morning in which he became deeply cyanotic, irritable, and tachypneic during a prolonged crying spell while having his diaper changed. The mother reports he looked dusky around the lips and seemed limp briefly; the episode lasted several minutes and improved after she instinctively held him against her chest with his knees drawn up. He has had brief bluish spells with feeding and crying over the past month that his parents attributed to breath-holding. He was born at term; a soft murmur was noted at the newborn visit, but the family missed the scheduled cardiology follow-up. He feeds adequately but tires and sweats during feeds, and his growth has been at the lower percentiles. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. Immunizations are up to date. There is no family history of congenital heart disease, but a maternal cousin has a chromosomal disorder. He lives with both parents and has no sick contacts.
cases.exam: Irritable, mildly cyanotic infant with perioral and acrocyanosis, calmer when held with knees flexed. Anterior fontanelle soft. No respiratory distress at rest aside from tachypnea. Cardiac exam reveals a normal S1, a single loud S2, and a harsh grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border; the murmur becomes softer during the most cyanotic moments. No gallop or rub. Lungs are clear with good air entry. Abdomen soft, liver edge 1 cm below the costal margin. Mild central cyanosis of the lips and nail beds; clubbing is early. Pulses are 2+ and symmetric. No hepatomegaly beyond noted, no edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The infant is acutely cyanotic and irritable. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Place the infant supine and administer intravenous furosemide
Give intravenous nitroprusside to reduce afterload in this clinical context
Bring the knees to the chest, give supplemental oxygen,
Administer nebulized albuterol for presumed bronchospasm
Start an isoproterenol infusion to increase heart rate
stages.explanation: This is a hypercyanotic (tet) spell. First-line treatment is the knee-to-chest position, which raises systemic vascular resistance and decreases right-to-left shunting across the ventricular septal defect, plus supplemental oxygen and a calm environment to break the cycle of crying, tachypnea, and worsening infundibular spasm. Supine positioning and furosemide do not address the shunt and can worsen hypoxemia. Isoproterenol is contraindicated because beta-agonism increases infundibular contractility and right-to-left shunting.
