A protective infrapatellar strap can help. Symptoms typically resolve as the apophysis fuses with skeletal maturity. Casting is unnecessary and promotes deconditioning. Corticosteroid injection into the tubercle is not recommended — it risks subcutaneous atrophy and tendon weakening without proven benefit. Surgical excision of a persistent ossicle is reserved for the rare patient with refractory symptoms after skeletal maturity, not initial management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What should you counsel the patient and family about the expected course?
options: He must stop all sports permanently to avoid joint destruction with appropriate follow-up
The condition is usually self-limited and resolves with skeletal maturity
This will progress to early osteoarthritis requiring knee replacement with appropriate follow-up
He needs urgent oncology referral given the tubercle changes on x-ray with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Families should be reassured that Osgood-Schlatter disease is benign and self-limited, typically resolving over months to a couple of years as the tibial tubercle apophysis ossifies and fuses. Activity can continue guided by pain (the 'pain is acceptable if it resolves with rest and isn't worsening' principle); complete cessation of sport is not required. A residual painless bony prominence may persist into adulthood but does not cause dysfunction. It does not cause joint destruction or mandate knee replacement. The radiographic fragmentation is expected and benign — there are no oncologic red flags (no mass, lytic lesion, periosteal reaction, night pain, or systemic symptoms), so oncology referral is not warranted.

pearls: Osgood-Schlatter is a traction apophysitis of the patellar tendon insertion at the tibial tubercle, classic in adolescent athletes during the growth spurt.
It is a clinical diagnosis — focal tubercle tenderness reproduced by resisted knee extension; imaging is reserved for atypical or red-flag features.
Manage conservatively with activity modification, ice, NSAIDs, and quadriceps/hamstring stretching; avoid steroid injection.
Red flags (night pain, weight loss, rest pain, a mass) should prompt radiographs to exclude osteosarcoma, which favors the distal femur and proximal tibia.

cases.title: Toddler Who Refuses to Walk
cases.chiefComplaint: Refusal to bear weight on the right leg with fever for 2 days.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because he refuses to stand or walk and cries when his right hip is moved. Two days ago he became fussy and febrile to 39.2 °C at home, and since yesterday he will not bear any weight on the right leg, holding it slightly flexed and turned outward. There is no history of trauma. He had a mild upper respiratory illness about 10 days ago that resolved. He has had decreased appetite and poor oral intake today. He has had no rash, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, or recent travel. Immunizations are up to date. Past medical history is unremarkable with no prior joint or bone infections, sickle cell disease, or immunodeficiency. He takes no medications except acetaminophen given last night. He has no known drug allergies. He lives with his parents and an older sibling and attends daycare. Family history is noncontributory. Birth history was uncomplicated and developmental milestones have been normal; he was walking and running normally until this illness began.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, irritable toddler who cries with examination of the right leg. He holds the right hip in flexion, abduction, and external rotation and resists all passive movement, with marked guarding and pain on the slightest internal rotation or extension. There is no overlying erythema, but the hip region is diffusely tender and warm to the deep examiner. He refuses to bear any weight and will not crawl. The knee, ankle, and contralateral hip are nontender with full painless range of motion. There is no rash or other joint swelling. Capillary refill is brisk and distal pulses are intact. Abdomen is soft. No spinal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a febrile, non–weight-bearing toddler with a painful hip held in flexion, abduction, and external rotation, what is the most appropriate immediate priority?
options: Reassure and discharge with ibuprofen, as this is most likely a viral process
Urgently evaluate for septic arthritis with labs, hip ultrasound,
Begin empiric oral antibiotics and arrange clinic follow-up in 48 hours
Order an MRI of the lumbar spine first to exclude diskitis as the first step today
stages.explanation: A toxic-appearing, febrile toddler refusing to bear weight with the hip held in flexion-abduction-external rotation (the position that maximizes intracapsular volume and minimizes pain) must be evaluated emergently for septic arthritis, a surgical emergency that can destroy the joint within days. The appropriate immediate workup is CBC, ESR, CRP, blood cultures, and a hip ultrasound to detect an effusion, with orthopedics involved early for possible aspiration. Reassurance/discharge is dangerous given the toxic presentation. Oral antibiotics before joint aspiration can obscure cultures and undertreat a surgical infection. Lumbar MRI for diskitis is not the first step when localizing signs point to the hip; diskitis usually causes refusal to walk/sit with back findings rather than a positional, exquisitely painful hip.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL (84% neutrophils)
ESR 62 mm/hr
CRP 9.4 mg/dL
Blood culture: pending
Hip radiograph: widened joint space, no fracture or lytic lesion
Hip ultrasound: moderate right hip joint effusion

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Using the Kocher criteria, how many of the four predictors does this child meet, and what does that imply?
options: All 4 of 4 — very high probability of septic arthritis
0 of 4 — septic arthritis effectively excluded
2 of 4 — intermediate probability, no further action needed
1 of 4 — low probability, observe only in this setting
stages.explanation: The four Kocher criteria for septic arthritis of the hip are: non–weight-bearing, fever >38.5 °C, ESR >40 mm/hr, and WBC >12,000/µL. This child meets all four (refuses to bear weight, Temp 39.4 °C, ESR 62, WBC 18.6k), which predicts a probability of septic arthritis exceeding 99%. (CRP >2 mg/dL is an additional strong independent predictor and is markedly elevated here at 9.4.) Meeting all four mandates urgent definitive evaluation, not observation. The other options misstate the count: he clearly satisfies every criterion, so 'intermediate,' 'low,' or 'excluded' interpretations are incorrect and would dangerously underestimate his risk.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Repeat the CRP in 24 hours to assess the trend as the primary diagnosis
Perform urgent ultrasound-guided arthrocentesis of the hip with synovial fluid analysis
Start IV antibiotics and observe for clinical improvement as the primary diagnosis
Obtain an MRI with contrast before any intervention as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: With all Kocher criteria met and a confirmed effusion, the definitive diagnostic step is urgent arthrocentesis (ultrasound-guided for the deep hip joint) before antibiotics, sending synovial fluid for cell count, Gram stain, and culture. Septic fluid typically shows WBC >50,000/µL with neutrophil predominance, low glucose, and possibly organisms on Gram stain. Aspiration both confirms the diagnosis and guides therapy, and the hip in particular usually requires surgical drainage. MRI delays definitive care and is not required to diagnose a clinically obvious septic hip. Repeating CRP wastes critical time. Starting antibiotics before obtaining joint fluid can sterilize cultures and compromise pathogen identification; aspiration should precede or immediately accompany antibiotic initiation.

stages.label: SYNOVIAL FLUID
stages.content:
Synovial fluid WBC 88,000/µL (92% neutrophils)
Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in clusters
Synovial glucose: low relative to serum
Crystals: none
Fluid culture: pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Oral cephalexin and outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection and physical therapy as initial therapy
NSAIDs and observation, treating as transient synovitis as initial therapy
Urgent surgical drainage/irrigation of the hip plus empiric IV antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus
stages.explanation: Confirmed septic arthritis of the hip is an orthopedic emergency requiring urgent surgical drainage (arthrotomy or arthroscopic irrigation) because the hip is a deep joint where retained purulent fluid and proteolytic enzymes rapidly destroy cartilage and threaten the femoral head blood supply. Empiric IV antibiotics targeting the most common pathogen, S. aureus (including MRSA coverage where prevalent, e.g., vancomycin or clindamycin), are started after fluid is obtained and narrowed by culture; Kingella kingae is also common in this age group. Intra-articular steroids are contraindicated in infection. Oral antibiotics alone are inadequate for an undrained septic hip. Treating as transient synovitis with NSAIDs would be a critical error given toxic appearance, all Kocher criteria positive, and frankly purulent synovial fluid.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most serious long-term complication if treatment of a septic hip is delayed?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
Transient anemia that resolves spontaneously as the leading concern
Reversible muscle wasting that fully recovers with physical therapy
A self-limited reactive arthritis with no sequelae as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Delayed drainage of a septic hip allows pressure from purulent fluid and bacterial/inflammatory proteolytic enzymes to compromise the blood supply to the femoral head, producing avascular necrosis, along with cartilage destruction, growth-plate (physeal) damage, leg-length discrepancy, and chondrolysis — permanent, function-limiting sequelae that make early surgical drainage time-critical. Muscle wasting can occur from disuse but is not the principal feared outcome and does not capture the irreversible joint damage. A benign self-limited reactive arthritis describes a different, post-infectious entity and would dangerously trivialize a true pyogenic joint infection. Transient anemia is not a meaningful long-term complication of septic arthritis. Recognizing the avascular-necrosis risk reinforces why the hip is drained emergently rather than observed.

cases.diagnosis: Septic arthritis of the right hip (likely Staphylococcus aureus)
pearls: A febrile toddler refusing to bear weight with the hip held flexed/abducted/externally rotated is septic arthritis until proven otherwise — a surgical emergency.
Kocher criteria: non–weight-bearing, fever >38.5 °C, ESR >40, WBC >12k; 4/4 confers >99% probability. Elevated CRP (>2) is a strong additional predictor.
Arthrocentesis is diagnostic (synovial WBC typically >50,000 with neutrophil predominance) and should precede antibiotics; obtain fluid for Gram stain and culture.
Septic hip requires urgent surgical drainage plus empiric IV anti-staphylococcal antibiotics; cover S. aureus (including MRSA) and consider Kingella kingae in young children.

cases.title: Limping Boy After a Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: Limping and complaining of left hip and thigh pain for 2 days.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is brought to clinic with a 2-day history of limping and intermittent left hip and knee pain. About a week ago he had a runny nose, mild cough, and low-grade fevers that have since resolved. He is now afebrile at home and remains playful, eating well and engaging in his usual activities, though he favors the left leg and occasionally asks to be carried. The pain seems mild and he can walk, albeit with a limp. There is no history of trauma, no rash, no night sweats, and no weight loss. Immunizations are up to date. Past medical history is unremarkable, with no prior joint problems, hemoglobinopathy, or recent antibiotic use. He takes no regular medications; his parents gave one dose of ibuprofen yesterday with apparent improvement. He has no known allergies. He attends kindergarten where several classmates recently had colds. Family history is noncontributory. Developmental milestones have been normal and he was previously active and well, running and climbing without difficulty before this episode.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable, interactive child who is afebrile and in no distress. He walks with a mild antalgic limp favoring the left leg. The left hip has a mildly reduced and slightly uncomfortable range of motion at the extremes of internal rotation and abduction, but he tolerates gentle passive motion and will bear weight and even attempt to run when encouraged. There is no warmth, erythema, or visible swelling over the hip. The log-roll test elicits mild guarding only. The knee, ankle, and contralateral hip are normal. There is no rash, lymphadenopathy, or other joint involvement. Abdomen is soft and nontender; no spinal tenderness or abnormal neurologic findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important initial task in evaluating this limping child?
options: Obtain an MRI of both hips today as the first step today
Immediately start oral antibiotics for presumed bone infection as the first step today
Reassure and discharge without any examination of inflammatory markers
Distinguish a benign self-limited process from septic arthritis using clinical features
stages.explanation: The key initial task in any limping child is to differentiate benign transient synovitis from septic arthritis, because the management and urgency differ dramatically. This child is well-appearing, afebrile, bearing weight, and has a recent viral prodrome — features favoring transient synovitis — but septic arthritis can present subtly, so risk stratification (Kocher criteria, selective labs) is appropriate. Starting empiric antibiotics is premature without evidence of infection and would obscure cultures if a septic joint were later suspected. MRI of both hips is not the first-line study; ultrasound and labs come first. Reflexive reassurance without any assessment risks missing a septic hip, the can't-miss diagnosis, so some objective evaluation is warranted before final disposition.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 8.9 ×10³/µL (normal differential)
ESR 18 mm/hr
CRP 0.6 mg/dL
Hip radiograph: normal, no fracture or bony lesion
Hip ultrasound: small left hip joint effusion

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: How do these findings affect the likelihood of septic arthritis using the Kocher criteria?
options: He meets 3 of 4 criteria, so septic arthritis is highly likely in this setting
He meets 0 of 4 criteria, making septic arthritis very unlikely
Normal labs cannot lower the probability of a septic joint at all in this setting
The small effusion on ultrasound confirms septic arthritis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: He satisfies none of the four Kocher predictors: he is bearing weight, afebrile (37.1 °C, below 38.5), ESR is 18 (below 40), and WBC is 8.9k (below 12k); CRP is also low at 0.6. With 0/4 criteria, the predicted probability of septic arthritis is very low (under ~2%), and transient (toxic) synovitis — a benign post-viral reactive effusion — is the most likely diagnosis. A small effusion on ultrasound is expected in transient synovitis and does NOT distinguish it from a septic joint; effusion alone is non-specific. While no rule is perfect, a reassuring clinical picture combined with normal inflammatory markers substantially lowers the probability and supports observation rather than aspiration.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most reliably favors transient synovitis over septic arthritis in this child?
options: Well appearance, afebrile status, ability to bear weight,
Pain referred to the knee as the primary diagnosis
History of a recent viral upper respiratory infection alone
Presence of a joint effusion on ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The combination of a nontoxic, well-appearing child who is afebrile, able to bear weight, and has normal/low WBC, ESR, and CRP most reliably favors transient synovitis. Each of these maps to an absent Kocher criterion, and together they make a septic hip very unlikely. An effusion on ultrasound is non-specific — it occurs in both conditions — so it cannot distinguish them. Knee-referred pain is common to both hip processes (obturator nerve referral) and is not discriminating. A viral prodrome is suggestive but, by itself, is insufficient because children with septic arthritis may also report recent illness; the discriminating power comes from the overall benign clinical and laboratory profile.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management and follow-up plan?
options: Schedule a bone biopsy to exclude malignancy as initial therapy
Rest, NSAIDs for comfort,
Admit for IV antibiotics and surgical hip drainage as initial therapy
Begin a prolonged oral steroid taper as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Transient synovitis is self-limited and managed supportively: relative rest, NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) for analgesia, and reassurance, with close follow-up in 24–48 hours and clear return precautions (worsening pain, fever, refusal to bear weight). Symptoms typically resolve within several days up to a week or two. Crucially, the child should be re-evaluated to confirm improvement, since persistent or worsening symptoms would prompt reconsideration of septic arthritis or other diagnoses. IV antibiotics and surgical drainage are reserved for septic arthritis, which is very unlikely here. Systemic steroids are not indicated. A bone biopsy/malignancy workup is unwarranted absent red flags (night pain, weight loss, mass, abnormal labs), none of which are present.

cases.diagnosis: Transient (toxic) synovitis of the left hip
pearls: Transient synovitis is the most common cause of acute hip pain/limp in young children and classically follows a viral illness in a well-appearing, afebrile child.
Use the Kocher criteria to risk-stratify against septic arthritis: this child meeting 0/4 with a low CRP makes a septic hip very unlikely.
A joint effusion on ultrasound is non-specific — it appears in both transient synovitis and septic arthritis, so it cannot distinguish them.
Manage with rest and NSAIDs, expect resolution within days to a couple of weeks, and arrange close follow-up with return precautions for fever or inability to bear weight.

cases.title: Infant With Fever Then a Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: Pink rash appeared as a 3-day high fever finally broke.
cases.history: An 11-month-old girl is brought to clinic because a rash appeared this morning just as her fever resolved. For the past 3 days she had high fevers up to 39.7 °C that responded to acetaminophen but recurred; during the febrile days she was a bit fussy but continued to take liquids and had no respiratory distress. This morning the fever broke, and shortly afterward her mother noticed a fine pink rash on the trunk and neck. The child is now alert, smiling, and feeding well. There was no cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, vomiting, diarrhea, or known sick contacts beyond a busy daycare. She has had no seizures. Immunizations are up to date for age (she has not yet received MMR, which is given at 12 months). She takes no medications other than the acetaminophen used during the fever, and she has no allergies. She lives with her parents and attends daycare. Family history is unremarkable. Birth history was uncomplicated, growth and development are normal, and she had been entirely well prior to this febrile illness.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, smiling, well-hydrated infant in no distress. Skin shows numerous blanching, rose-pink macules and maculopapules, 2–5 mm, concentrated on the trunk and neck with some spread to the proximal extremities and face; lesions are non-pruritic and there are no vesicles, pustules, or petechiae. The oropharynx is mildly erythematous without exudate or Koplik spots; a few small lymph nodes are palpable at the occiput and posterior cervical chain. Tympanic membranes are normal. The anterior fontanelle is soft and flat. Lungs are clear and the heart has a normal rhythm without murmur. The abdomen is soft with no hepatosplenomegaly. She is neurologically appropriate and consolable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which classic feature of this illness is best illustrated by the timing of the rash?
options: Several days of high fever followed by defervescence,
Vesicular rash in multiple stages appearing on day 1 as the first step today
Rash and fever peak simultaneously at illness onset as the first step today
Rash that begins on the face and spreads downward while fever worsens
stages.explanation: Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum, HHV-6) is defined by 3–5 days of high fever in a relatively well-appearing infant, followed by abrupt defervescence and the emergence of a blanching rose-pink maculopapular rash on the trunk and neck after the fever breaks — exactly the sequence here. Simultaneous peak of rash and high fever is more typical of other exanthems. A cephalocaudal rash worsening with persistent fever and prominent cough/coryza/conjunctivitis describes measles. A vesicular rash in multiple simultaneous stages on day 1 describes varicella. The hallmark 'fever first, then rash as fever resolves' pattern is the single most distinguishing feature of roseola.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic approach for this infant?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture given the rash as the primary diagnosis
Obtain blood cultures and start empiric antibiotics in this clinical context
Send HHV-6 PCR and serologies to confirm before management
Diagnose clinically based on the classic febrile-then-rash pattern
stages.explanation: Roseola is a clinical diagnosis recognized by the pathognomonic sequence of high fever for several days in a well infant, followed by a characteristic rash appearing as the fever defervesces. Laboratory confirmation (HHV-6 PCR/serology) is not needed in a well-appearing child and is reserved for atypical or severe cases. Because the child is well-appearing and the rash is classic, blood cultures and empiric antibiotics are unnecessary and would represent overtreatment of a benign viral illness. A lumbar puncture is not indicated in a smiling, neurologically normal infant with a benign blanching exanthem and no meningeal signs; the rash is non-petechiae and reassuring.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is most classically associated with this infection?
options: Febrile seizures during the high-fever phase
Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis
Coronary artery aneurysms
Orchitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: HHV-6 is one of the most common causes of febrile seizures in infancy, occurring during the high-fever phase before the rash appears; these are typically simple febrile seizures with a benign prognosis. Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis is a rare, fatal late complication of measles, not roseola. Orchitis is a complication of mumps. Coronary artery aneurysms are the feared complication of Kawasaki disease (which can mimic febrile exanthems but features prolonged fever ≥5 days, conjunctival injection, mucositis, extremity changes, and lymphadenopathy). Recognizing that roseola accounts for many infantile febrile seizures helps reassure families that the seizure, while frightening, is usually benign.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management and counseling?
options: Isolate the child for 21 days and report to public health as initial therapy
Start a 10-day course of amoxicillin as initial therapy
Prescribe oral acyclovir to shorten the illness as initial therapy
Supportive care with antipyretics and hydration
stages.explanation: Roseola is benign and self-limited; management is supportive with antipyretics for comfort and adequate hydration. The faint rose-pink rash typically fades within 1–2 days and requires no treatment, and families should be reassured. Antivirals such as acyclovir are not indicated in healthy immunocompetent infants (HHV-6 antivirals are reserved for severe disease in immunocompromised patients). Antibiotics like amoxicillin have no role in a viral exanthem and could cause harm. Prolonged isolation and public-health reporting are not required for roseola, which is mildly contagious and not a reportable disease — unlike measles, which is reportable and demands airborne isolation.

pearls: Roseola = 3–5 days of high fever in a well-appearing infant, then a rose-pink truncal maculopapular rash that erupts as the fever breaks.
It is a leading cause of simple febrile seizures in infancy during the high-fever phase.
Diagnosis is clinical; management is supportive with antipyretics and hydration — no antibiotics or antivirals in healthy infants.
The 'fever first, rash after defervescence' sequence distinguishes roseola from measles (cephalocaudal rash with worsening fever) and varicella (vesicles in multiple stages).

cases.title: Unvaccinated Child With Fever and a Spreading Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: High fever with cough, red eyes, and a rash spreading from the face downward.
cases.history: A 4-year-old unvaccinated boy presents with 4 days of high fever, worsening dry cough, profuse runny nose, and red, watery eyes that are sensitive to light. Two days ago his parents noticed tiny white spots inside his cheeks, and today a blotchy red rash began on his face and behind the ears and is now spreading down the neck and trunk. He appears miserable, with reduced appetite and fatigue. The family recently returned from international travel to a region with a measles outbreak. He has received no childhood vaccinations because of parental vaccine hesitancy. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He lives with his parents and a 6-month-old sibling who is too young to be fully immunized. There is no immunodeficiency in the family. Birth and developmental history are normal. He attends a small preschool. Review of systems is otherwise negative for vomiting, diarrhea, neck stiffness, or difficulty breathing, though the parents note the cough has been quite prominent.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, febrile child. There is marked conjunctival injection with watery discharge and photophobia, copious clear rhinorrhea, and a frequent barky-sounding dry cough. On the buccal mucosa opposite the lower molars are several tiny bluish-white papules on an erythematous base (Koplik spots). An erythematous, blanching, blotchy maculopapular rash is present on the face, behind the ears, and over the upper neck and trunk, becoming confluent on the face and spreading caudally. There is mild cervical lymphadenopathy. The oropharynx is erythematous without tonsillar exudate. Lungs are clear without focal findings. There is no neck stiffness, no hepatosplenomegaly, and the neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding is essentially pathognomonic for this diagnosis?
options: Koplik spots — tiny bluish-white papules on the buccal mucosa
A slapped-cheek facial erythema as the first step today
Vesicles in multiple stages of healing in this clinical context
Strawberry tongue with circumoral pallor in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Koplik spots — small bluish-white papules on an erythematous base on the buccal mucosa opposite the molars — are essentially pathognomonic for measles and appear 1–2 days before the exanthem, exactly as described. Combined with the classic prodrome of cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis (the 'three C's') plus high fever in an unvaccinated, recently traveled child, this is measles. A strawberry tongue with circumoral pallor characterizes scarlet fever. Slapped-cheek erythema is the hallmark of erythema infectiosum (parvovirus B19). Vesicles in multiple stages of healing describe varicella. Recognizing Koplik spots allows early diagnosis and prompt public-health action before the rash even fully develops.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory testing and immediate public-health action?
options: No testing needed; simply treat symptomatically and send home without precautions as the primary diagnosis
Obtain measles-specific IgM serology and/or RT-PCR , place the patient in airborne isolation,
Order a throat culture for group A streptococcus and start penicillin as the primary diagnosis
Check a parvovirus B19 IgM and reassure the family as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Measles is a nationally notifiable disease requiring rapid laboratory confirmation with measles-specific IgM serology and/or RT-PCR (nasopharyngeal/throat swab, with urine as an additional source), immediate airborne isolation (negative-pressure room, N95), and prompt notification of public health to enable contact tracing and post-exposure prophylaxis. Sending the patient home without precautions risks widespread transmission, as measles is among the most contagious pathogens. A strep throat workup and penicillin address scarlet fever, not this viral exanthem. Parvovirus B19 testing is the workup for fifth disease, which presents with slapped cheeks and a lacy rash rather than Koplik spots and the three C's; it is the wrong diagnosis here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond supportive care, which specific intervention is recommended for children with measles?
options: Empiric ganciclovir as initial therapy
Vitamin A supplementation
High-dose corticosteroids
Routine systemic antibiotics for all patients
stages.explanation: Vitamin A is recommended for children with measles because it reduces measles-associated morbidity and mortality, particularly the risk of severe disease and complications; the WHO and AAP recommend age-based dosing (typically two daily doses). Supportive care (antipyretics, hydration) is the mainstay otherwise. Corticosteroids are not part of routine measles management. Ganciclovir treats certain herpesvirus infections (e.g., CMV), not measles. Routine systemic antibiotics are not given to all patients; antibiotics are reserved for documented bacterial superinfections such as otitis media or pneumonia, which are common complications. Thus vitamin A is the specific evidence-based adjunct distinguishing measles management from generic viral supportive care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most common serious complication of measles and the leading cause of measles-related death in young children?
options: Acute glomerulonephritis
Pneumonia
Coronary artery aneurysm
Orchitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Pneumonia — either primary measles (giant-cell) pneumonia or secondary bacterial superinfection — is the most common serious complication and the leading cause of measles-related death in young children. Other recognized complications include otitis media (the most common overall), acute encephalitis, and the rare, fatal late complication subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE). Orchitis is a complication of mumps, not measles. Coronary artery aneurysms are characteristic of Kawasaki disease. Post-streptococcal acute glomerulonephritis follows group A streptococcal infection (e.g., after scarlet fever or impetigo), not measles. Anticipating pneumonia guides monitoring for respiratory deterioration and prompt treatment of bacterial superinfection.

pearls: Measles classically presents with the three C's — cough, coryza, conjunctivitis — plus high fever, then a cephalocaudal maculopapular rash beginning on the face.
Koplik spots (bluish-white buccal papules) are pathognomonic and precede the rash by 1–2 days.
Confirm with measles IgM/RT-PCR, institute airborne isolation, and notify public health; give vitamin A to reduce morbidity and mortality.
Pneumonia is the leading cause of measles death; watch for otitis media, encephalitis, and the rare late SSPE.

cases.title: Itchy Spots in Many Stages
cases.chiefComplaint: Itchy spots that started on the trunk and are now all over.
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl is brought to clinic with 2 days of an intensely itchy rash. It began on the trunk and scalp as a few red spots and has rapidly spread to the face, arms, and legs; some spots have turned into little fluid-filled blisters while new red bumps continue to appear. She had a low-grade fever and felt tired and irritable a day before the rash started. She is otherwise eating and drinking. There is no respiratory distress, neck stiffness, or lethargy. A classmate had a similar rash about 2 weeks ago. She did not receive the varicella vaccine because the family fell behind on the schedule. Past medical history is unremarkable; she takes no medications and has no allergies. She lives with her parents and a healthy older brother; her mother is in the first trimester of pregnancy. Family history is noncontributory and there is no immunodeficiency. Birth and developmental history are normal. The parents are scratching their heads about whether she can return to school.
cases.exam: Well-appearing but uncomfortable, frequently scratching. The skin shows a generalized rash with lesions in MULTIPLE simultaneous stages: erythematous macules, raised papules, tense clear vesicles on an erythematous base ('dewdrops on a rose petal'), and several crusted lesions. The rash is concentrated on the trunk and face with centripetal distribution and includes a few lesions on the scalp and oral mucosa. There are no purpura, no target lesions, and no signs of secondary bacterial infection (no surrounding cellulitis, warmth, or purulence). Mucous membranes are moist. The neck is supple. Lungs are clear, and there is no respiratory distress. The neurologic exam is normal and she is well hydrated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of the rash is most characteristic of the likely diagnosis?
options: Sandpaper-textured rash with skin desquamation as the first step today
Lesions present in multiple stages simultaneously
A uniform rash where all lesions are at the same stage as the first step today
A rash that spares the trunk and involves only the palms and soles
stages.explanation: Varicella (chickenpox) is characterized by lesions in multiple simultaneous stages — macules, papules, vesicles ('dewdrops on a rose petal'), and crusts coexisting — appearing in successive crops, with a centripetal (trunk-predominant) distribution and frequent involvement of the scalp and mucosa. A monomorphic rash with all lesions at the same stage is more typical of smallpox or other exanthems, not varicella. A palm-and-sole–predominant, trunk-sparing rash suggests hand-foot-mouth disease or secondary syphilis. A sandpaper rash with desquamation describes scarlet fever. The pleomorphic, multi-stage appearance is the single most distinguishing clue for varicella.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate way to confirm the diagnosis in this child?
options: Skin biopsy with electron microscopy as the primary diagnosis
Clinical diagnosis based on the classic appearance
Patch testing for contact allergens as the primary diagnosis
Blood culture and lumbar puncture as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Varicella is typically a clinical diagnosis based on the pathognomonic pleomorphic rash with lesions in multiple stages in an exposed, unvaccinated child. Laboratory confirmation is reserved for atypical, severe, or immunocompromised cases and is best done with PCR of vesicle fluid (more sensitive than direct fluorescent antibody or Tzanck smear). Skin biopsy with electron microscopy is unnecessary for routine diagnosis. Blood culture and lumbar puncture are not indicated in a well-appearing child without signs of bacteremia or meningitis. Patch testing evaluates allergic contact dermatitis, which produces an eczematous, not vesicular-cropping, eruption and is unrelated to this presentation. Recognizing the classic picture avoids unnecessary testing.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this otherwise healthy child?
options: Oral acyclovir is mandatory for all healthy children with varicella as initial therapy
Supportive care — antihistamines/oatmeal baths for itch, antipyretics, keep nails trimmed; avoid aspirin
Topical and oral antibiotics for all lesions as initial therapy
Systemic corticosteroids to speed resolution as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For an otherwise healthy child, varicella is managed supportively: antipruritics (oral antihistamines, calamine, oatmeal baths), antipyretics, hydration, and keeping fingernails trimmed to reduce scratching and secondary bacterial infection. Aspirin must be avoided because of the risk of Reye syndrome. Routine oral acyclovir is NOT mandatory for healthy young children with uncomplicated varicella; antivirals are recommended for those at higher risk of severe disease (e.g., older than 12, chronic skin/lung disease, immunocompromised, or secondary household cases). Systemic corticosteroids are not indicated and may worsen the infection. Antibiotics are reserved for documented bacterial superinfection (e.g., impetiginized lesions or cellulitis), not prophylaxis of all lesions.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: The child's mother is in her first trimester of pregnancy and reports no history of chickenpox or vaccination. What is the most appropriate advice regarding her exposure?
options: Determine her immunity
She should start high-dose oral aspirin prophylactically as the immediate next step
She should receive the varicella vaccine immediately as the immediate next step
No action is needed because adults cannot catch chickenpox as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A susceptible pregnant woman exposed to varicella is at risk for severe maternal varicella and, depending on timing, congenital varicella syndrome or neonatal varicella. The correct step is to assess her immunity (history or VZV IgG serology); if susceptible, she should promptly contact her obstetrician about varicella-zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) for post-exposure prophylaxis. The live-attenuated varicella vaccine is contraindicated during pregnancy, so immediate vaccination is wrong. Claiming adults cannot be infected is false and dangerous — non-immune adults are susceptible and often have more severe disease. Aspirin has no preventive role and is hazardous. Prompt obstetric coordination for VZIG is the key protective action.

pearls: Varicella's hallmark is a pruritic, centripetal rash with lesions in multiple stages simultaneously — 'dewdrops on a rose petal.'
Diagnosis is clinical; reserve PCR of vesicle fluid for atypical or severe/immunocompromised cases.
Healthy children get supportive care; avoid aspirin (Reye syndrome). Reserve acyclovir for higher-risk patients.
A susceptible exposed pregnant woman needs immunity assessment and possible VZIG — the live vaccine is contraindicated in pregnancy.

cases.title: Sore Throat Followed by a Rough Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever and sore throat for 2 days, now with a widespread rough red rash.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy presents with 2 days of fever, a painful sore throat, and headache, and since this morning a diffuse red rash that his mother says feels 'like sandpaper.' The rash started on the neck and chest and spread to the trunk and extremities, sparing the area around his mouth. He has had no cough, runny nose, or red eyes, and no recent travel. Several children in his class have had strep throat. He had decreased appetite but is drinking. There is no joint pain, abdominal pain, or dark urine. Immunizations are up to date. Past medical history is unremarkable; he has had a couple of episodes of strep throat in the past, both treated with antibiotics. He takes no daily medications and has no known drug allergies (he has tolerated amoxicillin previously). He lives with his parents and a younger sister and attends elementary school. Family history is noncontributory. Birth and developmental history are normal, and he was entirely well until this illness began.
cases.exam: Febrile, mildly uncomfortable but nontoxic. The pharynx is markedly erythematous with enlarged tonsils bearing patchy white exudate; there are tender, enlarged anterior cervical lymph nodes. The tongue initially shows a white coating with hyperemic papillae and later appears beefy-red ('strawberry tongue'). The skin has a diffuse, finely papular, blanching erythematous rash with a rough sandpaper texture, accentuated in skin folds (linear petechiae in the antecubital creases — Pastia's lines), with notable circumoral pallor (perioral sparing). There is no conjunctival injection, no cough, and no rhinorrhea. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and nontender without hepatosplenomegaly. There is no joint swelling and the neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings best supports the suspected diagnosis?
options: Sandpaper rash with circumoral pallor, strawberry tongue, Pastia's lines,
Vesicles in multiple stages with a centripetal distribution as the first step today
Cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and Koplik spots as the first step today
Slapped cheeks followed by a lacy rash on the extremities as the first step today
stages.explanation: Scarlet fever is caused by erythrogenic (pyrogenic) exotoxin–producing group A Streptococcus and presents with exudative pharyngitis plus a fine, sandpaper-textured erythematous rash, circumoral (perioral) pallor, strawberry tongue, and Pastia's lines (linear petechiae in skin folds) — exactly this constellation. Cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and Koplik spots describe measles. Vesicles in multiple stages with centripetal spread describe varicella. Slapped cheeks followed by a lacy reticular rash describe erythema infectiosum (parvovirus B19). The combination of streptococcal pharyngitis findings with the characteristic toxin-mediated rash and oral signs is what defines scarlet fever.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Order antistreptolysin O (ASO) titers to confirm acute infection as the primary diagnosis
Perform a rapid antigen detection test for group A Streptococcus, with throat culture backup if negative
Empirically treat without testing because the rash is diagnostic enough as the primary diagnosis
Obtain blood cultures and start IV antibiotics as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The standard confirmatory step is a rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for group A Streptococcus from a throat swab; because RADT sensitivity is imperfect in children, a negative result should be backed up by throat culture. Although the clinical picture is highly suggestive, microbiologic confirmation is recommended before antibiotics to ensure appropriate treatment and antibiotic stewardship. ASO titers reflect a prior immune response and rise over weeks, so they confirm recent — not acute — infection and are used for post-streptococcal sequelae, not initial diagnosis. Blood cultures and IV antibiotics are unnecessary in this nontoxic child with localized pharyngitis; oral therapy is appropriate once GAS is confirmed.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Rapid antigen detection test (throat swab): positive for group A Streptococcus
Throat culture: pending (confirmatory)
No leukocytosis workup indicated for nontoxic outpatient

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment for this condition (assuming no penicillin allergy)?
options: Topical mupirocin to the rash as initial therapy
A 5-day course of azithromycin as initial therapy
Oral penicillin V or amoxicillin for a 10-day course
Supportive care only, since the illness is self-limited
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for group A streptococcal pharyngitis/scarlet fever in a non–penicillin-allergic child is a full 10-day course of oral penicillin V or amoxicillin (a single dose of intramuscular benzathine penicillin G is an alternative). Completing the full course is essential to eradicate the organism and prevent acute rheumatic fever. Azithromycin/macrolides are reserved for true penicillin allergy because of growing macrolide resistance, so it is not first-line here. Topical mupirocin treats localized impetigo, not pharyngitis or the systemic toxin-mediated rash. Supportive care alone is inadequate — antibiotics are needed primarily to prevent rheumatic fever and to shorten illness and reduce transmission.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Timely antibiotic treatment most reliably prevents which complication of group A streptococcal infection?
options: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Acute rheumatic fever
Coronary artery aneurysms
stages.explanation: Prompt antibiotic treatment of group A streptococcal pharyngitis reliably prevents acute rheumatic fever, the immune-mediated sequela that can cause carditis and permanent valvular damage; this is the principal reason to treat. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is also a non-suppurative sequela, but antibiotic therapy does NOT reliably prevent it — its occurrence depends on nephritogenic strains regardless of treatment. Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis is a late complication of measles, unrelated to streptococcus. Coronary artery aneurysms are a feature of Kawasaki disease. Thus, while both rheumatic fever and glomerulonephritis follow GAS infection, only rheumatic fever is reliably prevented by timely antibiotics — the key teaching point.

cases.diagnosis: Scarlet fever (group A streptococcal pharyngitis with toxin-mediated rash)
pearls: Scarlet fever = GAS exudative pharyngitis plus a sandpaper rash, circumoral pallor, strawberry tongue, and Pastia's lines.
Confirm with a rapid strep test; back up a negative RADT with throat culture in children.
Treat with a 10-day course of penicillin V or amoxicillin (macrolides only for true penicillin allergy).
Antibiotics reliably prevent acute rheumatic fever but do not reliably prevent post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

cases.title: Bright Red Cheeks and a Lacy Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: Bright red cheeks for a day, now with a lacy rash on the arms.
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl is brought to clinic because yesterday her cheeks became strikingly red, as if she had been slapped, and today a pink, lacy, net-like rash appeared on her arms and thighs. About a week ago she had mild cold symptoms — a runny nose, low-grade fever, and a slight headache — that have since resolved, and she now feels well. The facial redness spares the area around the mouth. The rash seems to come and go and looks more pronounced when she is warm or after a bath. She has no joint pain, no difficulty breathing, and is eating and playing normally. Immunizations are up to date. Past medical history is unremarkable, with no chronic illnesses, no hemolytic anemia, and no immunodeficiency. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She attends first grade, where a few classmates have had similar red cheeks. She lives with her parents; her mother is not pregnant. Family history is noncontributory. Birth and developmental history are normal, and she has otherwise been thriving.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful, afebrile child in no distress. There is intense confluent erythema over both cheeks with circumoral (perioral) sparing, giving a 'slapped-cheek' appearance. On the extensor surfaces of the arms and the thighs there is a reticular (lacy), blanching, pink maculopapular rash. There are no vesicles, petechiae, or mucosal lesions. The oropharynx is unremarkable without exudate or strawberry tongue. There is no conjunctival injection or significant lymphadenopathy. Joints show no swelling, warmth, or tenderness with full range of motion. Lungs are clear and the abdomen is soft without hepatosplenomegaly. The neurologic exam is normal, and she is well hydrated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which sequence of rash findings is most characteristic of the likely diagnosis?
options: High fever for 3 days, then a rose-pink truncal rash as fever breaks
Cephalocaudal blotchy rash with Koplik spots as the first step today
A slapped-cheek facial erythema followed by a lacy, reticular rash on the extremities
Sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue after a sore throat as the first step today
stages.explanation: Erythema infectiosum (fifth disease, parvovirus B19) classically begins with bright 'slapped-cheek' facial erythema (with perioral sparing), followed a day or two later by a lacy, reticular rash on the extensor surfaces of the extremities that waxes and wanes with warmth — exactly the pattern here. High fever for 3 days then a truncal rash as the fever breaks describes roseola. A cephalocaudal blotchy rash with Koplik spots describes measles. A sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue after pharyngitis describes scarlet fever. The biphasic slapped-cheek-then-lacy-rash evolution is the distinguishing hallmark of fifth disease.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic approach in this well-appearing child?
options: Perform a skin biopsy of the cheek as the primary diagnosis
Diagnose clinically based on the classic rash
Order parvovirus B19 PCR and IgM/IgG on every child to confirm in this clinical context
Obtain a throat swab for group A Streptococcus as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Fifth disease is a clinical diagnosis in a healthy child based on the characteristic slapped-cheek and subsequent lacy reticular rash; routine serologic or PCR testing is unnecessary. Targeted testing (B19 IgM/IgG or PCR) is reserved for specific situations — pregnant women, immunocompromised patients, or those with hemolytic anemia at risk for aplastic crisis — not for the typical well child. A skin biopsy is not indicated for this benign exanthem. A throat swab for group A Streptococcus would be appropriate if scarlet fever were suspected (sandpaper rash, exudative pharyngitis, strawberry tongue), but those features are absent here, so it does not fit this presentation.

stages.label: INFECTION CONTROL
stages.question: Regarding contagiousness, what should you tell the family about school attendance now that the rash has appeared?
options: She must be isolated for 21 days because she is now most contagious in this setting
By the time the characteristic rash appears she is generally no longer contagious
She needs airborne isolation and public-health reporting as the immediate next step
She should receive a course of oral acyclovir before returning before discharge planning
stages.explanation: With parvovirus B19, the patient is most contagious during the viremic prodrome (the earlier nonspecific 'cold' phase), and by the time the immune-mediated slapped-cheek and lacy rashes appear, the child is generally no longer infectious and may attend school. Prolonged 21-day isolation is unnecessary and incorrect. Acyclovir treats herpesviruses and has no role in parvovirus B19. Airborne isolation and public-health reporting are required for measles, not fifth disease. The key counseling point is that once the rash is visible the transmissible phase has largely passed, which reassures families and avoids needless school exclusion.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: In which patient is parvovirus B19 infection most likely to cause a dangerous transient aplastic crisis?
options: A child with a chronic hemolytic anemia such as sickle cell disease or hereditary spherocytosis
A healthy child like this patient as the leading concern
A child with a peanut allergy as the leading concern
A child who has completed all vaccinations as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Parvovirus B19 infects and transiently halts erythroid progenitor cells. In a healthy child with a normal red-cell lifespan this causes only a brief, clinically silent dip. But in patients with chronic hemolytic anemia (e.g., sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis), whose red cells already have a shortened lifespan and high turnover, the abrupt cessation of erythropoiesis precipitates a transient aplastic crisis with severe, life-threatening anemia. A fully vaccinated child is not specifically protected (there is no routine B19 vaccine) but baseline risk remains low without hemolysis. A peanut allergy is unrelated to erythropoiesis. Recognizing the hemolytic-anemia population is critical for anticipating aplastic crisis.

pearls: Fifth disease starts with a 'slapped-cheek' facial erythema, then a lacy reticular rash on the extremities that recurs with warmth.
It is a clinical diagnosis in healthy children; reserve B19 serology/PCR for pregnant, immunocompromised, or hemolytic-anemia patients.
Contagiousness peaks during the prodrome — once the rash appears the child is generally no longer infectious and can attend school.
Parvovirus B19 causes transient aplastic crisis in chronic hemolytic anemias (e.g., sickle cell) and can cause hydrops fetalis in pregnancy.

cases.title: Swollen Cheeks in an Undervaccinated Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful swelling of both cheeks and jaw with fever for 2 days.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy presents with 2 days of progressively painful swelling over both cheeks and along the jaw angle, along with fever, headache, and malaise. The swelling makes chewing uncomfortable, and he reports the pain worsens when he eats or tastes sour foods. He had a few days of low-grade fever and fatigue beforehand. There is no sore throat, drooling, trismus that limits airway, or difficulty breathing, and no rash. He is only partially vaccinated; the family declined the second MMR dose and his records show just one MMR in early childhood. Past medical history is unremarkable, with no recurrent parotid swelling and no stones. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He lives with his parents and attends school, where another student was recently diagnosed with a similar illness. Family history is noncontributory. Birth and developmental history are normal. The parents are particularly worried because they read that this illness can affect the testicles, though he currently has no scrotal complaints.
cases.exam: Well-hydrated but uncomfortable child. There is bilateral, tender, doughy swelling over the parotid regions that lifts the earlobes outward and obscures the angle of the mandible; the overlying skin is not erythematous or warm. The opening of Stensen's duct (opposite the upper second molar) is mildly erythematous without frank pus. There is no pharyngeal exudate, no peritonsillar bulge, and the tonsils are normal. The neck is supple with no meningismus. The testes are normal in size, nontender, and without swelling or erythema. The abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. Lungs are clear and the neurologic exam is normal. No skin rash is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports the suspected diagnosis over bacterial (suppurative) parotitis?
options: High fever with overlying skin erythema and fluctuance as the first step today
A stone palpable in the submandibular duct as the first step today
Bilateral, tender, non-erythematous parotid swelling without expressible pus, after a viral prodrome in an undervaccinated child
Unilateral swelling with frank pus expressible from Stensen's duct as the first step today
stages.explanation: Mumps classically causes bilateral, tender, non-erythematous parotid swelling that lifts the earlobe and obscures the mandibular angle, preceded by a nonspecific viral prodrome, especially in an undervaccinated child during an outbreak. Bacterial (suppurative) parotitis is typically unilateral with overlying erythema, warmth, fluctuance, and purulent drainage expressible from Stensen's duct — the opposite of this exam. A palpable ductal stone suggests obstructive sialadenitis/sialolithiasis, not mumps. Skin erythema with fluctuance again points to a bacterial abscess. The bilateral, non-suppurative, doughy swelling with a viral prodrome and pain worsened by sour foods is the hallmark of viral parotitis from mumps.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory testing and public-health step?
options: Send a serum amylase only and treat empirically with antibiotics as the primary diagnosis
Obtain mumps RT-PCR and/or mumps IgM serology,
No testing or reporting is needed for parotid swelling as the primary diagnosis
Order a throat culture for group A Streptococcus as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Mumps is a reportable disease. Confirmation is best with RT-PCR from a buccal/parotid (Stensen's duct) swab, ideally collected early, supplemented by mumps IgM serology; the case should be reported to public health to enable outbreak control and contact management. No-testing/no-reporting is incorrect given its reportable status and outbreak implications. A throat culture for group A Streptococcus addresses pharyngitis/scarlet fever, not parotitis. Serum amylase is often elevated in mumps (reflecting salivary gland involvement) and can support the diagnosis, but it is nonspecific and inadequate alone, and empiric antibiotics are not indicated for a viral parotitis without evidence of bacterial superinfection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this child?
options: Supportive care — analgesics/antipyretics, hydration,
Surgical drainage of the parotid glands as initial therapy
A 10-day course of oral amoxicillin as initial therapy
Oral acyclovir to shorten the course as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Mumps is a self-limited viral illness managed supportively: analgesics and antipyretics for pain and fever, adequate hydration, soft diet, and avoidance of sour foods that provoke salivary pain. Because mumps spreads by respiratory droplets, the patient should be isolated (droplet precautions, typically for about 5 days after parotid swelling onset) and contacts assessed for immunization. Amoxicillin is ineffective against a virus and is reserved for documented bacterial superinfection. Surgical drainage is appropriate for a bacterial parotid abscess, not viral parotitis. Acyclovir targets herpesviruses and has no role in mumps. Supportive care plus appropriate isolation and public-health coordination is the correct approach.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The parents ask about the testicular complication they read about. Which statement is most accurate regarding orchitis in mumps?
options: Orchitis occurs only in girls as the leading concern
Orchitis is the most common complication in prepubertal boys and always causes infertility
Orchitis is more common in post-pubertal males, is usually unilateral,
Orchitis is prevented by giving antibiotics during the parotitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Mumps orchitis predominantly affects post-pubertal males and is uncommon before puberty, so this prepubertal boy is at lower risk now. When it occurs, it is usually unilateral, presenting with testicular pain and swelling; complete sterility is rare even with bilateral involvement, though some reduction in fertility can follow. Stating it is most common in prepubertal boys and always causes infertility overstates both frequency and consequence. Orchitis obviously cannot occur in girls (mumps can instead cause oophoritis). Antibiotics do not prevent orchitis because mumps is viral; prevention rests on MMR vaccination. Aseptic meningitis and, less commonly, deafness and pancreatitis are other recognized complications.

pearls: Mumps causes bilateral, tender, non-erythematous parotid swelling that lifts the earlobe; pain worsens with sour foods.
Confirm with RT-PCR (buccal/Stensen's duct swab) and/or IgM serology; mumps is a reportable disease — notify public health.
Management is supportive with droplet isolation; antibiotics and antivirals have no role in uncomplicated viral parotitis.
Orchitis mainly affects post-pubertal males and is usually unilateral; other complications include aseptic meningitis, deafness, and pancreatitis. Prevent with MMR.

cases.title: Coughing Fits and Spells in a Young Infant
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening coughing fits with vomiting and episodes of turning blue.
cases.history: A 2-month-old girl is admitted to the pediatric ward for evaluation of severe coughing fits. For about 10 days she had a runny nose and mild cough (a catarrhal phase), but over the last several days the cough has become paroxysmal — sudden bursts of rapid coughs during which she turns red, and afterward she vomits. Her parents have witnessed two episodes where she briefly stopped breathing and turned dusky, prompting them to come in. Between fits she can appear deceptively well. She feeds poorly during the fits and has had decreased wet diapers. She was born at term without complications. She has received no vaccines yet (her first set, including DTaP, is due at 2 months and has not been given). Her mother had a lingering cough for a couple of weeks before the baby got sick and did not receive Tdap during pregnancy. There are no known drug allergies and she is on no medications. She lives with both parents and an older sibling who recently had a cold. Family history is otherwise noncontributory. Growth had been normal until this illness.
cases.exam: An infant who appears comfortable and even smiling between episodes but has dramatic paroxysmal coughing spells with facial plethora, followed by post-tussive emesis; an inspiratory 'whoop' is appreciated after some fits. During paroxysms she becomes dusky with oxygen desaturation and one witnessed apneic pause with bradycardia that self-resolved. Between spells the lungs are surprisingly clear to auscultation without focal crackles or wheeze, and she is afebrile. There are scattered subconjunctival hemorrhages and facial petechiae from the force of coughing. She is mildly tachycardic. Capillary refill is under 2 seconds and the anterior fontanelle is soft. There is no rash, no hepatosplenomegaly, and the neurologic exam between spells is appropriate for age.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a young unvaccinated infant with this catarrhal-then-paroxysmal cough, post-tussive emesis, and apnea, what is the most appropriate immediate priority?
options: Discharge home with cough suppressants since she looks well between fits as the first step today
Start inhaled bronchodilators and oral steroids for presumed asthma as the first step today
Obtain an outpatient chest CT before any treatment as the first step today
Admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring with attention to apnea/desaturation
stages.explanation: A young infant with paroxysmal cough, inspiratory whoop, post-tussive emesis, and especially apnea/desaturation has potentially life-threatening pertussis and requires inpatient cardiorespiratory monitoring because apnea and bradycardia can be sudden and severe. The deceptively well appearance between fits is classic and must not lead to discharge. Appropriate steps include continuous monitoring, supportive oxygen/feeding support, droplet isolation, and confirmatory testing. Bronchodilators and steroids for 'asthma' are inappropriate — wheeze is absent, the lungs are clear between spells, and this is not a reactive-airways picture. A chest CT is unnecessary and unsafe to pursue in an unstable infant; the diagnosis is made clinically and microbiologically, not by CT.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 32.5 ×10³/µL with marked lymphocytosis (78% lymphocytes)
Nasopharyngeal PCR: positive for Bordetella pertussis
Chest radiograph: mild perihilar infiltrate, no focal consolidation; 'shaggy' heart border
Blood culture: pending
Electrolytes within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory finding is most classically associated with this infection in infants and children?
options: Marked lymphocytosis with an elevated total white count
Pancytopenia as the primary diagnosis
Eosinophilia as the primary diagnosis
Neutrophilic leukocytosis with toxic granulation
stages.explanation: Pertussis characteristically produces a marked absolute lymphocytosis with an elevated total WBC, driven by pertussis toxin's effect of preventing lymphocyte egress from the circulation; very high lymphocyte counts in young infants correlate with severe disease and pulmonary hypertension. A neutrophilic leukocytosis with toxic granulation suggests a pyogenic bacterial infection, not pertussis. Eosinophilia points toward allergic or parasitic processes. Pancytopenia indicates marrow suppression or infiltration and is unrelated to typical pertussis. The combination of lymphocyte-predominant leukocytosis with the clinical paroxysmal cough and a positive nasopharyngeal PCR confirms the diagnosis here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate antimicrobial therapy?
options: No antibiotics, since they never change the disease course
A macrolide such as azithromycin
Oral acyclovir as initial therapy
High-dose amoxicillin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The treatment of choice for pertussis is a macrolide — azithromycin is preferred in young infants (with monitoring given the association between macrolides and infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis in neonates, but azithromycin remains recommended). Antibiotics are most effective when started early (catarrhal/early paroxysmal phase); they may not shorten an established cough but importantly reduce transmissibility to others. Amoxicillin and other beta-lactams are not effective against Bordetella pertussis. Acyclovir is an antiviral with no activity against this bacterium. Saying antibiotics never matter is incorrect — even when symptom benefit is limited, they curtail contagiousness and are indicated, alongside prophylaxis for close contacts.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate measure to protect this infant's household and prevent future cases?
options: Quarantine the household for 30 days without any medication as the immediate next step
Give post-exposure macrolide prophylaxis to close contacts
Administer varicella-zoster immunoglobulin to contacts as the immediate next step
No prevention is possible once a case occurs as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Close contacts of a pertussis case should receive post-exposure macrolide prophylaxis (e.g., azithromycin) regardless of immunization status to limit spread, and the family should be counseled on prevention: maternal Tdap during every pregnancy (ideally 27–36 weeks) provides transplacental antibody that protects newborns before their own vaccines, and infants should receive on-time DTaP beginning at 2 months. This infant was vulnerable precisely because she was too young to be vaccinated and her mother missed prenatal Tdap. A 30-day medication-free quarantine is ineffective without prophylaxis. VZIG prevents varicella, not pertussis. Asserting prevention is impossible is wrong — chemoprophylaxis and vaccination ('cocooning' and maternal immunization) are effective protective strategies.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given her age and witnessed apnea, what is the most appropriate disposition for this infant?
options: Observe for 2 hours in clinic and follow up by phone tomorrow with appropriate follow-up
Transfer to a general adult medical ward for observation with appropriate follow-up
Admit with continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring, ideally in a setting capable of escalating care, because infants under 4 months are at highest risk of apnea, pulmonary hypertension,
Discharge home once a single feeding is tolerated between fits with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Infants under about 4 months with pertussis — especially those with witnessed apnea, desaturation, or bradycardia — are at the highest risk of life-threatening complications including recurrent apnea, secondary pneumonia, refractory pulmonary hypertension, seizures, and death, and they warrant admission with continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring in a setting that can rapidly escalate to intensive care. Discharge after one tolerated feeding is unsafe because the deceptively well inter-paroxysmal appearance masks the risk of sudden apnea. A general adult ward is inappropriate for a critically vulnerable young infant. Brief clinic observation with phone follow-up grossly underestimates the danger. Close monitoring allows immediate intervention for apneic or hypoxic spells, which is the cornerstone of safe management for young infants with pertussis.

pearls: Pertussis progresses through catarrhal, paroxysmal (whoop, post-tussive emesis, apnea in infants), and convalescent phases; young infants can present with apnea and look well between fits.
A marked lymphocyte-predominant leukocytosis is classic; very high counts in infants portend severe disease and pulmonary hypertension.
Confirm with nasopharyngeal PCR; treat with a macrolide (azithromycin) — antibiotics mainly reduce transmission, so start early and isolate.
Protect infants with post-exposure prophylaxis for contacts, maternal Tdap each pregnancy (27–36 weeks), and on-time DTaP.

cases.title: A Yellow 3-Day-Old on the Postpartum Ward
cases.chiefComplaint: Nursing reports the infant appears increasingly yellow on day-of-life 3.
cases.history: A 3-day-old boy born at 38 weeks by spontaneous vaginal delivery to a 27-year-old G2P2 mother is noted by the bedside nurse to be progressively yellow, now extending to the chest. Pregnancy was uncomplicated; rupture of membranes was 6 hours before delivery with no maternal fever. The infant is exclusively breastfeeding, latching roughly every 4 hours but feeding poorly, with only 3 wet diapers in the last 24 hours and one meconium stool. Birth weight was 3.4 kg; current weight is 3.05 kg (10% loss). He has had no fever, lethargy, or high-pitched cry. Maternal blood type is O-positive; the infant's type is not yet resulted. There is no family history of splenectomy, anemia, gallstones, or neonatal exchange transfusion. Mother is of Mediterranean descent. No medications. Vitamin K and hepatitis B vaccine were administered at birth. The newborn metabolic screen was sent on day 1. Mother had no history of gestational diabetes and is rubella immune; GBS status was negative.
cases.exam: Alert but somewhat sleepy infant who rouses to stimulation. Scleral icterus present with cephalocaudal jaundice extending to the upper chest and abdomen. Anterior fontanelle soft and slightly sunken; mucous membranes tacky. Cardiac exam regular without murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft and non-distended with no hepatosplenomegaly. No cephalohematoma or extensive bruising. Tone is normal with intact Moro and suck reflexes; no opisthotonus, no high-pitched cry, no setting-sun sign. Skin without petechiae or pallor. Cord stump clean and drying.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to assess the severity and risk of this infant's jaundice?
options: Obtain a total and direct serum bilirubin and plot it on an hour-specific nomogram
Order an abdominal ultrasound to evaluate for biliary obstruction as the first step today
Reassure the family that jaundice on day 3 is always physiologic and requires no testing
Begin phototherapy empirically based on visual estimate of jaundice level
stages.explanation: Visual assessment of jaundice is unreliable, so a quantitative total and direct (conjugated) serum bilirubin should be obtained and interpreted against the infant's age in HOURS using the Bhutani nomogram, which stratifies risk and guides phototherapy. Reassurance alone is wrong because the combination of poor feeding, 10% weight loss, and visible jaundice mandates measurement. Empiric phototherapy without a bilirubin level is inappropriate—the level and risk zone determine whether treatment is even indicated. An abdominal ultrasound is premature; direct hyperbilirubinemia (which would prompt obstruction workup) has not yet been demonstrated, and at 72 hours an unconjugated process is far more likely.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total bilirubin: 16.8 mg/dL
Direct (conjugated) bilirubin: 0.5 mg/dL
Hemoglobin: 16.2 g/dL
Reticulocyte count: 2.4%
Infant blood type: O-positive (Coombs/DAT negative)
Peripheral smear: normal RBC morphology, no spherocytes
Age at draw: 72 hours of life

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a total bilirubin of 16.8 mg/dL that is almost entirely unconjugated, a negative DAT, and ABO compatibility, what is the most likely underlying mechanism?
options: Crigler-Najjar syndrome type I as the primary diagnosis
Biliary atresia as the primary diagnosis
ABO hemolytic disease of the newborn as the primary diagnosis
Exaggerated physiologic jaundice compounded by suboptimal breastfeeding
stages.explanation: The picture—unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia peaking around day 3-5, a negative DAT, normal hemoglobin and reticulocyte count, no spherocytes, poor intake with 10% weight loss and few wet diapers—fits exaggerated physiologic jaundice worsened by inadequate breast-milk intake (breastfeeding/lactation failure jaundice), which increases enterohepatic circulation. ABO disease is unlikely because mother is O and infant is O (no setup) with a negative DAT. Biliary atresia causes CONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia, not seen here. Crigler-Najjar type I produces extreme unconjugated bilirubin (often >20-25) from birth with no hemolysis and is rare; the clinical context and timing here point to a far more common benign cause.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Using AAP thresholds for a 72-hour-old term infant at this bilirubin level, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Discharge home with outpatient recheck in 1 week as initial therapy
Stop breastfeeding permanently and switch to formula as initial therapy
Initiate intensive phototherapy
Proceed directly to exchange transfusion as initial therapy
stages.explanation: At 72 hours, a total bilirubin of 16.8 mg/dL is at or above the phototherapy threshold for most term infants, so intensive phototherapy plus optimization of feeding (more frequent breastfeeding, lactation support, and supplementation if intake remains poor) is correct; rehydration alone reduces enterohepatic recirculation. Discharge with a 1-week recheck is unsafe given the rising level. Exchange transfusion is reserved for much higher levels (typically ≥20-25 at this age) or signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy. Permanently stopping breastfeeding is unnecessary; temporary supplementation suffices, and breastfeeding can usually continue during phototherapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this infant's bilirubin were instead allowed to rise unchecked, which finding would be the earliest sign of acute bilirubin encephalopathy?
options: Lethargy, hypotonia, and poor feeding
Sensorineural hearing loss and choreoathetoid cerebral palsy
Permanent upward gaze palsy (setting-sun sign)
Fixed retrocollis and opisthotonus in this presentation
stages.explanation: Acute bilirubin encephalopathy evolves in phases: the EARLIEST (phase 1) signs are subtle—lethargy, hypotonia, and poor feeding/sucking—which are reversible if bilirubin is lowered promptly. Retrocollis and opisthotonus are intermediate/advanced (phase 2) findings indicating progression. Sensorineural hearing loss and choreoathetoid cerebral palsy are features of chronic kernicterus, the permanent late sequela. The setting-sun sign is also a later neurologic finding. Recognizing the early, reversible phase is the key teaching point because intervention at that stage can prevent permanent kernicterus.

cases.diagnosis: Exaggerated physiologic (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinemia with breastfeeding/lactation failure jaundice, above phototherapy threshold
pearls: Always fractionate bilirubin: a conjugated (direct) fraction >1 mg/dL or >20% of total signals a pathologic, cholestatic process (e.g., biliary atresia) and is never physiologic.
Plot total bilirubin against age in HOURS on the Bhutani nomogram—not age in days—to decide on phototherapy and follow-up timing.

Breastfeeding failure jaundice (first week, poor intake, dehydration) is fixed by improving feeding; breast-milk jaundice (peaks week 2, well-fed thriving infant) is benign and breastfeeding continues.
The earliest signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy—lethargy, hypotonia, poor feeding—are reversible; prompt phototherapy prevents irreversible kernicterus.

cases.title: Six Weeks of Jaundice and Pale Stools
cases.chiefComplaint: A 6-week-old girl is brought in for persistent yellowing of the skin and eyes that her parents say never went away.
cases.history: A 6-week-old girl born at term presents to clinic because her jaundice, first noted in the newborn nursery, has never resolved and the parents now think she looks more yellow. She is exclusively breastfed and feeds well with appropriate weight gain. Over the past 1-2 weeks her parents report her stools have become pale, clay-colored, and her urine appears dark and stains the diaper. She has had no fevers, vomiting, or lethargy. Birth weight was 3.2 kg; she has gained appropriately. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated; newborn metabolic screen was reported normal and she passed her hearing screen. There is no family history of liver disease, metabolic disorders, or consanguinity. She received vitamin K and hepatitis B vaccine at birth. No medications other than vitamin D drops. The mother is blood type A-positive; the infant is also A-positive. The family has no pets and no recent travel; no sick contacts.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, vigorous infant with obvious scleral icterus and jaundiced skin extending below the umbilicus. Abdomen is soft but with a firm liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin; spleen tip is just palpable. No ascites or visible abdominal wall veins. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal with no murmur. Anterior fontanelle is soft and flat. A diaper examination reveals pale, acholic (clay-colored) stool. No dysmorphic features, no cataracts on red reflex, and neurologic tone is age-appropriate.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important first laboratory test in this 6-week-old with prolonged jaundice and pale stools?
options: A complete blood count with reticulocyte count
Thyroid-stimulating hormone and free T4
A fractionated bilirubin
Serum ammonia level as the first step today
stages.explanation: Any infant jaundiced beyond 2 weeks of age requires a FRACTIONATED bilirubin to distinguish unconjugated from conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. A conjugated (direct) bilirubin >1 mg/dL or >20% of total defines cholestasis and mandates urgent evaluation for biliary atresia. The pale, acholic stools and dark urine described here strongly suggest a conjugated/obstructive process. A CBC with reticulocytes targets hemolysis (unconjugated) and would miss the point. TSH/T4 screens for hypothyroidism, a cause of prolonged UNCONJUGATED jaundice, but does not explain acholic stools.
