Ammonia is relevant for metabolic encephalopathy, not the priority in a well-appearing cholestatic infant.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total bilirubin: 9.2 mg/dL
Direct (conjugated) bilirubin: 6.4 mg/dL
AST: 210 U/L
ALT: 188 U/L
GGT: 640 U/L (markedly elevated)
Alkaline phosphatase: 410 U/L
Albumin: 3.6 g/dL
INR: 1.1 (normal after vitamin K)
Alpha-1 antitrypsin level/phenotype: normal (MM)
TORCH/viral serologies: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, markedly elevated GGT, acholic stools, and hepatomegaly in a 6-week-old, which diagnosis is most urgent to confirm or exclude?
options: Biliary atresia
Gilbert syndrome
Crigler-Najjar syndrome
Breast-milk jaundice
stages.explanation: Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with a high GGT, acholic stools, dark urine, and hepatomegaly in an infant points to obstructive cholestasis, and biliary atresia is the most important diagnosis to confirm urgently because outcomes are time-dependent (the Kasai portoenterostomy works best before 45-60 days of life). Breast-milk jaundice and Gilbert syndrome produce UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia with normal stools and would never cause acholic stools or a high GGT. Crigler-Najjar is also unconjugated. Recognizing that 'conjugated jaundice + acholic stool' equals biliary atresia until proven otherwise is the central, time-sensitive teaching point.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: After an abdominal ultrasound shows a small/absent gallbladder and a 'triangular cord sign,' what is the definitive test to confirm biliary atresia and guide surgery?
options: Hepatobiliary (HIDA) scan alone as the immediate next step
Percutaneous liver biopsy followed by intraoperative cholangiogram
Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP)
Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) in this setting
stages.explanation: The diagnostic pathway combines a liver biopsy (showing bile duct proliferation, bile plugs, and portal fibrosis) with an INTRAOPERATIVE cholangiogram, which directly confirms obstruction of the extrahepatic biliary tree and allows immediate proceeding to Kasai portoenterostomy in the same operation. A HIDA scan supports the diagnosis (no tracer excretion into the bowel) but cannot exclude it definitively—nonexcretion occurs in any severe cholestasis. MRCP is insensitive for small infant ducts. ERCP is technically difficult and not standard in young infants. The biopsy-plus-cholangiogram approach is both confirmatory and therapeutic in the operating room.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Why is rapid diagnosis and surgical referral the priority in confirmed biliary atresia?
options: Because biliary atresia spontaneously resolves if surgery is delayed past 90 days as initial therapy
Because medical therapy with ursodeoxycholic acid alone reliably cures the disease as initial therapy
Because the Kasai portoenterostomy has markedly better bile drainage
Because the infant requires emergent liver transplantation within 48 hours regardless of age as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Biliary atresia is progressive: untreated, it leads to biliary cirrhosis and death. The Kasai hepatoportoenterostomy restores bile flow far more successfully when done early—best results occur before about 60 days of life, after which progressive fibrosis sharply reduces success. Ursodeoxycholic acid is supportive adjunct, not curative. The disease never resolves spontaneously; delay worsens outcomes. While many children eventually need transplantation, the Kasai is the time-sensitive first operation, not an emergent same-week transplant—transplant is reserved for Kasai failure or end-stage disease. Hence the urgency of the diagnostic workup.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Several weeks after a successful Kasai procedure, the infant develops fever, rising bilirubin, and clinical worsening. What is the most common postoperative complication to recognize and treat?
options: Portal vein thrombosis in this presentation
Ascending (bacterial) cholangitis
Anastomotic small-bowel obstruction
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis from ascites
stages.explanation: Ascending cholangitis is the most common complication after a Kasai portoenterostomy, because the Roux limb creates a conduit for enteric bacteria to reach the liver; it presents with fever, recurrent or rising jaundice/bilirubin, and clinical deterioration, and is treated promptly with IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (recurrent episodes worsen the prognosis and may hasten transplant need). Anastomotic small-bowel obstruction is less common and would present with bilious vomiting and distension, not a febrile cholestatic picture. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis requires established ascites and is later/less common. Portal vein thrombosis is uncommon acutely. Recognizing and rapidly treating post-Kasai cholangitis preserves bile drainage and native-liver survival.

cases.diagnosis: Biliary atresia (extrahepatic) presenting with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and acholic stools
pearls: Jaundice persisting beyond 2 weeks of age requires a fractionated bilirubin; conjugated >1 mg/dL or >20% of total is cholestasis and is pathologic until proven otherwise.
Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia + acholic stools + dark urine = biliary atresia until excluded; this is a surgical clock, not a watch-and-wait.
The Kasai portoenterostomy outcomes hinge on timing—ideally before ~45-60 days of life—so workup must be expedited, not deferred.
Ultrasound clues include a small/absent gallbladder and the triangular cord sign; intraoperative cholangiogram plus liver biopsy is definitive.

cases.title: A Newborn Who Hasn't Stooled and a Swelling Belly
cases.chiefComplaint: A 3-day-old boy has not passed meconium and his abdomen is becoming distended.
cases.history: A 3-day-old boy born at 39 weeks via spontaneous vaginal delivery is evaluated on the ward because he has not passed meconium and his abdomen appears progressively distended. The nursing staff confirm no documented stool since birth (now ~70 hours). He has been feeding but is increasingly fussy with feeds and had two episodes of bilious-tinged emesis today. Birth weight was 3.3 kg. Pregnancy was uncomplicated; prenatal ultrasounds were normal. Mother is 30 years old, G1P1, with no diabetes; GBS negative; membranes ruptured 8 hours before delivery without fever. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis or known bowel disease, but a paternal uncle reportedly had 'a colon operation as a baby.' The infant has subtle low-set ears but no other obvious dysmorphism; no prenatal concern for Down syndrome, though confirmatory testing was declined. Newborn screen pending. No medications. He is afebrile and has had adequate urine output.
cases.exam: Mildly irritable but consolable infant. Abdomen is distended and tympanitic to percussion with visible loops; it is soft without guarding and non-tender, and there is no erythema or wall edema. Bowel sounds are present and high-pitched. No palpable masses or hepatosplenomegaly. On digital rectal examination the anal canal feels tight with a snug rectal vault, and upon withdrawal of the examining finger there is an explosive release of gas and liquid stool. The anus is patent and normally positioned. No sacral dimple or tuft. Cardiac and lung exams normal; no murmur. Tone and reflexes are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The 'squirt' or 'blast' sign (explosive expulsion of stool and gas after withdrawing the examining finger) in a neonate with delayed meconium and distension most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Hirschsprung disease
Necrotizing enterocolitis in this clinical context
Meconium ileus as the first step today
Anal stenosis from imperforate anus
stages.explanation: Delayed passage of meconium beyond 48 hours, abdominal distension, and the classic 'squirt/blast' sign—forceful expulsion of stool and gas when the obstructing tonic internal sphincter is released after rectal exam—are hallmarks of Hirschsprung disease, in which an aganglionic distal segment fails to relax. Meconium ileus (often the earliest sign of cystic fibrosis) causes obstruction but lacks the tight 'squirt' physiology and is associated with inspissated meconium. Imperforate anus would be evident as an abnormal/absent anal opening on exam; here the anus is patent and normally positioned. NEC typically presents later with feeding intolerance, bloody stools, pneumatosis, and a sick, often premature infant.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study to support the suspected diagnosis?
options: Right upper quadrant ultrasound
Contrast enema looking for a transition zone
Abdominal CT with contrast in this setting
Upper GI series with small-bowel follow-through
stages.explanation: A contrast (barium or water-soluble) enema is the standard initial imaging for suspected Hirschsprung disease; it demonstrates a transition zone between the narrow distal aganglionic segment and the dilated proximal normally innervated colon, and may show retained contrast on delayed films. CT adds radiation without advantage in a neonate. RUQ ultrasound evaluates the biliary tree/pylorus, not distal colonic obstruction. An upper GI series is the test for malrotation/midgut volvulus (proximal bilious obstruction), not for a distal colonic transition zone. Note that imaging supports but does not definitively diagnose Hirschsprung.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Contrast enema: narrowed rectosigmoid segment with a clear transition zone to dilated proximal colon
Delayed films: significant retention of contrast at 24 hours
Abdominal radiograph: dilated bowel loops with paucity of rectal gas
No pneumatosis intestinalis; no free air

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the gold-standard test to definitively confirm Hirschsprung disease?
options: A repeat contrast enema in 1 week as the primary diagnosis
Rectal suction biopsy showing absence of ganglion cells
Anorectal manometry alone as the primary diagnosis
Stool calprotectin and fecal elastase as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The definitive diagnosis of Hirschsprung disease is histologic: a rectal suction biopsy demonstrating ABSENCE of ganglion cells in the submucosal and myenteric plexuses, typically with increased acetylcholinesterase-positive nerve fibers. The contrast enema and manometry are supportive but not confirmatory—manometry shows absence of the rectoanal inhibitory reflex but can be unreliable in neonates. A repeat enema adds nothing diagnostic. Stool calprotectin/fecal elastase assess inflammation and pancreatic function (e.g., cystic fibrosis), not aganglionosis. Establishing the histologic diagnosis is essential before surgical pull-through.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While awaiting definitive surgery, the infant develops fever, explosive foul-smelling diarrhea, marked distension, and lethargy. What is this life-threatening complication and its initial management?
options: Viral gastroenteritis—provide oral rehydration and discharge as the leading concern
Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis
Intussusception—obtain an air-contrast enema for reduction as the leading concern
Lactose intolerance—switch to a lactose-free formula as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) is the most dangerous complication, presenting with fever, abdominal distension, explosive/foul diarrhea, and signs of sepsis; it can progress to toxic megacolon and perforation. Management is urgent: IV fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics (covering enteric flora, often with metronidazole), bowel rest/decompression, and rectal irrigations to evacuate the obstructed colon. Viral gastroenteritis and lactose intolerance do not explain a septic-appearing neonate with known aganglionosis and would be dangerous misdiagnoses. Intussusception is uncommon at this age and presents with currant-jelly stools and a palpable mass, not this enterocolitic picture; reduction enema would not address HAEC.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After stabilization and biopsy confirmation, what is the definitive treatment for Hirschsprung disease?
options: Lifelong daily stool softeners and dietary fiber with appropriate follow-up
Permanent end colostomy in all patients with no further surgery with appropriate follow-up
Botulinum toxin injection of the internal anal sphincter as definitive cure
Surgical pull-through procedure that removes the aganglionic segment
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment is surgical resection of the aganglionic segment with a pull-through procedure (e.g., Swenson, Soave, or Duhamel), bringing normally innervated (ganglionic) bowel down to the anus; this restores functional continuity. Stool softeners and fiber manage functional constipation, not a fixed aganglionic obstruction. Botulinum toxin to the internal sphincter is an adjunct for residual postoperative obstructive symptoms, not a definitive cure. A leveling/diverting colostomy may be used temporarily (especially with enterocolitis or in long-segment disease) but is not the permanent endpoint for most patients—the pull-through is the goal, often performed in a single stage in stable neonates.

cases.diagnosis: Hirschsprung disease (congenital aganglionic megacolon) of the rectosigmoid
pearls: Failure to pass meconium within 48 hours of birth is the cardinal red flag for Hirschsprung disease; the 'squirt/blast' sign on rectal exam supports it.
Contrast enema shows a transition zone and retained contrast, but rectal suction biopsy (absent ganglion cells, increased acetylcholinesterase) is the gold-standard diagnosis.
Hirschsprung disease is associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21) and RET mutations; ~80% of cases involve the rectosigmoid (short-segment).
Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis is the leading cause of mortality—treat fever, distension, and explosive diarrhea with fluids, antibiotics, and rectal decompression.

cases.title: Sudden Green Vomiting in a 5-Day-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: A previously well 5-day-old boy is brought to the ED for the abrupt onset of bright green vomiting.
cases.history: A 5-day-old boy born at 40 weeks via uncomplicated vaginal delivery is brought to the emergency department by his parents after he suddenly began vomiting bright green (bilious) material about 4 hours ago. He had been feeding and stooling normally and passed meconium on day 1. The vomiting started abruptly during a feed; he has had three further bilious emeses and now refuses to feed and is increasingly fussy then intermittently lethargic. He has had one normal stool today but the parents thought they saw a streak of dark blood in the last diaper. Birth weight 3.5 kg, current 3.4 kg. Pregnancy was unremarkable; prenatal ultrasounds were reported normal. No family history of GI disease or cystic fibrosis. He is exclusively breastfed. Mother is 28, G1P1, GBS negative, afebrile at delivery. No medications. The parents deny fever at home. There is no prior surgery and the newborn screen is pending.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, fussy neonate who intermittently becomes quiet and poorly responsive. Capillary refill is 3-4 seconds; extremities are cool. The abdomen is initially relatively soft with mild distension and diffuse tenderness on palpation; bowel sounds are decreased. There is no abdominal wall erythema yet. Anus is patent. Mucous membranes tacky; anterior fontanelle slightly sunken. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia without murmur; lungs clear. No hepatosplenomegaly palpable. A small amount of dark blood is noted on rectal exam. Tone is mildly decreased; he is irritable when handled.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bilious vomiting in a neonate must be treated as which until proven otherwise?
options: Malrotation with midgut volvulus
Pyloric stenosis as the first step today
Physiologic gastroesophageal reflux
Overfeeding as the first step today
stages.explanation: Bilious (green) emesis in a neonate is a surgical emergency and represents malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise, because volvulus twists the midgut around the superior mesenteric artery and can produce ischemic necrosis of the entire small bowel within hours. Physiologic reflux produces non-bilious spit-up in a thriving infant. Pyloric stenosis causes NON-bilious projectile vomiting at 3-6 weeks of age (the obstruction is proximal to the ampulla of Vater). Overfeeding causes benign non-bilious regurgitation. The reflex to recognize bilious emesis as a potential catastrophe drives the entire emergent workup and is the single most important teaching point.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging imaging, what are the most appropriate immediate stabilizing steps?
options: Start oral erythromycin as a prokinetic as initial therapy
Place an NG tube for decompression, obtain IV access, give a fluid bolus, make NPO,
Administer ondansetron and discharge with reflux precautions as initial therapy
Give oral rehydration solution and observe for improvement as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The unstable neonate with bilious emesis needs immediate resuscitation in parallel with urgent surgical consultation: nasogastric decompression to relieve the obstructed, distended stomach/bowel; IV access with an isotonic fluid bolus to treat shock; NPO status; and emergent pediatric surgery involvement. Oral rehydration is contraindicated in a vomiting, possibly obstructed and shocky infant who must remain NPO. Ondansetron with discharge would be lethal—this is not benign vomiting. Erythromycin as a prokinetic is irrelevant and could worsen a mechanical obstruction. Stabilize, decompress, and get the surgeon while imaging is obtained.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Upper GI series: duodenum fails to cross the midline; 'corkscrew' configuration of contrast in the proximal small bowel; ligament of Treitz located to the right of midline
Abdominal radiograph: relative paucity of distal bowel gas
Venous blood gas: pH 7.28, lactate 4.2 mmol/L
Na 138 / K 4.0 / HCO3 16 mEq/L
WBC 18.5 ×10³/µL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The upper GI series shows the duodenum failing to cross midline with a 'corkscrew' appearance and an abnormally positioned ligament of Treitz. What is the diagnosis?
options: Malrotation with midgut volvulus
Duodenal atresia
Necrotizing enterocolitis
Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
stages.explanation: These are the classic upper GI findings of malrotation with midgut volvulus: an abnormally positioned ligament of Treitz (to the right of midline rather than left), the duodenum failing to cross the midline, and a 'corkscrew' spiral of contrast as bowel twists around the SMA. Duodenal atresia produces a 'double bubble' with NO distal gas and presents typically with vomiting in the first day or two, often with Down syndrome—not a corkscrew. Pyloric stenosis shows a 'string sign' and non-bilious vomiting at weeks of age. NEC shows pneumatosis intestinalis on radiograph, not these contrast findings. The rising lactate and acidosis signal evolving bowel ischemia.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for malrotation with midgut volvulus?
options: Nonoperative bowel rest and serial abdominal exams
Emergent laparotomy with detorsion
Pyloromyotomy with appropriate follow-up
Air-contrast enema reduction in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Midgut volvulus requires EMERGENT operative exploration: the bowel is untwisted (counterclockwise detorsion), viability assessed, and a Ladd procedure performed—dividing Ladd bands, widening the mesenteric base, placing the small bowel on the right and colon on the left, and removing the appendix. Delay risks infarction of the entire midgut. Nonoperative observation is unacceptable given the time-critical ischemia. Pyloromyotomy treats pyloric stenosis, an entirely different (non-bilious) entity. Air-contrast enema reduces intussusception, not volvulus. The combination of bilious emesis, corkscrew sign, and rising lactate mandates the OR without delay.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If extensive midgut necrosis requires massive small-bowel resection at surgery, which long-term complication should the family be counseled about?
options: Inevitable lifelong need for a permanent colostomy with no enteral feeding ever
Complete spontaneous regeneration of the resected bowel within weeks
Short bowel syndrome with malabsorption
Development of pyloric stenosis as a direct consequence as the leading concern
stages.explanation: When volvulus causes extensive ischemic necrosis requiring massive small-bowel resection, the major long-term sequela is short bowel syndrome—inadequate absorptive surface leading to malabsorption, diarrhea, growth failure, and often prolonged dependence on parenteral nutrition with its own complications (intestinal failure-associated liver disease, line infections). This is precisely why early diagnosis to salvage bowel is critical. A permanent colostomy with no enteral feeding is not the universal outcome; enteral feeds are advanced as the gut adapts. Bowel does not regenerate to replace resected length, though the remnant undergoes adaptation. Pyloric stenosis is unrelated. Counseling on short bowel syndrome reinforces the value of timely surgery.

pearls: Bilious emesis in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise—obtain an emergent upper GI series and call surgery; do not wait.
The upper GI series is the gold standard: look for the duodenum not crossing midline, a corkscrew configuration, and a malpositioned ligament of Treitz.
Definitive treatment is the Ladd procedure (detorsion, division of Ladd bands, broadening the mesentery, appendectomy); delay risks necrosis of the entire midgut.
Rising lactate, acidosis, and bloody stool signal bowel ischemia—stabilize with NG decompression, IV fluids, and NPO while expediting surgery.

cases.title: The Two-Month-Old Who Spits Up After Every Feed
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents of a 2-month-old boy are worried about frequent spitting up after feeds.
cases.history: A 2-month-old boy is brought to clinic by his parents who are anxious about how often he spits up. They report that after most feeds he effortlessly brings up a small amount of milk, sometimes over his bib and occasionally out of his nose, but he seems comfortable and content, even smiling shortly afterward. There is no forceful or projectile vomiting, no green or bloody material, and no choking spells or apnea. He is formula-fed about 4 ounces every 3 hours and is sometimes laid flat right after feeds. He has no fever, no diarrhea, and his stools are normal and yellow. He is gaining weight well, and the parents have no concerns about his energy or feeding vigor. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated; he was born at term. No medications, no allergies, no eczema. Family history is notable only for seasonal allergies in the father. He lives at home with both parents; no tobacco exposure.
cases.exam: A well-appearing, smiling, vigorous infant in no distress. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Mucous membranes moist. Lungs clear without wheeze or stridor; no increased work of breathing. Cardiac exam regular without murmur. Abdomen soft, non-distended, non-tender, no palpable olive-shaped mass in the epigastrium, no organomegaly. No eczema or skin rash. Growth parameters demonstrate appropriate weight gain crossing upward through percentiles. Neurologic exam age-appropriate with good tone and social smile. Hydration status normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly indicates this is physiologic gastroesophageal reflux (a 'happy spitter') rather than pathologic GERD or another disorder?
options: Normal, appropriate weight gain with a comfortable, thriving infant
The infant is formula-fed as the primary diagnosis
The presence of effortless spit-up after feeds in this clinical context
The spit-up occasionally comes out of the nose in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The defining distinction between benign physiologic reflux and pathologic GERD is the presence or absence of complications: a 'happy spitter' thrives—normal weight gain, comfort, and no warning signs—as seen here. Effortless spit-up after feeds occurs in BOTH reflux and GERD, so it alone does not distinguish them. Being formula-fed is not a discriminator (reflux occurs with breast and formula feeding). Spit-up exiting the nose is simply a route of regurgitation and not a marker of pathology. Documented good growth in a comfortable infant is the reassuring finding that defines physiologic gastroesophageal reflux.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which of the following findings, if present, would be a RED FLAG suggesting a pathologic process rather than benign reflux?
options: Spitting up 3-5 times per day with weight gain in this setting
Mild fussiness that resolves with burping in this setting
Occasional hiccups after feeds as the immediate next step
Bilious or projectile vomiting, poor weight gain, or hematemesis
stages.explanation: Bilious (green) vomiting, forceful/projectile vomiting, blood in the vomit, poor weight gain or weight loss, lethargy, or significant respiratory symptoms are red flags that point away from benign reflux toward conditions like pyloric stenosis, malrotation, GERD with complications, or allergy, and warrant further evaluation. Frequent but effortless spit-up WITH good weight gain is the classic benign pattern. Hiccups and mild fussiness relieved by burping are normal infant behaviors. Recognizing the alarm features that separate the happy spitter from a dangerous mimic is the key safety teaching point in this otherwise reassuring case.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this thriving 2-month-old with physiologic reflux?
options: Start a proton pump inhibitor (e.g., omeprazole) daily as initial therapy
Reassurance plus conservative measures: smaller/more frequent feeds, upright positioning after feeds,
Switch immediately to a hydrolyzed formula and start an H2 blocker as initial therapy
Obtain an upper GI series and pH probe as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Physiologic reflux in a thriving infant is managed with reassurance and conservative measures: smaller, more frequent feeds; keeping the infant upright for 20-30 minutes after feeding; frequent burping; and avoiding overfeeding. Acid suppression with a PPI is not recommended for uncomplicated reflux—it does not reduce regurgitation in 'happy spitters' and carries risks (infections, fractures). Imaging like an upper GI series or pH probe is unnecessary without red flags. Empirically switching to hydrolyzed formula plus an H2 blocker is overtreatment here, though a brief hydrolyzed-formula trial is reasonable only if milk protein allergy is suspected, which it is not in this comfortable, well-growing infant.

stages.label: ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE
stages.question: What should the parents be told about the expected natural course of physiologic infant reflux?
options: It usually peaks around 4 months
It indicates a high risk of developing asthma as the immediate next step
It typically worsens through toddlerhood and requires lifelong therapy in this setting
It will resolve only if breastfeeding is stopped as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Physiologic reflux is developmental and self-limited: regurgitation peaks around 4 months of age and resolves in the great majority of infants by 12-18 months as the lower esophageal sphincter matures, the infant spends more time upright, and solid foods are introduced. It does not typically worsen into toddlerhood or require lifelong therapy. It is not a predictor of asthma. Stopping breastfeeding is not indicated—breast milk is protective and reflux resolves regardless. Setting accurate expectations reassures anxious parents and reduces unnecessary testing and medication.

pearls: The 'happy spitter' thrives: effortless regurgitation with normal weight gain and a comfortable infant needs reassurance, not medication.
Red flags that argue against benign reflux include bilious/projectile vomiting, hematemesis, poor weight gain, lethargy, or respiratory compromise.
Acid suppression (PPIs/H2 blockers) is not indicated for uncomplicated physiologic reflux and carries real risks; conservative feeding measures are first-line.
Reflux peaks around 4 months and resolves in most infants by 12-18 months as they upright and begin solids.

cases.title: Blood-Streaked Stools in a Fussy Breastfed Infant
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents bring in a 6-week-old breastfed girl for streaks of blood in her stool and increased fussiness.
cases.history: A 6-week-old exclusively breastfed girl is brought to clinic because her parents have noticed thin streaks of bright red blood and mucus mixed into several of her stools over the past week. She has also become noticeably fussier, drawing up her legs and crying during and after feeds, with increased spit-up. She continues to feed and is still gaining weight, though slightly less than before. She has no fever, no vomiting blood, no bilious emesis, and no lethargy. Stools are otherwise soft and seedy, not hard. There has been no fever or sick contacts. The mother drinks two glasses of cow's milk daily and eats dairy regularly. Birth at term, uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. Family history is notable for eczema and asthma in the father and a sibling with a milk allergy. No medications other than vitamin D drops. Mother takes a prenatal vitamin. No recent antibiotics. The infant has mild facial eczema.
cases.exam: Generally well-appearing but intermittently fussy infant who is consolable. Mild erythematous patches of eczema on the cheeks. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat; mucous membranes moist. Lungs clear; cardiac exam regular without murmur. Abdomen soft, non-distended, non-tender, with normal bowel sounds and no masses or hepatosplenomegaly. Perianal skin shows mild erythema but no fissure, fistula, or significant excoriation. A diaper contains a soft, seedy stool with visible streaks of blood and mucus. Hydration is normal; capillary refill brisk. No pallor. Neurologic exam age-appropriate.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: In a well-appearing, growing, breastfed 6-week-old with blood-and-mucus-streaked stools, eczema, and a strong atopic family history, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Anal fissure as the primary diagnosis
Cow's milk protein allergy
Necrotizing enterocolitis
Infectious (bacterial) colitis
stages.explanation: The classic presentation of cow's milk protein-induced allergic proctocolitis is a well-appearing, thriving (or near-thriving) breastfed infant in the first months of life with blood- and mucus-streaked stools, often with fussiness and an atopic background (eczema, family history of allergy). Maternal dairy ingestion passes antigenic proteins into breast milk. An anal fissure can cause bright blood but the exam shows no fissure and would not explain mucus or systemic fussiness. NEC presents with a sick, often premature infant, distension, and pneumatosis—not this well infant. Bacterial colitis usually causes fever, larger-volume bloody diarrhea, and ill appearance; this benign, indolent course favors milk protein allergy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: This mother wishes to continue breastfeeding. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Have the mother eliminate all cow's milk from her diet
Stop breastfeeding entirely and switch the infant to a standard cow's-milk-based formula
Begin empiric oral antibiotics for presumed bacterial colitis as initial therapy
Start the infant on a proton pump inhibitor as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Because breastfeeding can and should continue, the first-line treatment is strict MATERNAL elimination of cow's milk protein (all dairy), since the antigen reaches the infant through breast milk; soy is often eliminated too given cross-reactivity. Symptoms typically improve within 2-4 weeks. Switching to a standard cow's-milk formula would expose the infant to the very allergen causing symptoms and is wrong; if formula were needed, an extensively hydrolyzed (or amino-acid) formula would be chosen. A PPI does not treat an allergic colitis. Antibiotics are inappropriate without evidence of infection and would not address the immunologic mechanism.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
After 3 weeks of strict maternal dairy elimination:
Stool: no longer grossly bloody; occasional mucus only
Infant fussiness markedly improved per parents
Weight: 5.1 kg (resumed tracking along 25th percentile)
No fever, no pallor; hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL
Maternal report: full adherence to dairy-free diet

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: The parents ask whether their daughter will outgrow this. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: Cow's milk protein allergy is lifelong and she should avoid all dairy permanently as the immediate next step
This allergy guarantees she will develop a severe peanut anaphylaxis as the immediate next step
Most infants outgrow cow's milk protein allergy by about 1 year of age,
She should avoid the MMR vaccine because it contains milk protein as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Non-IgE-mediated cow's milk protein allergy (allergic proctocolitis) carries an excellent prognosis: the majority of infants develop tolerance by around 12 months, and many by 6 months, so a supervised gradual reintroduction of dairy (a 'milk ladder' or in-office challenge) is reasonable later in infancy. It is not lifelong for most. While atopic infants have higher rates of other allergies, this condition does not 'guarantee' peanut anaphylaxis. The MMR vaccine does not contain cow's milk protein and is not contraindicated by milk allergy. Accurate, optimistic counseling supports adherence and avoids unnecessary lifelong restriction.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If maternal dairy elimination had NOT improved symptoms and the infant were formula-fed instead, what would be the most appropriate formula choice?
options: Extensively hydrolyzed formula
Lactose-free cow's-milk formula in this setting
Standard soy-based formula in this setting
Goat's milk formula as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: For a formula-fed infant with cow's milk protein allergy, the recommended choice is an EXTENSIVELY HYDROLYZED formula, escalating to an amino-acid-based (elemental) formula if symptoms persist, because the protein is broken into non-antigenic fragments. Standard soy formula is avoided as a first choice because a meaningful proportion of milk-allergic infants also react to soy (cross-reactivity). A lactose-free cow's-milk formula still contains intact cow's milk PROTEIN—the allergen is the protein, not the lactose—so it would not help. Goat's milk shares cross-reactive proteins with cow's milk and is also inappropriate. Hydrolyzed/elemental formulas remove the offending antigen.

cases.diagnosis: Cow's milk protein allergy (allergic proctocolitis) in a breastfed infant
pearls: Cow's milk protein allergic proctocolitis presents in a well-appearing, thriving infant with blood- and mucus-streaked stools, often atopic background—distinct from the sick infant of NEC or infectious colitis.
Breastfeeding can continue; first-line therapy is strict maternal elimination of dairy (and often soy), with improvement over 2-4 weeks.
The allergen is the milk PROTEIN, not lactose—lactose-free formula does not help; use extensively hydrolyzed, then amino-acid-based, formula.
Prognosis is excellent: most infants develop tolerance by about 1 year, allowing supervised dairy reintroduction.

cases.title: A 10-Month-Old Falling Off His Growth Curve
cases.chiefComplaint: A 10-month-old boy is referred because his weight has been crossing downward across percentiles.
cases.history: A 10-month-old boy is brought to clinic after his pediatrician noted his weight has dropped from the 50th percentile at 4 months to below the 5th percentile now, crossing two major percentile lines, while length has remained near the 25th percentile and head circumference is preserved. He was breastfed until 5 months, then transitioned to formula; the family has been diluting formula to 'make it last' and offering mostly juice and water in a bottle. Solid food introduction has been minimal and mealtimes are reportedly stressful and rushed. He has no vomiting, no chronic diarrhea, no recurrent infections, and no respiratory symptoms. Stools are normal. He met early motor milestones but seems less interactive lately. Born at term, appropriate birth weight (3.3 kg). No significant past medical history, no medications, no known allergies. Social history: young single mother with limited financial resources and food insecurity; maternal depression is suspected. No family history of cystic fibrosis, celiac, or thyroid disease. Immunizations are up to date.
cases.exam: Thin, somewhat withdrawn infant with decreased subcutaneous fat and mild loss of buttock fat folds. Anterior fontanelle soft. Mucous membranes moist. No dysmorphic features. Lungs clear; cardiac exam regular without murmur. Abdomen soft, non-distended, non-tender, no organomegaly or masses. No edema, no rashes suggestive of zinc or essential fatty acid deficiency. Hair and skin normal. No lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam shows age-appropriate tone but reduced social engagement and eye contact with the examiner. No signs of trauma or neglect-related injury. Hydration adequate.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate FIRST step in evaluating this infant's failure to thrive?
options: Order an extensive panel of laboratory and genetic tests immediately
Admit for a hospital-based evaluation and central line placement
Take a detailed dietary, feeding,
Start appetite-stimulant medication as the first step today
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of the failure-to-thrive workup is a thorough history—detailed dietary/caloric intake, feeding technique, and psychosocial assessment—plus direct observation of a feeding, because the overwhelming majority of FTT is due to inadequate caloric intake, often with psychosocial contributors, rather than occult organic disease. Reflexive broad lab/genetic testing has low yield when the history points to intake problems and should be guided by clinical clues. Hospital admission is reserved for severe malnutrition, safety concerns, or failed outpatient management. Appetite stimulants are not appropriate first-line therapy and don't address the underlying cause. History and feeding observation direct everything that follows.

stages.label: DIETARY & SOCIAL ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Caloric intake estimate: ~60% of recommended for age/weight
Formula being over-diluted (extra water added to stretch supply)
Excessive juice/water intake displacing calorie-dense feeds
Feeding observation: distracted, hurried feeds; poor caregiver-infant interaction
Household: food insecurity present; maternal PHQ-9 screen positive for moderate depression
Basic labs: CBC mild iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 9.8 g/dL); CMP, TSH, celiac screen, urinalysis all normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on this assessment, what is the most likely etiology of this child's failure to thrive?
options: Psychosocial/nonorganic FTT from inadequate caloric intake
Organic FTT from an occult malabsorptive disorder as the primary diagnosis
Congenital hypothyroidism as the primary diagnosis
Cystic fibrosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The data point clearly to nonorganic (psychosocial) failure to thrive driven by inadequate calories: documented intake at ~60% of needs, over-diluted formula, calorie-poor juice/water, disrupted feeding interactions, food insecurity, and maternal depression—with a normal screening workup. The pattern (weight affected first, then length, with relatively preserved head circumference) is typical of caloric undernutrition. An occult malabsorptive disorder is unlikely given normal celiac screen, normal stools, and no malabsorptive signs. The normal TSH excludes hypothyroidism, and the absence of respiratory/GI symptoms with normal labs makes cystic fibrosis improbable. The iron-deficiency anemia is a consequence of poor nutrition, not a separate organic cause.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Refer immediately to gastroenterology for endoscopy as initial therapy
Report the family to child protective services for neglect as the first action as initial therapy
Provide nutritional rehabilitation with calorie-dense feeds, correct formula preparation, connect the family to food/social resources, treat maternal depression,
Prescribe a high-dose multivitamin and recheck in 6 months as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management of nonorganic FTT is multidisciplinary and centers on increasing caloric intake (correct formula reconstitution, calorie-dense foods, limiting juice), addressing the social determinants (WIC/food assistance, social work), treating maternal depression, supporting feeding behaviors, and frequent weight checks to confirm catch-up growth. Immediate GI referral/endoscopy is unwarranted with a normal screen and a clear nutritional cause. CPS reporting is reserved for confirmed abuse/neglect or non-engagement after support is offered—jumping to it first is inappropriate when the issue is poverty and depression amenable to help. A multivitamin with a 6-month recheck is far too passive for an undernourished infant who needs close monitoring.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: When initiating nutritional rehabilitation in a chronically undernourished infant, which complication must be anticipated and prevented?
options: Malignant hyperthermia as the leading concern
Acute hemolytic anemia as the leading concern
Immediate hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state
Refeeding syndrome with hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia,
stages.explanation: Reintroducing nutrition (especially carbohydrates) to a malnourished child triggers an insulin surge that drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium intracellularly—refeeding syndrome—which can cause arrhythmias, weakness, and cardiac failure. Prevention involves advancing calories gradually, monitoring and repleting electrolytes (particularly phosphate), and supplementing thiamine. Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state is a complication of uncontrolled diabetes, not refeeding. Acute hemolytic anemia is unrelated to refeeding. Malignant hyperthermia is an anesthetic reaction. Anticipating refeeding syndrome and monitoring electrolytes during catch-up nutrition is the key safety point in refeeding a child with established malnutrition.

cases.diagnosis: Nonorganic (psychosocial) failure to thrive due to inadequate caloric intake
pearls: Most failure to thrive is inadequate caloric intake; a careful diet/feeding history, psychosocial assessment, and observed feeding outyield reflexive broad lab testing.
The undernutrition growth pattern affects weight first, then length, with head circumference relatively spared; symmetric early stunting suggests genetic or intrauterine causes.
Treat the cause: correct formula dilution, add calorie-dense feeds, limit juice, address food insecurity and caregiver mental health, and follow weight closely.
Advance feeds gradually and monitor phosphate, potassium, and magnesium to prevent refeeding syndrome in chronically malnourished children.

cases.title: A Positive Newborn Screen in a Sleepy 2-Week-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: A 2-week-old girl is brought in after the state newborn screen flagged an abnormal result.
cases.history: A 2-week-old girl is brought to clinic after the state newborn screening laboratory reported an abnormal thyroid result and recommended urgent follow-up. The parents had been unconcerned: she sleeps a great deal—often more than they expect—and they describe her as a 'very calm' baby who rarely cries. They report some difficulty with feeding (slow, sleepy feeds) and increasing constipation, with stools only every 2-3 days. They also note her tongue seems large and she still has prolonged jaundice. Birth at 40 weeks by uncomplicated delivery; birth weight 3.6 kg. She was discharged at 48 hours and the newborn screen was drawn at 36 hours. No family history of thyroid disease is reported, and the pregnancy involved no maternal thyroid medication or iodine exposure. No other medications. The family recently moved from a region with limited prenatal care. Immunizations are on schedule (hepatitis B given).
cases.exam: Sleepy but arousable infant with a hoarse, low-pitched cry. Notable for a large protruding tongue (macroglossia) and persistent jaundice. The anterior fontanelle is large and a posterior fontanelle is also palpable and enlarged. There is an umbilical hernia. Skin is cool, dry, and mottled. Cardiac exam regular without murmur; lungs clear. Abdomen soft with mild distension and the umbilical hernia; no hepatosplenomegaly. Tone is mildly decreased (hypotonia) with sluggish reflexes. No dysmorphic facies beyond those noted. Capillary refill slightly prolonged. No goiter is palpable in the neck.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The newborn screen flagged thyroid disease. What confirmatory testing should be obtained immediately?
options: Serum cortisol and ACTH stimulation test
Thyroid ultrasound before any blood work
Serum (venous) TSH and free T4
Repeat the newborn screening filter-paper card only
stages.explanation: An abnormal newborn thyroid screen must be confirmed promptly with SERUM TSH and free T4, because congenital hypothyroidism requires urgent treatment to protect the developing brain and the diagnosis cannot rest on a screening card alone. Simply repeating the filter-paper screen delays definitive confirmation and treatment. A thyroid ultrasound (or scintigraphy) helps determine the ETIOLOGY (e.g., dysgenesis, ectopic gland, agenesis) but should NOT delay confirmatory bloodwork or the start of therapy. Cortisol/ACTH testing evaluates the adrenal axis, not the thyroid, and is irrelevant to this presentation. Confirmatory serum thyroid function tests are the immediate priority.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY LABS
stages.content:
Serum TSH: 145 µIU/mL (markedly elevated)
Free T4: 0.4 ng/dL (low)
Total T4: 3.1 µg/dL (low)
Thyroid ultrasound: ectopic sublingual thyroid tissue; no orthotopic gland in the thyroid bed
Newborn screen (repeat reference): consistent with primary congenital hypothyroidism

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These results (high TSH, low free T4, ectopic gland) confirm which diagnosis, and what is its most common underlying cause?
options: Primary congenital hypothyroidism, most commonly due to thyroid dysgenesis
Transient hypothyroidism from maternal iodine deficiency only as the primary diagnosis
Central (secondary) hypothyroidism from pituitary failure as the primary diagnosis
Congenital hyperthyroidism (neonatal Graves disease) as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An elevated TSH with low free T4 indicates PRIMARY hypothyroidism (the thyroid gland itself is failing while the pituitary appropriately raises TSH), and the most common cause of permanent congenital hypothyroidism worldwide (in iodine-sufficient regions) is thyroid dysgenesis—agenesis, hypoplasia, or an ectopic gland, as confirmed here by the sublingual ectopic tissue. Central hypothyroidism would show LOW or inappropriately normal TSH with low T4. Iodine deficiency causes transient/dyshormonogenetic disease and would not explain an ectopic gland. Neonatal Graves (hyperthyroidism) presents with tachycardia, irritability, and weight loss—the opposite of this hypothyroid, sleepy, constipated infant.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate treatment and how urgently should it begin?
options: Start methimazole to suppress the thyroid as initial therapy
Observe and re-test in 4 weeks before starting any therapy
Start levothyroxine immediately at full replacement dose
Begin a low dose of levothyroxine and titrate slowly over several months
stages.explanation: Congenital hypothyroidism demands PROMPT initiation of levothyroxine at a relatively high starting dose (approximately 10-15 µg/kg/day) to normalize thyroid hormone quickly, because the developing brain is critically dependent on thyroid hormone and delays cause irreversible intellectual disability. Starting low and titrating slowly risks undertreatment during the vulnerable neurodevelopmental window. Observation/re-testing wastes precious time once the diagnosis is confirmed. Methimazole treats hyperTHYROIDism and would be exactly the wrong drug here. The therapeutic goal is to normalize free T4 within ~2 weeks and TSH within ~1 month, with close monitoring during infancy.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the key reason for the urgency in treating congenital hypothyroidism?
options: To prevent immediate cardiac arrest as the immediate next step
To prevent a thyroid storm as the immediate next step
To prevent irreversible intellectual disability
Because untreated disease causes rapid weight loss and dehydration within days in this setting
stages.explanation: Thyroid hormone is essential for early brain development; untreated congenital hypothyroidism causes permanent intellectual disability (historically termed cretinism). The entire rationale for universal newborn screening and prompt levothyroxine is that early treatment (ideally within the first 2 weeks of life) largely prevents these neurodevelopmental sequelae, and outcomes are excellent when treatment begins early. Immediate cardiac arrest is not the concern. Thyroid storm is a hyperthyroid emergency, not relevant here. While feeding is sluggish, the danger is not acute dehydration within days but the slow, irreversible neurocognitive damage—hence the emphasis on early, adequate hormone replacement and follow-up.

cases.diagnosis: Primary congenital hypothyroidism (due to thyroid dysgenesis—ectopic gland)
pearls: Confirm an abnormal newborn thyroid screen with serum TSH and free T4 immediately; do not delay treatment for etiologic imaging.
High TSH + low free T4 = primary hypothyroidism; thyroid dysgenesis (ectopic/aplastic gland) is the most common permanent cause.
Early signs are subtle—prolonged jaundice, lethargy, poor feeding, constipation, macroglossia, large fontanelles, umbilical hernia, hypotonia.
Start levothyroxine promptly at ~10-15 µg/kg/day; early adequate treatment prevents otherwise irreversible intellectual disability.

cases.title: An Abnormal Metabolic Screen in a Healthy Newborn
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents of a healthy-appearing newborn boy are called back after an abnormal newborn metabolic screen.
cases.history: A term newborn boy, now 10 days old, is brought to clinic after the state newborn screening program reported an elevated amino acid on the first screen drawn at 30 hours of life, with a recommendation for urgent confirmatory testing. The baby appears entirely well: he is breastfeeding, gaining weight, and has no vomiting, lethargy, or seizures. The parents, who are first cousins, report no symptoms at home but mention a maternal cousin who had 'severe intellectual disability of unknown cause.' Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated; birth weight 3.4 kg. There is no maternal history of a restricted diet during pregnancy, and the mother is healthy. No medications. The family recently immigrated and the infant had the screen drawn appropriately after feeding had begun. On the call-back, parents note the baby sometimes has a faint 'musty' odor to his urine. No prior children. Immunizations: hepatitis B at birth.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, vigorous newborn with normal tone and a strong suck. Fair skin and light hair compared with the parents' complexion, and blue eyes. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. No dysmorphic features. Lungs clear; cardiac exam regular without murmur. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Neurologic exam normal for age with intact primitive reflexes; no jitteriness or seizures. A faint musty/mousy odor is appreciated. Skin without eczema currently. No hepatosplenomegaly. Hydration normal; capillary refill brisk.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The newborn screen flagged an elevated amino acid and the infant has a musty odor and relative hypopigmentation. What confirmatory test should be obtained?
options: A muscle biopsy as the first step today
Serum ammonia and lactate only as the first step today
Quantitative plasma amino acids
Urine organic acids alone as the first step today
stages.explanation: The clinical picture—elevated amino acid on screen, musty/mousy odor, fair skin/hair, light eyes—strongly suggests phenylketonuria (PKU). Confirmation requires QUANTITATIVE plasma amino acids to document elevated phenylalanine (with a low/normal tyrosine), and BH4 (tetrahydrobiopterin) cofactor evaluation must be assessed because BH4-deficient variants are managed differently (they need cofactor and neurotransmitter precursor therapy, not diet alone). Ammonia/lactate target urea cycle and mitochondrial disorders, not PKU. Urine organic acids screen for organic acidemias (a different class). A muscle biopsy is irrelevant to an aminoacidopathy. Confirming hyperphenylalaninemia and clarifying the BH4 status directs correct therapy.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY LABS
stages.content:
Plasma phenylalanine: 22 mg/dL (1330 µmol/L) — markedly elevated (normal <2 mg/dL)
Plasma tyrosine: low-normal
Phenylalanine:tyrosine ratio: markedly elevated
BH4 (tetrahydrobiopterin) loading test: no significant drop in phenylalanine (BH4-unresponsive)
Urine pterins: pattern consistent with classic PAH deficiency, not a BH4 synthesis defect

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These results confirm classic phenylketonuria. What is the underlying enzymatic defect?
options: Deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase
Deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase
Deficiency of homogentisate oxidase as the primary diagnosis
Deficiency of branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase
stages.explanation: Classic PKU is caused by deficiency of PHENYLALANINE HYDROXYLASE (PAH), the enzyme that converts phenylalanine to tyrosine using the cofactor tetrahydrobiopterin; phenylalanine accumulates (toxic to the developing brain) while tyrosine becomes relatively deficient (explaining hypopigmentation, since tyrosine feeds melanin synthesis). Branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase deficiency causes maple syrup urine disease (a sweet/maple odor, not musty). Galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase deficiency causes galactosemia. Homogentisate oxidase deficiency causes alkaptonuria (dark urine, ochronosis). The musty odor, fair coloring, elevated Phe with low tyrosine, and BH4-unresponsiveness pinpoint PAH deficiency.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for classic PKU, and when should it begin?
options: Liver transplantation in the neonatal period for all patients as initial therapy
A high-protein diet to overcome the enzyme block as initial therapy
Galactose elimination from the diet as initial therapy
A lifelong low-phenylalanine diet started as early as possible in the neonatal period
stages.explanation: Treatment of PKU is dietary: strict restriction of phenylalanine (limiting natural protein and providing a special phenylalanine-free formula/medical foods with adequate tyrosine and nutrition), begun as soon as possible after diagnosis and continued for life, with regular monitoring of blood phenylalanine. Early initiation is critical to prevent the otherwise inevitable intellectual disability. A high-protein diet would flood the body with phenylalanine and is exactly wrong. Galactose elimination treats galactosemia, a different disorder. Liver transplantation is not standard therapy for PKU. BH4-responsive patients may benefit from sapropterin, but this infant was BH4-unresponsive, so diet is the mainstay.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Years later, the patient's sister, also with PKU, becomes pregnant. What is the key concern of 'maternal PKU,' and how is it prevented?
options: She must avoid all dietary protein entirely during pregnancy as the leading concern
There is no fetal risk because the fetus is a carrier as the leading concern
The main risk is neonatal hypoglycemia treated after birth as the leading concern
High maternal phenylalanine is teratogenic
stages.explanation: Maternal PKU syndrome occurs when a mother with poorly controlled (high) phenylalanine exposes the fetus to teratogenic Phe levels, causing microcephaly, intrauterine growth restriction, congenital heart disease, and intellectual disability—even though the fetus itself may not have PKU. Prevention requires achieving tight maternal phenylalanine control BEFORE conception and maintaining it throughout pregnancy. The fetus is not protected simply by being a carrier. The concern is teratogenicity, not neonatal hypoglycemia. Eliminating ALL protein is unsafe—she needs a carefully controlled low-Phe diet with adequate (but limited) protein and supplementation, not total protein elimination.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: While on the low-phenylalanine diet, which clinical features would untreated or poorly controlled classic PKU produce, underscoring the need for strict lifelong control?
options: Cataracts and premature ovarian insufficiency as the immediate next step
Hemolytic anemia and gallstones as the immediate next step
Progressive intellectual disability, seizures, hyperactivity/behavioral problems, eczema,
Tall stature and accelerated puberty as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Untreated or poorly controlled PKU causes progressive, irreversible intellectual disability, seizures, hyperactivity and behavioral/psychiatric problems, eczematous skin, hypopigmentation, and a characteristic musty/mousy odor from phenylketones—which is why lifelong dietary phenylalanine control and regular blood-Phe monitoring are essential, including through adulthood. Tall stature and accelerated puberty are not features. Hemolytic anemia and gallstones suggest a hemolytic process, not PKU. Cataracts and premature ovarian insufficiency are hallmarks of GALACTOSEMIA, a different metabolic disease, not PKU. Recognizing the neurologic and dermatologic consequences reinforces why control cannot be relaxed in childhood or beyond.

pearls: PKU is an autosomal recessive deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase; clues include a musty/mousy odor, fair skin/hair, blue eyes, and—if untreated—intellectual disability, seizures, and eczema.
Confirm with quantitative plasma amino acids (high Phe, low tyrosine) and assess BH4 responsiveness, since cofactor-deficient forms need different therapy.
Treat with a lifelong low-phenylalanine diet plus Phe-free medical formula begun as early as possible to prevent irreversible neurologic damage.
Maternal PKU: uncontrolled maternal phenylalanine is teratogenic (microcephaly, cardiac defects); achieve strict Phe control before and throughout pregnancy.

cases.title: Jaundice, Vomiting, and a Crashing 5-Day-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: A 5-day-old boy admitted for jaundice now has vomiting, poor feeding, and signs of sepsis.
cases.history: A 5-day-old boy was admitted to the ward for worsening jaundice and is now deteriorating with persistent vomiting, poor feeding, and increasing lethargy. He was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy and was discharged at 48 hours, feeding well with breast milk and formula. Over the past two days, since milk feeds increased, he has become progressively jaundiced, vomits after most feeds, and is feeding poorly. The parents note he seems 'floppy' and less alert. He has had decreasing urine output. Birth weight 3.5 kg, now 3.2 kg. There is parental consanguinity (the parents are second cousins), and a prior sibling died in the neonatal period of presumed 'infection' that was never fully explained. No maternal infections during pregnancy; GBS negative; membranes ruptured 6 hours before delivery. The newborn metabolic screen was drawn at 30 hours but results are not yet back. No medications. Family is otherwise healthy.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, lethargic, jaundiced neonate with poor perfusion (capillary refill 4 seconds) and cool extremities. Scleral icterus and diffuse jaundice. Anterior fontanelle soft, mildly sunken. Mucous membranes dry. Cardiac exam tachycardic without murmur; lungs clear but tachypneic. Abdomen soft with hepatomegaly—liver edge 3 cm below the costal margin; no obvious mass. On ophthalmologic exam there is bilateral clouding of the lenses (cataracts) noted on red reflex. Tone is decreased (hypotonia) and he is poorly responsive to stimulation. No focal rash; no petechiae. He vomits during the exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This septic-appearing neonate needs immediate stabilization. Beyond resuscitation, which initial workup and empiric treatment are most appropriate?
options: Immediate exchange transfusion as the first intervention as the first step today
Reassurance and continued milk feeds with outpatient follow-up as the first step today
Full sepsis evaluation , broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, IV fluids,
Phototherapy alone for the jaundice as the first step today
stages.explanation: A lethargic, hypotensive, febrile neonate must be treated as sepsis: obtain blood, urine, and CSF cultures and start empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, give isotonic fluids, and check glucose, while simultaneously considering a metabolic emergency given the conjugated/cholestatic jaundice, hepatomegaly, vomiting after MILK feeds, cataracts, consanguinity, and a prior unexplained neonatal sibling death. Reassurance and continued feeds would be lethal in a crashing septic infant. Phototherapy treats the bilirubin but ignores the life-threatening systemic illness and underlying cause. Exchange transfusion is not the first-line intervention here. The combination of sepsis management plus metabolic suspicion drives correct care.

stages.label: LABS & CULTURES
stages.content:
Glucose: 38 mg/dL (low)
Total bilirubin 14 mg/dL with elevated DIRECT (conjugated) fraction 5.8 mg/dL
AST 320 / ALT 240 U/L; INR 1.8 (coagulopathy)
Blood culture: gram-negative rods — Escherichia coli growing
Urine reducing substances: POSITIVE; urine negative for glucose on dipstick (glucose oxidase)
Newborn screen (just returned): abnormal galactosemia marker; galactose-1-phosphate elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Positive urine reducing substances with a NEGATIVE urine glucose dipstick, E. coli sepsis, cataracts, hepatomegaly, and decompensation after milk feeds point to which diagnosis?
options: Classic galactosemia
Neonatal hemochromatosis as the primary diagnosis
Glycogen storage disease type I as the primary diagnosis
Hereditary fructose intolerance as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Classic galactosemia (GALT deficiency) presents in the first week after lactose (galactose) exposure with jaundice, vomiting, hepatomegaly, hypoglycemia, coagulopathy, cataracts, and a striking predisposition to E. coli sepsis. The lab signature is POSITIVE urine reducing substances (galactose) with a NEGATIVE glucose-specific dipstick—because the reducing sugar is galactose, not glucose. Hereditary fructose intolerance presents when fructose/sucrose is introduced (typically later, with solids/fruit), not with milk. Glycogen storage disease type I causes hypoglycemia and hepatomegaly but not E. coli sepsis or cataracts after milk. Neonatal hemochromatosis causes liver failure but lacks the reducing-substance and milk-trigger picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important dietary intervention once galactosemia is suspected?
options: Restrict dietary fructose and sucrose as initial therapy
Immediately STOP all lactose/galactose
Begin a low-protein, phenylalanine-free formula as initial therapy
Continue breast milk but add a digestive enzyme as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The life-saving intervention in galactosemia is to immediately ELIMINATE all galactose/lactose—stop breast milk and lactose-containing (standard cow's-milk) formula—and switch to a galactose-free formula, typically SOY-based (or elemental). This halts the accumulation of toxic galactose-1-phosphate and can rapidly reverse the acute decompensation. Breast milk is high in lactose and must be stopped; no enzyme supplement fixes the missing GALT. Fructose/sucrose restriction treats hereditary fructose intolerance, not galactosemia. A phenylalanine-free formula treats PKU. Note that, uniquely, breastfeeding is contraindicated in classic galactosemia because of the lactose load.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Even with strict dietary galactose restriction, the parents should be counseled that which long-term complication may still occur?
options: Long-term complications such as developmental delays, speech problems,
Inevitable progression to liver transplantation in all patients as the leading concern
Complete reversal of all sequelae with no residual problems as the leading concern
Acute hemolysis with every meal as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Although prompt galactose elimination reverses the acute, life-threatening manifestations (sepsis risk, liver dysfunction, cataracts can regress), classic galactosemia has well-recognized LONG-TERM complications that occur even with good dietary control—including cognitive and speech/language delays, motor abnormalities, and primary (premature) ovarian insufficiency in females—because endogenous galactose production and prenatal exposure contribute to injury. Thus a 'complete cure with no residual problems' is incorrect. Liver transplantation is not the universal endpoint once diet is controlled. Acute hemolysis with every meal is not a feature. Honest counseling about persistent neurodevelopmental and ovarian risks is essential despite excellent acute response to diet.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: The parents are second cousins and a prior child died in the neonatal period. What is the inheritance pattern of classic galactosemia and the recurrence risk for future pregnancies?
options: Mitochondrial; inherited only from the mother as the immediate next step
Autosomal recessive
Autosomal dominant; 50% recurrence risk as the immediate next step
X-linked recessive; only sons are affected as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Classic galactosemia is AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE (GALT gene mutations). Both parents being unaffected carriers gives a 25% recurrence risk with each pregnancy, and consanguinity (second cousins) raises the likelihood that both parents carry the same rare mutation—consistent with the prior sibling's unexplained neonatal death (likely undiagnosed galactosemia with E. coli sepsis). It is not X-linked—both sexes are affected equally (the patient here is male, and ovarian insufficiency affects affected females). It is not autosomal dominant (carriers are asymptomatic, so a 50% pattern with unaffected parents is wrong). It is not mitochondrial. Genetic counseling and prenatal/newborn diagnosis should be offered for future pregnancies.

cases.diagnosis: Classic galactosemia (galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase deficiency) with E. coli sepsis
pearls: Suspect classic galactosemia in a neonate with jaundice, vomiting, hepatomegaly, hypoglycemia, and cataracts who decompensates after milk feeds—classically with E. coli sepsis.
The lab clue is positive urine reducing substances with a negative glucose-specific dipstick (the reducing sugar is galactose, not glucose).
Treatment is immediate, lifelong elimination of all galactose/lactose; breastfeeding is contraindicated—switch to a soy-based or elemental formula.
Despite dietary control, long-term complications persist: developmental/speech delays and premature ovarian insufficiency in females.

cases.title: Newborn with Atypical Genitalia and Poor Feeding
cases.chiefComplaint: A 2-week-old phenotypically female-appearing infant presents with poor feeding, lethargy, and vomiting.
cases.history: A 2-week-old infant, born at 39 weeks via spontaneous vaginal delivery to a 28-year-old G2P2 mother, presents to the ICU with 2 days of progressively worsening feeding, vomiting, and lethargy. The pregnancy was uncomplicated; prenatal labs were normal. At birth the infant was noted to have atypical genitalia with clitoromegaly and partial labial fusion, prompting a 'sex assignment pending' note, but the family was discharged at 48 hours before workup completed. There is no fever, diarrhea, or sick contacts. The infant has lost 12% of birth weight. There were no medications during pregnancy. No allergies. The parents are non-consanguineous of Mediterranean descent. Family history is notable for an infant who died in the first month of life of unclear cause on the maternal side. The newborn metabolic screen was drawn but results have not yet returned. Feeding had been by formula every 3 hours but the infant now refuses the bottle and has had fewer wet diapers over the past 24 hours.
cases.exam: The infant is lethargic with delayed capillary refill (4 seconds), dry mucous membranes, and a sunken anterior fontanelle. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia without murmur. Abdomen is soft, non-distended, without palpable masses or organomegaly. Genitourinary exam shows clitoromegaly with a single urogenital opening and partial labial fusion; no palpable gonads in the labioscrotal folds bilaterally. Skin shows diffuse hyperpigmentation, most prominent at the areolae and labial folds. There is no rash or jaundice. Neurologic exam shows hypotonia and a weak cry. Lungs are clear bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the atypical genitalia, dehydration, and shock, what is the most appropriate immediate initial intervention?
options: Administer normal saline 20 mL/kg IV bolus
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and obtain blood cultures only in this clinical context
Perform emergent karyotype and assign sex before any treatment as the first step today
Give oral rehydration solution and observe for improvement as the first step today
stages.explanation: This infant is in hypovolemic/shock physiology (tachycardia, hypotension, delayed cap refill, weight loss). The immediate priority is volume resuscitation with isotonic fluid (NS 20 mL/kg) while simultaneously obtaining electrolytes, glucose, and a 'critical sample' (cortisol, 17-OHP, ACTH, renin, aldosterone) before steroids are given. Karyotype is important but never takes priority over resuscitating a crashing neonate. Antibiotics may be reasonable empirically for possible sepsis, but they do not address the underlying salt-wasting crisis and should not be the sole intervention. Oral rehydration is inadequate for a hemodynamically unstable, vomiting neonate and risks aspiration and continued decompensation. Recognizing the combination of atypical genitalia plus salt-wasting points strongly toward congenital adrenal hyperplasia.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 121 mEq/L
K 7.2 mEq/L
Cl 95 mEq/L
HCO3 16 mEq/L
Glucose 44 mg/dL
BUN 38 mg/dL
Cr 0.7 mg/dL
17-hydroxyprogesterone 18,500 ng/dL (markedly elevated)
Cortisol 2.1 µg/dL (low)
ACTH 410 pg/mL (elevated)
Plasma renin activity markedly elevated
Pelvic ultrasound: presence of a uterus and ovaries; no testes identified
ECG: peaked T waves

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which enzyme deficiency best explains this constellation of hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, virilization, and markedly elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone?
options: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency
21-hydroxylase deficiency
3β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase deficiency
11β-hydroxylase deficiency
stages.explanation: 21-hydroxylase deficiency is the cause in ~90-95% of congenital adrenal hyperplasia. The block prevents conversion of 17-OHP to 11-deoxycortisol and progesterone to deoxycorticosterone, so 17-OHP accumulates massively (the diagnostic marker) and is shunted into androgens, causing virilization of a 46,XX infant. Loss of mineralocorticoid synthesis produces the salt-wasting crisis (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia). 11β-hydroxylase deficiency causes virilization but, because deoxycorticosterone (a mineralocorticoid) accumulates, it typically causes hypertension and hypokalemia, not salt-wasting. 17α-hydroxylase deficiency causes hypertension and undervirilization (not ambiguous virilized 46,XX). 3β-HSD deficiency is rare and would not elevate 17-OHP to this degree; it elevates DHEA/17-OH-pregnenolone.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After fluid resuscitation, what is the most appropriate next pharmacologic step?
options: Oral fludrocortisone alone as initial therapy
Insulin and glucose infusion alone to drive down potassium
IV hydrocortisone plus correction of hyperkalemia
IV dexamethasone to suppress 17-OHP for diagnostic clarity
stages.explanation: Once the critical sample is obtained, the cornerstone of acute CAH crisis management is stress-dose IV hydrocortisone, which provides both glucocorticoid and (at high dose) some mineralocorticoid effect, halting the crisis. Concurrently, the life-threatening hyperkalemia and hypoglycemia must be treated (calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilization if ECG changes, insulin/glucose, and IV dextrose for hypoglycemia). Fludrocortisone is essential for long-term maintenance but is oral and too slow for acute crisis. Dexamethasone is avoided acutely because it is long-acting, lacks mineralocorticoid activity, and would suppress the diagnostic cortisol/17-OHP response if more sampling is needed. Treating potassium alone ignores the underlying cortisol deficiency driving the crisis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The infant's ECG shows peaked T waves with a widening QRS. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention for this finding?
options: Oral fludrocortisone in this presentation
Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) enema
Albuterol nebulization alone
IV calcium gluconate
stages.explanation: Peaked T waves with QRS widening signal cardiac membrane instability from severe hyperkalemia—a medical emergency. IV calcium gluconate (or chloride) is given first to stabilize the myocardium; it does not lower potassium but protects the heart while other measures (insulin/glucose, bicarbonate, beta-agonists) shift potassium intracellularly and removal therapies (Kayexalate, dialysis) eliminate it. Kayexalate works slowly and carries risk of intestinal necrosis in neonates, so it is not the immediate step for cardiac toxicity. Fludrocortisone addresses the underlying mineralocorticoid deficiency over hours to days, not the acute arrhythmia risk. Albuterol shifts potassium but is adjunctive and slower than calcium for membrane stabilization in a child with active ECG changes.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding long-term management and the family discussion about this 46,XX infant, which statement is most appropriate?
options: The infant requires lifelong glucocorticoid
Genetic counseling is unnecessary because CAH is not heritable with appropriate follow-up
Mineralocorticoid replacement can be stopped once the acute crisis resolves with appropriate follow-up
The infant should be raised male given the degree of virilization with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Classic salt-wasting 21-hydroxylase deficiency requires lifelong glucocorticoid (hydrocortisone) and mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) replacement, with added oral sodium chloride supplementation during infancy because of high salt-wasting needs. This infant is genetically 46,XX with normal internal female structures (uterus, ovaries), so female sex of rearing is standard; virilization of external genitalia reflects prenatal androgen excess, not chromosomal sex. Mineralocorticoid replacement is permanent in salt-wasters, not stopped after the crisis. CAH is autosomal recessive, so genetic counseling and discussion of recurrence risk (25%) and carrier testing/prenatal options for future pregnancies are essential. Stress-dosing education for illness is also critical to prevent future adrenal crises.

cases.diagnosis: Classic salt-wasting congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency
pearls: Any virilized 46,XX neonate with vomiting, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia in the second week of life has CAH salt-wasting crisis until proven otherwise.
Markedly elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone is the diagnostic hallmark of 21-hydroxylase deficiency, the cause of ~90-95% of CAH.
Acute management: isotonic fluids, stress-dose IV hydrocortisone, treat hyperkalemia (calcium gluconate first if ECG changes) and hypoglycemia—obtain the critical sample before steroids.
Salt-wasters need lifelong hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone with sodium supplementation in infancy; counsel families on autosomal recessive inheritance and sick-day stress dosing.

cases.title: Adolescent with Vomiting and Rapid Deep Breathing
cases.chiefComplaint: A 12-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes presents with one day of vomiting, abdominal pain, and rapid breathing.
cases.history: A 12-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 3 years ago) is brought to the ICU with 1 day of nausea, repeated nonbloody vomiting, and diffuse abdominal pain. Over the past several days she had a viral upper respiratory illness, and her mother reports she missed several insulin doses because she 'wasn't eating much.' Her home regimen is insulin glargine 18 units nightly and insulin aspart with meals per carbohydrate ratio. She has had polyuria, polydipsia, and progressive fatigue for 2 days. No fever currently. No dysuria. No recent travel. No known allergies. She lives with her mother and younger brother; she is in 7th grade. Family history is notable for type 1 diabetes in a maternal aunt and Hashimoto thyroiditis in her mother. Her last hemoglobin A1c three months ago was 9.4%. She has had two prior episodes of diabetic ketoacidosis, both associated with intercurrent illness and insulin omission. On arrival she is drowsy but arousable and complains that bright light bothers her eyes.
cases.exam: The patient is lethargic but arousable, oriented to person and place. She has deep, sighing respirations (Kussmaul) and a fruity odor on her breath. Mucous membranes are dry and capillary refill is 3 seconds. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia without murmur. The abdomen is diffusely tender without rebound or guarding; bowel sounds are present. Neurologic exam shows no focal deficits, pupils equal and reactive, no neck stiffness. There is no rash. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally with no crackles. Skin turgor is reduced.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step in managing this patient?
options: Begin isotonic fluid resuscitation
Give oral fluids and recheck glucose in 2 hours as the first step today
Administer IV sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidosis as the first step today
Immediate IV bolus of regular insulin 0.1 unit/kg as the first step today
stages.explanation: The first priority in pediatric DKA is restoring circulating volume with isotonic crystalloid (0.9% NaCl), typically 10-20 mL/kg over the first hour, while obtaining baseline labs (glucose, venous blood gas, electrolytes, beta-hydroxybutyrate). Fluids alone begin to improve perfusion and lower glucose.
