stages.explanation: For pediatric ITP, management is guided by bleeding severity rather than the platelet number alone. A child with only minor cutaneous bleeding (skin and brief epistaxis) and no wet purpura or serious hemorrhage can be safely observed, since childhood ITP is usually self-limited and resolves within weeks to months. Anticipatory guidance, avoiding contact sports and antiplatelet drugs, and return precautions are appropriate. Platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening bleeding because transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed. Splenectomy and cytotoxic agents like cyclophosphamide are last-resort options for chronic, refractory disease, not first-line therapy. If pharmacologic therapy were needed for more significant bleeding, IVIG, anti-D, or corticosteroids would be first-line — not transfusion or surgery.

stages.label: ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE
stages.question: What is the most important sign the parents should be counseled to watch for and seek emergency care?
options: Mild bruising after rough play in this setting
Headache, vomiting, or altered mental status
A few new petechiae on the legs
A single short nosebleed that stops with pressure
stages.explanation: The most feared complication of ITP is intracranial hemorrhage, which is rare (<1%) but potentially fatal. Parents must be told to seek emergency care for headache, vomiting, lethargy, altered mental status, or any neurologic change, as these may signal CNS bleeding. New petechiae and minor bruising are expected manifestations of thrombocytopenia and do not require emergency evaluation. A brief nosebleed that resolves with pressure is minor cutaneous bleeding and can be managed at home. Recognizing the warning signs of serious bleeding, particularly intracranial hemorrhage, is the cornerstone of safe outpatient observation.

pearls: ITP classically presents as isolated thrombocytopenia in a well-appearing child 1-2 weeks after a viral illness, with a normal exam aside from bleeding.
Hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, bone pain, or additional cytopenias should prompt evaluation for leukemia before attributing to ITP.
Treat the patient, not the platelet count: observation suffices for minor bleeding; reserve IVIG, anti-D, or steroids for significant bleeding.
Intracranial hemorrhage is the rare but life-threatening complication — counsel on headache, vomiting, and neurologic changes.

cases.title: Bloody Diarrhea and Decreasing Urine in a Preschooler
cases.chiefComplaint: Five days of bloody diarrhea now with pallor, lethargy, and markedly decreased urine output.
cases.history: A 3-year-old girl is transferred to the PICU after 5 days of crampy abdominal pain and diarrhea that became grossly bloody on day 3. Over the last 24 hours she has become pale, increasingly tired, and her parents note she has made only one small wet diaper since yesterday. She had been at a petting zoo and ate an undercooked hamburger at a family barbecue about a week before symptoms began. There has been no high fever; vomiting has been intermittent. She takes no chronic medications. Notably, the family was given no antibiotics, and no antimotility agents were used. She has no known drug allergies. Immunizations are current. Birth and developmental history are unremarkable. Family history is negative for renal disease, bleeding disorders, or hemolytic anemia. No similar prior episodes. A sibling who attended the same barbecue had brief non-bloody diarrhea that resolved. There is no travel outside the region and no known toxin exposure beyond the food history.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, pale, and lethargic but arousable. Notable scleral icterus and conjunctival pallor. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. Mild periorbital and pedal edema is present. Abdomen is mildly distended with diffuse tenderness, hyperactive bowel sounds, and no rebound or guarding; liver and spleen are not enlarged. No purpura but a few scattered petechiae are noted on the lower legs. Cardiovascular exam shows tachycardia with a soft flow murmur and no gallop. Lungs are clear. Neurologic exam shows lethargy without focal deficit or seizure activity. The blood pressure is elevated for age, consistent with volume overload and renal involvement.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given bloody diarrhea after a foodborne exposure, which organism and toxin are most likely driving this evolving illness?
options: Salmonella enteritidis enterotoxin
Rotavirus as the first step today
Clostridioides difficile toxin B
Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli
stages.explanation: The combination of bloody diarrhea following undercooked ground beef and farm-animal exposure, now progressing to pallor and oliguria, is the classic prodrome of Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (commonly O157:H7) leading to hemolytic uremic syndrome. Shiga toxin damages endothelium, especially in the renal microvasculature, triggering microangiopathic hemolysis, platelet consumption, and acute kidney injury. Salmonella causes invasive gastroenteritis but is not the typical cause of HUS. C. difficile causes pseudomembranous colitis, usually after antibiotics, not classic STEC-HUS. Rotavirus causes watery, non-bloody diarrhea and dehydration, not hemolytic anemia and renal failure. The food and animal exposures plus the hemolytic/renal evolution point clearly to STEC.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL
MCV 88 fL
Platelets 38 ×10³/µL
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: numerous schistocytes and helmet cells, polychromasia
Reticulocyte count 5.8%
LDH 1850 U/L
Haptoglobin <10 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL (indirect 2.6)
Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline ~0.3)
BUN 64 mg/dL
Potassium 6.1 mEq/L
Direct Coombs: negative
Stool PCR: positive for Shiga toxin genes (stx1/stx2)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which triad confirms the diagnosis in this patient?
options: Anemia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated PT/aPTT in this clinical context
Hemolytic anemia, positive Coombs test, and splenomegaly
Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia,
Thrombocytopenia, fever, and a positive ANA as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Hemolytic uremic syndrome is defined by the triad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (Coombs-negative hemolysis with schistocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, high reticulocytes), thrombocytopenia from platelet consumption in microthrombi, and acute kidney injury (rising creatinine/BUN). The negative Coombs test confirms a mechanical, non-immune hemolysis. Coagulation studies (PT/aPTT) are characteristically normal in HUS, distinguishing it from DIC, so the first option is wrong. A positive Coombs would indicate autoimmune hemolysis, not microangiopathy, making the third option wrong. Fever with a positive ANA suggests lupus, not HUS. The laboratory triad here — schistocytes, low platelets, and azotemia — is diagnostic of HUS.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention should be specifically AVOIDED in STEC-associated HUS because it worsens outcomes?
options: Renal replacement therapy for refractory hyperkalemia or fluid overload
Careful fluid and electrolyte management as initial therapy
Antibiotic therapy directed at the E. coli
Packed red blood cell transfusion for symptomatic anemia
stages.explanation: Antibiotics are contraindicated in STEC-associated HUS because bacterial killing and the resulting stress response can increase Shiga toxin release, and observational data link antibiotic use to a higher risk of progression to HUS. Management is therefore supportive: meticulous fluid and electrolyte balance (this patient has hyperkalemia and likely volume overload), transfusion of packed red cells for symptomatic anemia, and dialysis when there is refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, uremia, or fluid overload. Antimotility agents should also be avoided. The other listed interventions are all appropriate supportive measures. Recognizing that antibiotics may worsen STEC-HUS is a high-yield, counterintuitive teaching point.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Her potassium is 6.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves on ECG, and she remains oliguric despite cautious fluids. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Initiate renal replacement therapy (dialysis)
Administer a large normal saline bolus and recheck in 6 hours
Give oral potassium binder and continue observation
Start an ACE inhibitor for the hypertension
stages.explanation: This child has life-threatening hyperkalemia with ECG changes in the setting of oliguric acute kidney injury that is not responding to conservative measures. Dialysis (renal replacement therapy) is indicated for the classic refractory indications — hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, uremia, and fluid overload — and is the definitive next step here. Temporizing measures (calcium gluconate for membrane stabilization, insulin/glucose, beta-agonists) can buy time, but with anuria and persistent hyperkalemia, dialysis is required. A large saline bolus is dangerous given volume overload and could precipitate pulmonary edema and worsen hypertension. Oral binders act too slowly for an emergency with ECG changes. ACE inhibitors reduce renal perfusion and are inappropriate in acute oliguric AKI.

pearls: Classic HUS triad: Coombs-negative microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury, typically days after STEC bloody diarrhea.
Normal PT/aPTT distinguishes HUS from DIC; schistocytes and a negative Coombs confirm mechanical, non-immune hemolysis.
Avoid antibiotics and antimotility agents in suspected STEC — both are associated with progression to HUS.
Supportive care is the mainstay; dialysis is indicated for refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, uremia, or fluid overload.

cases.title: Sudden Dark Urine and Pallor in a Young Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute onset of dark cola-colored urine, fatigue, and pallor after a family meal.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the ED with one day of dark, tea-colored urine, increasing tiredness, and pallor. His mother reports he became fussy and yellow-tinged this morning. Two days ago at a family gathering he ate a dish containing fava beans for the first time. He had a mild cold last week but is otherwise healthy. He has had no recent new medications, though his mother recalls he was given a sulfa antibiotic for a presumed ear infection several months ago, after which he had brief dark urine that she attributed to dehydration. There is no abdominal pain, no diarrhea, and no bleeding. He has no known drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. The family emigrated from a Mediterranean region. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle who had a similar episode of jaundice after taking an antimalarial. Birth history was normal; he had mild neonatal jaundice requiring brief phototherapy. There is no recent travel and no toxic ingestion.
cases.exam: Pale, mildly icteric, tired-appearing but alert boy. Scleral icterus and conjunctival pallor are evident. Mucous membranes are moist. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia with a soft II/VI systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border and no gallop. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft; the spleen tip is palpable 1-2 cm below the costal margin and mildly tender; no hepatomegaly. No lymphadenopathy. Skin shows no petechiae or purpura. There is no rash and no joint swelling. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. Urine in the collection cup appears dark reddish-brown.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The dark urine, pallor, and jaundice after fava bean ingestion most likely reflect which process?
options: Acute intravascular hemolysis with hemoglobinuria
Rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria
Lower urinary tract bleeding in this clinical context
Acute glomerulonephritis with hematuria
stages.explanation: Sudden pallor and jaundice with cola-colored urine following an oxidative trigger (fava beans) indicates acute intravascular hemolysis; the dark urine is hemoglobinuria from free hemoglobin released by lysed red cells. The clinical context — a Mediterranean-ancestry boy with a prior episode after a sulfa drug and a family history of drug-induced jaundice — strongly suggests G6PD deficiency. Glomerulonephritis causes hematuria with red cell casts and often hypertension/edema, not jaundice and anemia. Rhabdomyolysis follows muscle injury with myalgia and very high CK, absent here. Lower urinary tract bleeding produces frank blood, not the brown hemoglobin pigment, and would not cause jaundice or anemia. The picture is classic oxidative hemolysis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 7.4 g/dL
MCV 86 fL
Reticulocyte count 7.5%
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL
Platelets 280 ×10³/µL
Total bilirubin 4.1 mg/dL (indirect 3.6)
LDH 980 U/L
Haptoglobin <10 mg/dL
Direct Coombs: negative
Peripheral smear: bite cells and blister cells; supravital stain reveals Heinz bodies
Urinalysis: hemoglobin positive, 0-2 RBC/hpf (no intact red cells)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The bite cells, Heinz bodies, and Coombs-negative hemolysis after an oxidant exposure are characteristic of which condition?
options: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency
Hereditary spherocytosis
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia
Sickle cell disease as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Heinz bodies (denatured, precipitated hemoglobin) and bite/blister cells (where splenic macrophages have removed the Heinz body) are the hallmark of oxidative hemolysis in G6PD deficiency. G6PD maintains glutathione in its reduced state to protect red cells from oxidative stress; deficiency leaves cells vulnerable to triggers like fava beans, infections, and drugs (sulfonamides, antimalarials, nitrofurantoin). The Coombs-negative result excludes autoimmune hemolysis. Hereditary spherocytosis shows spherocytes and increased osmotic fragility, not bite cells/Heinz bodies, and is not triggered by oxidants. Sickle cell disease shows sickle cells and is not provoked by fava beans. The X-linked inheritance fits the affected male and maternal uncle. Smear findings plus the oxidant trigger confirm G6PD deficiency.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for this acute episode and going forward?
options: Lifelong daily folate plus immediate splenectomy as initial therapy
Iron supplementation to correct the anemia as initial therapy
Supportive care, hydration, transfusion if severe,
Chronic corticosteroids to suppress hemolysis as initial therapy
stages.explanation: G6PD-related hemolysis is usually self-limited once the offending oxidant is removed, because only the older red cells with the lowest enzyme activity are destroyed. Management is supportive: maintain hydration to protect the kidneys from hemoglobin pigment, monitor hemoglobin, and transfuse packed red cells only for severe symptomatic anemia. The essential long-term measure is patient/family education to avoid known oxidative triggers (fava beans, sulfonamides, antimalarials, nitrofurantoin, naphthalene). Splenectomy is not indicated for G6PD deficiency. Corticosteroids treat autoimmune hemolysis, not oxidative hemolysis. Iron is inappropriate and potentially harmful — the anemia is from hemolysis, not iron deficiency, and iron is recycled from the lysed cells.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC PITFALL
stages.question: If a quantitative G6PD enzyme assay is drawn during this acute hemolytic episode, what is the key caveat?
options: Levels may be falsely normal during acute hemolysis because young reticulocytes have higher enzyme activity
The assay is unaffected by the timing of the hemolytic episode as the immediate next step
The assay must be performed only during an acute crisis to be valid as the immediate next step
A normal result definitively excludes the diagnosis at any time as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: During acute hemolysis the oldest, most enzyme-deficient red cells are preferentially destroyed and replaced by young reticulocytes, which have relatively high G6PD activity. A quantitative assay drawn at this time can therefore be falsely normal, masking the deficiency. The recommended approach is to repeat the assay 2-3 months after the acute episode once the red cell population has equilibrated, or to interpret an apparently normal value with caution in a high-suspicion case. The timing caveat is the opposite of options stating the assay is unaffected or must be drawn during crisis. A normal level during hemolysis does not exclude the diagnosis, so the last option is wrong. Repeat testing after recovery is key.

pearls: Bite cells and Heinz bodies plus Coombs-negative hemolysis after an oxidant (fava beans, sulfa drugs, antimalarials) define G6PD deficiency.
X-linked recessive inheritance: suspect it in males of Mediterranean, African, or Asian descent with episodic hemolysis.
Treatment is supportive with trigger avoidance; hemolysis is self-limited as older RBCs are cleared.
Enzyme assays can be falsely normal during acute hemolysis — repeat 2-3 months later for confirmation.

cases.title: Recurring Yellow Eyes and Tiredness in a Schoolgirl
cases.chiefComplaint: Intermittent jaundice, fatigue, and an enlarging mass felt in the left upper abdomen.
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl is referred to clinic for recurrent episodes of yellow eyes and tiredness, most recently worse during a viral illness 1 week ago. Her parents note she has always looked slightly pale and tires more easily than peers during sports. She has had several similar episodes over the years, sometimes with darker urine. There has been no bloody diarrhea, no bleeding, and no recent new medications or oxidant exposures. She takes no chronic medications and has no drug allergies. Immunizations are current. Birth history is notable for prolonged neonatal jaundice that required phototherapy for several days. Family history is significant: her father had his gallbladder and spleen removed as a young adult for 'a blood problem,' and a paternal aunt has anemia and gallstones. There is no consanguinity. Development is normal and she is doing well in school. No recent travel or sick contacts beyond the recent cold.
cases.exam: Alert, well-developed girl with mild scleral icterus and pale conjunctivae. Cardiovascular exam reveals a soft I-II/VI systolic flow murmur and regular rhythm. Lungs clear. The abdomen is soft; the spleen is palpable 4 cm below the left costal margin, firm and nontender, accounting for the mass her parents felt. The liver edge is at the costal margin without enlargement. There is no lymphadenopathy. Skin shows no petechiae, purpura, or rash. There is mild scleral icterus but no overt skin jaundice. Neurologic exam is normal. No frontal bossing or other dysmorphic features are noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The chronic episodic jaundice, neonatal jaundice, splenomegaly, and strong family history of splenectomy/gallstones most suggest which broad mechanism of anemia?
options: Impaired red cell production (e.g., aplastic anemia)
Chronic blood loss with iron deficiency
Inherited hemolytic anemia with a red cell membrane defect
Acute oxidative hemolysis from a recent drug
stages.explanation: Lifelong intermittent jaundice beginning with neonatal hyperbilirubinemia, splenomegaly, and an autosomal dominant family history of early splenectomy and pigment gallstones points to a chronic inherited hemolytic anemia caused by a red cell membrane defect — hereditary spherocytosis. The splenomegaly reflects extravascular hemolysis where the spleen culls abnormal cells. Aplastic anemia causes pancytopenia and no hemolysis or splenomegaly. Chronic blood loss causes microcytic iron deficiency without jaundice or splenomegaly. Acute oxidative hemolysis (G6PD) is episodic and trigger-related but is X-linked, lacks the dominant family pattern shown here, and would not explain a lifelong dominantly inherited pattern with splenectomy in the father. A membrane defect best fits.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL
MCV 82 fL
MCHC 37.5 g/dL (elevated)
Reticulocyte count 8.2%
WBC 7.4 ×10³/µL
Platelets 265 ×10³/µL
Total bilirubin 3.0 mg/dL (indirect 2.7)
LDH elevated; haptoglobin low
Direct Coombs: negative
Peripheral smear: numerous spherocytes lacking central pallor
Eosin-5-maleimide (EMA) binding test: decreased
Increased osmotic fragility on testing

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Spherocytes, an elevated MCHC, negative Coombs, decreased EMA binding, and increased osmotic fragility confirm which diagnosis?
options: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia
Hereditary spherocytosis
G6PD deficiency
Beta-thalassemia trait
stages.explanation: Hereditary spherocytosis results from defects in red cell membrane proteins (ankyrin, spectrin, band 3), producing spherocytes with a high MCHC (cells are dehydrated and densely hemoglobinized) and increased osmotic fragility. The negative direct Coombs test distinguishes it from autoimmune hemolytic anemia, which also shows spherocytes but is Coombs-positive. The eosin-5-maleimide binding test is decreased in HS and is now a preferred confirmatory test. Beta-thalassemia trait causes microcytosis with target cells and is not hemolytic with spherocytes. G6PD deficiency shows bite cells and Heinz bodies, not spherocytes, and has normal/decreased MCHC. The constellation — spherocytes, high MCHC, low EMA binding, increased osmotic fragility, Coombs-negative — is diagnostic of HS.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During her recent viral illness her hemoglobin dropped to 5.8 g/dL with a reticulocyte count of only 0.5%. Which complication has most likely occurred?
options: Acute iron deficiency as the leading concern
Hyperhemolytic crisis from sequestration
Splenic rupture as the leading concern
Aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 infection
stages.explanation: A sudden, severe drop in hemoglobin accompanied by an inappropriately LOW reticulocyte count in a child with chronic hemolysis is the signature of an aplastic crisis, classically caused by parvovirus B19, which transiently suppresses erythroid precursors in the marrow. Because patients with HS depend on brisk reticulocytosis to compensate for shortened red cell survival, halting production for even a week causes profound anemia. A hyperhemolytic or sequestration crisis would show a HIGH reticulocyte count as the marrow responds. Acute iron deficiency does not develop over days and would not suppress reticulocytes. Splenic rupture would present with acute pain, hypotension, and signs of intra-abdominal bleeding, not isolated reticulocytopenia. The low retic count points to parvovirus-induced aplastic crisis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After recovery, for long-term management of moderate hereditary spherocytosis, which counseling point is most appropriate?
options: Start chronic transfusions immediately to suppress the disease as initial therapy
Begin lifelong therapeutic phlebotomy to prevent iron overload as initial therapy
Recommend daily folic acid supplementation
Avoid all vaccinations to reduce hemolysis triggers as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For mild-to-moderate HS, supportive management includes folic acid supplementation to meet the demands of chronic increased erythropoiesis and surveillance for pigment (bilirubin) gallstones, which are common from chronic hemolysis. Splenectomy markedly reduces hemolysis and is curative of the anemia, but it is reserved for severe disease, growth failure, or refractory symptoms because of the lifelong infection risk; when performed, pre-splenectomy vaccination against encapsulated organisms is essential. Therapeutic phlebotomy is used for iron-overload states like hemochromatosis, not HS. Chronic transfusion is not indicated in mild-moderate disease and risks iron overload. Avoiding vaccines is incorrect and dangerous — vaccination, especially before any splenectomy, is critical. Folate plus monitoring is the right approach.

pearls: Spherocytes with elevated MCHC, increased osmotic fragility, and decreased EMA binding plus a negative Coombs define hereditary spherocytosis.
Autosomal dominant in most cases; suspect it with neonatal jaundice, splenomegaly, and a family history of gallstones or splenectomy.
A sudden hemoglobin drop with LOW reticulocytes signals a parvovirus B19 aplastic crisis in any chronic hemolytic anemia.
Manage with folic acid and gallstone surveillance; reserve splenectomy (with prior vaccination against encapsulated organisms) for severe disease.

cases.title: Pale, Poorly Growing Infant with a Big Belly
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive pallor, poor feeding, and abdominal distension in an infant of Mediterranean descent.
cases.history: A 1-year-old boy is brought to clinic for worsening pallor, irritability, and poor weight gain over the past several months. His parents report he has become increasingly tired, feeds poorly, and his abdomen looks larger. He was well at birth but became progressively pale starting around 6 months of age as fetal hemoglobin declined. He has no history of bleeding or dark urine and no oxidant drug exposures. He takes only a standard infant multivitamin and has no drug allergies. He is up to date on immunizations. He was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy; newborn screening in his country of origin was reportedly incomplete. The family recently emigrated from the eastern Mediterranean. Family history is highly relevant: both parents are healthy but are first cousins, and a maternal great-aunt died young of 'severe anemia.' Developmentally he is mildly delayed in gross motor milestones. There has been no recent travel since arrival and no toxic exposures.
cases.exam: Pale, small-for-age, irritable infant. Frontal bossing with prominent maxilla and mild widening of the skull are appreciated. Marked conjunctival pallor and faint scleral icterus. Cardiovascular exam shows tachycardia with a II/VI systolic flow murmur and active precordium. Lungs clear. The abdomen is distended; the liver is palpable 3 cm and the spleen 6 cm below the respective costal margins, both firm. No lymphadenopathy. Skin shows no petechiae or purpura. Growth parameters are below the 3rd percentile for weight. Neurologic exam shows mild gross-motor delay but is otherwise nonfocal. There is no edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The onset of severe anemia around 6 months of age, with hepatosplenomegaly and skeletal changes, is best explained by which mechanism?
options: Acute intravascular hemolysis from an oxidant exposure as the first step today
Autoimmune destruction of red cells as the first step today
Decline of protective fetal hemoglobin unmasking a defect in adult beta-globin production
Iron deficiency from inadequate dietary intake as the first step today
stages.explanation: Beta-globin synthesis defects do not manifest at birth because fetal hemoglobin (HbF, alpha2-gamma2) requires no beta chains. As HbF physiologically declines around 6 months and the infant must switch to adult hemoglobin (HbA, alpha2-beta2), a severe beta-globin defect (beta-thalassemia major) becomes clinically apparent with profound microcytic anemia, ineffective erythropoiesis, extramedullary hematopoiesis (hepatosplenomegaly), and marrow expansion causing skeletal changes such as frontal bossing. Acute oxidative hemolysis is episodic and trigger-related, not a chronic progressive course. Dietary iron deficiency would not cause hepatosplenomegaly or bony changes of this severity and is uncommon to this degree. Autoimmune hemolysis is not chronic from infancy with these skeletal findings. The timing tied to the HbF-to-HbA switch is the key clue.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 5.2 g/dL
MCV 58 fL
RDW elevated
Reticulocyte count 6% (inappropriately low for degree of anemia)
WBC 9.0 ×10³/µL
Platelets 320 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: marked microcytosis, hypochromia, target cells, nucleated RBCs, basophilic stippling
Ferritin 320 ng/mL (not low)
Hemoglobin electrophoresis: markedly elevated HbF, absent/very low HbA, elevated HbA2
Skull radiograph: 'hair-on-end' appearance of the calvarium

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The severe microcytic anemia with target cells, nucleated RBCs, a 'hair-on-end' skull, and electrophoresis showing high HbF/HbA2 with absent HbA confirms which diagnosis?
options: Iron deficiency anemia
Beta-thalassemia major
Alpha-thalassemia trait
Lead poisoning
stages.explanation: Beta-thalassemia major is confirmed by severe microcytic, hypochromic anemia with target cells and nucleated red cells, marrow expansion producing the 'hair-on-end' skull, and hemoglobin electrophoresis demonstrating markedly elevated HbF, elevated HbA2, and absent or minimal HbA (because beta-chain production is severely impaired). Iron deficiency would show LOW ferritin and a normal electrophoresis; this child's ferritin is normal/high. Lead poisoning causes microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling but does not produce absent HbA on electrophoresis or this degree of marrow expansion. Alpha-thalassemia trait causes mild microcytosis without transfusion dependence and a normal-to-borderline electrophoresis. The electrophoretic pattern of absent HbA with high HbF is the defining finding of beta-thalassemia major.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia major?
options: Intermittent oxidant avoidance and folate alone as initial therapy
Splenectomy as first-line definitive therapy as initial therapy
Regular chronic red cell transfusion programs with iron chelation, with allogeneic HSCT as the only curative option
Oral iron supplementation to correct the anemia as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Beta-thalassemia major requires a lifelong regular transfusion program to maintain hemoglobin, suppress ineffective endogenous erythropoiesis, and prevent the skeletal deformities and extramedullary hematopoiesis seen here. Because chronic transfusion inevitably causes iron overload, iron chelation therapy (deferasirox, deferoxamine, or deferiprone) is essential to prevent cardiac, hepatic, and endocrine iron toxicity. Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is currently the only curative therapy. Oral iron is strictly contraindicated — these patients are iron-overloaded, not iron-deficient. Folate is a useful adjunct but not the cornerstone. Splenectomy may reduce transfusion requirements in selected hypersplenic patients but is not first-line definitive therapy and carries infection risk. Transfusion plus chelation is the foundation of care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After years of regular transfusions, which complication is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality that chelation aims to prevent?
options: Progressive renal tubular acidosis
Recurrent oxidative hemolytic crises
Iron-overload cardiomyopathy
Vitamin B12 deficiency as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Transfusional iron overload is the dominant long-term threat in beta-thalassemia major. Deposited iron causes cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias (the leading cause of death), hepatic fibrosis/cirrhosis, and multiple endocrinopathies (diabetes, hypothyroidism, hypogonadism with delayed puberty, growth failure). Iron chelation therapy is specifically aimed at preventing these complications, and cardiac iron is monitored with T2* MRI. Vitamin B12 deficiency is not a characteristic complication. Oxidative hemolytic crises characterize G6PD deficiency, not thalassemia. Renal tubular acidosis is not a typical feature of iron overload. Recognizing iron-overload cardiomyopathy as the principal preventable cause of death — and the rationale for lifelong chelation — is the high-yield point.

pearls: Beta-thalassemia major presents around 6 months as protective HbF declines, with severe microcytic anemia, hepatosplenomegaly, and marrow-expansion skeletal changes (frontal bossing, 'hair-on-end' skull).
Electrophoresis shows markedly elevated HbF and HbA2 with absent or minimal HbA; ferritin is normal/high, not low.
Treatment is chronic transfusion plus iron chelation; allogeneic HSCT is the only cure. Never give oral iron.
Transfusional iron overload causes cardiomyopathy (leading cause of death) and endocrinopathies — the rationale for chelation and T2* MRI monitoring.

cases.title: Sudden Coughing Fit in a Playing Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt coughing and noisy breathing that began suddenly while playing.
cases.history: An 18-month-old boy is brought to the ED after a sudden episode of violent coughing, gagging, and brief color change that started abruptly about 2 hours ago while he was playing in the living room. His older sibling had been eating peanuts and snack foods nearby, and the parents found scattered nuts on the floor. After the initial paroxysm he calmed somewhat but has had persistent intermittent coughing and a new wheeze that the parents can hear. There was no preceding fever, runny nose, or illness. He has no history of asthma or recurrent wheeze. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. Birth and developmental history are normal; he is cruising and walking. Family history is unremarkable for cystic fibrosis or immunodeficiency. There has been no recent travel. He has been previously healthy with no prior episodes of choking, and the event had a clear, witnessed sudden onset during play near small food items.
cases.exam: Mildly distressed, intermittently coughing toddler who is alert and consolable between episodes. There is mild tachypnea with subtle intercostal retractions. Auscultation reveals decreased breath sounds and an expiratory wheeze localized to the RIGHT lung field, with clearer, more symmetric breath sounds on the left. There is no stridor at rest and no drooling. The oropharynx is clear without visible foreign body. There is no cyanosis at rest. Cardiovascular exam is normal aside from tachycardia. The abdomen is soft. He is well-hydrated. The key finding is the asymmetry of breath sounds with focal right-sided wheeze and air-entry reduction.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a witnessed sudden choking episode and focal unilateral wheeze, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: First presentation of reactive airway disease (asthma)
Bacterial pneumonia in this clinical context
Acute viral bronchiolitis
Foreign body aspiration
stages.explanation: A previously well toddler with an abrupt, witnessed choking event near small foods, followed by persistent cough and a FOCAL unilateral wheeze with asymmetric breath sounds, is the classic picture of foreign body aspiration. Toddlers are at peak risk because of oral exploration and immature airway protection, and aspirated objects (often peanuts/food) commonly lodge in the right main or lower lobe bronchus given its more vertical takeoff. Bronchiolitis causes diffuse, bilateral wheezing with a viral prodrome (absent here). A first asthma attack would also produce bilateral, not strictly localized, wheeze and usually has triggers/atopy. Bacterial pneumonia presents with fever and focal crackles, not a sudden choking onset. The sudden onset plus localized findings make aspiration the leading diagnosis.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Inspiratory chest radiograph: no obvious radiopaque foreign body; lungs appear roughly symmetric
Expiratory chest radiograph: hyperinflation/air trapping of the RIGHT lung with persistent lucency and mediastinal shift toward the LEFT on expiration
Lateral decubitus (right side down): right lung fails to deflate normally
No focal consolidation or effusion

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Why does the expiratory film show hyperinflation of the affected lung with mediastinal shift away from it?
options: The radiograph technique was performed incorrectly as the immediate next step
A ball-valve effect lets air in during inspiration but traps it on expiration
The foreign body completely obstructs the bronchus, causing distal collapse
There is a tension pneumothorax on the right as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A partially obstructing airway foreign body produces a ball-valve (check-valve) mechanism: the airway widens slightly on inspiration to admit air but narrows on expiration, trapping air distally. This causes the affected lung to hyperinflate and fail to deflate, which is best demonstrated on an expiratory or lateral decubitus film as persistent lucency with mediastinal shift AWAY from the affected side. Most aspirated foods like peanuts are radiolucent, so the inspiratory film is often normal — hence the value of expiratory views. Complete obstruction would instead cause distal atelectasis/collapse with shift toward the side. The technique is correct; these findings are expected. A tension pneumothorax would show absent lung markings with a visible pleural edge, not air trapping within an inflated lung.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The child is stable but has persistent wheeze and radiographic air trapping. What is the definitive next step?
options: Empiric antibiotics and outpatient follow-up
Rigid bronchoscopy for foreign body removal
Trial of inhaled bronchodilators and discharge if improved
Blind finger sweep of the oropharynx as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In a stable child with a strong history and imaging consistent with an aspirated foreign body, rigid bronchoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic and is the definitive management to retrieve the object. Delaying removal risks chronic obstruction, post-obstructive pneumonia, and bronchiectasis. Bronchodilators do not dislodge a mechanical obstruction and may give false reassurance. Antibiotics treat infection, not the obstruction, and do not address the underlying problem. A blind finger sweep is contraindicated because it can push the object deeper and worsen obstruction. For a fully obstructed, choking, unable-to-breathe child, basic life support back blows/abdominal thrusts apply — but this stable child with a lodged distal foreign body needs rigid bronchoscopy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this aspiration had gone unrecognized for weeks, which complication would be most likely?
options: Pulmonary embolism as the leading concern
Recurrent or persistent focal pneumonia
Spontaneous resolution with no sequelae
Development of chronic asthma in all cases
stages.explanation: A retained airway foreign body that goes unrecognized leads to chronic local inflammation and impaired clearance, producing recurrent or persistent pneumonia in the same lobe, atelectasis, and eventually bronchiectasis from chronic infection and airway damage. Organic material like peanuts is especially inflammatory. Spontaneous, sequelae-free resolution is not the expected outcome of a lodged object. While retained foreign bodies can cause chronic wheeze, they do not universally cause asthma. Pulmonary embolism is unrelated to airway foreign bodies. The classic teaching is to suspect retained foreign body in a child with recurrent focal pneumonia or localized persistent radiographic changes, which is why timely diagnosis and removal matter.

pearls: Suspect foreign body aspiration in a toddler with a sudden witnessed choking event and FOCAL unilateral wheeze with asymmetric breath sounds.
Most aspirated foods are radiolucent; an expiratory or lateral decubitus film shows air trapping with mediastinal shift AWAY from the affected side (ball-valve effect).
Rigid bronchoscopy is the definitive diagnostic and therapeutic procedure for a stable patient; avoid blind finger sweeps.
Unrecognized aspiration causes recurrent focal pneumonia and bronchiectasis — consider it when pneumonia recurs in the same location.

cases.title: Hives and Trouble Breathing After a Snack
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapid onset of widespread hives, wheezing, and faintness minutes after eating a cookie.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is rushed to the ED after developing diffuse hives, lip swelling, wheezing, and lightheadedness within 10 minutes of eating a cookie at a birthday party. The host later confirmed the cookie contained peanut. He has a known history of peanut allergy with a prior milder reaction (hives only) and carries an epinephrine auto-injector, but it was left at home today. He also has mild asthma controlled with an as-needed albuterol inhaler. He takes a daily cetirizine for allergic rhinitis and has no other medications. Allergies: peanuts (anaphylaxis) and tree nuts. Immunizations are current. He is developmentally normal and in 4th grade. Family history is notable for atopy — his mother has asthma and his sister has eczema. There was no recent illness. EMS reported he became increasingly anxious with audible wheeze and a feeling of throat tightness en route, and his symptoms were progressing rapidly at the time of arrival.
cases.exam: Anxious, flushed boy in moderate-to-severe respiratory distress. Diffuse urticaria over the trunk and extremities with angioedema of the lips and periorbital area. There is audible inspiratory stridor and expiratory wheeze; auscultation reveals diffuse bilateral wheezing with prolonged expiration and mild accessory muscle use. Cardiovascular exam shows marked tachycardia with weak peripheral pulses and delayed capillary refill (3 seconds), consistent with the hypotension. The abdomen is soft but he reports nausea and cramping. He is mentating but apprehensive. The combination of skin, respiratory, and cardiovascular involvement after a known allergen is evident on initial assessment.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This boy has urticaria/angioedema, respiratory compromise, and hypotension after a known allergen. What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Intravenous diphenhydramine as the first step today
Intramuscular epinephrine into the anterolateral thigh
Intravenous methylprednisolone as the first step today
Nebulized albuterol as the first step today
stages.explanation: This is anaphylaxis — multisystem involvement (skin, respiratory, cardiovascular) with hypotension after a known trigger. Intramuscular epinephrine into the anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) is the first-line, life-saving treatment and must be given immediately and without delay. Epinephrine reverses airway edema and bronchospasm (beta-2), supports blood pressure via vasoconstriction (alpha-1), and increases cardiac output (beta-1). Antihistamines like diphenhydramine treat cutaneous symptoms only and do nothing for airway or hypotension; they are adjuncts, never primary therapy. Albuterol helps bronchospasm but not laryngeal edema or shock. Corticosteroids have a slow onset and may help prevent biphasic reactions but do not treat the acute event. Nothing should delay IM epinephrine.

stages.label: DOSING & ROUTE
stages.question: What is the correct epinephrine dose and route for this 9-year-old (approximately 30 kg) in anaphylaxis?
options: 1 mg of 1:1000 epinephrine IM once as the immediate next step
0.3 mg of 1:10,000 epinephrine IV push immediately
0.15 mg of 1:1000 epinephrine subcutaneously in this setting
0.3 mg of 1:1000 epinephrine IM, may repeat every 5-15 minutes
stages.explanation: The correct dose is 0.01 mg/kg of 1:1000 (1 mg/mL) epinephrine intramuscularly, to a maximum of 0.3 mg in children (0.5 mg in adults). For a ~30 kg child that is 0.3 mg IM, equivalent to the 0.3 mg auto-injector, and it may be repeated every 5-15 minutes if symptoms persist. IV push epinephrine (using the dilute 1:10,000) is reserved for refractory shock or cardiac arrest in a monitored setting because bolus IV dosing risks arrhythmia and severe hypertension; it is not the initial route. A 1 mg IM dose is a dangerous overdose. The 0.15 mg dose and subcutaneous route are for smaller children/less effective absorption — subcutaneous absorption is too slow and unreliable in shock. IM into the thigh is standard.

stages.label: REASSESSMENT
stages.content:
After IM epinephrine, high-flow oxygen, IV access, and a normal saline bolus (20 mL/kg):
BP 102/64 mmHg
HR 124 bpm
RR 26 /min
SpO2 97% on oxygen
Urticaria fading, lip swelling decreasing, wheeze improved with adjunctive nebulized albuterol
Patient more comfortable and alert

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He has responded well. What is the most appropriate disposition and counseling plan?
options: Admit for a 7-day course of IV antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
Immediate discharge home now that symptoms resolved with appropriate follow-up
Observe for a period to monitor for a biphasic reaction, prescribe
Discharge with a prescription for daily oral corticosteroids for one month with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Even after a good response, patients require a period of observation because of the risk of a biphasic reaction (recurrence hours after initial resolution). The patient should be discharged with a prescription for TWO epinephrine auto-injectors, with hands-on training for the patient and caregivers on recognition and immediate use, a written anaphylaxis action plan, strict allergen avoidance counseling, and referral to an allergist. Immediate discharge ignores the biphasic risk and the need for an action plan/auto-injector — particularly relevant because he had left his device at home. Antibiotics are irrelevant to an allergic reaction. A month of oral steroids is unnecessary and not standard; short adjunctive steroids may be considered, but the cornerstone of discharge is epinephrine availability, education, and follow-up.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If his hypotension had NOT improved after repeated IM epinephrine and fluid boluses, what is the next appropriate step?
options: Stop epinephrine due to tachycardia as the leading concern
Administer subcutaneous epinephrine instead
Begin an IV epinephrine infusion with continuous monitoring
Switch entirely to IV antihistamines and steroids
stages.explanation: Refractory anaphylaxis — persistent hypotension despite repeated IM epinephrine and adequate fluid resuscitation — is treated with a continuous IV epinephrine infusion, titrated under continuous hemodynamic monitoring in a critical care setting. This provides controlled, sustained vasopressor and inotropic support that IM dosing cannot achieve in shock. Switching to antihistamines and steroids alone would be fatal, as neither addresses shock. Stopping epinephrine because of tachycardia is incorrect; tachycardia is expected and the drug is life-sustaining. Subcutaneous epinephrine has unreliable absorption in a shock state and is inferior to IM and far inferior to a controlled IV infusion. Escalation to an IV epinephrine infusion is the guideline-directed next step for refractory cases.

pearls: Anaphylaxis is a clinical diagnosis; IM epinephrine into the anterolateral thigh is first-line and must never be delayed for antihistamines or steroids.
Pediatric dose: 0.01 mg/kg of 1:1000 epinephrine IM (max 0.3 mg), repeat every 5-15 minutes as needed.
Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts only; albuterol treats bronchospasm but not laryngeal edema or shock.
Observe for biphasic reactions, discharge with two auto-injectors plus an action plan, and refer to allergy; refractory shock needs an IV epinephrine infusion.

cases.title: Irritable Infant with Unexplained Injuries
cases.chiefComplaint: Inconsolable crying, decreased feeding, and a brief episode of abnormal movements with no clear explanation.
cases.history: A 4-month-old boy is brought to the ED by his mother for several hours of inconsolable crying, poor feeding, and one brief episode of stiffening and eye deviation. The caregiver's account is vague and inconsistent: initially she says he 'rolled off the couch,' later that he was 'fine and just suddenly went limp.' A 4-month-old is not developmentally capable of rolling off a couch independently. There is no reported fever, vomiting beyond one episode, or diarrhea. He was born full-term without complications. He is exclusively formula fed and takes vitamin D drops; no other medications and no known allergies. Immunizations are slightly behind schedule. Birth weight was appropriate. Social history reveals a young, stressed single mother with limited support, recent housing instability, and a live-in partner who was watching the infant earlier today. There were two prior ED visits for 'fussiness.' Family history is noncontributory for bleeding or bone disorders. The timeline of the injury and the developmental implausibility of the stated mechanism are notable.
cases.exam: Lethargic, irritable infant with a tense, full anterior fontanelle. There is no external scalp laceration, but a small bruise is noted on the left cheek and another over the upper arm — unusual locations for an immobile infant. Pupils are slightly sluggish; fundoscopic exam by ophthalmology reveals multilayered retinal hemorrhages bilaterally. Chest exam reveals subtle crepitus over the posterolateral ribs. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. The infant is hypotonic with decreased spontaneous movement. No spontaneous bruising pattern suggestive of a bleeding diathesis elsewhere. Skin is otherwise intact. The combination of a bulging fontanelle, retinal hemorrhages, rib findings, and bruising in a non-mobile infant is the central concern.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bruises in a non-mobile 4-month-old, a changing/implausible history, retinal hemorrhages, and a bulging fontanelle should raise concern foremost for what?
options: Vitamin D deficiency rickets
Abusive head trauma / non-accidental trauma
Benign infantile self-limited fussiness
Routine viral illness in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The constellation — bruising in a precruising, non-mobile infant ('those who don't cruise rarely bruise'), a vague and inconsistent history that changes and is developmentally implausible, multilayered bilateral retinal hemorrhages, and a bulging fontanelle suggesting intracranial pathology — is highly concerning for abusive head trauma (non-accidental trauma). Caregiver risk factors (young stressed single parent, unrelated male caregiver, prior unexplained visits) add concern. Benign fussiness and routine viral illness do not explain bruising, retinal hemorrhages, or a tense fontanelle. Rickets causes bone fragility and metaphyseal changes but not retinal hemorrhages or this acute encephalopathy. Recognizing the pattern of unexplained injuries plus a discordant history is the first step toward protecting the child.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: bilateral subdural hemorrhages of differing densities (acute and subacute) with mild cerebral edema
Dilated fundoscopy: extensive multilayered retinal hemorrhages extending to the periphery, bilaterally
Skeletal survey: healing posterior rib fractures (multiple, callus formation) and a classic metaphyseal lesion of the distal femur
CBC, PT/aPTT, fibrinogen: normal (no coagulopathy)
LFTs and lipase: mildly elevated

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Why are POSTERIOR rib fractures and classic metaphyseal lesions considered highly specific for inflicted injury?
options: They are caused by anteroposterior thoracic squeezing/violent grasping
They commonly result from routine handling and CPR as the immediate next step
They occur spontaneously in vitamin D deficiency as the immediate next step
They are typical findings of birth trauma at 4 months as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Posterior rib fractures result from forceful anteroposterior compression of the chest (squeezing/grasping), levering the rib against the transverse process — a mechanism not produced by routine handling and only rarely by properly performed CPR. Classic metaphyseal lesions ('corner' or 'bucket-handle' fractures) arise from the shearing/tractional forces of violent shaking or yanking of a limb. Both are considered highly specific for abuse, especially with fractures of differing ages indicating repeated trauma. Birth trauma would be healed long before 4 months and would not explain new fractures. While rickets weakens bone, normal coagulation and the specific fracture pattern with retinal hemorrhages point to inflicted trauma. The biomechanics make these lesions strong markers of non-accidental trauma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond stabilizing the infant, what are the mandatory immediate next steps?
options: Report to child protective services , ensure the child's safety,
Counsel the parent on safe sleep and discharge as initial therapy
Wait for legal confirmation of abuse before notifying authorities as initial therapy
Discharge home with the mother once the seizure stops and arrange routine follow-up as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Physicians are mandated reporters: when there is reasonable suspicion of child abuse, you must report to child protective services immediately and ensure the child's safety, typically by admitting the child rather than discharging to a potentially unsafe environment. A multidisciplinary child-protection/child-abuse team, social work, and often law enforcement should be engaged, and documentation must be thorough and objective. Discharging home with the suspected caregiver endangers the child. You do NOT need proof or legal confirmation to report — reasonable suspicion is the threshold, and mandated reporters who report in good faith are protected. Safe-sleep counseling and routine follow-up are wholly inadequate for this life-threatening situation. Protecting the child and mandatory reporting are non-negotiable.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which additional evaluation is part of the standard workup to both screen for occult injury and exclude medical mimics?
options: Skeletal survey , coagulation studies,
Only a repeat head CT in 24 hours as the immediate next step
No further testing once the diagnosis is suspected as the immediate next step
Genetic testing for hemophilia only as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Standard evaluation for suspected physical abuse in an infant includes a full skeletal survey to detect occult fractures, often repeated in about 2 weeks to reveal healing fractures not initially visible, a dilated ophthalmologic exam to document retinal hemorrhages, and coagulation studies (PT/aPTT, platelets, sometimes factor levels) plus consideration of metabolic/bone disorders to exclude medical mimics such as bleeding diatheses or osteogenesis imperfecta. Neuroimaging (CT and often MRI) characterizes intracranial injury. A single repeat head CT is insufficient as a complete workup. Doing no further testing or testing only for hemophilia would miss occult injuries and other mimics. A comprehensive, protocolized workup both documents the injuries forensically and rules out medical explanations.

pearls: ‘Those who don't cruise rarely bruise’ — bruising or fractures in a non-mobile infant plus an inconsistent/implausible history is a red flag for abuse.
Posterior rib fractures and classic metaphyseal lesions are highly specific for inflicted injury; fractures of differing ages suggest repeated trauma.
The triad of subdural hemorrhage, retinal hemorrhages, and encephalopathy characterizes abusive head trauma; always exclude coagulopathy and bone-fragility mimics.
Physicians are mandated reporters — reasonable suspicion (not proof) triggers an immediate CPS report, child safety measures, and a multidisciplinary workup including a skeletal survey.

cases.title: Developmental Delay and Odd Eating Habits in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report slowed development, irritability, constipation, and eating non-food items.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to clinic because his parents feel his development has plateaued — he lost a few words he previously said and seems more irritable. They also describe pica: he repeatedly eats paint chips and dirt and chews on window sills. He has had intermittent abdominal pain and constipation. The family lives in a house built in the 1940s that is undergoing renovation, with peeling paint in his bedroom. He was previously meeting milestones. He takes a children's multivitamin without iron and has no known drug allergies. Diet is somewhat limited and possibly low in iron and calcium. Immunizations are up to date. Birth and perinatal history were normal. Family history is unremarkable. Social history: an older sibling at the same home had a borderline elevated screening level last year, and the parents missed the recommended follow-up. There has been no fever, no recent travel, and no bloody diarrhea. The parents are most worried about the developmental regression and the persistent ingestion of non-food materials in an older home with deteriorating paint.
cases.exam: Alert but irritable toddler who is mildly pale. He has limited expressive language for age. Cardiovascular exam is normal aside from a soft flow murmur. The abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness and palpable stool; no hepatosplenomegaly. There is no focal neurologic deficit, no ataxia at rest, and the gait is age-appropriate. There are no peripheral neuropathy findings on this young child. No petechiae or jaundice. Mucous membranes are moist. Conjunctival pallor is present. The exam is notable mainly for pallor, irritability, and a paucity of words relative to prior milestones, without acute encephalopathic signs such as altered consciousness or papilledema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given pica, an old home with peeling paint, developmental regression, and abdominal symptoms, which exposure should be screened for first?
options: Lead exposure
Carbon monoxide exposure
Arsenic exposure
Mercury exposure
stages.explanation: The combination of pica (eating paint chips/dirt), residence in pre-1978 housing with deteriorating lead-based paint undergoing renovation, developmental regression, irritability, constipation, and abdominal pain is the classic presentation of lead poisoning in a toddler. Young children absorb lead efficiently, and lead disrupts neurodevelopment and heme synthesis. A blood lead level is the appropriate first screen, especially given the affected sibling and missed follow-up. Mercury toxicity (e.g., from fish or thermometers) causes a different syndrome (acrodynia, tremor) and lacks the paint/pica link. Arsenic and carbon monoxide exposures have distinct sources and presentations and do not fit the housing/pica history. The environmental and behavioral clues point squarely to lead.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Venous blood lead level: 52 µg/dL (confirmed on venous draw)
Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL
MCV 68 fL (microcytic)
Peripheral smear: microcytic, hypochromic RBCs with basophilic stippling
Ferritin: low-normal (coexisting iron deficiency suspected)
Reticulocyte count: low-normal
Abdominal radiograph: radiopaque flecks in the bowel consistent with ingested paint/foreign material

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling and an elevated venous lead level of 52 µg/dL confirms lead poisoning. What does basophilic stippling reflect?
options: Precipitated denatured hemoglobin (Heinz bodies) as the primary diagnosis
Aggregated ribosomal RNA due to lead inhibition of pyrimidine 5'-nucleotidase
Antibody-coated red cells as the primary diagnosis
Iron-laden mitochondria in sideroblasts seen on routine smear
stages.explanation: Basophilic stippling represents aggregates of residual ribosomal RNA in red cells, which accumulate because lead inhibits pyrimidine 5'-nucleotidase, the enzyme that normally degrades RNA during red cell maturation. Lead also impairs heme synthesis (inhibiting ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase), contributing to the microcytic anemia. Heinz bodies are precipitated denatured hemoglobin seen in oxidative hemolysis (G6PD), not basophilic stippling. Ring sideroblasts (iron-laden mitochondria) are seen in marrow, not as basophilic stippling on a routine peripheral smear, and require special staining. Antibody-coated cells relate to immune hemolysis. Coexisting iron deficiency (common in these children and from a low-iron diet) worsens both the anemia and lead absorption. The stippling-RNA mechanism is the high-yield association.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With a confirmed level of 52 µg/dL but no encephalopathy, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate dual parenteral chelation with dimercaprol (BAL) plus CaNa2EDTA as monotherapy
Environmental abatement only, with no chelation at any level as initial therapy
Remove the child from the lead source, correct iron deficiency,
Observation and repeat the level in one year as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a confirmed blood lead level in the moderate-to-high range (typically ≥45 µg/dL) WITHOUT encephalopathy, management includes immediate removal from the exposure source (environmental investigation and abatement), correcting concurrent iron deficiency (iron deficiency increases lead absorption), and starting oral chelation with succimer (DMSA). Environmental abatement alone is appropriate for lower levels but is insufficient at 52 µg/dL. Dual parenteral chelation with dimercaprol (BAL) plus CaNa2EDTA is reserved for very high levels (typically ≥70 µg/dL) or lead encephalopathy, which this child does not have. Observation with a one-year recheck is unsafe at this level. Source removal plus oral chelation and iron repletion is the guideline-concordant approach for this level.

stages.label: PREVENTION & FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: What additional public-health action is essential in this case?
options: Restrict only the child's diet without addressing the home as the immediate next step
Recommend the family simply repaint over the old paint themselves as the immediate next step
Report to public health for home lead investigation/abatement
No further action once the child is treated as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Lead poisoning is a public-health issue: the case should be reported per local requirements so that a home environmental investigation and proper lead abatement can be performed, eliminating the ongoing source. Critically, the exposed sibling — who had a previously borderline level and missed follow-up — and any other children in the home must be screened, since they share the exposure. Treating only the index child while leaving the source and other children unaddressed allows continued and recurrent poisoning. Repainting over lead paint without proper abatement (and especially DIY sanding that aerosolizes lead) can worsen exposure and must be done by certified abatement. Diet alone does not remove the hazard. Source control and screening contacts are essential.

pearls: Suspect lead poisoning in a toddler with pica, developmental regression, irritability, and constipation living in pre-1978 housing with deteriorating paint.
Microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling reflects lead inhibition of heme synthesis and of pyrimidine 5'-nucleotidase (RNA aggregates); coexisting iron deficiency is common.
Levels ≥45 µg/dL without encephalopathy: remove from source, treat iron deficiency, and start oral succimer (DMSA); reserve BAL + CaNa2EDTA for ≥70 µg/dL or encephalopathy.
Report for home abatement and screen exposed siblings — lead poisoning is a preventable, environmental, public-health problem.

cases.title: Teen Brought In After an Intentional Pill Ingestion
cases.chiefComplaint: Nausea and vomiting after admitting to taking a large number of pills following an argument.
cases.history: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the ED by her parents after she disclosed taking 'a whole bottle' of an over-the-counter pain reliever following an argument, approximately 8 hours ago. She now reports nausea, several episodes of vomiting, and mild right-upper-quadrant discomfort but otherwise feels 'okay,' which is characteristic of the deceptively well-appearing early phase. She denies co-ingestants but is initially evasive; a careful history and a search of the home reveal an empty bottle of extra-strength acetaminophen and no alcohol or other medication containers. She has a history of depression and was recently started on an SSRI. She takes no other regular medications and has no drug allergies. She denies prior attempts but endorses recent low mood and an acute stressor. Social history: conflict at home, academic stress, and access to OTC medications. Family history includes maternal depression. There is no chronic liver disease, no regular alcohol use, and no acetaminophen use for a febrile illness preceding the event. The exact timing of ingestion is now established at roughly 8 hours prior to arrival.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative but tearful adolescent in no acute distress. She appears deceptively well. Mucous membranes are slightly dry from vomiting. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are normal. The abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant and no rebound, guarding, or hepatomegaly; bowel sounds are normal. There is no jaundice, no asterixis, and no scleral icterus at this early stage. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with normal mentation. Skin shows no injection sites or signs of chronic self-harm beyond what she reports. The principal concern is the time-dependent hepatotoxic risk despite her currently reassuring physical examination.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a known single acute acetaminophen ingestion presenting at a known time, which test best guides the decision to treat?
options: Only the AST/ALT, ignoring drug levels as the first step today
An immediate liver biopsy as the first step today
A serum acetaminophen level drawn at 4 hours post-ingestion , plotted on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram
A random acetaminophen level interpreted without regard to timing as the first step today
stages.explanation: For a single, acute ingestion at a known time, the serum acetaminophen concentration measured at 4 hours post-ingestion (or as soon as possible thereafter if already past 4 hours) plotted on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram determines the need for N-acetylcysteine. The nomogram relates the level and time-since-ingestion to hepatotoxicity risk and is only valid for single acute ingestions with a reliable time, from 4 to 24 hours. A liver biopsy is not used acutely for this decision. A level without timing cannot be interpreted on the nomogram, which is time-dependent. AST/ALT reflect injury that has already begun and are complementary but do not replace the nomogram-guided level for initial risk stratification. The timed level on the nomogram is the standard.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Time since ingestion: ~8 hours
Serum acetaminophen at 8 hours: 165 µg/mL (above the treatment line on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram)
AST 78 U/L, ALT 92 U/L (mildly elevated)
INR 1.2
Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL
Basic metabolic panel: normal; creatinine normal
Salicylate level: negative
Urine pregnancy test: negative
Ethanol: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her 8-hour level is above the treatment line. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Perform urgent hemodialysis as initial therapy
Administer activated charcoal now and discharge if she feels well
Wait for the AST to rise significantly before starting any antidote
Start N-acetylcysteine (NAC) without delay
stages.explanation: When the timed acetaminophen level is above the treatment line, N-acetylcysteine should be started immediately. NAC is the antidote and is most effective when given within 8 hours of ingestion, but it provides benefit even later, including in established hepatotoxicity, so therapy must not be delayed at ~8 hours. NAC replenishes glutathione, allowing detoxification of the reactive metabolite NAPQI that causes centrilobular hepatic necrosis. Activated charcoal is useful only early (generally within 1-2 hours) and is not appropriate at 8 hours, nor is discharge with a level above the line. Waiting for AST to rise wastes the critical early window and risks severe hepatotoxicity. Hemodialysis is reserved for massive ingestions with very high levels and acidosis/altered mental status, not standard therapy here. Start NAC now.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGY
stages.question: Why is N-acetylcysteine effective as the antidote for acetaminophen toxicity?
options: It replenishes hepatic glutathione to detoxify the reactive metabolite NAPQI
It directly chelates acetaminophen in the bloodstream as the immediate next step
It binds acetaminophen in the gut to prevent absorption as the immediate next step
It induces CYP450 to clear the drug faster as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Acetaminophen is partly metabolized by CYP2E1 to the toxic, highly reactive intermediate NAPQI, which is normally conjugated and neutralized by hepatic glutathione. In overdose, glutathione stores are depleted, allowing NAPQI to bind hepatocyte proteins and cause centrilobular necrosis. N-acetylcysteine works primarily by replenishing glutathione and supplying sulfhydryl (cysteine) groups that detoxify NAPQI, and it also has reductive and antioxidant effects that help in established liver injury. NAC does not work by binding acetaminophen in the gut (that is activated charcoal) or by chelating it in blood. Inducing CYP450 would worsen toxicity by generating more NAPQI. Restoring the glutathione pathway is the mechanism, which is why NAC remains beneficial even after the absorptive window.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond NAC and medical monitoring, what is an essential part of her care?
options: Discharge home once NAC is started, with outpatient labs with appropriate follow-up
Formal psychiatric evaluation
No psychiatric involvement since she now regrets it with appropriate follow-up
Immediate transfer to surgery for the abdominal pain with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: This was an intentional self-harm ingestion, so a formal psychiatric/mental-health evaluation with a structured suicide-risk assessment is essential, along with ensuring a safe environment (e.g., one-to-one observation, removing means) before any disposition. The medical and psychiatric needs must both be addressed; managing the overdose without addressing the suicide attempt is incomplete and dangerous. Discharging home once NAC is started ignores both the ongoing medical monitoring required (serial LFTs, INR, acetaminophen levels, completion of the NAC course) and the psychiatric risk. Assuming no psychiatric involvement is needed because she expresses regret is unsafe, as ambivalence is common. The RUQ pain reflects early hepatotoxicity, not a surgical abdomen. Integrated medical and psychiatric care is mandatory.

pearls: For a single acute ingestion at a known time, draw a 4-hour (or later) acetaminophen level and plot it on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram to decide on NAC.
Patients are often deceptively well early; transaminitis and hepatic necrosis evolve over 24-72 hours from NAPQI-induced centrilobular damage.
N-acetylcysteine works by replenishing glutathione to detoxify NAPQI; it is most effective within 8 hours but still beneficial later and in established hepatotoxicity — never delay.
Every intentional ingestion requires a formal psychiatric evaluation, suicide-risk assessment, and safety measures alongside medical management.

cases.title: Toddler Walking on His Toes
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report he walks on his toes and seems stiff in the legs.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to clinic because he walks almost exclusively on his toes and his legs feel rigid when his parents dress him. He was born at 28 weeks' gestation, weighed 1100 g, and spent 9 weeks in the NICU with a grade II intraventricular hemorrhage and a brief course of mechanical ventilation. He sat independently at 11 months, pulled to stand at 16 months, and took his first steps at 22 months with a scissoring gait. His language is age-appropriate: he speaks in two-word phrases and follows simple commands. He has no history of seizures. He takes a daily multivitamin with iron; no other medications and no known drug allergies. He lives with both parents and an older sibling who met milestones normally. Family history is negative for muscular dystrophy or neurologic disease. Parents note he frequently stumbles and tightens his legs when excited. There has been no regression of previously acquired skills. Immunizations are up to date, and growth has followed the 25th percentile since NICU discharge.
cases.exam: Alert, interactive toddler in no distress. Head circumference at the 25th percentile; anterior fontanelle closed. Cranial nerves intact; eyes track normally with no nystagmus. Lower-extremity tone is increased with spasticity at the hips, knees, and ankles; bilateral ankle clonus and brisk patellar reflexes are present. Both plantar responses are upgoing. He bears weight on his toes with adducted, scissoring legs and tight heel cords limiting passive ankle dorsiflexion. Upper extremities have normal tone, strength, and reflexes with good fine pinch. He uses both hands symmetrically. No muscle atrophy or fasciculations. Sensation appears intact; no joint contractures of the upper limbs. Spine is straight with no sacral dimple or hairy patch.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly points toward an upper motor neuron disorder rather than a primary muscle or peripheral nerve problem?
options: Spasticity with brisk reflexes, ankle clonus,
Toe-walking in isolation as the first step today
Delayed onset of independent walking as the first step today
History of prematurity and NICU admission
stages.explanation: Spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, and extensor plantar responses are hallmark upper motor neuron (corticospinal tract) signs, localizing the lesion centrally—classic for cerebral palsy. Delayed walking (option A) is nonspecific and occurs in many conditions including muscle disease. Prematurity (option C) is a risk factor and important context, but it is historical rather than a localizing exam finding. Isolated toe-walking (option D) is common and often idiopathic/benign in toddlers; it only becomes concerning when paired with spasticity and pathologic reflexes, as seen here. The combination of UMN signs in the legs with sparing of the arms is the key localizing pattern.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the bilateral leg spasticity sparing the arms in this ex-preemie, which clinical pattern best describes his motor disability?
options: Spastic diplegia
Spastic hemiplegia
Dyskinetic (athetoid) cerebral palsy
Ataxic cerebral palsy
stages.explanation: Spastic diplegia denotes spasticity predominantly affecting the lower extremities with relative sparing of the arms—the classic pattern in former premature infants with periventricular white-matter injury, where descending leg fibers run closest to the lateral ventricles. Hemiplegia (option A) affects one side of the body (arm and leg together), not symmetric legs. Dyskinetic CP (option B) features involuntary writhing/choreoathetoid movements and is linked to basal ganglia injury (e.g., kernicterus), not pure spasticity. Ataxic CP (option D) presents with incoordination, wide-based gait, and dysmetria rather than spasticity and clonus. This child's symmetric leg spasticity with normal arms fits spastic diplegia.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Brain MRI: Periventricular white-matter volume loss with mild lateral ventricular enlargement (periventricular leukomalacia)
No cortical malformation or acute infarct
Myelination otherwise appropriate for age
Metabolic screen (lactate, ammonia, plasma amino acids): within normal limits

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Because cerebral palsy is frequently associated with comorbidities, which additional screening is most appropriate at this visit?
options: Vision and hearing evaluation plus assessment for hip subluxation
Nerve conduction studies and electromyography in this setting
Echocardiogram and 24-hour Holter monitor in this setting
Muscle biopsy with dystrophin staining as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Children with CP have high rates of associated impairments, so guideline-based care includes screening vision and hearing and surveilling for hip displacement (subluxation/dislocation), which is common in spastic diplegia and is monitored with serial hip radiographs. Echocardiogram/Holter (option A) is unwarranted without cardiac findings. Muscle biopsy with dystrophin staining (option C) evaluates muscular dystrophy, but his UMN signs and normal arm strength argue against a primary myopathy. NCS/EMG (option D) assesses peripheral nerve or anterior horn disease, inconsistent with his central spastic picture. Screening for sensory and orthopedic comorbidities directly affects function and outcomes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate cornerstone of long-term management for this child's spastic diplegia?
options: Multidisciplinary therapy with physical/occupational therapy
Oral diazepam as monotherapy indefinitely
Strict bracing alone with no therapy referral as initial therapy
Immediate bilateral selective dorsal rhizotomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: CP management is multidisciplinary and individualized: physical and occupational therapy to maximize function and prevent contractures, orthoses (AFOs) for the tight heel cords, and targeted spasticity treatment such as botulinum toxin injections to focal muscle groups, with oral agents or intrathecal baclofen for diffuse spasticity. Indefinite diazepam monotherapy (option A) causes sedation and tolerance and is not a stand-alone strategy. Selective dorsal rhizotomy (option B) can help carefully selected ambulatory children but is not a first-line, immediate step for every patient. Bracing alone without therapy (option D) neglects strengthening, motor learning, and developmental support. A coordinated, function-focused program offers the best outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebral palsy (spastic diplegia) secondary to periventricular leukomalacia of prematurity
pearls: Spastic diplegia—legs more affected than arms—is the classic CP pattern in former premature infants due to periventricular leukomalacia.
UMN signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, upgoing plantar responses) localize the lesion centrally and distinguish CP from myopathy or neuropathy.
CP is a static (non-progressive) encephalopathy; regression of skills should prompt a search for an alternative, progressive disorder.
Screen for comorbidities—vision, hearing, seizures, hip subluxation, and learning—since these drive much of the long-term disability.

cases.title: Toddler Who Stopped Saying Words
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents are worried that he has lost words he used to say and does not respond to his name.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy presents for a developmental concern visit. His parents report that around 18 months he said about ten words including 'mama,' 'ball,' and 'milk,' but over the past year he has stopped using most of them and now rarely speaks. He does not point to objects he wants, does not bring toys to show his parents, and seldom makes eye contact. He prefers to line up his cars in long rows and becomes very upset if the order is disturbed or his routine changes. He flaps his hands when excited. He does not respond consistently when his name is called, which prompted a hearing test that was normal. Birth and medical history are unremarkable; he is fully immunized and takes no medications.
