No known drug allergies. He lives with both parents; a maternal cousin carries a diagnosis of autism. There is no history of seizures, head trauma, or toxic ingestion. Parents note he walks and runs well and feeds himself, but they cannot redirect his attention to shared activities.
cases.exam: Well-appearing boy who explores the room independently and does not look up when his name is called. He makes fleeting eye contact and does not respond to a parent's point or attempts to direct his gaze (poor joint attention). He repetitively spins the wheels of a toy car and lines several cars in a row. Hand-flapping noted with excitement. He has no dysmorphic features; head circumference is at the 50th percentile. Skin shows no hypopigmented macules, café-au-lait spots, or facial angiofibromas under Wood lamp. Neurologic exam: normal tone, symmetric strength, normal gait, and 2+ symmetric reflexes; no focal deficits. Cardiac and respiratory exams are normal. He uses no spoken words during the visit but hums to himself.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which validated screening tool is most appropriate to administer at this visit for this toddler?
options: Denver II as a definitive diagnostic test
Vanderbilt ADHD Diagnostic Rating Scale
PHQ-9 depression screen as the first step today
Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers, Revised
stages.explanation: The M-CHAT-R/F is the standard validated autism screening tool for children roughly 16-30 months (and useful up to ~36 months), assessing joint attention, response to name, pointing, and pretend play—exactly the deficits described. The Vanderbilt scale (option A) screens for ADHD, inappropriate at this age and for this presentation. The PHQ-9 (option C) screens adolescents/adults for depression. The Denver II (option D) is a general developmental screen, not a diagnostic test and not specific for autism; a positive screen still requires comprehensive evaluation. The M-CHAT-R/F best targets the social-communication red flags seen here.

stages.label: SCREENING RESULT
stages.content:
M-CHAT-R/F total score: 9 (high risk; range 0-20)
Follow-up interview confirms failed items: response to name, pointing to show interest, joint attention, pretend play
Formal audiology: hearing within normal limits bilaterally
Lead level: 2 µg/dL (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which constellation of findings best supports a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder in this child?
options: Hyperactivity and impulsivity present only at home as the primary diagnosis
Loss of motor milestones with hypotonia and hepatomegaly as the primary diagnosis
Sudden staring spells with eye fluttering as the primary diagnosis
Deficits in social communication plus restricted, repetitive behaviors with early onset
stages.explanation: ASD is defined by two core domains: persistent deficits in social communication and social interaction (poor eye contact, absent joint attention, no pointing/showing) plus restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior or interests (lining up cars, insistence on sameness, hand-flapping), with onset in early development. This child meets both. Hyperactivity confined to home (option A) suggests ADHD, which also requires cross-setting symptoms. Staring spells with eye fluttering (option C) describe absence seizures. Loss of motor milestones with hypotonia and organomegaly (option D) suggests a metabolic/neurodegenerative disorder, not ASD. His normal exam and hearing make those alternatives unlikely.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After clinical diagnosis, which first-tier genetic test is recommended in the etiologic workup of autism spectrum disorder?
options: Routine EEG for all children with ASD
Whole-body MRI as the immediate next step
Serum heavy-metal panel with chelation challenge
Chromosomal microarray
stages.explanation: Chromosomal microarray analysis is the recommended first-tier genetic test for ASD and yields the highest diagnostic identification of copy-number variants; fragile X testing is also standard, especially in boys, given its association with ASD and intellectual disability. Whole-body MRI (option B) has no role. A heavy-metal panel with chelation challenge (option C) is not indicated; his lead level is normal, and chelation challenge testing is not recommended. Routine EEG for all children with ASD (option D) is unnecessary—EEG is reserved for those with clinical seizures or suspected epileptiform regression. Microarray plus fragile X is the appropriate genetic evaluation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention has the strongest evidence to improve outcomes and should be initiated promptly?
options: Start a stimulant medication as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Reassure parents and re-evaluate in two years as initial therapy
Recommend a gluten-free, casein-free diet as primary treatment as initial therapy
Early intensive behavioral intervention with speech
stages.explanation: Early, intensive, structured behavioral intervention—commonly applied behavior analysis—combined with speech-language and occupational therapy is the evidence-based cornerstone of ASD management and works best when started early. Stimulants (option A) treat coexisting ADHD symptoms but are not first-line for core ASD features. A gluten-free/casein-free diet (option B) lacks supporting evidence as a primary treatment. Watchful waiting for two years (option D) squanders a critical developmental window; early referral to early-intervention services should not be delayed pending genetic results. Prompt enrollment in behavioral and developmental therapies offers the greatest functional benefit.

pearls: Use the M-CHAT-R/F to screen toddlers ~16-30 months; positive screens require the follow-up interview and a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation.
ASD requires both social-communication deficits and restricted/repetitive behaviors with early developmental onset.
Chromosomal microarray and fragile X testing are first-tier in the etiologic workup; routine EEG and MRI are not needed without specific indications.
Early intensive behavioral intervention plus speech and OT—started promptly—drives the best outcomes; don't wait.

cases.title: Boy Who Can't Sit Still in Class
cases.chiefComplaint: Teachers and parents report he cannot focus, fidgets constantly, and rarely finishes his work.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is brought in because his second-grade teacher says he cannot stay seated, blurts out answers, interrupts classmates, and rarely completes assignments. At home his parents describe similar behavior: he loses homework, forgets instructions, jumps from task to task, and 'is always on the go,' climbing on furniture and unable to wait his turn. These behaviors have been present since preschool and occur both at home and at school. His grades are slipping despite normal intelligence, and he has begun to feel discouraged. Birth and developmental history are normal; he walked and talked on time. He has no history of seizures, head injury, or chronic medical illness. He sleeps adequately, and there are no recent psychosocial stressors such as divorce, bullying, or a new sibling. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Family history is notable for a father who 'had trouble in school' and required tutoring. He lives with both parents and attends a mainstream classroom. Hearing and vision screens at school were normal this year.
cases.exam: Energetic boy who repeatedly gets out of his chair, fidgets with the exam paper, and interrupts the interview but is redirectable. He is pleasant and not oppositional. Speech is fluent and articulate; thought process is linear with no looseness of associations. No tics observed. Cranial nerves intact; normal tone, strength, coordination, and symmetric 2+ reflexes; gait normal. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rate without murmur. Thyroid is not enlarged, and there is no tremor, exophthalmos, or tachycardia. Skin shows no neurocutaneous stigmata. Growth parameters track at the 50th percentile. He is alert and oriented, with intact short-term recall when actively engaged, and shows no dysmorphic features.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which step is essential before this child's diagnosis can be established?
options: Order thyroid function tests and a sleep study in all patients as the first step today
Collect standardized rating scales from both parents
Obtain a brain MRI to exclude structural disease as the first step today
Begin a stimulant trial and judge the diagnosis by the response in this clinical context
stages.explanation: ADHD is a clinical diagnosis requiring symptoms in two or more settings (e.g., home and school) causing functional impairment, with onset before age 12. Collecting standardized informant scales such as the Vanderbilt from both parents and teachers is essential to confirm cross-setting symptoms and severity. Brain MRI (option A) is not indicated without focal neurologic findings. Routine thyroid testing and sleep studies for everyone (option C) are unnecessary; obtain them only when history suggests hyperthyroidism or a sleep disorder. A diagnostic stimulant trial (option D) is inappropriate—medication follows, not precedes, diagnosis. Multi-informant rating scales are the cornerstone of evaluation.

stages.label: RATING SCALES
stages.content:
Parent Vanderbilt: meets criteria for inattentive (7/9 items) and hyperactive-impulsive (7/9 items) domains; performance impairment noted
Teacher Vanderbilt: meets criteria for inattentive (8/9) and hyperactive-impulsive (6/9) domains; classroom performance below grade level
Screening items for oppositional defiant, conduct, and anxiety/depression: below threshold
Vision and hearing screening: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the Vanderbilt results and history, which diagnosis is most appropriate?
options: Absence epilepsy as the primary diagnosis
Oppositional defiant disorder as the primary diagnosis
Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, combined presentation
Generalized anxiety disorder as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: He meets the symptom threshold in both the inattentive and hyperactive-impulsive domains across home and school, with impairment and onset before age 12—diagnostic of ADHD, combined presentation. Oppositional defiant disorder (option A) involves a pattern of defiant, hostile behavior; his ODD screening items were below threshold, and he is redirectable and not oppositional. Generalized anxiety (option B) was also below threshold and does not explain his hyperactivity. Absence epilepsy (option C) produces brief, abrupt staring spells with impaired awareness, not chronic fidgeting, impulsivity, and disorganization with retained engagement. The cross-setting symptom pattern confirms ADHD.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this school-aged child, what is the recommended first-line treatment for ADHD?
options: Risperidone as initial therapy
Dietary elimination of all sugar and artificial dyes as monotherapy
FDA-approved stimulant medication combined with behavioral therapy
Atomoxetine alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For elementary school-aged children (≥6 years), guidelines recommend FDA-approved stimulants (methylphenidate or amphetamine class) as first-line pharmacotherapy, ideally combined with behavioral interventions including parent training and classroom supports. Atomoxetine (option A), a nonstimulant, is second-line, reserved for patients who cannot tolerate or do not respond to stimulants or have contraindications. Risperidone (option C), an antipsychotic, is not indicated for core ADHD and carries metabolic risks. Dietary sugar/dye elimination (option D) lacks robust evidence as primary therapy. Combining a stimulant with behavioral strategies provides the strongest symptom control and functional improvement.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: After starting a stimulant, which parameters should be routinely monitored at follow-up visits?
options: Serum drug levels and liver enzymes monthly in this setting
Fasting lipid panel every visit as the immediate next step
Height, weight, blood pressure, heart rate, sleep, appetite,
Periodic head CT scans as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Stimulants can suppress appetite and growth and modestly raise heart rate and blood pressure, so monitoring includes height and weight (plotted on growth curves), blood pressure, heart rate, sleep, appetite, mood, and symptom/functional response—plus screening for emergent tics. Routine serum drug levels and monthly liver enzymes (option A) are not standard for stimulants. Head CT scans (option C) have no role in monitoring. A fasting lipid panel every visit (option D) is unnecessary for stimulant therapy. Tracking growth, cardiovascular vitals, and treatment response while watching for side effects is the appropriate follow-up approach.

pearls: ADHD requires symptoms in ≥2 settings with functional impairment and onset before age 12; confirm with multi-informant rating scales (e.g., Vanderbilt).
Always screen for comorbid/mimicking conditions—ODD, anxiety/depression, sleep disorders, hearing/vision deficits—before settling on ADHD.
First-line pharmacotherapy for children ≥6 years is an FDA-approved stimulant plus behavioral therapy; atomoxetine and alpha-2 agonists are alternatives.
Monitor growth, heart rate, blood pressure, appetite, sleep, and tics at stimulant follow-ups.

cases.title: Girl With Brief Staring Spells
cases.chiefComplaint: Teacher reports she 'zones out' many times a day and seems to miss parts of lessons.
cases.history: A 7-year-old girl is referred after her teacher noticed she stares blankly and stops responding many times daily, each episode lasting only a few seconds. During an episode she abruptly halts whatever she is doing, has subtle eyelid fluttering, and then resumes the activity as if nothing happened, with no memory of the lapse. There is no falling, stiffening, jerking of the limbs, tongue-biting, incontinence, or postictal confusion. Episodes can occur 20 or more times a day and are sometimes triggered when she breathes hard while running. Her grades have slipped because she misses instructions. Birth, developmental, and medical histories are normal; she takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. She has had no head trauma, fever, or recent illness. There is no family history of epilepsy, though a maternal aunt had childhood 'spells' that resolved. She lives with both parents and is otherwise healthy and active, with normal sleep and appetite.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, cooperative girl in no distress. During the visit she has a brief 5-second episode of unresponsiveness with subtle eyelid flutter, then immediately resumes conversation without confusion. Cranial nerves are intact; pupils equal and reactive; no nystagmus. Motor exam shows normal tone and symmetric 5/5 strength; reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Coordination, finger-to-nose, and gait are normal. There are no neurocutaneous lesions on skin exam, and head circumference is normal. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm without murmur, and there is no orthostatic change in vitals. Mental status is age-appropriate between episodes with intact attention when actively engaged.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which simple bedside maneuver can help provoke a typical episode during evaluation?
options: Photic stimulation with a strobe only
Valsalva against a closed glottis
Three minutes of hyperventilation
Carotid sinus massage
stages.explanation: Hyperventilation for about 3 minutes reliably provokes typical absence seizures and the underlying 3-Hz generalized spike-and-wave discharge, making it a useful bedside and EEG activation technique; the history that exertional hard breathing triggers spells is a clue. Carotid sinus massage (option A) evaluates vasovagal/cardiac syncope, not absence seizures, and is inappropriate in children. Valsalva (option C) is used for syncope and cardiac maneuvers, not seizure provocation. Photic stimulation (option D) can elicit photosensitive epileptiform activity in some patients but is far less specific for childhood absence epilepsy than hyperventilation. Hyperventilation is the classic provocative test here.

stages.label: EEG
stages.content:
EEG: Generalized, bilaterally synchronous 3-Hz spike-and-wave discharges
Discharges provoked by hyperventilation, with clinical staring captured
Normal background rhythm between discharges
No focal slowing or asymmetry
MRI brain (obtained for thoroughness): normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Convulsive (generalized tonic-clonic) seizures
Focal seizures with impaired awareness (temporal lobe)
Childhood absence epilepsy
Daydreaming/inattention from ADHD in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Brief (seconds-long) staring spells with abrupt onset/offset, no aura, no postictal confusion, dozens per day, provoked by hyperventilation, with generalized 3-Hz spike-and-wave on EEG in a school-aged child are diagnostic of childhood absence epilepsy. Focal seizures with impaired awareness (option A) typically last longer (30 sec to minutes), often have an aura and automatisms, and show focal EEG changes with postictal confusion. ADHD daydreaming (option C) is not stopped by hyperventilation, lacks eyelid fluttering, and shows no EEG correlate. Generalized tonic-clonic seizures (option D) involve convulsions and prolonged postictal states, absent here. The classic semiology plus 3-Hz spike-wave confirms the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which medication is the preferred first-line therapy for typical childhood absence epilepsy?
options: Carbamazepine as initial therapy
Phenytoin as initial therapy
Ethosuximide
Vigabatrin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Ethosuximide is first-line for typical absence seizures and was shown to be as effective as valproic acid with fewer attentional side effects; valproic acid is preferred when generalized tonic-clonic seizures coexist. Carbamazepine (option B) and phenytoin (option C) are sodium-channel blockers that can worsen or exacerbate absence (and other generalized) seizures and are contraindicated. Vigabatrin (option D) is used for infantile spasms, not absence epilepsy, and risks irreversible visual-field loss. For pure absence epilepsy without convulsive seizures, ethosuximide is the agent of choice.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement best describes the expected prognosis of childhood absence epilepsy?
options: Most children respond to medication
It uniformly progresses to lifelong refractory epilepsy in this setting
It is associated with rapid neurodegeneration and developmental regression
Sudden death is the most common outcome as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Childhood absence epilepsy generally carries a favorable prognosis: most children respond well to appropriate medication, and a substantial proportion experience remission, with many outgrowing seizures by adolescence. It does not uniformly become refractory (option A); a minority have persistent seizures or later develop generalized tonic-clonic seizures, but most do well. It is not a neurodegenerative disorder (option C)—development and cognition are typically normal, though attention can be affected by frequent discharges. Sudden death (option D) is not characteristic of this benign syndrome. Reassurance with treatment and follow-up is appropriate.

pearls: Absence seizures are brief (seconds), abrupt-onset/offset staring spells without aura or postictal confusion—distinct from focal seizures and daydreaming.
Hyperventilation provokes the typical 3-Hz generalized spike-and-wave discharge—useful at bedside and during EEG.
Ethosuximide is first-line; avoid carbamazepine and phenytoin, which can worsen generalized/absence seizures.
Prognosis is generally good—most children respond to therapy and many remit by adolescence.

cases.title: Infant With Repeated Clusters of Jerks
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents describe sudden clusters of body jerks where he bends forward, occurring several times a day.
cases.history: A 6-month-old boy is admitted after his parents recorded repeated clusters of sudden movements in which he flexes his neck, draws his arms inward, and bends at the waist, each spasm lasting 1-2 seconds and occurring in runs of 10-20, often just after waking. The clusters began about three weeks ago and are increasing in frequency. Parents also report he no longer smiles socially or reaches for toys as he did a month ago, and he seems less interactive. He was born at term; the pregnancy and delivery were unremarkable. He has had no fevers, trauma, or ingestions. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Family history is noncontributory, and there is no consanguinity. On review, parents recall a few small, flat 'birthmarks' but otherwise no concerns until these episodes. Immunizations are up to date. He had been meeting milestones until the recent loss of social engagement. He feeds adequately and has normal stool and urine output.
cases.exam: Quiet, less interactive infant who does not consistently fix and follow or smile socially. During exam he has a witnessed cluster of brief, symmetric flexor spasms—sudden neck flexion with arm adduction and trunk flexion—each lasting 1-2 seconds, in a run of about 12. Anterior fontanelle is soft and flat; head circumference at the 50th percentile. Tone is mildly diminished axially. Pupils equal and reactive; no gaze deviation. Wood lamp examination of the skin reveals three hypopigmented (ash-leaf) macules on the trunk. Cardiac and respiratory exams are normal; abdomen soft without organomegaly. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric; no clonus. He is afebrile and well-perfused.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which diagnostic test should be obtained most urgently to characterize these episodes?
options: Routine awake-only EEG in one week as the first step today
Electroencephalogram , ideally including sleep, obtained promptly
Upper GI series to rule out reflux as the first step today
Lumbar puncture for cell count as the first step today
stages.explanation: These stereotyped flexor spasms in clusters with developmental regression are classic for infantile spasms, and an EEG—ideally capturing sleep—should be obtained promptly to look for hypsarrhythmia, the characteristic chaotic, high-amplitude pattern. Early recognition is urgent because treatment delay worsens developmental outcome. A routine awake-only study delayed a week (option A) risks missing sleep-activated hypsarrhythmia and dangerously delays care. Lumbar puncture (option C) is not the immediate step without signs of infection. An upper GI series (option D) chases reflux/Sandifer mimics but should not precede EEG given the highly characteristic semiology and regression. EEG is the key urgent test.

stages.label: EEG & WORKUP
stages.content:
EEG: Hypsarrhythmia — chaotic, high-amplitude, disorganized slow waves with multifocal spikes
Clinical spasms captured with electrodecremental response
Wood lamp: 3 hypopigmented macules
Brain MRI: cortical/subcortical tubers and subependymal nodules
Genetic testing pending for TSC1/TSC2

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the unifying diagnosis and most likely underlying etiology?
options: Gastroesophageal reflux causing Sandifer syndrome
Benign myoclonus of infancy due to a self-limited condition
Febrile seizures from an occult urinary tract infection
Infantile spasms due to tuberous sclerosis complex
stages.explanation: The triad of epileptic spasms, hypsarrhythmia on EEG, and developmental regression defines West syndrome (infantile spasms). The hypopigmented macules plus MRI cortical tubers and subependymal nodules point to tuberous sclerosis complex as the etiology—the most common identifiable cause of infantile spasms. Benign myoclonus of infancy (option A) has a normal EEG and no regression. Sandifer syndrome (option C) causes dystonic posturing with reflux but no hypsarrhythmia or regression and a normal EEG. Febrile seizures from a UTI (option D) require fever (he is afebrile) and do not produce hypsarrhythmia. The clinical-EEG-imaging picture confirms West syndrome from TSC.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the underlying tuberous sclerosis complex, which is the preferred first-line therapy for his infantile spasms?
options: Phenobarbital as initial therapy
Ketogenic diet as initial monotherapy
Vigabatrin
Ethosuximide as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Vigabatrin is the preferred first-line agent for infantile spasms caused by tuberous sclerosis complex and is highly effective in this subgroup; hormonal therapy (ACTH or high-dose corticosteroids) is first-line for most other etiologies. Ethosuximide (option B) treats absence seizures and is ineffective for spasms. Phenobarbital (option C) is not a first-line treatment for infantile spasms and does not address hypsarrhythmia adequately. The ketogenic diet (option D) is a useful second-line option but is not standard initial monotherapy. Prompt treatment—vigabatrin in TSC—is essential to resolve spasms and hypsarrhythmia and protect development; clinicians must monitor for vigabatrin-associated visual-field constriction.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Why is rapid treatment of infantile spasms considered a neurologic emergency?
options: Spasms cause immediate airway obstruction and asphyxia as the leading concern
Untreated hypsarrhythmia is associated with worsening developmental regression
They cause acute, life-threatening hyperkalemia as the leading concern
Spasms invariably evolve into status epilepticus within hours as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Infantile spasms are a developmental emergency: ongoing hypsarrhythmia and spasms are associated with progressive developmental regression and poor long-term cognitive outcomes, and shorter time-to-treatment correlates with better development. Spasms themselves are brief and do not cause airway obstruction/asphyxia (option A). They do not invariably progress to status epilepticus within hours (option C); the urgency is about cumulative developmental harm, not acute convulsive status. They are not associated with hyperkalemia (option D). The rationale for urgent diagnosis and treatment is to halt the epileptic encephalopathy and preserve neurodevelopment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond seizure control, which long-term surveillance is most important given his underlying diagnosis?
options: Routine bone marrow biopsy every year with appropriate follow-up
Multisystem monitoring including renal angiomyolipomas, cardiac rhabdomyomas, ophthalmologic,
Annual screening for testicular torsion with appropriate follow-up
No further follow-up once spasms resolve with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Tuberous sclerosis complex is multisystem, so surveillance includes serial brain MRI (for subependymal giant cell astrocytomas), renal imaging for angiomyolipomas, echocardiography for cardiac rhabdomyomas, ophthalmologic exams for retinal hamartomas, dermatologic monitoring, blood pressure, and ongoing neurodevelopmental and epilepsy follow-up. Screening for testicular torsion (option A) is unrelated to TSC. Routine annual bone marrow biopsy (option C) has no role. Discontinuing follow-up after spasms resolve (option D) is unsafe given progressive, organ-threatening manifestations. Comprehensive multisystem surveillance is the standard of care for TSC.

cases.diagnosis: Infantile spasms (West syndrome) due to tuberous sclerosis complex
pearls: West syndrome triad: epileptic spasms (often in clusters on waking) + hypsarrhythmia on EEG + developmental regression.
Infantile spasms are a neurologic emergency—prompt EEG (with sleep) and rapid treatment improve developmental outcomes.
Vigabatrin is first-line when the cause is tuberous sclerosis complex; ACTH/steroids are first-line for most other etiologies.
Always examine the skin (Wood lamp for ash-leaf macules) and image the brain—TSC is the most common identifiable cause of infantile spasms.

cases.title: Newborn With Breathing Trouble on Day One
cases.chiefComplaint: A term newborn develops grunting, fast breathing, and an unstable temperature within hours of birth.
cases.history: A 1-day-old girl is transferred to the NICU about 8 hours after birth for respiratory distress and temperature instability. She was born at 39 weeks by vaginal delivery to a 27-year-old mother. The pregnancy was notable for rupture of membranes 20 hours before delivery and a maternal intrapartum fever to 38.5 °C; the mother's group B Streptococcus screening status was unknown and she did not receive intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis. Apgars were 8 and 9. Over the first hours of life the baby became increasingly tachypneic with grunting and poor feeding, and her temperature swung from 36.0 °C to 38.2 °C. There were no maternal illnesses, no known drug allergies, and the mother took only prenatal vitamins. Family history is unremarkable. The infant has received vitamin K and erythromycin eye ointment. She has not yet been fully fed and has voided once. She is the first child; there is no NICU history in siblings.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, lethargic newborn with poor tone. Respiratory exam shows tachypnea, audible grunting, nasal flaring, and intercostal and subcostal retractions with decreased air entry bilaterally. She is mottled with delayed capillary refill of 4 seconds and cool extremities. The anterior fontanelle is soft. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia without a murmur; peripheral pulses are weak. Abdomen is soft without hepatosplenomegaly. There is no rash or omphalitis, and no obvious focal source of infection. She is hypotonic with a weak suck and diminished Moro reflex. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. No meconium staining of the skin or cord was noted at delivery.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic and management strategy?
options: Begin antiviral therapy alone for presumed neonatal HSV as the first step today
Obtain blood culture, CBC,
Start antibiotics only after lumbar puncture results return as the first step today
Observe and recheck vitals in 6 hours before acting as the first step today
stages.explanation: A toxic-appearing neonate with respiratory distress, temperature instability, and perinatal risk factors (prolonged ROM, maternal fever, unknown GBS, no prophylaxis) must be treated as sepsis until proven otherwise. The correct approach is a sepsis evaluation—blood culture, CBC with differential, and other indicated cultures—followed by prompt empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics; do not wait for results. Observation (option A) is dangerous in a deteriorating septic newborn. Withholding antibiotics until the LP returns (option C) inappropriately delays therapy; antibiotics should not be delayed for the LP. Antivirals alone (option D) ignore the high bacterial risk; HSV coverage may be added if features suggest it, but empiric antibacterials are essential here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 2.1 ×10³/µL with neutropenia; I:T ratio 0.32
Platelets 88 ×10³/µL
CRP elevated; blood glucose 44 mg/dL
ABG: pH 7.22, PCO2 52 mmHg, base deficit -8
Chest radiograph: bilateral reticulogranular opacities with air bronchograms
Blood culture: gram-positive cocci in chains (preliminary), later Streptococcus agalactiae

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate for suspected early-onset neonatal sepsis?
options: Oral amoxicillin as monotherapy
Ciprofloxacin monotherapy
Vancomycin and ceftriaxone
Ampicillin and gentamicin
stages.explanation: Ampicillin plus gentamicin is the standard empiric regimen for early-onset neonatal sepsis: ampicillin covers group B Streptococcus and Listeria, while gentamicin covers gram-negative organisms such as E. coli and provides synergy. Vancomycin/ceftriaxone (option A) is wrong—ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates because it displaces bilirubin (kernicterus risk) and can precipitate with calcium; vancomycin is reserved for suspected MRSA/late-onset line infections. Oral amoxicillin (option C) is inappropriate for a critically ill neonate who needs IV therapy. Ciprofloxacin monotherapy (option D) is not standard empiric neonatal coverage. Ampicillin and gentamicin remain first-line empiric therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Because of the positive blood culture, which additional study is important to guide duration and detect a common complication?
options: Skeletal survey for nonaccidental trauma
Sweat chloride test in this setting
Abdominal ultrasound for pyloric stenosis
Lumbar puncture to evaluate for meningitis
stages.explanation: GBS sepsis frequently coexists with meningitis, which alters antibiotic dosing and duration, so a lumbar puncture (once the infant is stable enough) is important to detect CNS involvement and guide treatment length. Abdominal ultrasound for pyloric stenosis (option B) is irrelevant here—pyloric stenosis presents later with nonbilious projectile vomiting. A skeletal survey (option C) addresses suspected abuse, not indicated. A sweat chloride test (option D) evaluates cystic fibrosis, unrelated to this acute septic picture. Confirming or excluding meningitis directly affects management and outcomes in neonatal GBS infection.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which intervention would most likely have reduced this infant's risk of early-onset GBS disease?
options: Maternal intrapartum penicillin prophylaxis based on GBS screening at 36-37 weeks
Neonatal GBS vaccination on day one of life as the immediate next step
Routine cesarean delivery for all mothers as the immediate next step
Postnatal oral antibiotics for the newborn at birth as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Universal antenatal GBS screening at 36-37 weeks with intrapartum intravenous penicillin (or ampicillin) prophylaxis for colonized mothers—or for risk factors such as prolonged ROM, intrapartum fever, or unknown status with risk—is the proven strategy to prevent early-onset GBS disease. This mother had unknown status with risk factors and received no prophylaxis. Postnatal oral antibiotics for the newborn (option A) do not prevent intrapartum transmission and are not a strategy. Routine cesarean for all (option C) is unnecessary and does not reliably prevent GBS with intact membranes. A licensed neonatal GBS vaccine (option D) is not standard practice. Intrapartum maternal prophylaxis is the key preventive measure.

cases.diagnosis: Early-onset neonatal sepsis due to group B Streptococcus
pearls: Suspect early-onset sepsis in any neonate with respiratory distress and temperature instability plus risk factors (prolonged ROM, maternal fever, GBS+/unknown without prophylaxis).
Draw cultures, then start empiric ampicillin + gentamicin promptly—do not delay antibiotics for the LP.
Avoid ceftriaxone in neonates (bilirubin displacement → kernicterus; calcium precipitation).
Universal GBS screening at 36-37 weeks with intrapartum penicillin prophylaxis prevents early-onset GBS disease.

cases.title: Premature Baby Struggling to Breathe at Birth
cases.chiefComplaint: A premature newborn develops worsening grunting and retractions minutes after delivery.
cases.history: A male infant is delivered at 30 weeks' gestation to a 24-year-old mother after preterm labor that could not be stopped. Within minutes of birth he develops grunting, nasal flaring, and progressively worsening retractions, requiring escalating respiratory support in the delivery room. The mother presented in active preterm labor and delivered before a full course of antenatal corticosteroids could be completed—she received only a single dose of betamethasone about 6 hours prior. The pregnancy was otherwise uncomplicated, with no diabetes, no prolonged rupture of membranes, and no maternal fever. There were no known drug allergies and only prenatal vitamins were taken. Apgars were 5 at 1 minute and 7 at 5 minutes. There is no family history of lung disease. The infant has received vitamin K. Birth weight is 1350 g, appropriate for gestational age. There was no meconium staining, and amniotic fluid was clear.
cases.exam: Small preterm infant in moderate-to-severe respiratory distress. He has audible expiratory grunting, nasal flaring, and marked intercostal, subcostal, and suprasternal retractions. Breath sounds are diminished bilaterally with poor air entry; chest expansion is shallow. He appears dusky despite supplemental oxygen and CPAP. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with no murmur and adequate but slightly delayed capillary refill. The abdomen is soft and nondistended without organomegaly. Tone is appropriately reduced for gestational age. There is no meconium staining of skin or cord, and no cyanosis differential between upper and lower extremities. The anterior fontanelle is soft and flat.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial imaging study best supports the suspected diagnosis in this preterm infant?
options: Abdominal radiograph
Chest radiograph
Echocardiogram first, before any imaging
Head ultrasound
stages.explanation: A chest radiograph is the key initial study and in surfactant deficiency classically shows low lung volumes with diffuse, fine reticulogranular ('ground-glass') opacities and air bronchograms. A head ultrasound (option A) screens for intraventricular hemorrhage but does not explain acute respiratory distress at birth. An echocardiogram first (option C) is not the initial step for a preterm infant with classic respiratory distress, though it may be used later to evaluate a patent ductus arteriosus or pulmonary hypertension. An abdominal radiograph (option D) is unrelated to the respiratory presentation. The chest film directly confirms the radiographic hallmark of respiratory distress syndrome.

stages.label: IMAGING & GASES
stages.content:
Chest radiograph: diffuse, fine ground-glass (reticulogranular) opacities with air bronchograms and low lung volumes
ABG (on CPAP FiO2 0.40): pH 7.24, PCO2 58 mmHg, PO2 46 mmHg
Blood glucose 62 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 9.4 ×10³/µL, platelets 210 ×10³/µL (no left shift)
Echocardiogram: structurally normal heart

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the underlying pathophysiology of this infant's respiratory distress syndrome?
options: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia compressing the lung
Retained fetal lung fluid (transient tachypnea) in this clinical context
Aspiration of meconium causing chemical pneumonitis
Surfactant deficiency causing alveolar collapse
stages.explanation: Respiratory distress syndrome results from a deficiency of pulmonary surfactant (produced by type II pneumocytes), leading to high alveolar surface tension, diffuse atelectasis, decreased compliance, and ventilation-perfusion mismatch—accounting for the ground-glass film and hypoxemia. Meconium aspiration (option A) requires meconium-stained fluid (absent here) and is a post-term, not preterm, problem. Transient tachypnea (option C) reflects retained lung fluid, typically milder, self-limited, and shows fluid in fissures rather than ground-glass atelectasis. Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (option D) shows bowel in the chest with mediastinal shift, not diffuse ground-glass opacities. Prematurity with incomplete antenatal steroids strongly supports surfactant deficiency.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given worsening hypercarbia and hypoxemia despite CPAP, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Give a fluid bolus of 60 mL/kg normal saline as monotherapy
Start inhaled nitric oxide as first-line therapy
Begin broad-spectrum antibiotics and stop respiratory support
Intubate and administer exogenous surfactant
stages.explanation: When an infant with RDS fails CPAP—rising PCO2, persistent hypoxemia, increasing oxygen need—the appropriate step is intubation with administration of exogenous surfactant, which rapidly improves compliance and oxygenation. Antibiotics may be started while ruling out sepsis, but stopping respiratory support (option B) would be harmful in a hypoxemic, hypercarbic infant. A large fluid bolus (option C) is not indicated and risks worsening a patent ductus arteriosus and pulmonary edema. Inhaled nitric oxide (option D) treats persistent pulmonary hypertension, not primary surfactant deficiency, and is not first-line for RDS. Surfactant replacement with respiratory support is the targeted treatment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Prolonged mechanical ventilation and oxygen exposure in this infant most predisposes to which chronic complication?
options: Cystic fibrosis
Bronchopulmonary dysplasia
Asthma cured by surfactant
Pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia—chronic lung disease defined by a continued oxygen requirement (typically at 36 weeks' postmenstrual age)—is a major complication of RDS, driven by lung immaturity, barotrauma/volutrauma, and oxygen toxicity from prolonged ventilation. Cystic fibrosis (option A) is a genetic disorder unrelated to ventilator exposure. Asthma is not 'cured' by surfactant (option C), and surfactant does not prevent later airway disease in that way. Pulmonary embolism (option D) is not a characteristic neonatal RDS complication. Strategies such as antenatal steroids, gentle ventilation, and surfactant aim to reduce the risk of bronchopulmonary dysplasia.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which antenatal intervention most effectively reduces the incidence and severity of RDS in anticipated preterm birth?
options: Routine elective cesarean at 30 weeks in this setting
A complete course of antenatal corticosteroids
Maternal antibiotics in all preterm labor in this setting
Maternal high-flow oxygen during labor in this setting
stages.explanation: A complete course of antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone or dexamethasone) given to mothers at risk of preterm delivery accelerates fetal lung maturation and surfactant production, significantly reducing the incidence and severity of RDS—this infant received only one of two doses. Maternal antibiotics (option A) treat infection/prolong latency in some settings but do not mature the lungs. Maternal high-flow oxygen during labor (option C) does not prevent RDS. Routine elective cesarean at 30 weeks (option D) would increase prematurity-related morbidity, not reduce RDS. Completing antenatal steroids is the most effective preventive strategy.

cases.diagnosis: Respiratory distress syndrome of prematurity (surfactant deficiency)
pearls: RDS = surfactant deficiency → diffuse atelectasis, low compliance; chest film shows low-volume ground-glass opacities with air bronchograms.
Manage with early CPAP; intubate and give exogenous surfactant when CPAP fails (rising CO2, persistent hypoxemia).
A complete antenatal corticosteroid course is the most effective prevention—accelerates fetal lung maturity.
Watch for bronchopulmonary dysplasia from prolonged oxygen/ventilation; use lung-protective strategies.

cases.title: Post-Term Newborn Born Through Stained Fluid
cases.chiefComplaint: A post-term newborn delivered through dark, stained amniotic fluid develops respiratory distress.
cases.history: A male infant is born at 42 weeks' gestation after a labor complicated by thick, dark green ('pea-soup') meconium-stained amniotic fluid and a non-reassuring fetal heart tracing. He was depressed at birth, requiring positive-pressure ventilation, with Apgars of 3 at 1 minute and 6 at 5 minutes. Shortly after resuscitation he develops grunting, tachypnea, and worsening cyanosis with high oxygen requirements. The mother is a 31-year-old whose pregnancy was uncomplicated until post-dates; there was no maternal fever, diabetes, or prolonged rupture of membranes, and GBS screening was negative. No known drug allergies; only prenatal vitamins were taken. Family history is noncontributory. The infant has received vitamin K. Birth weight is 3950 g. His skin and nails appear meconium-stained. He has not yet fed and has voided once. There were no antenatal anomalies on prenatal ultrasound, and the placenta appeared mature with meconium staining of the membranes.
cases.exam: Post-term, meconium-stained newborn in significant respiratory distress. He has tachypnea, grunting, nasal flaring, and intercostal retractions, with a barrel-shaped, overexpanded chest. Auscultation reveals coarse crackles and scattered rhonchi with patchy decreased air entry. Skin, nails, and umbilical cord are yellow-green from meconium. He appears cyanotic with a notable difference between preductal (right hand) and postductal (lower limb) saturations. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia and a loud, single second heart sound; capillary refill is 4 seconds with cool extremities. Abdomen is soft. Tone is mildly decreased. There is no scaphoid abdomen to suggest diaphragmatic hernia, and the trachea is midline.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What does the difference between higher preductal and lower postductal oxygen saturations most strongly suggest?
options: Methemoglobinemia as the first step today
Right-to-left shunting from persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn
Left-to-right shunting through a small VSD as the first step today
Pure airway obstruction without shunting as the first step today
stages.explanation: A preductal (right hand) saturation higher than the postductal (lower limb) saturation indicates right-to-left shunting across the ductus arteriosus, the hallmark of persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN)—a well-known complication of meconium aspiration. Deoxygenated blood from the pulmonary artery crosses the ductus into the descending aorta, lowering postductal saturations. A small left-to-right VSD shunt (option A) does not cause differential cyanosis. Pure airway obstruction without shunting (option C) would lower both saturations equally, not create a pre/postductal gradient. Methemoglobinemia (option D) causes cyanosis unresponsive to oxygen but not a ductal saturation split. The gradient points to PPHN.

stages.label: IMAGING & ECHO
stages.content:
Chest radiograph: patchy, asymmetric infiltrates with areas of hyperinflation and flattened diaphragms
No focal lobar consolidation; small right pneumothorax not present
ABG (on high FiO2): pH 7.20, PCO2 60 mmHg, PO2 38 mmHg
Echocardiogram: structurally normal heart with right-to-left shunting across the PDA and elevated pulmonary artery pressures (suprasystemic)
Preductal/postductal SpO2 gradient confirmed

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best unifies the meconium-stained fluid, patchy hyperinflated chest film, and differential cyanosis?
options: Congenital cyanotic heart disease (transposition of the great arteries)
Meconium aspiration syndrome complicated by persistent pulmonary hypertension
Respiratory distress syndrome from surfactant deficiency in this clinical context
Transient tachypnea of the newborn as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Meconium aspiration syndrome occurs when a (often post-term, stressed) infant inhales meconium-stained fluid, causing airway obstruction, air-trapping/hyperinflation, chemical pneumonitis, and surfactant inactivation—producing patchy infiltrates with hyperinflation. It is frequently complicated by PPHN, explaining the right-to-left ductal shunt and differential cyanosis confirmed on echo. RDS (option A) occurs in preterm infants with surfactant deficiency and ground-glass low-volume lungs, not this post-term hyperinflated picture. Transient tachypnea (option C) is mild and self-limited. Transposition (option D) is structural cyanotic heart disease, but the echo shows a structurally normal heart with PPHN physiology. MAS with PPHN fits all findings.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given confirmed PPHN with severe hypoxemia, which therapy specifically targets the pulmonary hypertension?
options: Systemic corticosteroids
Oral propranolol as monotherapy
Intravenous furosemide bolus
Inhaled nitric oxide
stages.explanation: Inhaled nitric oxide is a selective pulmonary vasodilator that reduces pulmonary vascular resistance and improves oxygenation in PPHN, and is the targeted therapy alongside optimal oxygenation/ventilation and sometimes ECMO for refractory cases. Oral propranolol (option B), a nonselective beta-blocker, would be harmful—it does not treat PPHN and can worsen hemodynamics. A furosemide bolus (option C) addresses fluid overload, not pulmonary vasoconstriction, and could worsen perfusion if the infant is hypovolemic. Systemic corticosteroids (option D) are not a primary treatment for PPHN. Inhaled nitric oxide directly addresses the elevated pulmonary pressures driving the right-to-left shunt.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Air-trapping from meconium plugging makes this infant especially vulnerable to which acute complication?
options: Biliary atresia
Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
Air leak such as pneumothorax
Necrotizing enterocolitis
stages.explanation: Meconium creates a ball-valve airway obstruction with distal air-trapping and hyperinflation, predisposing to alveolar overdistension and rupture—producing air leaks such as pneumothorax and pneumomediastinum; a sudden deterioration with decreased breath sounds and rising oxygen need should prompt evaluation for pneumothorax. Necrotizing enterocolitis (option A) is a gut complication more typical of preterm infants and not a direct consequence of meconium air-trapping. Biliary atresia (option C) is an unrelated cholestatic disorder presenting weeks later. Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (option D) presents around 3-6 weeks with projectile vomiting. The mechanical air-trapping of MAS makes pneumothorax the key acute risk.

stages.label: DELIVERY ROOM
stages.question: For a meconium-stained newborn who is non-vigorous at birth, what does current resuscitation guidance recommend?
options: Follow standard neonatal resuscitation, prioritizing effective ventilation
Immediate ECMO cannulation in the delivery room as the immediate next step
Routine intubation and tracheal suctioning of every meconium-stained infant in this setting
Vigorous chest compressions before any ventilation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Current neonatal resuscitation guidance no longer recommends routine endotracheal suctioning of meconium-stained newborns—even non-vigorous ones. Instead, resuscitation follows the standard algorithm with emphasis on establishing effective ventilation; intubation/suctioning is reserved for airway obstruction that impedes ventilation. Routine intubation and tracheal suction of every meconium-stained infant (option A) reflects outdated practice and delays effective ventilation. Vigorous chest compressions before ventilation (option C) violates the resuscitation sequence—ventilation comes first. Delivery-room ECMO (option D) is not a standard initial step. Prioritizing effective ventilation per the standard algorithm is the recommended approach.

cases.diagnosis: Meconium aspiration syndrome with persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn
pearls: MAS occurs in stressed, often post-term infants who aspirate meconium—causing airway obstruction, air-trapping, chemical pneumonitis, and surfactant inactivation.
A higher preductal than postductal SpO2 (differential cyanosis) signals right-to-left ductal shunting from PPHN.
Treat PPHN with inhaled nitric oxide and optimal oxygenation/ventilation; consider ECMO if refractory.
Air-trapping predisposes to pneumothorax; routine tracheal suctioning of meconium-stained infants is no longer recommended—prioritize ventilation.

cases.title: Preemie With a Constant Whooshing Murmur
cases.chiefComplaint: A premature infant develops a continuous murmur and bounding pulses while recovering in the NICU.
cases.history: A girl born at 28 weeks' gestation, now 6 days old, is being managed in the NICU for respiratory distress syndrome treated with surfactant and CPAP. Over the past day the team notes she is increasingly difficult to wean from respiratory support, with rising oxygen requirements and several apneic episodes. On rounds a continuous 'machinery-like' murmur is appreciated, along with bounding peripheral pulses. She had been making steady progress until now. Birth weight was 1050 g, appropriate for gestational age. The mother received a partial course of antenatal steroids. There were no maternal infections, no prolonged rupture of membranes, and GBS screening was negative; no known drug allergies. The infant is on caffeine for apnea of prematurity and is receiving intravenous fluids with trophic feeds. Family history is noncontributory. She has had no fever, and recent sepsis evaluation was negative. Her hematocrit and electrolytes have been stable, and renal function is normal.
cases.exam: Preterm infant in mild respiratory distress with mild retractions. Cardiac exam reveals a hyperdynamic precordium and a continuous machinery-like murmur best heard at the left upper sternal border/infraclavicular area, extending through systole into diastole. Peripheral pulses are bounding with a widened pulse pressure. The point of maximal impulse is laterally displaced. Lungs have scattered crackles. The liver edge is palpable 2 cm below the costal margin. Capillary refill is brisk at 2 seconds, and extremities are warm. The anterior fontanelle is soft. There is no differential cyanosis between upper and lower extremities, and femoral pulses are easily palpable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding on this exam most specifically suggests a hemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus?
options: A palpable liver edge alone as the first step today
Continuous machinery murmur with bounding pulses
Scattered pulmonary crackles as the first step today
Mild intercostal retractions as the first step today
stages.explanation: A continuous 'machinery' murmur combined with bounding pulses and a widened pulse pressure reflects the diastolic run-off of blood from the aorta through the ductus into the lower-pressure pulmonary circulation—classic for a hemodynamically significant PDA. Pulmonary crackles (option A) are nonspecific and could reflect RDS, pulmonary edema, or fluid overload. A palpable liver edge alone (option C) can be normal in neonates and is nonspecific without other heart-failure signs. Mild retractions (option D) reflect respiratory distress generally, not PDA specifically. The wide pulse pressure with bounding pulses and a continuous murmur is the most specific bedside indicator of a significant PDA.

stages.label: ECHOCARDIOGRAM
stages.content:
Echocardiogram: patent ductus arteriosus, 2.5 mm, with continuous left-to-right shunt
Left atrium-to-aortic root ratio elevated (LA:Ao 1.8)
Dilated left atrium and left ventricle with volume overload
No other structural defect; pulmonary pressures not suprasystemic
Chest radiograph: mild cardiomegaly with increased pulmonary vascular markings

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the direction and consequence of the shunt across this preterm infant's ductus arteriosus?
options: Left-to-right shunt causing pulmonary overcirculation
No shunt; the murmur is innocent as the primary diagnosis
Bidirectional shunt with Eisenmenger physiology in this clinical context
Right-to-left shunt causing differential cyanosis
stages.explanation: In a significant PDA without pulmonary hypertension, blood shunts left-to-right (aorta to pulmonary artery) because systemic pressure exceeds pulmonary pressure, causing pulmonary overcirculation and volume overload of the left atrium and ventricle—reflected here by the elevated LA:Ao ratio and chamber dilation. A right-to-left shunt with differential cyanosis (option A) would require suprasystemic pulmonary pressures (PPHN/Eisenmenger physiology), which the echo excludes. Eisenmenger physiology (option C) is a late, irreversible complication not present in a 6-day-old. An innocent murmur (option D) is excluded by the echocardiographic shunt and chamber overload. The left-to-right shunt explains the failure to wean and heart-failure signs.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After conservative measures, what is the preferred pharmacologic treatment to close a symptomatic PDA in a preterm infant?
options: A COX inhibitor such as indomethacin
Digoxin as initial therapy
Inhaled nitric oxide as initial therapy
Prostaglandin E1 infusion as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Ductal patency is maintained by prostaglandins, so a cyclooxygenase inhibitor—indomethacin or ibuprofen (and in some centers acetaminophen)—is used to promote closure of a symptomatic PDA in preterm infants after conservative measures (fluid restriction, optimizing respiratory support). Prostaglandin E1 (option A) keeps the ductus open and is used in duct-dependent congenital heart disease—exactly the opposite of what is needed here. Digoxin (option C) is not the treatment to close a PDA. Inhaled nitric oxide (option D) treats pulmonary hypertension, not PDA closure. When pharmacologic therapy fails or is contraindicated, surgical ligation or transcatheter closure is considered.

stages.label: SAFETY
stages.question: Before giving indomethacin, which parameters are most important to check given its known adverse effects?
options: Thyroid function tests as the immediate next step
Serum ceruloplasmin and copper as the immediate next step
Renal function , platelet count,
Sweat chloride concentration as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Indomethacin (a COX inhibitor) can impair renal perfusion (reducing urine output and raising creatinine), inhibit platelet function (bleeding risk), and is associated with gastrointestinal complications including necrotizing enterocolitis and bleeding—so renal function, platelet count, urine output, and GI status must be assessed and monitored, and the drug avoided with active bleeding, significant renal dysfunction, thrombocytopenia, or NEC. Ceruloplasmin/copper (option B) relate to Wilson disease, irrelevant here. Thyroid tests (option C) and sweat chloride (option D) are unrelated to indomethacin safety. Checking renal, hematologic, and GI parameters directly targets the drug's principal toxicities.

cases.diagnosis: Hemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus of prematurity
pearls: Suspect a significant PDA in a preterm infant who is hard to wean and has a continuous machinery murmur, bounding pulses, and widened pulse pressure.
Echo shows left-to-right shunting with left-heart volume overload (elevated LA:Ao ratio) when pulmonary pressures are normal.
Prostaglandins keep the duct open—use a COX inhibitor (indomethacin/ibuprofen) to close it; PGE1 is for duct-dependent lesions (opposite goal).
Before indomethacin, check renal function, platelets, urine output, and GI status; avoid with bleeding, renal failure, or NEC.

cases.title: Infant With Poor Feeding and Fast Breathing
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report he tires and sweats while feeding, breathes fast, and isn't gaining weight well.
cases.history: A 4-month-old boy is brought to clinic because he has been feeding poorly, taking small volumes, sweating and becoming short of breath during feeds, and tiring before finishing a bottle. His parents note rapid breathing even at rest and inadequate weight gain over the past two months; his weight has fallen from the 50th to the 10th percentile. He was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery, and was well at birth with no murmur noted on the newborn exam. There has been no cyanosis, fever, or cough. He takes a standard infant formula and a vitamin D supplement; no other medications and no known drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. Family history is unremarkable for congenital heart disease. He lives with both parents. Parents recall that a soft murmur was first mentioned at his 2-month visit. He has had no hospitalizations and is otherwise developing appropriately, though he fatigues easily during play and feeds.
cases.exam: Thin, tachypneic infant with mild subcostal retractions and an active precordium. Cardiac exam reveals a harsh, grade III-IV/VI holosystolic murmur best heard at the left lower sternal border, with a palpable thrill; a soft mid-diastolic rumble is appreciated at the apex. The point of maximal impulse is mildly displaced. The liver edge is palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin. Lungs have fine bibasilar crackles. Peripheral pulses are normal and symmetric with no radio-femoral delay; capillary refill is 2 seconds and there is no cyanosis or clubbing. He is diaphoretic. There is no differential cyanosis, and femoral pulses are easily palpable bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which murmur characteristic best localizes this infant's lesion?
options: A harsh holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border with a thrill
A fixed, widely split S2 with a systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border
A crescendo-decrescendo murmur radiating to the carotids in this clinical context
A continuous machinery murmur at the left infraclavicular area
stages.explanation: A harsh holosystolic (pansystolic) murmur loudest at the left lower sternal border, often with a palpable thrill, is the classic finding of a ventricular septal defect, produced by high-velocity flow across the defect throughout systole. A continuous machinery murmur (option A) describes a patent ductus arteriosus. A fixed, widely split S2 with an upper-sternal-border ejection murmur (option C) is characteristic of an atrial septal defect. A crescendo-decrescendo murmur radiating to the carotids (option D) suggests aortic stenosis or outflow obstruction. The pansystolic LLSB murmur with thrill points specifically to a VSD; the apical diastolic rumble reflects increased flow across the mitral valve from the large shunt.

stages.label: ECHO & IMAGING
stages.content:
Echocardiogram: large perimembranous ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunt
Dilated left atrium and left ventricle (volume overload)
Elevated pulmonary blood flow; pulmonary pressures mildly elevated
Chest radiograph: cardiomegaly with increased pulmonary vascular markings
ECG: biventricular hypertrophy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Why does this VSD present at 4 months rather than at birth, and what is its physiologic consequence?
options: Pulmonary vascular resistance falls over the first weeks, increasing the left-to-right shunt
Symptoms are unrelated to the defect size as the primary diagnosis
It only forms after birth from valve degeneration as the primary diagnosis
The murmur reflects a right-to-left shunt causing cyanosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: At birth, pulmonary vascular resistance is high, limiting left-to-right flow across a VSD, so murmurs and symptoms may be minimal. As pulmonary vascular resistance physiologically falls over the first weeks to months, the left-to-right shunt increases, causing pulmonary overcirculation and volume overload that manifests as the classic infant heart-failure picture—poor feeding, diaphoresis with feeds, tachypnea, hepatomegaly, and failure to thrive—explaining the 4-month presentation. The defect is congenital, not acquired from valve degeneration (option A). The shunt is left-to-right and acyanotic (option C), consistent with normal SpO2. Symptom severity correlates strongly with defect size and shunt magnitude (option D is false). Falling PVR unmasking the shunt is the key concept.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial medical management of this infant's heart failure symptoms?
options: Beta-blocker monotherapy and fluid restriction alone as initial therapy
Diuretics with nutritional support/caloric fortification,
Immediate surgical closure for all VSDs regardless of size as initial therapy
Prostaglandin E1 infusion as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial management of a symptomatic VSD with heart failure is medical: diuretics such as furosemide to reduce pulmonary congestion, calorie-dense feeds/nutritional support to address failure to thrive, and consideration of afterload reduction (e.g., an ACE inhibitor) to decrease the left-to-right shunt. Many VSDs (especially muscular) close or shrink spontaneously, so immediate surgical closure for all (option A) is not warranted; surgery is reserved for large defects with intractable failure, failure to thrive, or pulmonary hypertension. Prostaglandin E1 (option C) maintains ductal patency and has no role in VSD. Beta-blocker monotherapy with fluid restriction alone (option D) does not address congestion or growth. Diuretics plus nutrition is the appropriate first step.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If a large VSD is left untreated for years, which serious complication may develop?
options: Rheumatic mitral stenosis as the leading concern
Spontaneous resolution with no risk as the leading concern
Coarctation of the aorta as the leading concern
Eisenmenger syndrome from pulmonary hypertension with shunt reversal
stages.explanation: A large, uncorrected VSD causes chronic pulmonary overcirculation, leading to progressive pulmonary vascular disease and pulmonary hypertension; eventually pulmonary pressures may exceed systemic pressures, reversing the shunt to right-to-left—Eisenmenger syndrome—producing cyanosis and rendering the defect inoperable. Coarctation of the aorta (option B) is a separate congenital lesion, not a consequence of a VSD. Although small VSDs commonly close spontaneously, a large defect does not reliably resolve and carries real risk (option C is false). Rheumatic mitral stenosis (option D) results from rheumatic fever, unrelated to VSD. Preventing irreversible pulmonary vascular disease is why significant VSDs are repaired before Eisenmenger physiology develops.

cases.diagnosis: Ventricular septal defect with congestive heart failure
pearls: A harsh holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border with a thrill is classic for VSD; an apical diastolic rumble signals a large shunt.
VSDs often present at 4-8 weeks as pulmonary vascular resistance falls and the left-to-right shunt grows—causing feeding intolerance, diaphoresis, tachypnea, and failure to thrive.
Initial therapy is medical: diuretics, caloric fortification, and afterload reduction; many small/muscular VSDs close spontaneously.
Untreated large VSDs risk pulmonary hypertension and Eisenmenger syndrome (shunt reversal, cyanosis)—repair before it becomes irreversible.
